Top-secret intelligence alleges some MPs aided foreign interference. This might be a way to publicly name them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/top-secret-intelligence-alleges-some-mps-aided/docview/3065620390/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top-secret+intelligence+alleges+some+MPs+aided+foreign+interference.+This+might+be+a+way+to+publicly+name+them&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - MPs named by top-secret intelligence as aiding foreign interference in Canadian politics continue to go unidentified by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government, despite opposition demands to say publicly who they are.
But at least one constitutional expert says it's possible - and entirely in line with long-established rules - for certain MPs to simply disclose the names from the floor of the House of Commons.
Opposition parliamentarians - and even some Liberals - say not providing their names casts a shadow over all of their peers, and is patently unfair to Canadians who have a right to know.
The issue is now evolving into a partisan free-for-all.
As Trudeau's Liberals are accused of hiding the names to protect themselves, they accuse Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre of deliberately refusing to accept the necessary security clearance to view the intelligence, allowing him to ignore the facts in a bid to score political points.
Politics aside, there is a distinction between MPs on the powerful security committee that published the bombshell allegations this week, and others who don't sit on the committee and learn the information through separate security briefings.
Here's a look.
Can the MPs on the committee say the names?
The report comes from the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, known as NSICOP, which is made up of MPs and senators from all parties.
The committee's responsibilities are enshrined in law. Among the requirements: that members swear an oath they "will not communicate or use without due authority any information obtained in confidence by me in that capacity." Further, the members are all permanently bound to secrecy under the Security of Information Act.
So, if any member of the committee were to disclose publicly what they know, they could face years in prison.
This legal requirement for secrecy also extends to an area where MPs are typically shielded from prosecution: the floor of the House of Commons.
MPs have several rights deemed necessary to allow them to fully do their jobs in Parliament, known as parliamentary privileges. Among them is a right to free speech with total immunity from prosecution or civil liability.
But section 12 of the law establishing NSICOP removes that right from the MPs on the committee as it relates to those duties. If they disclose any information before Parliament, they can be prosecuted.
Whether that's constitutional might end up before Canada's top court.
In 2022, the Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled in favour of law professor Ryan Alford's argument that section 12 is unconstitutional, on the grounds that if parliamentary privilege is to be limited, it requires a constitutional amendment.
In April of this year, the Ontario Appeal court overturned that judgment. Alford told the Star he will ask the Supreme Court to hear an appeal.
In theory, MPs on NSICOP could test the law by standing up in the Commons and just saying what they know - which in turn would test the willingness of the government to prosecute them.
The MPs or senators would be disobeying the prime minister and ignoring the law, Alford said.
"It would be not merely an act of conscience," he said .
"It would be an act of civil disobedience, one that would be taken very seriously. I don't think they should be in that position, especially given the fact that if I'm correct and the Supreme Court agrees with me that that section of the statute is unconstitutional."
Could other MPs spill the beans?
The situation is entirely different for MPs who don't sit on NSICOP, according to Carleton University professor and constitutional expert Philippe Lagassé.
The law trumping parliamentary privilege for those on the committee doesn't apply to those who don't sit on it, he explained. That means, should an MP like the prime minister or an opposition leader get a security briefing that includes the names of parliamentarians suspected of collaborating with hostile foreign governments, that MP could disclose the names to the public, Lagassé said.
All they'd have to do is walk into the House of Commons and say them out loud.
"Ironically enough, the only ones who can't say the names are members of NSICOP, because their legislation removes that privilege from them," he said. "If you had another parliamentarian who is not a member of NSICOP, who learned the information, then they could disclose it and be shielded from prosecution."
Asked if there is any debate about whether that's true, Lagassé said it's unambiguous.
"This is very clear," he said.
Which MPs don't sit on NSICOP but might know the names?
It depends what they're told in top secret briefings that people like Trudeau and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc receive from security officials. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has also said he's willing to get the required security clearance to have access to the intelligence so he could know what's at stake, as he has in similar past situations.
Poilievre - who has called on the government to release the names - has not been willing to take those briefings, claiming he doesn't want to accept the information if it means he can't share or act on what he knows. Instead, the Conservatives argue LeBlanc could disclose the names under the public disclosure provisions of the Security of Information Act.
The law says if someone discloses information because they are acting in the public interest, they can be spared.
But the government has suggested it is not interested in unilaterally disclosing the names.
LeBlanc told a Commons committee Thursday morning he has no intention of violating the Security of Information Act by disclosing what he knows. He's also downplayed the committee findings by suggesting that unspecified information in the report is uncorroborated.
"The idea there is a perfect list of names that is entirely reliable that should be released to the public is simply irresponsible," LeBlanc told the committee.
Alford, a professor at the Bora Laskin faculty of law, argues that too often it's only by MPs using their privilege that the government's claims of national security can be tested.
He points to the years-long battle over documents related to a security breach at Winnipeg's National Microbiology Laboratory which the government said were too sensitive to release to a House of Commons committee.
That fight went all the way to Federal Court, and ultimately a panel of judges was set up to review what could be disclosed.
They concluded the information was being withheld to protect people from embarrassment for failures of policy and implementation, not national security. The documents were released.
"If things really are so confidential, they shouldn't be received," Alford said.
Could we find out about the MPs' names another way?
Another way the names could be disclosed is if charges are laid against any of the MPs alleged to have collaborated with foreign powers. David McGuinty, chair of NSICOP, told reporters this week that it's now up to the RCMP to investigate .
But the NSICOP report doesn't conclude whether what the MPs or parliamentarians were doing was illegal, noting that charges would be hard to lay given long-standing issues around how classified information can be used in judicial processes.
In a statement, the RCMP said it's not been given the entirety of the information in the report.
It doesn't specify whether it means the information about MPs or something else - the report is broader than just those bombshell allegations.
But the national police force also said it won't confirm or deny it is investigating anybody.
"The RCMP must exercise significant caution with respect to public statements related to ongoing investigations," the statement says.
"An RCMP confirmation of such an investigation has the potential to cause damage to reputations prior to meeting an appropriate level of proof, or to interfere with an ongoing investigation."
CAPTION: Liberal MP David McGuinty, chair of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, responds to questions from reporters before heading into a meeting of the Liberal caucus in Ottawa on June 5, 2024.

Subject: Civil disobedience; Committees; Investigations; National security; Politics; Prime ministers; Immunity from prosecution; Security clearances

People: McGuinty, David Joseph

Oosterhoff charged up about cabinet promotion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oosterhoff-charged-up-about-cabinet-promotion/docview/3065620377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oosterhoff+charged+up+about+cabinet+promotion&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sam Oosterhoff said he's looking forward to the new challenge.
The Niagara West Tory MPP has been named associate minister of energy-intensive industries as a result of a Thursday cabinet shuffle by Premier Doug Ford.
"It's a sign of trust the premier has placed on me and I don't take that lightly,"Oosterhoff said while attending an Ontario Trillium Foundation funding announcement for a new boiler at the Niagara West YMCA in Grimsby Friday morning.
Oosterhoff said his job will be to oversee upgrades to Ontario's electrical grid and to bring power to industry.
"To get the energy for where it's being produced, whether it's Bruce or Niagara Falls or Darlington, and getting it to the places that need it," Oosterhoff said.
"Whether that's ArcelorMittal Dofasco changing over from coal fire to electric arc furnace, whether that's the new Volkswagen plant or Stellantis plant, they need vast amounts of energy, and we need to have the infrastructure working with local utilities (and) local municipalities to actually get the energy that they need there."
Oosterhoff said he will be working with Stephen Lecce, who was moved from the education portfolio and is now minister of energy and electrification.
Some political observers have suggested the cabinet shuffle is a prelude to a provincial election, but Oosterhoff wouldn't go there.
"I'm not going to speculate on when the election would be," he said.
CAPTION: Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff is now associate minister of energy-intensive industries.

Subject: Energy; Education portfolios

June 7: Readers sound off on 'beyond outrageous' washroom project and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-7-readers-sound-off-on-beyond-outrageous/docview/3065368247/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+7%3A+Readers+sound+off+on+%27beyond+outrageous%27+washroom+project+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Washroom project 'beyond outrageous'Re: Reconstruction of torched Hamilton park washroom to cost up to $1.3 million (June 4)
Picking up my June 4 Spectator, I was neither shocked nor surprised to read that our elected spendthrifts at city hall are planning to spend $1.3 million to replace the washroom at Woodlands Park. This is beyond outrageous.
Curious, I began looking at local real estate listings (including Spec Homes) to gauge the quantity and quality of homes for sale in this price range. There are many luxurious homes for sale and the purchase price includes ownership of the land. The city already owns this park and the services to make it function are right there.
This unjustifiably expensive project is emblematic of everything that is wrong at city hall and our current council. When it's completed, who is going to ensure that the facility is not again defaced, damaged and probably burned down again? Oh well, Hamilton taxpayers have deep pockets, don't they councillors?
Cameron Stringer, Hamilton
Fire could've been avoided
This fire would never (have) happened if a councillor had listened to the neighbourhood on that washroom. We complained about the barbecue and the propane tanks that homeless people were using. That very morning pictures were sent to councillors and the mayor of a barbecue going into the washroom. But nothing was done. Then a fire happens. Now, wasted taxes.
Cindy Addley, Hamilton
Council needs to pinch pennies
I think we need a Hazel McCallion at Hamilton City Hall. The citizens of this city deserve a leader who knows how to pinch pennies and not throw taxpayers' money around like a drunken sailor. Are city councillors living in reality? Why on earth would you waste so much money on fixing a washroom, even if it has a change room, when citizens are going hungry, living in tents and dying from the heat? Let's not forget the wasted money spent on endless studies designed to ensure the feasibility of finding other ways to tax us all. City council, please wake up and smell the coffee.
Elena Trkulja, Hamilton
Reconsider washroom rebuild
The cost and timeline for the Woodlands Park washroom is insane. Many more questions need to be answered before this project goes ahead. City council needs to stop this before it starts. The building has been sitting, untended, since January. Perhaps it's not a necessity and if it cannot be rebuilt for $500,000 and not cost the taxpayers anything, the rebuild should not happen. The washroom facility will also need security 24-7 to make sure it isn't burnt down again while being used by the homeless that are in the encampment there.
I cannot believe they are also building a splash pad in this park. With the drug paraphernalia and present environment, I sure wouldn't take my kids there.
Now that the nice weather is here we need to address the growing number of encampments in our city parks, they are getting out of hand.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Fund people instead of washroom
It doesn't matter how the geniuses at city hall spin it. The whole affair is absolutely ridiculous! You could build a mansion anywhere in the province for $1.3 million! How about don't build anything there and spend it on food and shelter for those who need it?
Rob Millen, Hamilton
No wonder taxes are rising
One councillor says the cost is ridiculous and a second councillor says the price is an absolute high number. And Coun. Nrinder Nann said "in this economy" she was "not surprised" by the seven-figure cost estimate. Is Coun. Nann's comment because of construction cost experience or her experience spending taxpayer money?
We need to ask why it is costing a ridiculous seven figures to rebuild a washroom. And we have a city official stating that 12 months is a realistic schedule to rebuild a washroom. Is there any doubt why our taxes keep climbing when it seems no one at city hall cares enough to question the schedule or ridiculous costs?
Derek Lawson, Hamilton
Price controls don't workRe: Federal NDP eye price cap on staples (June 4)
It seems the battle between rhetoric and the truth is never ending. Things like price caps and wage and price controls have all been proven by history to be failing policies. Furthermore, they worsen the situation eventually. Notwithstanding Jagmeet Singh's crocodile tears about grocery prices, he is simply lowering himself to the level of Donald Trump by giving voice to such clichés as corporate price gouging. The real culprit is state intervention in the economy which causes distortions in markets.
Dan Riga, Burlington
Thanks for telling your storyRe: 'You good?': Hamilton police officer's suicide inspires colleague to share story (June 5)
What a delightful change to see Wednesday's newspaper without any crime stories on the front page. Perhaps you may like to make a habit of this.
Kudos to Nicole O'Reilly for a very sensitive article on Pete Wiesner and making this front and centre for all to read and hopefully learn from.
Kudos to Pete Wiesner for telling his story. Your presence with our vulnerable people will be missed. I hope that the person who follows you is equally filled with compassion. Good luck in your next chapter.
Suki Garson, Dundas
Aberdeen Avenue a sign of the timesRe: CAA releases list of top 10 worst roads in Ontario and you might be surprised with the number 1 pick (May 28)
Considering that Aberdeen Avenue (recently deemed the worst road in the province) has been allowed to deteriorate to such a deplorable state, perhaps it's time that all councillors be required to publish a platform outlining their public priorities to enable residents to make a more informed choice at the ballot box. There once was a time when it was presumed the preservation of basic infrastructure was one of the primary responsibilities of municipal governments.
We now seem to live in an era where personal agendas take precedence over citizens' reasonable expectations. Given our current crisis in both taxes and revenue, more transparency is required before councillors are sent to city hall to represent the needs and concerns of their constituents, too many of which are totally ignored.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
CAPTION: The city says the Woodlands Park washrooms should be open again at the end of 2025. The work could cost as much as $1.3 million, which has Spectator readers fuming.

Subject: Municipal government; Construction costs; Taxes; Price regulations; Councils; Homeless people; Wage & price controls; Cost estimates

Doug Ford's Tories end legislative session early and extend break till October

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-end-legislative-session-early/docview/3065467294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+end+legislative+session+early+and+extend+break+till+October&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Summer is arriving a week early for MPPs - and ending six weeks later - after Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives ended the legislative session Thursday and extended the break.
"I know that there are some out there - and all parties will agree - that (think) everyone's sitting on the beach for the next few months. That's the furthest from the truth," Ford told the legislature.
"I know each and every one of you will be out in your ridings with your constituents and visiting events within your ridings, answering calls," said the premier, who is pondering a cabinet shuffle.
MPPs, who had been expected to resume their legislative duties Sept. 9, will now return to Queen's Park on Oct. 21.
"They can't get out of here fast enough," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, noting the unusually long break after a session that saw several key policy reversals, including the $8.28 billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under investigation by the RCMP.
"They obviously don't want to spend any time here being held to account."
In terms of political strategy, the extended break also sets the table for Ford to prorogue the legislature in the fall, which would allow the Tories to deliver a throne speech outlining their agenda ahead of a possible election in spring 2025.
The unusually long recess comes two weeks after the premier dropped hints of an early election before the next scheduled vote in June 2026 despite a housing shortage, overcrowded hospital emergency rooms, temporary ER closures in some towns, a shortage of family doctors and the cost of living after years of high inflation.
But Ford is enjoying a healthy lead in the latest Abacus monthly tracking poll for the Star. His Tories were at 39 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for the NDP under Stiles and nine per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
Crombie said Ford was in a rush to adjourn the legislature because of the heat he's taking over what the Liberals estimate is the $1-billion cost of putting beer, wine, cider and mixed drinks into convenience stores a year ahead of schedule.
"What kind of leader prioritizes this when you can't get a family doctor ... when your grocery bill is through the roof and you're scraping to make ends meet?" said Crombie, calling the deal a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
"They're utterly failing to address the housing affordability crisis, and so I can understand why this government doesn't want to come back," Green Leader Mike Schreiner told reporters.
The early rising of the house means a universally popular bill to protect children from drowning remains stalled.
Proposed by Progressive Conservative MPP Goldie Ghamari, it would require life jackets for children 12 and under while out on the water, making Ontario the first province to do so.
The bill is named Joshua's Law (Lifejackets for Life), in honour of a young boy from her Ottawa-area riding of Carleton who died in a boating accident.
"It's mandatory to have life jackets for everyone on a boat, but it's not mandatory to wear them," Ghamari said, adding she was initially "caught off guard because I didn't realize they weren't mandatory for children. I think it's just something that everyone assumed."
She said about 80 per cent of people who died in boating accidents in Canada between 2008 and 2017 weren't wearing a personal flotation device, and another five per cent were wearing one improperly.
The bill has the support of all parties, and recently passed second reading unanimously.
It is already the law in a number of U.S. states, as well as in Calgary after a bylaw there mandated life jackets for all ages when in a boat or other watercraft. Violators face a mandatory court appearance and a fine of up to $500.
Ghamari's bill would levy $200 fines against parents or guardians, but would exempt children who are competing in activities such as supervised rowing races, or when in an enclosed cabin on board.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a convenience store in Toronto on Dec. 14, 2023.

Subject: Families & family life; Convenience stores; Legislatures; Boating accidents & safety

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores

People: Schreiner, Mike

Are grocery prices starting to fall in Ontario?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-grocery-prices-starting-fall-ontario/docview/3065003240/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+grocery+prices+starting+to+fall+in+Ontario%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After feeling the pinch at the grocery store for more than a year, some prices are starting to come back down.
According to Yahoo! Finance last month, grocery store prices were likely starting to flatten out, and potentially returning to pre-pandemic prices.
"We are starting to see supply chains recover, a stabilization - and in some cases reduction - in commodity prices and shipping costs beginning to return to pre-pandemic rates," Loblaw Companies Ltd. said in a March press release.
Looking at the Consumer Price Index, it does show most food items dropped in price from February to March. However, some items rose again in April.
"Using only scanner data (also known as point-of-sale data), Statistics Canada produces the monthly average retail prices for selected products," it said on its website.
May grocery store prices were not yet available.
Many staple items like fruits and vegetables, milk and butter were down slightly in March, and remain less than what people paid in February.
But some items, like infant formula, chicken breasts and salmon had increased in price.
"From April 2021 to April 2024, prices for food purchased from stores increased 21.4 per cent," Statistics Canada said on its website.
As fresh produce becomes available in Ontario in the next few months, prices may change again, as area retailers switch to supporting local-grown goods.
NDP proposes grocery price cap
On June 4, federal NDP leader Jagmeet Singh tried to get the government to approve a price cap on grocery store staples.
"We're talking about an essential basket of goods that should be affordable for Canadians, and we want to force grocery stores to do that, otherwise we will impose a price cap," Singh said at a press conference.
However, on June 5 the Liberals and Conservatives rejected the idea. Already, the government is working on a code of conduct with the large grocery chains.
Here's what you need to know about the code of conduct:
"People need help now. Food bank usage is at a 35-year high and people are putting groceries back on the shelf," said Singh in a press release. "The government's approach-asking grocery giants to sign onto a voluntary, toothless code of conduct and asking them nicely to lower prices-is just not working. Instead of taking meaningful, urgent action to lower prices for Canadians, the Liberals and Conservatives continue to give public money to grocery CEOs."
CAPTION: As fresh produce becomes available in Ontario in the next few months, prices may change again, as area retailers switch to supporting local-grown goods..

Subject: Consumer Price Index; Pandemics; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumer Price Index Grocery stores

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Title: Are grocery prices starting to fall in Ontario?

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-grocery-prices-starting-fall-ontario/docview/3065003240/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 6 of 500

June 6: Remembering D-Day, beer convenience isn't a crisis and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-6-remembering-d-day-beer-convenience-isnt/docview/3065003223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+6%3A+Remembering+D-Day%2C+beer+convenience+isn%27t+a+crisis+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Remembering D-Day
Today, June 6, 2024, marks the 80th anniversary of the D-Day invasion at Normandy, France. That morning, thousands of young soldiers from Canada, the U.K., the U.S. and other Allied nations landed along the coast of Normandy on beaches code named Juno, Gold, Sword, Omaha and Utah. They landed, in many cases under heavy enemy fire, but did not waver. Their goal was to defeat Nazism and fascism and liberate Europe from Hitler and his henchmen. Many fell that day. Many more were wounded, but they believed that what they were doing was just and right for a civilized world that was worth fighting for. As President Franklin Roosevelt said at the time of invasion, "They fight to end conquest. They fight to liberate."
Much has changed in the past 80 years on our planet, but one thing still stands out. We owe the utmost gratitude and respect to that generation who on the morning of June 6 landed on enemy beaches at Normandy knowing that they might not survive the day. So, all of us should pause today for a few moments and thank those young soldiers, many who made the ultimate sacrifice that day so that our world would be a much better place to live for future generations.
Lest we forget.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Will new washroom burn, too?
Re: Reconstruction of torched Hamilton park washroom to cost up to $1.3 million (June 4)
More wasted money! Until the encampment issue in this park is resolved and removed, nothing should be done in this park. The homeless will likely just burn down another washroom and as long as they are in the park the washrooms will be unusable by others, as will the splash pad they plan to put there.
Don't worry that the burnt building is an eyesore, the whole city is getting that way.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
A great man in turbulent times
Re: What if Bobby Kennedy had lived? (June 4)
Thank you, Craig Wallace, for reminding us all of the memory of Robert F. Kennedy. Also, not just of the man, but of the turbulent times. Yes, what if he had lived indeed. Our future would most certainly have been different. A great man, I believe, recalled by a finely worded and informative article.
Wray Brown, Dundas
Direct funds to health care
So, who gave Doug Ford permission to spend $225 million of taxpayers' money to get out of the Beer Store contract? Doesn't he work for the people of Ontario? I don't want my taxes going to pay off the Beer Store when its contract expires next year. We need a long-term old-age home with all the amenities way more than we need to waste millions of taxpayers dollars to pay out a contract that expires next year. How disgusting that Mr. Ford is willing to waste taxpayers' money on beer rather than helping our aging population.
Robin Kovljenic, Hamilton
Beer convenience isn't a crisis
Doug Ford claims the PCs are the party of business in Ontario. What a joke. No business leader in their right mind would unnecessarily spend what amounts to nearly $975 million ($225-million payout and $375-million rebate to the privately-run Beer Store, $300 million in licence rebates to retailers and a $74-million wholesale discount to the grocery giants) to put beer and wine into convenience stores. There's already lots of access to beer and wine. No crisis here.
But we do have major crises in housing supply and affordability, in homelessness, in climate disaster and in health care (front-line burnout, opioid addiction, privatization of LTC and other health services).
The Ford government needs to stop this nonsense. Stop wasting taxpayers' money on a shameless grab for low-hanging votes and start putting it toward solving the real crises we Ontarians are living with day in and out.
Carol Greene, Hamilton
A chance to revamp returns
Doug Ford is getting rid of the Beer Store almost-monopoly ahead of schedule with penalties for the early break from the contractual obligations. Then convenience stores will start to sell beer.
What's not being discussed is the deposit return portion that the Beer Store provides for all alcohol containers which have a deposit. There could be a silver lining if this is done well, but that's unlikely with Doug.
Other jurisdictions have a wider system of deposit return, not just alcohol containers. What would be ideal is a reverse-vending-machine system placed in numerous locations that are available at all hours, not only when the Beer Stores are open. These wouldn't necessarily dispense cash, but more often provide a receipt, or chit, that can be used in local stores, heck it could even be a digital deposit to an account.
Ilpo Lehto, Hamilton
CAPTION: Roses lay ashore during a wreath-laying ceremony at Utah Beach Wednesday in Normandy, France. Second World War veterans from Canada, the U.K. and the U.S. are in Normandy this week to mark 80 years since the D-Day landings that helped lead to Hitler's defeat.

Subject: Beer; Convenience stores; Homeless people; Beaches

Document 7 of 500

City ban on pay-for-plasma clinic 'symbolic,' says Canadian Blood Services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-ban-on-pay-plasma-clinic-symbolic-says/docview/3065002754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+ban+on+pay-for-plasma+clinic+%27symbolic%2C%27+says+Canadian+Blood+Services&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadian Blood Services (CBS) insinuated Hamilton city councillors did not understand the facts when they passed a "symbolic" motion opposing a for-profit blood collection centre that plans to pay for plasma when it opens next year on Barton Street East.
"We appreciate the thoughtful debate and recognize this is a complex matter," the independent not-for-profit organization said in a statement. "We look forward to ongoing dialogue to support better understanding of the facts."
CBS also appeared to dash any hope that declaring the city a "paid-plasma-free zone" Monday will alter its plan to operate a private clinic in Hamilton under a deal struck with Spanish health-care company Grifols.
While CBS would not directly answer questions about whether it will forge ahead in a city that has taken a stand against pay-for-plasma, the organization called the motion "symbolic" and stressed the only applicable legislation was the province's Voluntary Blood Donations Act.
The provincial act bans paying for plasma donation in Ontario, but CBS has declared itself exempt from the law as the charity that runs the blood system nationally. It claims Grifols is also exempt because the private company from Spain is acting as its agent when pay-for-plasma collection sites open in Hamilton, Cambridge, Whitby and two other yet-to-be-determined Ontario locations.
Mayor Andrea Horwath played a role as leader of the NDP in getting the provincial law passed in 2014 to stop a different private company - Canadian Plasma Resources - from opening at 370 Main St. E. and in two downtown Toronto locations.
"It explicitly and incontrovertibly prohibits pay-for-plasma centres," said Horwath about the provincial law while introducing her motion to ban the clinics in Hamilton at the city's public health committee. "What this is about is taking a stand in our community that says we don't want to see vulnerable people having to sell parts of their body in order to try to make ends meet."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones gave no signal Tuesday that she'll intervene despite Hamilton's opposition to the pay-for-plasma clinic that Horwath said would be located at 2255 Barton St. E., near the Red Hill Valley Parkway and Nash Road North.
The minister's spokesperson Hannah Jensen referred all questions about Hamilton's unanimous vote to CBS.
The province has not yet taken a clear stand on the pay-for-plasma clinics. On one hand, Jensen has said the centres legally conform to the legislation. But she also said the health minister has not provided CBS and Grifols with written confirmation of the legal loophole.
At issue is Canada's reliance on the global market for medications made from plasma called immunoglobulins, which amounts to 85 per cent of Canada's supply. Most are sourced from the United States, where donors are paid.
CBS says the pay-for-plasma clinics are necessary to "ensure domestic security of lifesaving immunoglobulins for patients in Canada."
But Horwath alleges the for-profit clinics are "predatory" and that 80 per cent of the paid plasma collected in the United States is from vulnerable and poor populations.
One patient group told city councillors before the vote that paying the poor for their blood is safer than other ways society's most vulnerable make money.
"As an affordable housing and homelessness advocate, I considered what my friends from the warming centres currently do to make $50 and I recognize getting paid to donate plasma - if they even qualify - would be the least exploitive and dangerous option many of them have," said Jennifer van Gennip, executive director of the Network of Rare Blood Disorder Organizations.
Coun. John-Paul Danko said people who need cash have a right to choose to sell their plasma.
"If your blood has a value and you make the choice and have an opportunity to make money by selling that, I don't really have a problem with that," he said at the meeting.
However, he ultimately voted for the pay-for-plasma ban because he felt the importance of public confidence in the Canadian blood supply was paramount.
The CBS deal with Grifols breaks a key recommendation against paid donation in the 1997 report of the Krever Commission in response to the tainted blood scandal in the 1980s that left more than 30,000 Canadians infected with HIV and hepatitis C through blood transfusions and plasma products.
But van Gennip argues the deal is safe because it's not a "free-for-all" that lets any company set up shop. Under the plan, CBS will open 11 of its own voluntary centres and partner with Grifols to operate 17 pay-for-plasma clinics across the country. Grifols would also manufacture the plasma collected at the sites into immunoglobulins at its Montreal plant, once it opens in 2026.
"Plasma is a highly regulated industry," Christine Duncan Wilson, chair of the board of directors at ImmUnity Canada, told city councillors. "There are no safety concerns either for the patient receiving the product or the plasma donor."
In total, three groups representing patients who rely on the medication to survive appealed to city councillors to support the Hamilton clinic as part of the CBS plan to bring reliance on global markets down to 50 per cent.
"With the sheer number of plasma donors needed ... this is a problem of numbers and that wishful thinking alone will not solve," said Donna Hartlen, executive director of the GBS/CIDP Foundation of Canada.
But three other groups argued the risks of pay-for-plasma clinics outweigh any benefit to patients.
"The premise that this Grifols deal will secure our blood supply is an absolute sham. There are no federal or provincial laws that restrict the company from exporting our lifesaving resources," said Kat Lanteigne, executive director of BloodWatch.org. "There is no reason why CBS cannot invest in a (voluntary) plasma centre in Hamilton. We hope you will send a strong message ... that the blood of the people of Hamilton is not for sale."
While councillors expressed compassion for the patients, they ultimately voted to take a stand against paying for plasma.
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Canadian Blood Services appeared to dash any hope that declaring the city a "paid-plasma-free zone" Monday will alter its plan to operate a private clinic in Hamilton under a deal struck with Spanish health-care company Grifols.

Subject: Patients; Bans; Plasma; Immunoglobulins; Blood & organ donations; Clinics; Scandals; Regulated industries

Business indexing term: Subject: Regulated industries

Document 8 of 500

Stewart-Haas to shut down NASCAR ops

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stewart-haas-shut-down-nascar-ops/docview/3065002741/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stewart-Haas+to+shut+down+NASCAR+ops&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few days ago, Stewart-Haas Racing (SHR) announced it will shut down its NASCAR operations at the end of this season. The announcement was presented in a joint statement by team principals Tony Stewart and Gene Haas.
"We have made the difficult decision to close Stewart-Haas Racing at the conclusion of the 2024 season," read the statement. "It is a decision that did not come easily, nor was it made quickly.
"Racing is a labour-intensive, humbling sport. It requires unwavering commitment and vast resources, with a 365-day mindset to be better than everyone else. It's part of what makes success so rewarding."
Founded in 2008 by three-time NASCAR Cup champion Tony Stewart and Gene Haas, the founder and owner of a large CNC machine tool builder, SHR has built up an impressive record with 69 Cup wins and two series titles. The team fostered drivers such as Kevin Harvick, Clint Bowyer and Danica Patrick over the years. At the end of this racing season, four SHR teams, with drivers Chase Briscoe, Noah Gragson, Josh Berry and Ryan Preece, will be off SHR's payroll, along with the 300 other employees at the Kannapolis, N.C., headquarters and racing shops.
Rumours of this shutdown were floating around, but now it is official. Ford has not committed to any support in the future, and loss of sponsorship from Smithfield and Anheuser Busch has lessened SHR's chances to continue.
A master of performing well behind the wheel of many different classes of race cars, 53-year-old Stewart will continue his newest ride in a Top Fuel Dragster (he was eliminated in the quarter-finals at the recent NHRA race in New Hampshire), and Haas plans to continue with his Formula One team.
Former SHR driver Clint Bowyer believes the team made its mark in the sport and the aftermath will be positive.
"A lot of good things in NASCAR became of Gene Haas's initial investment," said Bowyer, who won two of 10 races during his SHR days. "I'm talking hundreds of millions of dollars he invested in NASCAR. That's not an easy void to fill. But the good news is, with the charter system, they're available. That means they are going to some other place."
The charter system is like a subscription to race and allows a team to collect series points and prize money. These charters can be bought, sold and leased between teams.
Force talks future
He's still winning at 75 years old.
John Force took the Funny Car final at the NHRA New England Nationals held last weekend in New Hampshire. After his victory, which included eliminating teammate Austin Prock in the final round, Force hinted at his future.
"I'm not being dramatic, but my time is up. It was up when I was 65," said the 16-time champion. "Hell, it was up when I turned 50. The world is changing for me, and I've got to do some real thinking. I'm not quitting today. I'm going to Bristol, but I just know I'm not what I used to be."
Doug Kalitta won Top Fuel Dragster at Epping and Troy Coughlin Jr. won Pro Stock. There were some Canadians in the event, including Smax Smith and Dan Mercier. Both Top Fuel drivers who were eliminated in the first round, as was Pro Stock driver Eric Latino.
Weekend winners
The season got underway at Ohsweken Speedway on Friday when 119 race cars went to battle at Six Nations Community Night, presented by Six Nations Bingo and Aecon-Six Nations. Taking the 25-lap Kool Kidz-Corr/Pak 360 Sprint Cars feature was Dylan Westbrook of Scotland. Jesse Costa of St. Thomas won the Strickland's Crate Sprint Cars main, Ryan Beagle of Vittoria won the Middleport Mechanical Thunder Stocks feature, and St. Catharines' Alex Riley won the HRW Automotive Mini Stocks feature ... Tyler Hawn of Oro Medonte won the 46-lap Bill Zardo Memorial at Flamboro Speedway over the weekend. Named in honour of the late oval track racer, the race consisted of cars from the Hot Rod division of the APC United Racing Series. Taking the win in the APC Modified class was Steve Trendall of Fergus. Bobby Mercer of Dundas won the Mini-stock feature, Rodney Rutherford, also of Dundas, won the Canadian Vintage Modified feature and Craig Cole won his third straight Pure Stock feature.
Tim Miller is a freelance contributor to The Hamilton Spectator whose focus is motorsports. He is the author of several books on auto racing.
CAPTION: Ryan Beagleof Vittoria won the Middleport Mechanical Thunder Stocks feature at Ohsweken Speedway on Friday.

Subject: Stocks; Automobiles; Automobile racing; Shutdowns; Teams

Document 9 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3064875119/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Remembering D-Day
Today, June 6, 2024, marks the 80th anniversary of the D-Day invasion at Normandy, France. That morning, thousands of young soldiers from Canada, the U.K., the U.S. and other Allied nations landed along the coast of Normandy on beaches code named Juno, Gold, Sword, Omaha and Utah. They landed, in many cases under heavy enemy fire, but did not waver. Their goal was to defeat Nazism and fascism and liberate Europe from Hitler and his henchmen. Many fell that day. Many more were wounded, but they believed that what they were doing was just and right for a civilized world that was worth fighting for. As U.S. president Franklin Roosevelt said at the time of invasion, "They fight to end conquest. They fight to liberate."
Much has changed in the past 80 years on our planet, but one thing still stands out. We owe the utmost gratitude and respect to that generation who on the morning of June 6 landed on enemy beaches at Normandy knowing that they might not survive the day. So, all of us should pause today for a few moments and thank those young soldiers, many who made the ultimate sacrifice that day so that our world would be a much better place to live for future generations.
Lest we forget.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek

washroom to cost up to $1.3 million (June 4)

We need a long-term old-age home with all the amenities way more than we need to waste millions of taxpayers dollars to pay out a contract that expires next year. How disgusting that Mr. Ford is willing to waste taxpayers' money on beer rather than helping our aging population.

Document 10 of 500

Doug Ford isn't the only one who has fumbled on antisemitism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-isnt-only-one-who-has-fumbled-on/docview/3064875096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+isn%27t+the+only+one+who+has+fumbled+on+antisemitism&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford surely missed the mark when he blamed immigrants for the latest wave of antisemitic shootings.
But he's not the only one guilty of going off target.
A great many Canadians - new and old alike - are also missing the bigger lesson: How to bring people together while calling out hate.
Amid the ongoing controversy over Ford's faux pas, we're losing our focus over the original sins - the shootings and attacks outside Jewish schools and synagogues in Toronto, Montreal and Vancouver within a matter of weeks.
To be sure, the premier's perennial missteps are fair game. But the daily news cycle has defaulted to more of the same Ford-bashing (I too am guilty), while devoting only fleeting coverage to recurring Jew hatred (I'm guilty again).
Is anyone still talking about the bullets fired outside the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School in Toronto last month? Bullets fired at the Belz Yeshiva Ketana school in a Montreal synagogue? The firebombing of a Vancouver synagogue last week? Vandalism at the Anshei Minsk synagogue in Toronto's Kensington Market this week?
Sharing a podium with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who forcefully condemned the latest antisemitic outburst in Toronto, Ford went way off script: "You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world," he hectored.
By scapegoating the "other," the premier missed an opportunity to address the "us" - all Ontarians and all Canadians - with a teachable moment on tolerance. Let's not lose the moment.
Blaming immigrants for importing antisemitism and hatred here isn't just wrong-headed in spirit. It's wrong in fact.
For there is a long history of homegrown Jew hatred in this country. The latest bigotry by old stock Canadians and newcomers is little different.
Antisemitism comes in every skin colour and from every country of origin - including this country. The difference is that it now goes by another name.
A few decades ago in Ontario you could find legal papers that excluded land sales or rentals to anyone of "the Jewish, Hebrew, Semitic, Negro or coloured race or blood." Jews were excluded from universities and excoriated as "Hebes" or "kikes."
Today, Zionists or "Zios" are the preferred proxies for denigrating Jews, even calling for their exclusion ("No Zios on campus," as the protest slogan goes).
The old phrase "Zionism is racism," once discredited, has been reincarnated as "Zionism is colonialism" - which inevitably diminishes and denigrates most Jews. Why?
While Zionism takes many expressions, most Jews believe in its most common definition - self-determination for Jews (who have their own claims to indigenity in Israel). If you insult or isolate Zionists, treating them as adversaries, you are inevitably singling out most Jews for opprobrium and discrimination - your friends, neighbours, co-workers.
If you call yourself anti-Zionist (as opposed to merely non-Zionist) you are effectively opposed to Israel's raison d'être, which raises the question: What is to become of the Jewish state and its nine million citizens (of whom about one in five within its borders are Palestinian citizens who can vote - not to be confused with five million Palestinians proper living under Israeli occupation or control in the West Bank or Gaza).
To be sure, critics of Israel - of which I am one - are not necessarily anti-Israeli (or anti-Jewish). But a good many are so adamantly opposed to the existence of the state of Israel, for reasons of history or bigotry, that you have to ask:
Where would those millions of Jews go? Back to Poland, as some like to taunt? Here to Canada, where they feel increasingly besieged? Stay where they are in a single state where "Palestine shall be free, from the river to the sea," subsuming and consuming the Jewish state?
Israel is guilty of many sins during its long decades of occupation, although neither side is blameless about missed opportunities. After the Oct. 7 Hamas massacre of more than 1,200 Jews and the taking of hostages, Israel's overreaction and overreach transformed a just war of defence into a war without justifiable limits.
More than 36,000 Palestinians are now dead, according to the Hamas-run health authority (which does not tally armed fighters; Israel claims roughly one-third of Gaza's dead are combatants). It is undeniably a human disaster that could and should have been avoided.
Yet today, Israel alone is accused of genocide. One doesn't see other, proven genocides protested with similar ferocity - not even the most recent ones alleged to have taken place in Ethiopia (as many as 300,000 Tigrayans dead as of 2022) and Sudan (another 2.5 million Darfuris are imperilled right now).
If Israel's sins are so bad that they don't pass the smell test, are its odours any worse than those of other offending countries? Or do Israel's excesses rise above all others on earth, for all time?
That's not whataboutism, it's an attempt to understand what it's all about in a time of tunnel vision. It's also a reminder for us to place the world's troubles in the Canadian context.
While protesters accuse Israel of committing genocide in Gaza, one still hears remarkably little about reported hate crimes here at home that target mostly Jews but also many others. More on that in my next column.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford suggested immigrants were to blame for the recent shooting at a Jewish school in Toronto despite police saying they have no information on the suspects. Ford made the remarks while flanked by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, and Mayor Olivia Chow, right, during a news conference at the facilities of vaccine producer Sanofi in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Document 11 of 500

Canadian Blood Services calls city's pay-for-plasma clinic ban 'symbolic': Health minister gave no sign she'll intervene despite Hamilton's opposition to for-profit clinic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-blood-services-calls-citys-pay-plasma/docview/3064874609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+Blood+Services+calls+city%27s+pay-for-plasma+clinic+ban+%27symbolic%27%3A+Health+minister+gave+no+sign+she%27ll+intervene+despite+Hamilton%27s+opposition+to+for-profit+clinic&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadian Blood Services (CBS) insinuated Hamilton city councillors did not understand the facts when they passed a "symbolic" motion opposing a for-profit blood collection centre that plans to pay for plasma when it opens next year on Barton Street East.
"We appreciate the thoughtful debate and recognize this is a complex matter," the independent not-for-profit organization said in a statement. "We look forward to ongoing dialogue to support better understanding of the facts."
CBS also appeared to dash any hope that declaring the city a "paid-plasma-free zone" Monday will alter its plan to operate a private clinic in Hamilton under a deal struck with Spanish health-care company Grifols.
While CBS would not directly answer questions about whether it will forge ahead in a city that has taken a stand against pay-for-plasma, the organization called the motion "symbolic" and stressed the only applicable legislation was the province's Voluntary Blood Donations Act.
The provincial act bans paying for plasma donation in Ontario, but CBS has declared itself exempt from the law as the charity that runs the blood system nationally. It claims Grifols is also exempt because the private company from Spain is acting as its agent when pay-for-plasma collection sites open in Hamilton, Cambridge, Whitby and two other yet-to-be-determined Ontario locations.
Mayor Andrea Horwath played a role as leader of the NDP in getting the provincial law passed in 2014 to stop a different private company - Canadian Plasma Resources - from opening at 370 Main St. E. and in two downtown Toronto locations.
"It explicitly and incontrovertibly prohibits pay-for-plasma centres," said Horwath about the provincial law while introducing her motion to ban the clinics in Hamilton at the city's public health committee. "What this is about is taking a stand in our community that says we don't want to see vulnerable people having to sell parts of their body in order to try to make ends meet."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones gave no signal Tuesday that she'll intervene despite Hamilton's opposition to the pay-for-plasma clinic that Horwath said would be located at 2255 Barton St. E., near the Red Hill Valley Parkway and Nash Road North.
The minister's spokesperson Hannah Jensen referred all questions about Hamilton's unanimous vote to CBS.
The province has not yet taken a clear stand on the pay-for-plasma clinics. On one hand, Jensen has said the centres legally conform to the legislation. But she also said the health minister has not provided CBS and Grifols with written confirmation of the legal loophole.
At issue is Canada's reliance on the global market for medications made from plasma called immunoglobulins, which amounts to 85 per cent of Canada's supply. Most are sourced from the United States, where donors are paid.
CBS says the pay-for-plasma clinics are necessary to "ensure domestic security of lifesaving immunoglobulins for patients in Canada."
But Horwath alleges the for-profit clinics are "predatory" and that 80 per cent of the paid plasma collected in the United States is from vulnerable and poor populations.
One patient group told city councillors before the vote that paying the poor for their blood is safer than other ways society's most vulnerable make money.
"As an affordable housing and homelessness advocate, I considered what my friends from the warming centres currently do to make $50 and I recognize getting paid to donate plasma - if they even qualify - would be the least exploitive and dangerous option many of them have," said Jennifer van Gennip, executive director of the Network of Rare Blood Disorder Organizations.
Coun. John-Paul Danko said people who need cash have a right to choose to sell their plasma.
"If your blood has a value and you make the choice and have an opportunity to make money by selling that, I don't really have a problem with that," he said at the meeting.
However, he ultimately voted for the pay-for-plasma ban because he felt the importance of public confidence in the Canadian blood supply was paramount.
The CBS deal with Grifols breaks a key recommendation against paid donation in the 1997 report of the Krever Commission in response to the tainted blood scandal in the 1980s that left more than 30,000 Canadians infected with HIV and hepatitis C through blood transfusions and plasma products.
But van Gennip argues the deal is safe because it's not a "free-for-all" that lets any company set up shop. Under the plan, CBS will open 11 of its own voluntary centres and partner with Grifols to operate 17 pay-for-plasma clinics across the country. Grifols would also manufacture the plasma collected at the sites into immunoglobulins at its Montreal plant, once it opens in 2026.
"Plasma is a highly regulated industry," Christine Duncan Wilson, chair of the board of directors at ImmUnity Canada, told city councillors. "There are no safety concerns either for the patient receiving the product or the plasma donor."
In total, three groups representing patients who rely on the medication to survive appealed to city councillors to support the Hamilton clinic as part of the CBS plan to bring reliance on global markets down to 50 per cent.
"With the sheer number of plasma donors needed ... this is a problem of numbers and that wishful thinking alone will not solve," said Donna Hartlen, executive director of the GBS/CIDP Foundation of Canada.
But three other groups argued the risks of pay-for-plasma clinics outweigh any benefit to patients.
"The premise that this Grifols deal will secure our blood supply is an absolute sham. There are no federal or provincial laws that restrict the company from exporting our lifesaving resources," said Kat Lanteigne, executive director of BloodWatch.org. "There is no reason why CBS cannot invest in a (voluntary) plasma centre in Hamilton. We hope you will send a strong message ... that the blood of the people of Hamilton is not for sale."
While councillors expressed compassion for the patients, they ultimately voted to take a stand against paying for plasma.
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Canadian Blood Services appeared to dash any hope that declaring the city a "paid-plasma-free zone" Monday will alter its plan to operate a private clinic in Hamilton under a deal struck with Spanish health-care company Grifols.Dan Pearce Metroland file photo

Stewart-Haas to shut down NASCAR ops: Will continue with NHRA and F1 racing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stewart-haas-shut-down-nascar-ops/docview/3064874600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stewart-Haas+to+shut+down+NASCAR+ops%3A+Will+continue+with+NHRA+and+F1+racing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few days ago, Stewart-Haas Racing (SHR) announced it will shut down its NASCAR operations at the end of this season. The announcement was presented in a joint statement by team principals Tony Stewart and Gene Haas.
"We have made the difficult decision to close Stewart-Haas Racing at the conclusion of the 2024 season," read the statement. "It is a decision that did not come easily, nor was it made quickly.
"Racing is a labour-intensive, humbling sport. It requires unwavering commitment and vast resources, with a 365-day mindset to be better than everyone else. It's part of what makes success so rewarding."
Founded in 2008 by three-time NASCAR Cup champion Stewart and Haas, the founder and owner of a large CNC machine tool builder, SHR has built up an impressive record with 69 Cup wins and two series titles.
The team fostered drivers such as Kevin Harvick, Clint Bowyer and Danica Patrick over the years. At the end of this racing season, four SHR teams, with drivers Chase Briscoe, Noah Gragson, Josh Berry and Ryan Preece, will be off SHR's payroll, along with the 300 other employees at the Kannapolis, N.C., headquarters and racing shops.
Rumours of this shutdown were floating around, but now it is official. Ford has not committed to any support in the future, and loss of sponsorship from Smithfield and Anheuser Busch has lessened SHR's chances to continue.
A master of performing well behind the wheel of many different classes of race cars, 53-year-old Stewart will continue his newest ride in a Top Fuel Dragster (he was eliminated in the quarterfinals at the recent NHRA race in New Hampshire), and Haas plans to continue with his Formula One team.
Former SHR driver Bowyer believes the team made its mark in the sport and the aftermath will be positive.
"A lot of good things in NASCAR became of Gene Haas's initial investment," said Bowyer, who won two of 10 races during his SHR days. "I'm talking hundreds of millions of dollars he invested in NASCAR. That's not an easy void to fill. But the good news is, with the charter system, they're available. That means they are going to some other place."
The charter system is like a subscription to race and allows a team to collect series points and prize money. These charters can be bought, sold and leased between teams.
Force talks future
He's still winning at 75 years old.
John Force took the Funny Car final at the NHRA New England Nationals held last weekend in New Hampshire. After his victory, which included eliminating teammate Austin Prock in the final round, Force hinted at his future.
"I'm not being dramatic, but my time is up. It was up when I was 65," said the 16-time champion. "Hell, it was up when I turned 50. The world is changing for me, and I've got to do some real thinking. I'm not quitting today. I'm going to Bristol, but I just know I'm not what I used to be."
Doug Kalitta won Top Fuel Dragster at Epping and Troy Coughlin Jr. won Pro Stock. There were some Canadians in the event, including Smax Smith and Dan Mercier. Both Top Fuel drivers who were eliminated in the first round, as was Pro Stock driver Eric Latino.
Weekend winners
The season got underway at Ohsweken Speedway on Friday when 119 race cars went to battle at Six Nations Community Night, presented by Six Nations Bingo and Aecon-Six Nations. Taking the 25-lap Kool Kidz-Corr/Pak 360 Sprint Cars feature was Dylan Westbrook of Scotland. Jesse Costa of St. Thomas won the Strickland's Crate Sprint Cars main, Ryan Beagle of Vittoria won the Middleport Mechanical Thunder Stocks feature, and St. Catharines' Alex Riley won the HRW Automotive Mini Stocks feature ... Tyler Hawn of Oro Medonte won the 46-lap Bill Zardo Memorial at Flamboro Speedway over the weekend. Named in honour of the late oval track racer, the race consisted of cars from the Hot Rod division of the APC United Racing Series. Taking the win in the APC Modified class was Steve Trendall of Fergus. Bobby Mercer of Dundas won the Mini-stock feature, Rodney Rutherford, also of Dundas, won the Canadian Vintage Modified feature and Craig Cole won his third straight Pure Stock feature.
Tim Miller is a freelance contributor to The Hamilton Spectator whose focus is motorsports. He is the author of several books on auto racing.

Document 13 of 500

Governments sign $4.7B deal to boost housing construction in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/governments-sign-4-7b-deal-boost-housing/docview/3064677501/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Governments+sign+%244.7B+deal+to+boost+housing+construction+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After months of squabbling and jockeying, Canada's three levels of government have come together to finalize $4.7 billion in funding from Ottawa to boost housing construction in Ontario.
Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser, Ontario Housing Minister Paul Calandra and Mayor Olivia Chow said Wednesday the "Canada Community-Building Fund," formerly known as the gas tax fund, would be renewed for another five years.
In a joint statement that was also signed by Association of Municipalities of Ontario president Colin Best, the federal, provincial and municipal governments said the money "will ensure that critical infrastructure that supports housing continues to be built, maintained and expanded."
While the funding had been announced in federal Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's April budget, it was dependent upon Queen's Park and the municipalities signing on.
"The renewal of this agreement means that our communities' roads, bridges, and transit systems will be positioned to accommodate growth," said the joint statement.
That $4.7 billion infusion of cash over five years will bankroll projects across Ontario.
"We recognize the importance of infrastructure in increasing Canada's housing supply. By working together to strategically invest in projects aligned with regional housing goals, we will help increase the number of affordable homes in Ontario," the three levels of government said.
"Through this agreement, we have aligned on three priority goals, including reporting on affordable units created to meet the needs of communities and increase capacity of the non-profit sector; working across all orders of government to leverage public lands to meet housing needs; and supporting innovation in construction techniques by committing to a provincial innovation strategy for modular and prefabricated housing."
That includes working with Ottawa "to develop and adopt a design catalogue" to build new houses more quickly and cheaply.
"We will work together to build healthy and vibrant neighbourhoods across the province," the politicians said.
"We are taking a Team Canada approach to build more inclusive and connected communities, which will help support more housing by creating the public infrastructure that Ontarians need."
Wednesday's announcement comes eight days after Fraser and Calandra set aside months of rancour and inked a deal ensuring $357 million in federal funds for the province to build affordable housing.
Under that agreement between Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, Ontario revised its plan for affordable housing projects, meaning at least 8,644 more units will be built by the end of next March.
Queen's Park, which insists it could actually meet its original federally mandated target of 19,660 new units by that deadline, will also submit its affordable housing target for 2025-28 by year's end.
That's in order to secure additional federal funding for the final years of the decade-long $70-billion National Housing Strategy agreement.
Ford's Progressive Conservatives, who have promised to build 1.5 million new homes by 2031, have been working closely with Trudeau's Liberals toward that goal.
But so far the Tories are nowhere near the average annual housing starts of 150,000 units that are needed to achieve that, according to the government's online tracker.
Last year, 109,011 new homes were built, including 9,835 long-term-care beds that the province now classifies as homes.
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on March 7, 2024.

Document 14 of 500

'You good?': Hamilton police officer's suicide inspires colleague to share story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-good-hamilton-police-officers-suicide/docview/3064675301/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27You+good%3F%27%3A+Hamilton+police+officer%27s+suicide+inspires+colleague+to+share+story&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pete Wiesner and his wife, Elise, were talking in their garage when the words just spilled out.
He had been sexually abused as a child.
Wiesner had never said those words aloud before. Not to his parents, not his first wife, not even to himself. Wiesner's wife didn't make a big deal of the revelation or push him for details; she supported him and let him decide what to do next.
They had been talking about the death of fellow Hamilton police officer Greg Mills, who died by suicide at Hamilton police central station last September. Mills' death affected many officers, including Wiesner, deeply. The loss was compounded by the deaths of other Hamilton officers, including several suicides, in recent years.
Talking about Mills death made Wiesner think of his own mental-health journey, unlocking the pain of his childhood trauma. From there, Wiesner said he decided to talk to family about what happened to him as a child. He was abused by someone in a position of trust, but who was not a family member. He also booked an appointment with his therapist.
That he had the tools to deal with these suppressed memories was a testament to the both the support he has in his life and the work he's done to address his own mental health in recent years.
Just seven years before, he came dangerously close to shooting himself at central station, but was stopped thanks to the interventions of caring colleagues.
Wiesner says he wants to share his story now because he wants people to know they're not alone and that help is available, especially for emergency service workers. The simple act of asking someone how they are doing and really listening for the answer can save lives - it saved his.
Over his 24-year career with Hamilton police, Wiesner became known for his work supporting vulnerable people, including working as the social navigator - a team that works to divert vulnerable people away from the criminal justice system - and later as the sergeant in charge of the crisis response branch, which also includes specialized teams for mental-health crisis calls and partnerships with other community services. Wiesner has presented at conferences internationally and led training at multiple police services. Last year he ran as an Ontario PC Party candidate for Hamilton Centre in a byelection, but lost.
Wiesner retired from policing earlier this year after being offered a job in the private sector consulting with Street Smarts VR, a United States-based tech company that is set to bring virtual reality crisis training to police services, including in Ontario. Wiesner stresses that the job change is not because he was turning his back on policing, but rather the time and opportunity just fit.
Chief Frank Bergen sent out an email to the Hamilton Police Service staff about Wiesner's retirement and his commitment to best serving Hamilton's most marginalized citizens. In the email, Bergen gave the last word to Wiesner, sharing a message from Wiesner himself.
"As I sit here, reminiscing on what has been the most rewarding career I could ever dream about - I ask myself, why am I stopping now?" Wiesner writes, adding that it's not a lack of love for policing or his colleagues.
"In policing, we see the worst of society. Unfortunately, the tragedies we see are sometimes within our own police family. In nearly 24 years, I've sadly attended 12 funerals for officers who took their own lives while serving on duty. Greg Mills' recent passing left the deepest impact on me, prompting me to revisit my darkest days in 2016 when I, well, wasn't so well."
Back then Wiesner was dealing with a lot of stress. He was dealing with the breakdown of his first marriage, complex mental-health issues within his family and internal politics at work. He also was haunted by the drowning death of an 11-year-old boy in Dundas.
Wiesner was among numerous officers and hundreds of volunteers who searched for the missing boy, who was found a day after he disappeared, submerged in a culvert about 200 metres from his home. Wiesner later learned that he was standing just above where the boy was found as officers did a grid search, but no one could see the boy.
That's when Wiesner came up with the plan to kill himself at work. But each day he planned, he was stopped by bumping into a colleague who would change the mood. One friend he jokingly wrestled with, another was a canine-unit officer and Wiesner paused to greet the dog.
On the third day, Wiesner was calmy determined. But Staff Sgt. Scott Balinson noticed something wasn't right.
"He called me into his office and asked me the simple question, 'You good?'" he said.
Wiesner remembers asking himself how Balison could have picked up on what was happening in his mind. Balison slid a piece of paper across the desk with a name and number of a therapist.
Wiesner didn't think it would work, but out of respect for "Scotty" he called -- and the decision ended up being life-changing. He now realizes it saved his life.
After Mills death last year, Wiesner found himself regretting having never shared that story with Mills.
"I know now it likely wouldn't have changed the outcome but knowing I could have had the conversation and never did (is a big regret)," he said.
Wiesner spoke to family, his therapist and fellow officers about his childhood trauma and his struggles after Mills' death, including with Bergen, who has offered much support, including allowing Wiesner to bring his dog, Buddy, to work with him. There was a much better atmosphere at the police service supporting members struggling with mental health this time, including a member support team, he said.
The work that Wiesner has done over the last several months has allowed him a new perspective on life. One where he's "ready to write the next chapter."
Along with his new job, he's also creating a foundation in honour of Mills and other colleagues who have died. The charity will provide scholarships for the children of emergency services workers who have died by suicide.
Wiesner is dedicating 25 per cent of his earnings from speaking engagements, beginning with a personal $5,000 donation. Details will be launched soon at everyonevsstigma.ca.
Mills did not have children, so first-year funds will provide a scholarship to a CANUSA athlete. Mills was also a long-standing volunteer with the games, as a coach, board member and past president.
Police friends of Mills have also raised funds for CANUSA in his memory through a "YOU GOOD?" campaign that sold T-shirts with the slogan.
Wiesner is also working to help establish a chapter of Canada Police Beyond the Blue, which supports the families of officers who died by suicides, in Hamilton.
The question "you good?" is code that all first responders know. It's a mental health check in that many need after a difficult call or stressful day. Wiesner wants to encourage others to ask it more often.
"Don't be afraid to check in," he said.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com
