Document 15 of 500

'You good?' Police officer's suicide inspires colleague to share story: In retirement, Pete Wiesner shares his journey from trauma to advocacy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/you-good-police-officers-suicide-inspires/docview/3064515124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27You+good%3F%27+Police+officer%27s+suicide+inspires+colleague+to+share+story%3A+In+retirement%2C+Pete+Wiesner+shares+his+journey+from+trauma+to+advocacy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pete Wiesner and his wife, Elise, were talking in their garage when the words just spilled out.
He had been sexually abused as a child.
Wiesner had never said those words aloud before. Not to his parents, not his first wife, not even to himself. Wiesner's wife didn't make a big deal of the revelation or push him for details; she supported him and let him decide what to do next.
They had been talking about the death of fellow Hamilton police officer Greg Mills, who died by suicide at Hamilton police central station last September. Mills' death affected many officers, including Wiesner, deeply. The loss was compounded by the deaths of other Hamilton officers, including several suicides, in recent years.
Talking about Mills' death made Wiesner think of his own mental-health journey, unlocking the pain of his childhood trauma. From there, Wiesner said he decided to talk to family about what happened to him as a child.
He was abused by someone in a position of trust, but who was not a family member. He also booked an appointment with his therapist.
That he had the tools to deal with these suppressed memories was a testament to the both the support he has in his life and the work he's done to address his own mental health in recent years.
Just seven years before, he came dangerously close to shooting himself at central station, but was stopped thanks to the interventions of caring colleagues.
Wiesner says he wants to share his story now because he wants people to know they're not alone and that help is available, especially for emergency service workers. The simple act of asking someone how they are doing and really listening for the answer can save lives - it saved his.
Over his 24-year career with Hamilton police, Wiesner became known for his work supporting vulnerable people, including working as the social navigator - a team that works to divert vulnerable people away from the criminal justice system - and later as the sergeant in charge of the crisis response branch, which also includes specialized teams for mental-health crisis calls and partnerships with other community services.
Wiesner has presented at conferences internationally and led training at multiple police services. Last year he ran as an Ontario PC Party candidate for Hamilton Centre in a byelection, but lost.
Wiesner retired from policing earlier this year after being offered a job in the private sector consulting with Street Smarts VR, a United States-based tech company that is set to bring virtual reality crisis training to police services, including in Ontario. Wiesner stresses that the job change is not because he was turning his back on policing, but rather the time and opportunity just fit.
Chief Frank Bergen sent out an email to the Hamilton Police Service staff about Wiesner's retirement and his commitment to best serving Hamilton's most marginalized citizens. In the email, Bergen gave the last word to Wiesner, sharing a message from Wiesner himself.
"As I sit here, reminiscing on what has been the most rewarding career I could ever dream about - I ask myself, why am I stopping now?" Wiesner writes, adding that it's not a lack of love for policing or his colleagues.
"In policing, we see the worst of society. Unfortunately, the tragedies we see are sometimes within our own police family. In nearly 24 years, I've sadly attended 12 funerals for officers who took their own lives while serving on duty. Greg Mills' recent passing left the deepest impact on me, prompting me to revisit my darkest days in 2016 when I, well, wasn't so well."
Back then Wiesner was dealing with a lot of stress. He was dealing with the breakdown of his first marriage, complex mental-health issues within his family and internal politics at work. He also was haunted by the drowning death of an 11-year-old boy in Dundas.
Wiesner was among numerous officers and hundreds of volunteers who searched for the missing boy, who was found a day after he disappeared, submerged in a culvert about 200 metres from his home. Wiesner later learned that he was standing just above where the boy was found as officers did a grid search, but no one could see the boy.
That's when Wiesner came up with the plan to kill himself at work. But each day he planned, he was stopped by bumping into a colleague who would change the mood. One friend he jokingly wrestled with, another was a canine-unit officer and Wiesner paused to greet the dog.
On the third day, Wiesner was calmy determined. But Staff Sgt. Scott Balinson noticed something wasn't right.
"He called me into his office and asked me the simple question, 'You good?' " he said.
Wiesner remembers asking himself how Balison could have picked up on what was happening in his mind. Balison slid a piece of paper across the desk with a name and number of a therapist.
Wiesner didn't think it would work, but out of respect for "Scotty" he called - and the decision ended up being life-changing. He now realizes it saved his life.
After the death of Mills last year, Wiesner found himself regretting having never shared that story with him.
"I know now it likely wouldn't have changed the outcome but knowing I could have had the conversation and never did (is a big regret)," he said.
Wiesner spoke to family, his therapist and fellow officers about his childhood trauma and his struggles after Mills' death, including with Bergen, who has offered much support, including allowing Wiesner to bring his dog, Buddy, to work with him. There was a much better atmosphere at the police service supporting members struggling with mental health this time, including a member support team, he said.
The work that Wiesner has done over the last several months has allowed him a new perspective on life. One where he's "ready to write the next chapter."
Along with his new job, he's also creating a foundation in honour of Mills and other colleagues who have died. The charity will provide scholarships for the children of emergency services workers who have died by suicide.
Wiesner is dedicating 25 per cent of his earnings from speaking engagements, beginning with a personal $5,000 donation. Details will be launched soon at everyonevsstigma.ca.
Mills did not have children, so first-year funds will provide a scholarship to a CANUSA athlete. Mills was also a long-standing volunteer with the games, as a coach, board member and past president.
Police friends of Mills have also raised funds for CANUSA in his memory through a "YOU GOOD?" campaign that sold T-shirts with the slogan.
Wiesner is also working to help establish a chapter of Canada Police Beyond the Blue, which supports the families of officers who died by suicides, in Hamilton.
The question "you good?" is code that all first responders know. It's a mental-health check in that many need after a difficult call or stressful day. Wiesner wants to encourage others to ask it more often.
"Don't be afraid to check in."
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

Document 16 of 500

Byelection a test for PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/byelection-test-pm/docview/3064514859/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Byelection+a+test+for+PM&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conventional wisdom suggests byelections are a consequence-free way for voters to send a message to the governing party that they are unhappy. But the results of a contest this month in the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's may prove quite consequential.
If the Liberals do not win this riding on June 24, and it looks increasingly like that's a possibility, there will be immense pressure on Justin Trudeau to resign as their leader immediately - "within days," a well-known Liberal suggested to me over the weekend.
So far, Liberal MPs have demonstrated no desire to show Trudeau the door, believing they are better off with him than without him. But a loss in Toronto-St. Paul's might change that calculation. "If we lose St. Paul's, maybe the knives come out," a Liberal caucus member said of his colleagues.
Both Trudeau and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre have spent time in the riding. Cabinet ministers have flocked there to knock on doors and make phone calls. Conservative shadow ministers have pounded the pavement, hoping to drive the vote out for the Tories.
What they've heard isn't pretty, for any of the leaders, and may reflect the general mood of the country.
People are tired of Trudeau. They dislike Poilievre. One person who knocked on doors reported Liberal voters feeling betrayed, and noted some planned to spoil their ballot. Others planned to vote Conservative despite their disdain for Poilievre, who they see as "too partisan" and "too mean."
Many didn't want to talk. In my 57 phone calls to the riding Monday, no one was ecstatic to chat.
"It would not at all be a surprise to see the Tories win," Innovative Research Group president Greg Lyle told me, although "not by a large amount."
Toronto-St. Paul's was once considered a bellwether riding. Barbara McDougall held the seat for the Progressive Conservatives in the 1980s under Brian Mulroney, but since 1993 it's been solidly Liberal. The outgoing MP, Carolyn Bennett, won the last nine elections with more than 40 per cent of the vote.
But last summer, after more than 26 years in Parliament and eight as a cabinet minister, Bennett, then 72, announced she wasn't running again. In January, Trudeau appointed her ambassador to Denmark.
Leslie Church is now running to replace her. A former chief of staff to Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland, Church doesn't have Bennett's deep roots in the riding, but her biography notes she went to law school at the University of Toronto, began her legal career in the city, got married and started her family there (she and her husband have three children).
The Conservative candidate, Don Stewart, has lived in the riding with his two daughters since 2016. He works for the Canadian Investment Regulatory Organization, according to his biography. His LinkedIn profile notes he spent one year working for Poilievre's key adviser Jenni Byrne's lobbying and government relations company as managing director.
With national polls showing a big double-digit lead for the Conservatives, this was always going to be a riding to watch.
Despite then having three - and now four - vacancies in the House of Commons, Trudeau called a byelection only for this riding. That meant the parties could throw all their efforts - and money and attention - into one contest.
But fatigue with Trudeau and the war in Gaza have made Toronto-St. Paul's a contest to watch.
"The Jewish community is pretty unhappy with the way the Liberals have been reacting to the war in Gaza," Lyle believes. Census data from 2021 estimates 10 to 15 per cent of the riding identifies as Jewish.
Philippe Fournier, who runs the polling aggregator website 338Canada.com, said he hasn't seen any local polling that's been made public. While he thinks the Liberals should still be able to win the byelection by a four- to eight-point margin, he also believes it could all come down to turnout. "If Liberal voters stay home, they have no chance," he said.
The Liberals seem concerned, with cabinet ministers tweeting out repeated reminders to vote at the residents of Toronto-St-Paul's. Unmotivated voters are a hard group to bring out to the polls - especially when who forms the government isn't at stake. But, in this case, who leads the Liberals might be.
Altha Raj is a national
columnist with Torstar.

Opposition parties say they're ready if Doug Ford calls a snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opposition-parties-say-theyre-ready-if-doug-ford/docview/3064715269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+parties+say+they%27re+ready+if+Doug+Ford+calls+a+snap+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford prepares for a possible early election before the scheduled June 2026 province-wide vote, his rivals say they are gearing up.
"We're already rolling up our sleeves," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"I mean, we've been working on this. We have our election planning committee in place. I think it's earlier than ever before. Our MPPs are out there knocking on doors. We're in the process of beginning the process," said Stiles.
"If he thinks that an early election is going to help him, he's wrong," she said.
The Star reported May 27 that Ford is considering a snap vote to get in front of an expected October 2025 federal election amid concerns that a federal Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre might politically hurt the provincial Progressive Conservatives.
That's due to fears Poilievre could unplug Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that's also a cornerstone of PC economic policy - and because Ontario voters usually elect different parties in Ottawa and Queen's Park at the same time.
To clear the decks for a possibly early call, Ford is paying The Beer Store $225 million to liberalize booze sales as of this fall - more than one year ahead of schedule - in a controversial deal critics warn could actually cost as much as $1 billion.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie - who wants to halve the small-business tax rate from 3.2 per cent to 1.6 per cent and raise the income threshold from $500,000 to $600,000 to enable more entrepreneurs to qualify - said her party is "gearing up" with policies and preparations.
"You heard that correct, Liberals are tax-cutters," she said, trying to counter the narrative in the Tories' election-style attack ad blitz that's been on the airwaves for months.
"This billion-dollar booze boondoggle will pay for two years of this tax."
Crombie, mindful her Liberals trail Ford's Tories in every public-opinion poll with the NDP in third place, said the Grits know they have their work cut out for them.
"We've got the bones in place. We've built capacity, hired organizational staff, more communication staff. We've got our fundraising team in place. We've got dates for future fundraisers. Later this month, we will be starting to nominate candidates," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner predicted that "spending a billion dollars on booze to facilitate an early election is going to backfire on the premier."
Still, the Greens are making plans for an early vote if Ford rolls the dice.
"We'll be ready. We're already starting to work on recruiting candidates and ... we're putting our election team together," said Schreiner.
Ford, for his part, told CFRA's Bill Carroll last Wednesday that "100 per cent we're not having an election this summer, this fall."
But the premier has repeatedly and pointedly not ruled out a campaign next spring.
"Stay tuned," he told Newstalk 1010's Jerry Agar the day before.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Document 18 of 500

City councillors oppose pay-for-plasma clinic in Hamilton - but will it open anyway?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-councillors-oppose-pay-plasma-clinic/docview/3064227387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+councillors+oppose+pay-for-plasma+clinic+in+Hamilton+-+but+will+it+open+anyway%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City councillors have voted to take a stand against a private, for-profit blood collection centre that plans to pay for plasma when it opens in Hamilton next year.
A motion put forward by Mayor Andrea Horwath to declare Hamilton a "paid-plasma-free zone" unanimously passed at the city's public health committee meeting Monday.
"We have a moral obligation here to do the right thing and not have people selling a part of their body as a solution to poverty," Horwath told the committee. "Any entity that preys upon folks in our community that are the most vulnerable and the most in need of resources doesn't belong in our city."
It's not clear if the city's opposition will prompt Canadian Blood Services (CBS) to alter its plan to make Hamilton one of five Ontario cities with pay-for-plasma collection sites as part of a deal struck with Spanish health-care company Grifols.
CBS could not be reached for comment by deadline Monday. But when Horwath's motion first appeared in April, the independent organization told The Spectator that the only applicable legislation was the province's Voluntary Blood Donations Act.
The provincial act bans paying for plasma donation in Ontario, but CBS has declared itself exempt from the law as the charity that runs the blood system nationally. It also claims Grifols is exempt because the private company is acting as its agent when pay-for-plasma collection sites open in Hamilton, Cambridge, Whitby and two other yet-to-be-determined Ontario locations.
This is the second time Horwath has fought against a pay-for-plasma private clinic in Hamilton. She played a role as leader of the NDP in getting the provincial law passed in 2014 to stop Canadian Plasma Resources from opening at 370 Main St. E. and in two downtown Toronto locations.
"Yet again, Hamilton is being targeted," said Horwath. "The for-profit plasma industry targets racialized and disadvantaged neighbourhoods, drawing from folks living in chronic poverty, folks who are unhoused, students and just everyday folks who are facing economic hardship and that is evident in the locations that they choose to set up."
Discussion at the committee Monday suggested the Hamilton site will be at 2255 Barton St. E., near the Red Hill Valley Parkway and Nash Road North.
The Ministry of Health did not respond by deadline on whether it will step in to stop the clinic being located in Hamilton now that city councillors have voted to oppose it.
The province has not taken a clear stand on the pay-for-plasma clinics. On one hand, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones has said that the centres legally conform to the legislation. But the spokesperson also said that the health minister has not provided CBS and Grifols with written confirmation of the legal loophole.
At issue is the country's reliance on the global market for plasma, a component of blood, and the need to ensure Canadians have reliable access to immunoglobulins, which is a medication made from plasma. About 85 per cent of Canada's supply comes from outside of the country with the majority sourced from the United States, where donors are paid.
"We just can't collect enough through voluntary programs to meet the needs of Canadian patients," Jennifer van Gennip, executive director of the Network of Rare Blood Disorder Organizations, told the committee in a delegation. "As country we are far too reliant on compensated plasma donations from the United States which puts us ... in a position where we could face shortages. We need to increase domestic donation collection."
CBS has a controversial plan to bring the reliance on global markets down to 50 per cent by opening 11 of its own voluntary centres and partnering with Grifols to operate 17 pay-for-plasma centres across the country.
"This deal was negotiated and signed behind closed doors," Krista Laing said during a delegation on behalf of the Canadian Union of Public Employees. "The secrecy surrounding this decision severely limits the public debate regarding its potential risks and dangers and it neglects consideration of alternatives to paid plasma collections."
The deal breaks a key recommendation against paid donation in the 1997 report of the Krever Commission in response to the tainted blood scandal in the 1980s that left more than 30,000 Canadians infected with HIV and hepatitis C through blood transfusions and plasma products.
While delegates from a number of patient organizations argued that the system has enough oversight now to ensure that paid plasma donation is safe, city councillors just couldn't get past the idea of vulnerable people having to sell parts of their body to make ends meet.
"Hamilton asserts itself as the best place to raise a family and grow old but it's hard to justify such a claim when those kept in poverty are forced to sell their blood to survive," Rosa Zetler from the Hamilton and District Labour Council said in a delegation. "Let us stand together as a community and send a powerful message."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: A motion put forward by Mayor Andrea Horwath to declare Hamilton a "paid-plasma-free zone" unanimously passed at the city's public health committee meeting Monday.

Document 19 of 500

Is Doug Ford drunk on power?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-doug-ford-drunk-on-power/docview/3064227380/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Doug+Ford+drunk+on+power%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The recent announcement by the Ford government to accelerate the timeline to put alcohol in local convenience stores, potentially costing taxpayers a billion dollars in compensation payments and lost fees, makes me wonder just how drunk Doug Ford is on the power of his office.
Ford claims this is just him following through on his promises, holding it up like a badge of honour. More beer for the minions! Yay?
This announcement was coupled with the speculation that Ford is moving up the booze timeline in order to call an early election. Up in the polls, Ford could be feeling confident that given his wins in two recent byelections he could return with more solid support. A possible Pierre Poilievre win at the federal level could jeopardize a Conservative win at the provincial level and conversely, an unpopular Trudeau government could assist Ford in winning.
That would mean more devastation for Ontario families, and in all likelihood countless deaths and increased morbidity due to untimely access to health services.
But an early election may be the gift that so many in Ontario have been waiting for: the chance to dump Ford.
Since taking office, the Ford government has shown time and again that it is incompetent, inept and possibly corrupt. We'll have to wait until the RCMP finish their investigation to get the definitive news on the corruption part.
What is taking so long is the question. Must be the reams of private emails sent by Ford's chief of staff through his non-government email accounts and then denying their existence to the integrity commissioner.
I've asked before, how is Ford able to keep his position while his office is under investigation? Lesser scandals have toppled greater leaders. Ford is not a great leader. Not even close.
A great leader doesn't sow division and hate among their people as Ford is doing right now with his comments about immigrants, implying they are behind the heinous acts of violence against Jewish schools when he has no proof or any indication at all of who was behind these crimes.
One would think Ford should have learned his "shut my big mouth" lesson after uncritically and unprofessionally jumping on the lynch mob bandwagon publicly vilifying an innocent man as a cop killer. Not surprisingly, the man in question was a brown man, an immigrant.
Great leaders aren't racists.
A great leader doesn't promise one thing and when elected turn around and break their promise almost immediately like Ford did with the Ontario basic income pilot. He promised he wouldn't cancel it and then did, brutally affecting people in our community. Or with his promise not to touch the Greenbelt, a lie worthy of the above mentioned RCMP investigation.
Great leaders aren't liars.
A great leader doesn't gorge at the public trough increasing the size of their personal retinue to an outlandish number while cutting wages and waging court battles against increases for public sector workers, particularly nurses and teachers.
Great leaders aren't thieves.
A great leader doesn't pander to special interests. They don't throw staffers under the bus declaring ignorance of their actions, which is itself quite an appalling admission. They don't take iconic Ontario landmarks like Ontario Place and the science centre and destroy their public legacy. A great leader listens to the people.
In his recent screed against immigrants, Ford declared he's "had enough" with immigrants breaking the law, on, I want to emphasize, no evidence they are. The people of Ontario, well, those people not distracted by easy access to alcohol and handily bribed with free car licensing, have had enough too.
They've had enough of the constant lying, the blatant attempts at public bribery, the growing crises in health care and housing, and the race and class divisions amplified by Ford's ignorant rhetoric and punishing policies.
Bring on an election. Dump Ford now.
CAPTION: The people of Ontario, well, those people not distracted by easy access to alcohol and handily bribed with free car licensing, have had enough of Premier Doug Ford, Margaret Shkimba writes.

June 4: A touching story from the Canadian Open, Pink Shirt Day is worthwhile and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-4-touching-story-canadian-open-pink-shirt/docview/3064227180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+4%3A+A+touching+story+from+the+Canadian+Open%2C+Pink+Shirt+Day+is+worthwhile+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A touching story from The Open
Re: In the midst of a terrific round, Mackenzie Hughes honours a friend (June 1)
Thanks to Scott Radley for his article on Mackenzie Hughes and how he honoured a friend. I followed Mackenzie for his round on Friday and noticed the jersey he wore on 13 and wondered what it represented. Nice to read a touching story of a special friendship.
Michael O'Reilly, Burlington
A beautiful sports piece
Thank you to Scott Radley for his beautiful piece in the sports section today regarding Mackenize Hughes and his friend, Bill Bath. Much needed by all of us these days.
R.J. Holloway, Oakville
Thank you, Hamilton
Re: Missing Hamilton senior with Alzheimer's found (May 29)
They say it takes a village to raise a child and take care of our seniors.
So grateful for dear family, friends and neighbours who did the civilian search and kept us nourished with encouragement, prayers and food. What a wonderful neighbourhood community we live in with people throughout the city so willing to lend a helping hand. Thank you to his beloved arts and east Mountain community. Hamilton still has a heart.
Alan and I would like to thank all the Hamilton police services, firefighters and paramedics who searched for and rescued Alan. Thank you to all the staff at Hamilton General Hospital who are doing a most excellent job to get him back home. God bless everyone.
Rosanne Peachey, Hamilton
Pink Shirt Day is worthwhile
Re: More than pink T-shirts needed to fix bullying (June 1)
There has always been, and sadly will always continue to be, bullying in schools. Even with no-tolerance policies there is only so much that educators can accomplish. Indeed, the first educators are the parents and the children who learn from their example how to interact and treat others. They also learn from what they are watching on television and screen time. Wearing pink T-shirts is important to highlight anti-bullying, and even this small act has some effect, from junior kindergarten through post-secondary. The author has examples based on her child, and they are heart-wrenching. However, from my experience both as an educator and a parent, Pink Shirt Day is a small but worthwhile activity.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Snap election would be welcomed
A few Spec readers have been speculating that recent actions by Premier Doug Ford indicate he may be considering calling a "quick election."
In light of the Greenbelt scandal, the Beer Store boondoggle, the health-care and housing crises and the totally unfounded - and Trump-like - blaming of "immigrants" for recent crimes, for many more, an election can't come quick enough.
Douglas Foley, Hamilton
'Mamma Mia,' what a show
My friend and I thoroughly enjoyed Sherwood Secondary School's production of "Mamma Mia!" Congratulations to the cast, crew, band, and production team. It takes many talents and hard work to put together such an entertaining and fun production. Thank you to staff: Mrs. George-Dalgleish, producer/director, Mr. R. Baker, producer/music director and Mr. Cividino, producer/technical director. Your students really shone.
Julie Morgan, Dundas
CAPTION: Mackenzie Hughes of Dundas, watches his tee shot on the fourth hole Friday in the second round of the Canadian Open in Hamilton. He would wear a jersey later that day in memory of a late friend.

National briefs: One dead after shooting outside Toronto schoolClimate change fund to help cities adapt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3064067690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+One+dead+after+shooting+outside+Toronto+schoolClimate+change+fund+to+help+cities+adapt&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
MRI, CT scans at private clinics to be expanded
Ontario wants to boost the number of publicly funded MRIs and CT scans offered through private clinics. Right now there are seven such clinics that provide one or both of the diagnostic imaging services. Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced Monday that Ontario has launched a call for applications to expand that number. The government says the expansion will help reduce wait times for the scans to 28 days in every region of the province - the Ontario average is currently 90 days for MRIs and 81 days for CT scans. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the clinic expansion furthers the government's "privatization agenda."
One dead after shooting outside Toronto school
One man was killed and four others were injured after suspects allegedly shot at a group of men gathered in a high school parking lot after a weekend soccer game, police said Monday. Police described the late-night attack at the North Albion Collegiate Institute as sudden and one-sided but said it was too early to confirm if it was random. Police said Sunday night's shooting came within a roughly 24-hour span of two others nearby that they described as random.
Canada
Dental program eligibility to expand June 27
The Liberal government says it will expand eligibility for the federal dental program to children and people with disabilities on June 27. Citizens' Services Minister Terry Beech says children under the age of 18 and people who receive the disability tax credit will be able to apply on that date. The government estimates that will extend eligibility to some 1.2 million people. To qualify, applicants must be uninsured and have a household income under $90,000 per year.
Climate change fund to help
cities adapt
Canadian cities and towns facing an uphill battle to stave off the effects of climate change will share more than half a billion dollars from a new federal adaptation fund, but the money is barely a blip in the bills municipalities are facing for floods, fires and other severe weather. A 2020 analysis done for the Federation of Canadian Municipalities and the Insurance Bureau of Canada said local governments need $5.3 billion a year to adapt. On Monday, Ottawa said it will provide $530 million over the next eight years for the new fund.
News services

Document 23 of 500

Taxpayers will be soaked

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/taxpayers-will-be-soaked/docview/3064067143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Taxpayers+will+be+soaked&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with Doug Ford's latest boondoggle over beer is that it amounts to double jeopardy.
First, taxpayers and teetotallers alike will be soaked by as much as $1 billion right off the top, according to opposition critics and industry analysts. Second, drinkers are destined to be hosed by higher prices as Ontario deregulates - and disaggregates - the distribution of beer in this province.
Which means the premier is giving the brewing giants taxpayers' money so they can then sell less beer to corner stores at a higher price point. Increased costs for brewers means more expensive beer for consumers.
How much more is hard to know. To understand the uncertainty, you first have to comprehend Ford's core agenda - and how he could have avoided taking on so much risk.
His objective isn't a better form of beer distribution. It's disruption for disruption's sake.
A decade ago, I wrote a series of columns investigating the iron grip of the Beer Store on beer distribution, together with the government-owned LCBO.
In 2017, the last Liberal government came up with an intelligent compromise solution - phasing out the Beer Store's stranglehold over the next decade while allowing supermarkets to get a piece of the action if they paid a licensing fee for the privilege. It turned out to be a practical, transitional plan that would take us to the end of 2026. But in 2018, Ford won power by promising "buck-a-beer" and "beer in corner stores."
The first slogan was based on an unsustainable and uneconomical model that promptly went flat. The second promise was equally unaffordable, because it required ripping up a binding contract with the Beer Store - and related contracts with big-box stores and supermarkets - merely to appease the convenience store sector.
Over time, Ford came to realize the infeasibility of that implausible promise, which would have triggered costly litigation amounting to as much as $1 billion in legal penalties and fees. He waited, and waited, until he could wait no longer.
Last week, Ford announced that the 10-year deal with the big brewers would be wound down ahead of schedule. But to get his way, we all have to pay.
There's $225 million in transition compensation to the Beer Store, plus another $375 million to rebate service fees that it pays the LCBO. The opposition Liberals argued a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains could total more than $150 million in lost revenue.
If you add in the forgone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores, that's at least $300 million more money left on the table. Which adds up to a "billion-dollar beer boondoggle," according to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Ford insists the Liberal calculation is unfounded (the government added later that it will in fact get $9 million in licensing fees), and counters the existing beer framework is costing the economy untold millions in unrealized savings, notably through increased competition.
And there is now reason to believe supermarkets may be rethinking their dedication to the distribution of beer. I've long argued for giving priority to supermarkets over so-called corner stores (no longer on every corner) that tend to charge inconveniently high prices as a premium for proximity.
Now, we may lose supermarket distribution while gaining more chain convenience stores selling a smaller selection of beer at higher prices. Which means higher costs for taxpayers and drinkers both.
We can't be certain how this will play out over the long term, but there is one absolute certainty over the short term: Ford could have hedged all that risk, avoided all that cost, eliminated all that exposure, by merely waiting out the original contract with the Beer Store until it lapsed in late 2026.
Net cost for waiting? Zero. Why not wait? The premier has persuaded himself that a promise is a promise, that beer in corner stores is popular in the polls and delivering on distribution somehow clears the decks for an early election, possibly next spring, while he is riding high in public opinion surveys.
A premier who seems so hungry for power he will spare no expense to make beer more expensive at the cash register and more costly for the treasury.
Unfortunately this isn't about affordability, it's about electability.
Martin Regg Cohn covers politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION;

Subject: Prices; Licenses; Convenience stores; Cost control; Public opinion surveys; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Cost control Public opinion surveys Supermarkets

Is Premier Ford drunk on power?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-premier-ford-drunk-on-power/docview/3064067112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Premier+Ford+drunk+on+power%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The recent announcement by the Ford government to accelerate the timeline to put alcohol in local convenience stores, potentially costing taxpayers $1 billion in compensation payments and lost fees, makes me wonder just how drunk Doug Ford is on the power of his office.
Ford claims this is just him following through on his promises, holding it up like a badge of honour. More beer for the minions! Yay?
This announcement was coupled with the speculation that Ford is moving up the booze timeline in order to call an early election. Up in the polls, Ford could be feeling confident that given his wins in two recent byelections he could return with more solid support. A possible Pierre Poilievre win at the federal level could jeopardize a Conservative win at the provincial level and conversely, an unpopular Trudeau government could assist Ford in winning.
That would mean more devastation for Ontario families, and in all likelihood countless deaths and increased morbidity due to untimely access to health services.
But an early election may be the gift that so many in Ontario have been waiting for: the chance to dump Ford.
Since taking office, the Ford government has shown time and again that it is incompetent, inept and possibly corrupt. We'll have to wait until the RCMP finishes its investigation to get the definitive news on the corruption part.
What is taking so long is the question. Must be the reams of private emails sent by Ford's chief of staff through his non-government email accounts and then denying their existence to the integrity commissioner.
I've asked before, how is Ford able to keep his position while his office is under investigation? Lesser scandals have toppled greater leaders. Ford is not a great leader. Not even close.
A great leader doesn't sow division and hate among their people as Ford is doing right now with his comments about immigrants, implying they are behind the heinous acts of violence against Jewish schools when he has no proof or any indication at all of who was behind these crimes.
One would think Ford should have learned his "shut my big mouth" lesson after uncritically and unprofessionally jumping on the lynch mob bandwagon publicly vilifying an innocent man as a cop killer. Not surprisingly, the man in question was a brown man, an immigrant.
Great leaders aren't racists.
A great leader doesn't promise one thing and when elected turn around and break their promise almost immediately like Ford did with the Ontario basic income pilot. He promised he wouldn't cancel it and then did, brutally affecting people in our community. Or with his promise not to touch the Greenbelt, a lie worthy of the above mentioned RCMP investigation.
Great leaders aren't liars.
A great leader doesn't gorge at the public trough increasing the size of their personal retinue to an outlandish number while cutting wages and waging court battles against increases for public sector workers, particularly nurses and teachers.
Great leaders aren't thieves.
A great leader doesn't pander to special interests. They don't throw staffers under the bus declaring ignorance of their actions, which is itself quite an appalling admission. They don't take iconic Ontario landmarks like Ontario Place and the science centre and destroy their public legacy.
A great leader listens to the people.
In his recent screed against immigrants, Ford declared he's "had enough" with immigrants breaking the law, on, I want to emphasize, no evidence they are. The people of Ontario, well, those people not distracted by easy access to alcohol and handily bribed with free car licensing, have had enough too.
They've had enough of the constant lying, the blatant attempts at public bribery, the growing crises in health care and housing, and the race and class divisions amplified by Ford's ignorant rhetoric and punishing policies.
Bring on an election. Dump Ford now.
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and friend her on Facebook.

Title: Is Premier Ford drunk on power?

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.11

Publication year: 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 11899417

Document 25 of 500

Will opposition to plasma clinic matter?: City declared a 'paid-plasma-free zone' but no indication blood agency will react

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-opposition-plasma-clinic-matter/docview/3064066914/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+opposition+to+plasma+clinic+matter%3F%3A+City+declared+a+%27paid-plasma-free+zone%27+but+no+indication+blood+agency+will+react&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City councillors have voted to take a stand against a private, for-profit blood collection centre that plans to pay for plasma when it opens in Hamilton next year.
A motion put forward by Mayor Andrea Horwath to declare Hamilton a "paid-plasma-free zone" unanimously passed at the city's public health committee meeting Monday.
"We have a moral obligation here to do the right thing and not have people selling a part of their body as a solution to poverty," Horwath told the committee. "Any entity that preys upon folks in our community that are the most vulnerable and the most in need of resources doesn't belong in our city."
It's not clear if the city's opposition will prompt Canadian Blood Services (CBS) to alter its plan to make Hamilton one of five Ontario cities with pay-for-plasma collection sites as part of a deal struck with Spanish health-care company Grifols.
CBS could not be reached for comment by deadline Monday. But when Horwath's motion first appeared in April, the independent organization told The Spectator that the only applicable legislation was the province's Voluntary Blood Donations Act.
The provincial act bans paying for plasma donation in Ontario, but CBS has declared itself exempt from the law as the charity that runs the blood system nationally. It also claims Grifols is exempt because the private company is acting as its agent when pay-for-plasma collection sites open in Hamilton, Cambridge, Whitby and two other yet-to-be-determined Ontario locations.
This is the second time Horwath has fought against a pay-for-plasma private clinic in Hamilton. She played a role as leader of the NDP in getting the provincial law passed in 2014 to stop Canadian Plasma Resources from opening at 370 Main St. E. and in two downtown Toronto locations.
"Yet again, Hamilton is being targeted," said Horwath. "The for-profit plasma industry targets racialized and disadvantaged neighbourhoods, drawing from folks living in chronic poverty, folks who are unhoused, students and just everyday folks who are facing economic hardship and that is evident in the locations that they choose to set up."
Discussion at the committee Monday suggested the Hamilton site will be at 2255 Barton St. E., near the Red Hill Valley Parkway and Nash Road North.
The Ministry of Health did not respond by deadline on whether it will step in to stop the clinic being located in Hamilton now that city councillors have voted to oppose it.
The province has not taken a clear stand on the pay-for-plasma clinics. On one hand, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones has said that the centres legally conform to the legislation. But the spokesperson also said that the health minister has not provided CBS and Grifols with written confirmation of the legal loophole.
At issue is the country's reliance on the global market for plasma, a component of blood, and the need to ensure Canadians have reliable access to immunoglobulins, which is a medication made from plasma. About 85 per cent of Canada's supply comes from outside of the country with the majority sourced from the United States, where donors are paid.
"We just can't collect enough through voluntary programs to meet the needs of Canadian patients," Jennifer van Gennip, executive director of the Network of Rare Blood Disorder Organizations, told the committee in a delegation.
"As country we are far too reliant on compensated plasma donations from the United States which puts us ... in a position where we could face shortages. We need to increase domestic donation collection."
CBS has a controversial plan to bring the reliance on global markets down to 50 per cent by opening 11 of its own voluntary centres and partnering with Grifols to operate 17 pay-for-plasma centres across the country.
"This deal was negotiated and signed behind closed doors," Krista Laing said during a delegation on behalf of the Canadian Union of Public Employees. "The secrecy surrounding this decision severely limits the public debate regarding its potential risks and dangers and it neglects consideration of alternatives to paid plasma collections."
The deal breaks a key recommendation against paid donation in the 1997 report of the Krever Commission in response to the tainted blood scandal in the 1980s that left more than 30,000 Canadians infected with HIV and hepatitis C through blood transfusions and plasma products.
While delegates from a number of patient organizations argued that the system has enough oversight now to ensure that paid plasma donation is safe, city councillors just couldn't get past the idea of vulnerable people having to sell parts of their body to make ends meet.
"Hamilton asserts itself as the best place to raise a family and grow old but it's hard to justify such a claim when those kept in poverty are forced to sell their blood to survive," Rosa Zetler from the Hamilton and District Labour Council said in a delegation.
"Let us stand together as a community and send a powerful message."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: A spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the for-profit centres legally conform to the legislation, but also said the health minister has not provided CBS and Grifols with written confirmation of the legal loophole.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter

Bonnie Crombie slams Doug Ford for 'wrong-headed' comments about attack on Jewish school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-slams-doug-ford-wrong-headed/docview/3064299804/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+slams+Doug+Ford+for+%27wrong-headed%27+comments+about+attack+on+Jewish+school&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is imploring Premier Doug Ford to be "a statesman" instead of rushing to judgment about who is to blame for an attack on a Jewish girls' school.
Ford has been criticized after suggesting immigrants were to blame for the pre-dawn May 25 shooting at North York's Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School.
"You know something, I stick with what I said. We're tired of lawlessness from ... anyone, it doesn't matter if you're here, you're new, whatever," the premier said Friday.
That was the day after he had said "you're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians," even though Toronto police have not yet identified two people sought in connection with the shooting. Police have said it is still too early to determine if the shooting was hate-motivated.
Still, Ford said he was buoyed by calls and texts from the public in support of what he said.
"It's very, very clear. My phone is blowing up from every community saying you're bang on - 99.9 per cent of everyone gets along and that's what I believe in."
But Crombie said "the premier should be a statesman and should bring people together rather than divide people."
"This is a terrible tragedy and we are seeing a rise in antisemitism, which we should all be taking very seriously," she said, pointing out Ford has erred in the past by jumping to conclusions.
"I thought he would have learned about prejudging a situation from the Umar Zameer case. So once again, he's stepping beyond himself when he should be bringing communities together and in times of heightened tension."
That was a reference to the premier's controversial denouncement of bail being granted for the man charged in the death of Toronto police Const. Jeffrey Northrup in the underground parking lot below Nathan Phillips Square in July 2021.
Zameer was acquitted by a jury in April of all charges. Ford did not apologize for his comments of three years ago, but he said he was working with "limited information" when he made them.
Crombie said it's "wrong-headed" of him to be exhibiting "very intolerant behaviour" when hate crimes reported to Toronto police have risen sharply in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war.
Police said Friday antisemitic incidents jumped by 37 per cent last year with anti-Muslim incidents up seven per cent.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Thursday she was "appalled by the premier's racist remarks" by suggesting immigrants were to blame for the shooting.
"Fighting hate with hate has never worked. Fighting antisemitism with xenophobia won't keep communities safe. He should apologize," said Stiles.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it was "dangerous and unacceptable rhetoric from Premier Ford" in the wake of "an alarming and unacceptable act of antisemitism," said Schreiner.
Amira Elghawaby, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's special representative for combating Islamophobia, said "legitimizing irrational fears, scapegoating or spreading unfounded conspiracies about immigrants, or any group, undermines social cohesion and safety."
"We can and should condemn antisemitism and the heinous acts against Jewish schools without fuelling further hate and division," said Elghawaby.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to party members at an event in Peterborough on May 21, 2024.

'Terrible deal': Doug Ford attacked over possible $1B price tag for liberalized booze sales

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/terrible-deal-doug-ford-attacked-over-possible-1b/docview/3064299580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Terrible+deal%27%3A+Doug+Ford+attacked+over+possible+%241B+price+tag+for+liberalized+booze+sales&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Under fire for what opposition rivals charge is a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle," Premier Doug Ford is firing back.
Ford is defending his decision to spend hundreds of millions of dollars of taxpayers' money to liberalize alcohol sales a little more than a year earlier than scheduled.
While Ford maintains it will cost $225 million to end the "master framework agreement" this fall with The Beer Store, owned by Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, critics argue the tab could ultimately be three or four times higher.
That's due to an additional $375 million in rebates to The Beer Store as well as foregone tax revenue and a 10 per cent discount for the retailers that will buy products from the LCBO, which could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
"Everyone from the wine growers to the wine producers, the beer producers, they're all for it. The only people who aren't for it are the NDP and the Liberals," the Progressive Conservative premier told the legislature Monday.
"Each and every one of you, why don't you give us your word you'll never show up to a convenience store, you'll never show up to a retail store," he said, goading New Democratic and Liberal MPPs across the chamber floor.
"Never going to happen - never, ever going to happen."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "people are shaking their heads at this terrible deal" at a time when more than 2.3 million Ontarians can't find a family doctor.
"A billion dollars to get out of a deal that was going to expire in a year? Something doesn't smell right about this. We know it, the people of Ontario know it. We're not going to stop fighting to find out more," said Stiles.
"This smells just like the Greenbelt," she said, referring to the Tories' $8.28-billion land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said all Ontarians should be paying attention to Ford's booze gambit since they are footing the bill.
"Let's be very clear what's happening here ... taxpayers' money is being used to expedite a deal one year," said Crombie, adding Ford is "using your money whether you believe the numbers are $225 million (and) I think they're closer to $1 billion ... it's your money."
"This is a billion-dollar booze boondoggle. This is a significant problem when we have priorities of Ontarians that are not being addressed," she said, pointing to the need for more doctors and other health-care professionals.
"Nobody is asking for more access to beer and spirits at the corner store. What they're asking for is access to a family doctor. Let's get our priorities right."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "the premier's putting booze before beds for hospitals."
"I mean, the fact you have 2.3 million Ontarians without lack access to a family doctor and this guy ...is wasting a billion dollars to put booze in corner stores one year early - it's outrageous."
With buzz about an early election next spring, a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote, Schreiner said Ford is clearly under the gun.
"He's feeling the pressure of delivering on a promise that he's failed to deliver on for six years - and now he's willing to spend a billion dollars of our money to further his own political ends."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces that beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in Toronto, May 24, 2024. Andrew Francis Wallace/Toronto Star

Financial watchdog launches review of Ford government's ServiceOntario deal with Staples

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/financial-watchdog-launches-review-ford/docview/3064299563/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Financial+watchdog+launches+review+of+Ford+government%27s+ServiceOntario+deal+with+Staples&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The government's move of nine ServiceOntario centres into Staples stores to offer longer hours and easy parking is being reviewed by the province's independent Financial Accountability Office.
Prompted by Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman over concerns about the financial arrangements, the effort will compare "the costs and benefits" of the Staples deal compared with the alternative of continuing contracts with independent private operators, the office said.
"We welcome this," Public and Business Service Delivery Minister Todd McCarthy told reporters Monday.
"We're saving a million dollars over three years, and we're getting more convenience, more access, longer hours, weeknights to eight or nine ... all day Saturdays."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she hopes the Financial Accountability Office can shed some light on the sole-sourced deal that was sealed without competitive bidding.
"We're wondering who benefits ... and why we're driving business into American big box international stores at the expense of small franchise, mom-and-pop businesses," she added in a reference to independent operators whose contracts were not renewed.
"This is very concerning."
Bowman (Don Valley West) said more information is needed on what she calls a "backroom deal" between Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government and Staples, which will benefit from increased foot traffic in its stores and possibly from taxpayer-funded renovations.
"We really don't know who's benefitting," said Bowman.
McCarthy has defended the contract as a pilot project and noted it is not a new concept because the province - even under a previous Liberal government - has been using retail partners such as Canadian Tire, IDA and Home Hardware for ServiceOntario locations for years to provide more convenience for customers.
Government officials said the savings in the deal are the difference between the estimated cost of $10.2 million in operating the ServiceOntario outlets in Staples over three years versus $11.1 million at their previous locations under independent operators.
No details or assumptions behind those calculations have been released by the government.
The Financial Accountability Office said it expects to report back early next year, but warned it may not be able to disclose some information about the deal if it is deemed "commercially sensitive."
CAPTION: A ServiceOntario location is shown in Whitby, Ont. on Jan. 27, 2024.

Document 29 of 500

Ford government issues call to private clinics to help ease MRI and CT backlogs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-issues-call-private-clinics-help/docview/3064298944/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+issues+call+to+private+clinics+to+help+ease+MRI+and+CT+backlogs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wanted: more MRI and CT clinics to ease waiting lists.
Eleven months after unveiling a controversial plan to move thousands more surgeries and diagnostic procedures out of hospitals, Premier Doug Ford's government has put out a call for applications from private clinics.
Job one is applications for CT and MRI clinic licences to be issued in the fall, with separate calls for applications from clinics to provide gastrointestinal endoscopies and orthopedic surgeries to come this summer and fall.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the aim is to provide an extra 100,000 diagnostic scans annually, all paid for with patients' OHIP cards.
"Increasing the number of MRI and CT scans being done each year is the next step we're taking to reduce wait times ... ensuring people get the care they need, when they need it," she said in a statement.
Opposition parties raised concerns the Progressive Conservatives are pushing more privatization in the health-care system instead of investing money in public hospitals, leaving room for investors to profit from medical treatments.
"I still hear (of) hospitals that are desperately fundraising ... to try to get an MRI," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, warning "we are seeing the dismantling of our health-care system."
"We shouldn't have a system like this, right? We should have a system where people can know they can show up and get the kind of care they need," she added, referring in part to regular emergency room closures at smaller hospitals with severe staff shortages.
"People are showing up at emergency rooms only to find the doors closed."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the move is "just one more step in the privatization of our health-care system" amid increasing fears that "people will be going into these clinics with their credit cards."
Her party's health critic, MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), cautioned there is the risk of patients being "upsold" in private clinics for scans or surgeries to the point where they will have to pay out of pocket for some services or extras they may not need but feel under pressure to buy at a vulnerable time.
"Within the for-profit sector, there are many opportunities to be upsold," added Shamji, an emergency room physician, citing full-body MRIs as one example.
Jones said Ontarians have been going to private X-ray and other clinics for years and said the goal is to shorten wait lists for CTs and MRIs to 28 days. Applications for the clinics close Aug. 12.
In a statement, her office offered reassurance about the use of credit cards.
"No centre can refuse an (OHIP) insured service to a patient who chooses not to purchase uninsured upgrades and no patient can pay to receive insured services faster than anyone else."
Jones also moved to counter concerns from critics that new private clinics will poach doctors, nurses, technicians and other workers from hospitals already struggling with staff shortages.
She said legislation passed earlier this year requires new clinics to submit detailed staffing plans showing their linkages to the public health-care system and how they consulted with partners in the health system.
A private Windsor laser eye clinic doing cataract surgeries under OHIP has emerged as a prototype for the new service model, with doctors who have privileges at the border city's hospital doing procedures at the clinic.
CAPTION: File: Sylvia Jones, Deputy Premier and Minister of Health, right, at a press conference on Thursday February 1, 2024 in Peterborough, Ont.

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Privatization; Bone surgery; Clinics; Labor shortages

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Privatization Labor shortages

Document 30 of 500

We need to stop funding the premier's personal vanity projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-need-stop-funding-premiers-personal-vanity/docview/3063665112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+to+stop+funding+the+premier%27s+personal+vanity+projects&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What can you get for $63 these days?
If you have an efficient car, $63 might get you a tank of gas. A keen deal hunter could probably find a patio chair or cookware set for around $63. For my fellow cat parents out there, $63 is enough for a medium-size bag of dry cat food, assuming your furry friends don't have expensive tastes.
In Doug Ford's Ontario, though, you have no choice but to spend that money on beer. Putting beer and wine into convenience stores, to be exact.
Between a $225-million payout and $375-million rebate to the privately-run Beer Store, $300 million in licence rebates to retailers and a $74-million wholesale discount to the grocery giants, Ontarians are on the hook for nearly $975 million, which works out to around $63 for every adult and child in the province. That does not even take into account the hundreds of millions in lost revenue for the LCBO - revenue that helps fund this province's essential services.
While Ford is the leader of the Ontario Progressive Conservatives, his fiscal track record is neither "progressive" nor "conservative." In fact, it seems like the current policy of the PCs is to throw public money at the premier's personal vanity projects, whether Ontarians want them or not.
Ford's fiddling with Ontario's alcohol market is only the most recent in a long line of strange, fiscally reckless policy decisions that are more populist than practical.
Take the government's insistence on spending $10 billion on the unnecessary Highway 413, for example.
Experts say we do not need another massive highway that will quickly become clogged with more oversized personal trucks and SUVs. Farmers, conservation advocates and Indigenous leaders have all said saving less than 30 minutes' commute time is not worth losing 800 hectares of rich, bountiful farmland and endangering 85 waterways. And yet, Ford's obsession with the personal automobile has won out and the Highway 413 is moving ahead.
Or take the example of the $650-million price tag associated with the Ontario Place redevelopment scheme. Ford has long been fixated on the space, once even pitching a splashy waterfront casino or entertainment space in an effort to remake Toronto's waterfront in the image of his beloved Chicago. Community advocates have rallied to oppose the privatization of the modernist landmark, but the premier's obsession means the Austria-based Therme Spa group will gobble up most of the redeveloped space with a healthy taxpayer subsidy.
Ontarians cannot ignore Ford's spending spree no matter how hard we try. Enter any store that plays music, turn any dial on your car radio, or click on any YouTube video and, chances are, you will be subjected to one of the government's self-congratulatory ads about their schemes. Millions of taxpayer dollars have been dedicated to telling taxpayers about how Ford is spending taxpayer money.
Ford's spending on personal projects continues as the crises we face become worse. A failure to invest in housing means young Ontarians are fleeing the province. Dwindling investment in public health care and mental health support means doctors and nurses are burning out while those in the most need of help - seniors, children, and people with complex mental health needs - are left to fend for themselves. Defunding public education means overworked teachers face ballooning class sizes and crumbling schools.
And now, adding insult to injury, Ford appears poised to call an early election. Despite his party holding nearly two-thirds of the seats at Queen's Park, Ford is willing to force an expensive election on Ontarians so he can blame this province's financial problems on Justin Trudeau, rather than take responsibility for his own financial mismanagement.
Ontarians deserve better than that. We deserve a rational, predicable government that pursues fact-based policies that work for everyone. Or, at the very least, we deserve a government that recognizes $63 is too high a price for a convenience store beer.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, left, and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy after announcing on May 24 that beer and wine will be available at corner stores after Labour Day. Chris Erl estimates the move will cost every Ontarian about $63.
