Document 31 of 500

June 3: 'Once again the world is wrong,' enough of the 'mansplaining' and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-3-once-again-world-is-wrong-enough/docview/3063664377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+3%3A+%27Once+again+the+world+is+wrong%2C%27+enough+of+the+%27mansplaining%27+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'Once again, the world is wrong'
Re: It's past time Israel listened to the world (May 30)
The May 30 editorial is replete with unfounded claims. As in several past incidents, the rush to wrongly blame Israel for the recent deaths of civilians in Rafah is astounding. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu claimed it was a "tragic mishap" and categorically denied that Israeli forces purposely attacked a refugee camp. In fact, recent evidence points to Hamas munitions as the likely cause for the conflagration.
Hamas declared war on Israel on Oct. 7 and has turned down almost all ceasefire proposals. They continue to hold Israeli hostages (women and children and the elderly), continue to launch rockets and continue to fight. They have never changed their charter's aim to destroy Israel and kill all Jews. "Negotiating" a unilateral Israeli withdrawal will only embolden their premeditated campaign.
Once again, the world is wrong!
Sharon Marcovitz, Ancaster
Pressure Hamas to surrender
Your editorial has it backwards. It's past time the world listened to Israel.
If you believe Hamas cannot be destroyed by bombs, do you think it will disappear through negotiations? Do you seriously think that 1,400 years of religiously mandated hatred will all go away with signatures on a paper? Do you honestly believe any of the major wars and battles initiated against Israel since 1947 had nothing to do with Israel being Jewish? And who exactly is Israel supposed to negotiate with? Hamas? Hezbollah? The Palestinian Authority? Iran?
If the world wants this current war to end, it must pressure Hamas to surrender, to return the hostages and to forfeit its arms. To prevent further wars, stop sending billions of dollars to Hamas via UNRWA and other programs, much of which it's believed funds tunnels and armaments and lines the pockets of Hamas' leaders. Stop supporting Iran's nuclear program. Stop publishing Hamas propaganda as fact while casting doubt on Israel's claims. Start listening to Israel.
Steven Scheffer, Burlington
Reason a slave to passion
The editorial in Thursday's Spectator was a very cogent call for reason to prevail in the current Israel-Hamas war. It is too bad we don't live in an age of reason. We probably never have. According to the philosopher David Hume, "Reason is a slave to the passions." Or as Mike Tyson famously stated it, "Everyone's got a plan until they get punched in the face."
Israel has a long history of being punched in the face. In 722 BCE, the most feared nation in the ancient world, Assyria (whose soldiers were so vicious they make Hamas look like a bunch of Boy Scouts) levelled the country. The Babylonians cleared the country once again in 512 BCE. After the Romans had enough of their rebellious province of Judea, they finished the annihilation in 71 CE.
So now that the Jewish people have their homeland again, they aren't going anywhere. Gaza was the part of ancient Israel where David took on Goliath. Benjamin Netanyahu says the war will continue till the end of the year. But he didn't say which year.
David McInnis, Ancaster
Another promise is kept
Education Minister Stephen Lecce continues his push to "get back to basics" by introducing a slew of subjects that he has "heard so often from business and parents" need to be included in the school curriculum. The need to learn how to cook, understand good nutrition and basic skills like how to shop on a budget. Fortunately for the minister, these skills and many others are already being taught in Ontario schools. So once again, a promise made is a promise kept.
Phil Beard, Dundas
Install centre guard rails
Re: Hamilton to review tightest curve on Red Hill Valley Parkway (May 28)
The first thing that could be done without affecting any widening of the highway is to install centre guard rails. It is obvious (to me) that if a car is headed across the median, a guard rail will impede its progress into a head-on crash. The fact that the Haudenosaunee Development Institute (HDI) wants to put a hold on doing this until money is budgeted for a review seems self-serving. Nothing they see will alter the fact that there is already a centre median and drivers need protection from cars coming in the opposite direction.
Andy Price, Ancaster
No such thing as bad press?
Re: We're finally number 1 (May 30)
I totally agree with letter giving congratulations to the city of Hamilton for having the worst road three years in a row! Bravo for being on the top 10 list!
Actually, I think the city of Hamilton loves chaos and the recognition that goes with it. Wait until Main Street changes and LRT comes in. Will the fun ever end?
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Enough of the 'mansplaining'
Re: Melania Trump sentences herself to solitary confinement as Donald Trump awaits verdict (May 28)
Whatever his views on American politics, Vinay Menon stoops to a new low by mansplaining the situation of a woman he has never had occasion to interact with. What condescending arrogance!
Henrietta Wasik, Stoney Creek
Thanks for the superb care
I just wanted to give a shout out to the nursing team on 8 South at Hamilton General, especially Mom's nurse, Kaitlin. Also thanks to Dr. Yousef and his residents. They provided superb medical care to my 98-year-old mother recently. From the emergency department to 8 South, she was in the hands of true professionals. Mom's discharge was well organized as she returned home to her retirement home with all the necessary services. Thank you, Hamilton General.
Janet Connelly, Stoney Creek
Our 'race to the bottom' premier
Premier Doug Ford is about to pay the international beer conglomerates that own the Beer Store $225 million of our tax dollars so he can get beer and wine into corner stores faster so he can win an election because, by wasting this money, his approval rating will undoubtedly go up. All this while he nefariously starves our health care and education systems. I don't need help understanding the misguided priorities of our "race to the bottom" premier. After so many years of this foolishness, I have grown to understand him. But, in what bizarro world does he get away with and get rewarded for the constant wasting and foolish spending of our tax dollars? Oh, Ontario, for thee I shed a tear.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge
Why is city still struggling with cyberattack?
How can the city of Hamilton still be suffering the effects of a cyberattack? If this had happened to a corporation, it would have gone out of business in March. Case in point: Stelco underwent an attack a few years ago. With the ingenuity and drive of many in the company, they overcame the intrusion relatively quickly, getting production back on line and continuing to profit. I feel bad for the citizens of the city I grew up in. They deserve better.
Paul Reynolds, Welland
CAPTION: Palestinians flee from the southern Gaza city of Rafah during an Israeli ground and air offensive in the city on May 28. Negotiating a unilateral Israeli withdrawal will only embolden Hamas, a reader says.

Prepping for possible TTC strike on Friday: Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/prepping-possible-ttc-strike-on-friday-heres-what/docview/3063664131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prepping+for+possible+TTC+strike+on+Friday%3A+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Torontonians brace for a possible TTC strike on Friday, many outside the GTA - who also rely on transit to catch a game, play or show - will also be impacted.
Here's what you need to know:
 A potential strike can start Friday, June 7 at midnight, for workers with the ATU Local 113.
 A walkout by the TTC's approximate 11,500 operators, collectors, maintenance workers, station staff and other front-line employees could mean a full shutdown of Toronto's bus, streetcar and subway network. However, it does not mean there will automatically be a labour disruption Friday; it only indicates the earliest date such a disruption could begin.
 It's the first time there's been talk of a strike in 16 years. In 2008, the strike only lasted a weekend before the Ontario Liberals passed legislation ordering TTC staff back to work.
 In 2023, the Superior Court of Justice ruled the legislation violated workers' charter rights.
 The current contract for TTC staff with that union expired on March 31.
 The TTC service takes patrons on almost 2.5 million trips each weekday.
 For tourists commuting downtown Toronto, GO Transit would still be an option should a strike occur. Check the schedule for a stop near you, and fare price at gotransit.com. Be aware there some parking and schedule changes at some locations over the next few weeks as GO Transit does repair work. And also be aware if there is a strike, there could be picket lines near Union Station.
 For those willing to drive to downtown Toronto, the city has a list of parking locations with its Green P Parking, including sites with electric vehicle charging spots. Visit parking.greenp.com.
 Other options could include a ride-booking service like Uber or Lyft, but the cost may be prohibitive for some.
 The City of Toronto is also prepared for a potential strike, but has not shared its contingency plan, as contract negotiations continue this week.
- with files from Torstar News Service
CAPTION: A looming TTC strike could impact those wanting to use the transit service to get into the city.

Subject: Contract negotiations

Document 33 of 500

We can't afford the premier's vanity projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-cant-afford-premiers-vanity-projects/docview/3063326929/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+can%27t+afford+the+premier%27s+vanity+projects&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What can you get for $63 these days?
If you have an efficient car, $63 might get you a tank of gas. A keen deal hunter could probably find a patio chair or cookware set for around $63. For my fellow cat parents out there, $63 is enough for a medium-size bag of dry cat food, assuming your furry friends don't have expensive tastes.
In Doug Ford's Ontario, though, you have no choice but to spend that money on beer. Putting beer and wine into convenience stores, to be exact.
Between a $225-million payout and $375-million rebate to the privately-run Beer Store, $300 million in licence rebates to retailers and a $74-million wholesale discount to the grocery giants, Ontarians are on the hook for nearly $975 million, which works out to around $63 for every adult and child in the province. That does not even take into account the hundreds of millions in lost revenue for the LCBO - revenue that helps fund this province's essential services.
While Ford is the leader of the Ontario Progressive Conservatives, his fiscal track record is neither "progressive" nor "conservative." In fact, it seems like the current policy of the PCs is to throw public money at the premier's personal vanity projects, whether Ontarians want them or not.
Ford's fiddling with Ontario's alcohol market is only the most recent in a long line of strange, fiscally reckless policy decisions that are more populist than practical.
Take the government's insistence on spending $10 billion on the unnecessary Highway 413, for example.
Experts say we do not need another massive highway that will quickly become clogged with more oversized personal trucks and SUVs. Farmers, conservation advocates and Indigenous leaders have all said saving less than 30 minutes' commute time is not worth losing 800 hectares of rich, bountiful farmland and endangering 85 waterways. And yet, Ford's obsession with the personal automobile has won out and Highway 413 is moving ahead.
Or take the example of the $650-million price tag associated with the Ontario Place redevelopment scheme. Ford has long been fixated on the space, once even pitching a splashy waterfront casino or entertainment space in an effort to remake Toronto's waterfront in the image of his beloved Chicago. Community advocates have rallied to oppose the privatization of the modernist landmark, but the premier's obsession means the Austria-based Therme Spa group will gobble up most of the redeveloped space with a healthy taxpayer subsidy.
Ontarians cannot ignore Ford's spending spree no matter how hard we try. Enter any store that plays music, turn any dial on your car radio, or click on any YouTube video and, chances are, you will be subjected to one of the government's self-congratulatory ads about their schemes. Millions of taxpayer dollars have been dedicated to telling taxpayers about how Ford is spending taxpayer money.
Ford's spending on personal projects continues as the crises we face become worse. A failure to invest in housing means young Ontarians are fleeing the province. Dwindling investment in public health care and mental health support means doctors and nurses are burning out while those in the most need of help - seniors, children, and people with complex mental health needs - are left to fend for themselves. Defunding public education means overworked teachers face ballooning class sizes and crumbling schools.
And now, adding insult to injury, Ford appears poised to call an early election. Despite his party holding nearly two-thirds of the seats at Queen's Park, Ford is willing to force an expensive election on Ontarians so he can blame this province's financial problems on Justin Trudeau, rather than take responsibility for his own financial mismanagement.
Ontarians deserve better than that. We deserve a rational, predicable government that pursues fact-based policies that work for everyone. Or, at the very least, we deserve a government that recognizes $63 is too high a price for a convenience store beer.
Chris Erl is a born and raised Hamiltonian working as a researcher, writer and
activist-academic. You
can read more of his work
at sewersocialists.ca.

Document 34 of 500

We need to define affordable housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-define-affordable-housing/docview/3063326629/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+to+define+affordable+housing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After months of failing to provide mandated data, the Ontario government finally received its latest tranche of funding from the federal government as part of the 10-year National Housing Strategy.
At the same time, the yawning gap between competing visions of "good housing" is widening. Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives stress that building more "single-dwelling homes" is the primary goal for its housing programs.
Meanwhile, 2024 federal government budget links infrastructure funding to allowing more "missing middle homes," including fourplexes and multi-family apartments, with higher densities near mass transit.
It's becoming increasingly clear that to address the housing crisis, all three levels of government need to get on the same page about what "affordable housing" means and how they're going to make it happen. As part of federal Housing Accelerator Fund, municipalities must undertake a standardized housing need assessment, with income categories and data derived from the Housing Assessment Resource Tools project (which I helped develop). However, when it comes to federal programs and research, the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) uses at least six different definitions of "housing affordability." Meanwhile, the Ontario government consistently substitutes the term "attainable housing" without defining what it means.
Although it is far from ideal, the standard that "affordable housing" costs no more than 30 per cent of pre-tax household income is the most common measure both internationally and in Canada.
In the 1980s, first time home ownership was affordable to moderate- and median-income households across Canada, with the average house price costing three times the average household income.
Now that multiple has tripled, meaning that buying a first home is only affordable to the top 10 per cent of incomes for those aged 25 to 34. So, the rental market is crowded by those who would otherwise own homes.
Meanwhile, the average asking rent for a one-bedroom apartment in a major Canadian city is $2,188 a month, about twice what a median-income individual can afford, let alone a family.
This affordable housing crisis, equivalent to the crisis facing Canada just after the Second World War, requires co-ordinated action from all three levels of government.
The federal government should be setting housing targets, with nonmarket housing sub-targets, for all provinces, territories, and municipalities. A 1944 federal report recommended one third nonmarket housing for low-income households, one third rent-regulated (either co-operative or market rental) for moderate and median income households, and one third market ownership. Those sub-targets still make sense. Scotiabank recommends doubling nonmarket housing stock over the next decade.
RBC recommends quadrupling current nonmarket housing construction rates, from an average of 14,000 to 57,000 per year. Across Canada, a target of 300,000 home completions a year, with a third each nonmarket, purpose-built rental, and market ownership, would be a great start to fixing the housing crisis.
There is a federal sub-target of a mere 19,660 nonmarket homes being produced in Ontario over the decade between 2018 and 2028 in return for federal funding support. To give a sense of how minimal this target is, more than 42,000 nonmarket homes were produced in Canada in the single year 1971, over half in Ontario. The provincial government was unable to say how it would meet even those exceedingly low targets.
But the federal government shouldn't be throwing bricks from its glass house: a 2022 report from its own National Housing Council found that only three per cent of the homes created by its most costly program, now renamed the Apartment Construction Loan Program, were affordable to the low income households most in need.
All levels of government must start by being honest and transparent about what they mean by affordable housing, which income categories and types of households they are helping with their programs, and then track housing completions according to targets and sub-targets set at the federal level, monitored through publicly accessible dashboards.
Merely encouraging single-family homes to stretch out into infinity isn't enough.
Carolyn Whitzman is a housing policy researcher and adjunct professor at the University of Ottawa. She is the author of
the forthcoming "Home Truths: Fixing Canada's Housing Crisis."
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Carolyn Whitzman;

Document 35 of 500

'A lot of people are very angry:' How a Doug Ford promise set off a health-care fight in cottage country

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lot-people-are-very-angry-how-doug-ford-promise/docview/3063326569/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+lot+of+people+are+very+angry%3A%27+How+a+Doug+Ford+promise+set+off+a+health-care+fight+in+cottage+country&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it Muskoka's Battle of the Beds.
As cottage season arrives, a feud is raging over new hospitals for the Bracebridge and Huntsville areas, where the population swells dramatically every summer.
A basic question still festers two years after the Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare system got a $14-million planning grant from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Which community in the picturesque Muskoka lakes region 200 km north of Toronto should have the most in-patient beds?
Sharp disagreement between south Muskoka (Bracebridge) and the north (Huntsville), a 30-minute drive up Highway 11, is overshadowing the premier's assurances from April 2022, just weeks before the last provincial election.
"It's time you get the modern hospital capacity you desperately need," pledged Ford - who has a cottage between the two towns - as he made the announcement in Huntsville to increase the bed count at both.
But with planning for the $1 billion in replacement hospitals now getting down to the nitty-gritty - both facilities will see their emergency departments doubled to 27 treatment bays, for example- complications are popping up.
That's because Bracebridge is losing in-patient beds and Huntsville is gaining them under the latest plan from Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare, the organization formed two decades ago to operate the previously independent hospitals within a joint health-care system.
"There's definitely been some drama," says Dr. Luke Wu, an internal medicine specialist in Bracebridge, referring to tense moments at community meetings held over the winter. More consultations are slated this month.
"A lot of people are very angry," adds Wu, one of dozens of Bracebridge doctors concerned about the long-term future of hospital care there and for nearby Gravenhurst.
While patients remain plentiful - a May 22 memo to staff enacted the hospital system's "gridlock policy'' when "high occupancy" forced the Bracebridge campus to temporarily put admitted patients in the day surgery unit - patience is wearing thin and the clock is ticking.
Officials at Muskoka Algonquin are trying to nail down a final plan to submit for provincial government approval so that construction can begin in the next few years.
"We are dedicated to meeting Muskoka's evolving health-care needs now and into the future," says chief executive officer Cheryl Harrison.
"Enhancing specialized services like the regional stroke program, Level 3 ICU (intensive care unit) and rehabilitation unit ensures timely, expert care. Doubling our emergency departments improves capacity during peak seasonal periods."
The latest version of the plan puts 36 in-patient beds in the new Bracebridge hospital - down from 67 now at the town's South Muskoka Memorial Hospital.
Huntsville is slated for 121 beds, up from 56 currently at Huntsville District Memorial Hospital.
Proponents of the plan maintain it is not practical to have hospitals in the two towns duplicating services.
That blueprint was described as a "strategic transition from operating two independent small hospitals to adopting a cohesive regional health-care approach" in a recent memo from chief of staff Dr. Khaled Abdel-Razek and more than a dozen department heads on the Muskoka Algonquin medical advisory committee.
With battle lines drawn, provincial officials say they're waiting for a community consensus to emerge.
"We know that there are supporters of the plan, depending on what part of the riding you're in, and there are detractors," says local MPP Graydon Smith (Parry Sound-Muskoka), Ford's minister of natural resources and a former mayor of Bracebridge.
"I hope there's a continued amount of conversation and engagement and everyone can reach a point where it's a win-win."
It doesn't look like it will be easy.
"There is no way for the current administration to pull this out of the fire," insists Tony Vanderstelt, a vice chair of the Save South Muskoka Hospital Committee.
"We're calling on the government to put in an independent investigator to come in and look," says Vanderstelt, who was one of thousands at an Ontario Health Coalition rally outside the legislature Thursday protesting emergency room closures, losses of hospital services and plans for more private clinics.
Huntsville gets more beds because it is designated by the Ministry of Health as a regional stroke treatment centre owing to its location mid-way between Barrie and North Bay, ensuring "timely access to critical care," says Bobbie Clark, spokesperson for Muskoka Algonquin. There will be a 10-bed intensive care unit and a 14-bed stroke unit at Huntsville.
"In contrast, the (new) Bracebridge site focuses on high-frequency outpatient services, such as day surgeries and diagnostic imaging ... including four ICU beds and an obstetrical labour and delivery room," Clark said.
The latest allotment of in-patient beds is a shift from January when the new Bracebridge hospital was to be given 14 compared with 139 for Huntsville.
That announcement set off a firestorm and sowed seeds of mistrust that linger despite the subsequent increase to the Bracebridge bed count.
"The well is definitely poisoned," says Vanderstelt, a local businessman.
The boosted bed count is not enough for many doctors in south Muskoka, where 47 physicians recently put their names on an open letter to the community.
The letter warned the reduction in in-patient beds means many local patients "will therefore need to be transferred to Huntsville," further away from their families and one step closer to getting treatment in hospitals in Barrie and Toronto, which are better equipped to deal with complicated procedures.
"Our hospital currently operates over capacity on a regular basis. The population in south Muskoka is growing and aging, and it is only rational to expect that the health-care needs of our community will be drastically increasing," the letter continues.
The letter also predicts the reduction in beds will make it tougher to attract doctors and raises concerns about "the long-term viability of the Bracebridge site."
Bracebridge doctors are keeping up the fight because they fear the 36 in-patient beds won't be nearly enough to meet the community's needs. The Muskoka area has more than 60,000 people, but during the summer months seasonal residents double the population.
"We're trying to advocate for our community," says Dr. Tina Kappos, who has been associated with the hospital for 26 years and calls the initial decision to slash the bed count "irrational."
There has been a flurry of communications from both sides as plans have been adjusted in recent months.
In the face of stiff opposition from the Bracebridge area to the original plans, Muskoka Algonquin recently issued a statement boasting of "extensive community consultation" that led to "significant modifications ... including maintaining obstetric services at both sites and adjusting bed numbers to better meet local needs."
In their open letter, the Bracebridge-area doctors urged locals to "reject this proposed model for hospital redevelopment," noting patients transferred to a new hospital in Huntsville would likely not be seen by their own doctors for acute care, as is the case now.
Bracebridge Mayor Rick Maloney is urging more discussion.
"For the hospital redevelopment plan to succeed, it must be endorsed by the doctors and key stakeholders in both north and south Muskoka. I encourage Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare to continue to work toward a solution," he wrote in a statement last month.
In Toronto, Health Minister Sylvia Jones seems to be keeping it a local issue.
"We understand that Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare is in their detailed planning phase and consulting with their community and local partners to ensure they are creating stable, consistent and connected care in the region," she said in a statement to the Star.
"We will continue to work together to ensure access to care in the region for years to come."
CAPTION: Protesters concerned about their Bracebridge hospital losing beds in a redevelopment plan were among thousands protesting the Ontario government's health-care changes on May 30, 2024 at Queen's Park in Toronto.

Subject: Health care access; Hospitals; Patients; Emergency medical care; Stroke; Health care policy; Intensive care; Planning; Physicians

Document 36 of 500

5 things we learned from a new book about Justin Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-things-we-learned-new-book-about-justin-trudeau/docview/3063116410/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+things+we+learned+from+a+new+book+about+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new book on Justin Trudeau, "The Prince" by Stephen Maher, documents a turbulent time at the top of this country. Here are five takeaways from the story of his reign, amassed through stories that hit the headlines and drama behind the scenes.
1. Even loyal MPs and close friends of Trudeau believe it's time for him to go and told Maher as much in the book, though they did so anonymously. "Every MP will tell you that their closest, most long-standing Liberal supporters all think it's time," one Liberal MP was quoted as saying in late 2023.
"I think of him more as the leader for a time and I think the times require a different kind of leader now," says one close friend.
2. When Trudeau first met Gerald Butts at the McGill University debating club, he went by the name of "Jason Tremblay" so as not to draw attention to his famous lineage. Granted, some news outlets had that little tidbit early after Trudeau became prime minister, but Maher's book reminds us of a time when Trudeau didn't even want to be called Justin or the son of a prime minister.
3. Mark Carney, the former governor of the central banks of Canada and the United Kingdom, was scared off running for the Liberals in the 2021 election because of the way he felt Trudeau's PMO has stuck a "shiv" in the career of former finance minister Bill Morneau. "Seeing Morneau being treated so shabbily seems to have given Carney pause, and he did not allow himself to be seduced. They spooked him by putting the shiv in."
4. Hanbury Strategy, a British data consultancy firm hired by the Conservatives for help in the 2021 election, flatly told leader Erin O'Toole and his entourage that their party's brand was "toxic" and that Canada was fundamentally a progressive country.
"They had no idea coming in," the 2021 national campaign manager, Fred DeLorey, told Maher. "They worked for conservative parties in Australia, U.K., mainland Europe. And we had such a toxic brand in our country. And they also identified, surprisingly to me, how progressive Canada is versus most European countries."
5. The ejection of Jody Wilson-Raybould and Jane Philpott from the Liberal caucus in 2019 was a drawn-out, messy affair and is often cast as Trudeau's imperious decision from on high. "The Prince" documents how the two dissenting ministers had lost support from much of the Liberal caucus too. In recounting a "lively" Liberal caucus gathering, Maher reports that meeting included some name-calling of Wilson-Raybould and appeals for the two women to be "banished to the worst seats in the backbench."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau greets supporters at a campaign volunteer event, in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Subject: State elections; Books; Prime ministers

Location: Canada; United Kingdom--UK

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 37 of 500

Some Liberals think Mark Carney could succeed Justin Trudeau. We talked to those who think he'd be a great leader - and others who roll their eyes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/some-liberals-think-mark-carney-could-succeed/docview/3062889402/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+Liberals+think+Mark+Carney+could+succeed+Justin+Trudeau.+We+talked+to+those+who+think+he%27d+be+a+great+leader+-+and+others+who+roll+their+eyes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The way former Liberal cabinet minister Catherine McKenna sees it, change is coming. Either her party supplies it, or they lose power.
She's quick to say that doesn't necessarily mean Justin Trudeau needs to go, though she observes that what's on offer "clearly" isn't resonating with voters.
But there is someone else - someone whose name keeps swirling around the never-ending carousel of speculation about the prime minister's future. Someone of "real substance" who, according to McKenna, understands the big challenges of our era and would stack up well against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the Liberals' archrival of the moment.
That someone is Mark Carney.
"I think he'd be a good candidate," McKenna said, when asked about the entrenched impression in political circles that the former central banker has eyes on the Liberal leadership.
"Someone like Carney is a very attractive option. And - as a contrast to Pierre Poilievre - he is experienced; he has an even temperament; he has had a life outside of politics; he has a plan. I think those are really important things."
It's hard to pin down what is going on with Carney. A former civil servant and head of central banks in two G7 countries, Canada and the United Kingdom, the 59-year-old Carney has tied his wagon to the Liberal horse. With his financier's mien, hockey references, and economics-oriented advocacy for climate action, he is not shying away from the public eye. Through Canada 2020, a Liberal-linked think tank situated just off Parliament Hill, he has a platform to expound on domestic Canadian policy from the sidelines of the political arena. Just this week, he entered the political money game when he appeared as a "special guest" at an evening fundraiser for Anna Gainey, a Liberal MP in Montreal and former party president who was part of the core group that helped Trudeau rise to the leadership and win power in 2015. He'll reprise the role on June 11, when he's slated to headline another fundraiser in Toronto for Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie.
All this has been grist for the rumour mill inside the Ottawa bubble. There was a flurry of political commentary about his perceived ambitions in recent days. Ever eager to poke at Trudeau, the Conservatives have taken to calling Carney "the next Liberal leader." But there are signs the Tories are taking him seriously, just in case. In April, Poilievre debuted a new nickname in Parliament, when he lampooned him as "Carbon Tax Carney." Other Conservatives have tried to paint Carney as an elitist, a would-be politician with "Bay Street buddies" who plans to descend from his "ivory tower" to take over the Liberal party.
Carney did not agree to an interview for this story. But some who count themselves as close to him said they're not aware of any serious organization that is getting staged for Carney to win the leadership. Whatever is happening appears less tangible than that, pre-positioning for a race that is still only hypothetical. Trudeau, after all, insists at every turn that he's not going anywhere. Carney has said he supports the prime minister and expects him to lead the Liberals in the next election.
Still. Everyone knows Trudeau can't stick around forever.
"I know for sure people constantly approach (Carney) ... They want him to do it," said Evan Solomon, the former CTV host and longtime friend of Carney's who now works with the Eurasia Group, a geopolitical consulting firm in New York City.
As Solomon explained, Carney is agreeing to help the Liberals and taking opportunities to deliver speeches, but is not "strategically weaponizing" the interest for an imminent leadership run.
"I think it's a much softer path. It's not hard organizing yet," Solomon said of Liberal leadership posturing in general. But, he added, if the leadership opened up and the interest kept growing, Carney would likely go for it.
"If an opportunity came up," Solomon said, "of course he would probably take that opportunity."
Along with McKenna, there are some Liberals willing to publicly express support for Carney, even if - with national polls consistently suggesting their party is en route to a harsh defeat at the hands of Poilievre and the Conservatives - they stop short of declaring it's time for Trudeau to go.
Responding to a query about his potential support for a Carney leadership bid, former Liberal justice minister David Lametti, who was turfed from Trudeau's cabinet before he resigned his Montreal seat early this year, replied by email: "Mark is an old friend of mine, and I have always thought highly of his intellect and his leadership skills. That is really all I am comfortable saying right now."
Others who remain in Trudeau's caucus are more critical of Carney. Some question his commitment to the party, given that he's been publicly Liberal for at least three years but has never put his name on a ballot.
"I think he should run for a seat in Parliament," one MP told the Star. "Come get in the trenches."
Another MP described Carney as smart and capable, but concluded - based on his manner of public speaking and his lack of experience on the front-lines of partisan politics - that he is not ready to step in and lead the Liberals. This MP said there is a perception in caucus that Carney only wants to jump into politics if he can do so as party leader.
"You can't just 'Ignatieff' this thing," the MP said, referring to the ex-Liberal leader Michael Ignatieff who entered politics after an academic career abroad and spearheaded the worst campaign defeat in the party's history in the 2011 election.
A third MP, who like the others agreed to speak about Carney on condition of anonymity, described him as the "love child of Bill Morneau and Michael Ignatieff" - another reference to the failed leader in 2011, combined with a nod to Trudeau's finance minister from 2015 to 2020, a corporate scion in Toronto who has since criticized the Liberal government for its high levels of spending.
"Honestly, (Carney)'s not much of a topic of conversation, other than to roll our eyes," the MP said. "If you're interested in being a future leader of a political party, it's best to put your name on a ballot and do the work."
McKenna, the former Liberal cabinet minister, rejected the comparison between Carney and Ignatieff as unfair. She cited Carney's years of experience as a public servant in Canada, and said his time abroad - including as governor of the Bank of England - is an asset, not a disadvantage, in an era of climate change and tensions between great powers. "He understands the world," she said of Carney.
For Tim Powers, a former Conservative adviser and chairman of Summa Strategies, Carney is a "wonderful human being" with an "impressive pedigree." He said: "Canadians, regardless of party, would be lucky if he entered the political arena."
Powers also argued Carney could craft a "compelling" narrative for more centrist voters, given his past in the upper ranks of the federal government when Conservative Jim Flaherty was finance minister, and how he was appointed governor of the Bank of Canada when Stephen Harper was prime minister. Born in the Northwest Territories and raised in Edmonton, Carney also brings a Western Canadian background to his international standing as a graduate from Harvard and Oxford who went on to work at the massive investment firm Goldman Sachs before he entered the public service.
Powers noted, however, that the Conservatives are already playing the "out-of-touch elite card" against Carney, which he predicted would be the challenge if the former Bank of Canada governor entered the political fray.
"If we're living in a moment where populism needs to express itself, and Pierre Poilievre is the vehicle for that expression, even someone from the outside with Mark Carney's very appealing resume and background may find it hard to win," Powers said.
Michelle Rempel Garner, a Conservative MP from Calgary, recently penned a column that argued Carney - if he came to replace Trudeau before the next election - would likely be fated to a short-lived prime ministership akin to the reign of Progressive Conservative Kim Campbell before the party's near wipeout defeat in the 1993 election.
Solomon, the former CTV host and Carney's friend, countered such analysis by pointing to examples of how long-ruling parties managed to stay in power after changing leaders before an election. That includes Christy Clark in British Columbia, and Kathleen Wynne in Ontario. "The idea that a change of leader can revivify a brand, there's lots of precedent on that," he said.
Of course, unless Trudeau changes his mind about running again, the leadership won't be available until after the next general election, at the earliest. But Carney is "not deaf" to the speculation about his ambitions, Solomon said. Like many people in politics, Carney has the personality of someone who wants a key responsibility when they jump in the game, he said.
If the captain's job comes up for grabs, why wouldn't he want to wear the "C"?
CAPTION: Mark Carney during the the Third "1+6" Roundtable Dialogue held by Chinese Premier Li Keqiang at Diaoyutai State Guesthouse on Nov. 6, 2018 in Beijing, China.Then-Governor of the Bank of England Mark Carney poses for photographs with a hockey stick as he represents the Canadian All-Stars team before the start of an exhibition hockey game against the TD Maple Leafs during a "Canada 150" celebration in Trafalgar Square, London, on July 1, 2017.

Subject: Conservatism; Political leadership; State elections; Central banks; Governors; Voters; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Central banks

People: Carney, Mark; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin; Ignatieff, Michael

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

June 1: A poor use of taxpayers' money, find green space for students and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-1-poor-use-taxpayers-money-find-green-space/docview/3062889332/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+1%3A+A+poor+use+of+taxpayers%27+money%2C+find+green+space+for+students+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A poor use of taxpayers' money
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol expansion (May 24)
Once again, our premier has played the booze card. Remember buck-a-beer? Well, he just spent a cool $225 million to buy out Ontario's contract with the Beer Store, paving the way for beer and wine in corner stores. Could there be a quick election in the wind?
The Globe and Mail notes, "$225 million is roughly $100 million more than the Ontario government will spend to boost nursing enrolment over the next three years. It represents nearly a third of what Ontario spends annually on autism services ($720-million)." Meanwhile, others claim the actual cost is at least $1 billion.
Let's be conservative and use $600 million as a realistic cost of this transaction. Now consider the impact $600 million could have on Ontario's 16,000 homeless people. The mathematical answer is $37,500 per person, and it represents thousands of unbuilt tiny homes and a lost opportunity for a significant increase to ODSP support. It's all so tawdry. And scandalous. And shameful. It's not the premier's money.
James Newman, Hamilton
Invest in affordable housing now
Re: Bill 185 will not provide more affordable housing (May 28)
This commentary was right on point and one of many published articles on the topic, in addition to the many letters to the editor echoing this stance. Doug Ford is ruining our beautiful province, piece by piece, with his greed (and making friends more wealthy). We need truly affordable homes and only these right now so that many young people are able to afford a small home or townhouse. We need intensification to provide homes to homeless people in the cities and those who choose to live in the cities. This may sound simple because it is. Why not put the $225 million headed for more alcohol convenience (like we need that!) into this and our health system, which he has also ruined.
Melissa J. Langevin, Stoney Creek
Disparities in the city are growing
Re: 'Difficulties' accessing Tim Hortons Field persist, school board says (May 30)
It's outrageous that the city and the school board can't give the kids at Bernie Custis more access to green space and playing time at the stadium. They messed up from Day 1 by putting too many buildings on too small a space, and making a completely unrealistic and unenforceable deal with a private entity - the Tiger-Cats.
There has been no "best effort" at any stage.
So now what? Level the final two mounds to create more playing space. Fence the entire area to keep balls in. Build a small set of bleachers at one end. Turn the far northwest corner into a decent, fenced, green outdoor space. And do it this summer.
Don't tell us it needs to go to committee, can't be done, is too complicated or whatever. Nonsense. Westdale had millions spent on its field only a few years ago. If money is needed, sell off one of the vast fields that surround Ancaster High. The disparities in our city are growing. It's beyond belief that nothing is done.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Find green space for students
When Scott Park was demolished and Bernie Custis built, there were promises that the students would have access to green space. Meanwhile, the former SJAM and Delta school properties still sit vacant, both of which have perfectly good green space. Yet another example of the skewed priorities of the city and the school board. Let's find the students some green space.
Aha Blume, Hamilton
An exciting visit for all
I can't help but compare the visit by hundreds of area kids at the Canadian Open on Wednesday to the enthusiastic young hockey fans at some school day Bulldogs' games in the past. They cheered loudly when a golfer waved at them or made a good shot. It was funny watching a young professional being mobbed by the elementary students looking for an autograph. I am sure it made his day. For the most part, they were well behaved and seemed interested in the staging of a professional golf tournament. Kudos to the teachers, their parent volunteers and the pros who made this a special day for the students.
Don Finlayson, Hamilton
P3 is actually P6
Re: Who really benefits from public-private partnerships? (May 30)
Paul Kahnert sums up the failures of public-private partnerships, called P3s, very well. If we more accurately labelled them P6s, "Public Partners Pay, Private Partners Profit," perhaps governments and voters would see past the catchy label to the boondoggles.
Jim Young, Burlington
Peace wanted, but not at any cost
Re: It's past time Israel listened to the world (May 29)
Your editorial states, "Justice, peace and security ... can be achieved only through a negotiated solution in which all can be free and secure side by side in the region. Tragically, neither Benjamin Netanyahu's government nor Hamas seems much interested in this outcome."
In fact, the Netanyahu government is intensely interested in a negotiated solution, but not at any cost, and despite being under intense domestic pressure to bring home the hostages. Hamas is hoping that international pressure will force Israel to lay down its arms so that the barbarity of Oct. 7 will reoccur over and over again. That is a price too high for any country.
Stuart Laughton, Oakville
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford recently announced that beer and wine sales will soon be allowed in Ontario's corner stores. It will cost at least $225 million to end a deal with the Beer Store earlier than planned. Some readers say that money would be better spent elsewhere.

Subject: Public private partnerships; Students; Beer; Professional golf; Letters to the editor; Affordable housing; Homeless people; School boards

Document 39 of 500

Doug Ford's thirst for power will have the rest of us crying in our beer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-thirst-power-will-have-rest-us-crying/docview/3062889141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+thirst+for+power+will+have+the+rest+of+us+crying+in+our+beer&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with Doug Ford's latest boondoggle over beer is that it amounts to double jeopardy.
First, taxpayers and teetotallers alike will be soaked by as much as $1 billion right off the top, according to opposition critics and industry analysts.
Second, drinkers are destined to be hosed by higher prices as Ontario deregulates - and disaggregates - the distribution of beer in this province.
Which means the premier is giving the brewing giants taxpayers' money so they can then sell less beer to corner stores at a higher price point. Increased costs for brewers, means more expensive beer for consumers.
How much more is hard to know. To understand the uncertainty, you first have to comprehend Ford's core agenda - and how he could have avoided taking on so much risk.
His objective isn't a better form of beer distribution. It's disruption for disruption's sake.
The premier opposes whatever his predecessors do and obsesses about what a populist should supposedly do to please the people.
A decade ago, I wrote a series of columns investigating the iron grip of The Beer Store on beer distribution, together with the government-owned LCBO. In fact, The Beer Store has always been controlled by Ontario's biggest brewers, who gave priority of placement to their own products at the expense of smaller craft brewers.
In 2017, the last Liberal government came up with an intelligent compromise solution - phasing out The Beer Store's stranglehold over the next decade while allowing supermarkets to get a piece of the action if they paid a licensing fee for the privilege. It turned out to be a practical, transitional plan that would take us to the end of 2026.
But in 2018, Ford won power by promising "buck-a-beer" and "beer in corner stores."
The first slogan was based on an unsustainable and uneconomical model that promptly went flat. The second promise was equally unaffordable, because it required ripping up a binding contract with The Beer Store - and related contracts with big box stores and supermarkets - merely to appease the convenience store sector.
Over time, Ford came to realize the infeasibility of that implausible promise, which would have triggered costly litigation amounting to as much as $1 billion in legal penalties and fees. He waited, and waited, until he could wait no longer.
Last week, Ford announced that the 10-year deal with the big brewers would be wound down ahead of schedule. But to get his way, we all have to pay.
There's $225 million in transition compensation to The Beer Store, plus another $375 million to rebate service fees that it pays the LCBO. The opposition Liberals argued this week that a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains could total more than $150 million in lost revenues.
If you add in the forgone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores (typically charged by other jurisdictions), that's at least $300 million more money left on the table. Which adds up to a "billion-dollar beer boondoggle," according to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Ford insists the Liberal calculation is unfounded (the government added later that it will in fact get $9 million in licensing fees), and counters that the existing beer framework is costing the economy untold millions in unrealized savings, notably through increased competition - though he provided no data to back his claim. For what it's worth, industry analysts told the Star's Robert Benzie this week that the Liberal analysis appears to be well grounded.
And as the Star's Josh Rubin reported, there is now reason to believe that supermarkets may be rethinking their dedication to the distribution of beer. I've long argued for giving priority to supermarkets over so-called corner stores (no longer on every corner) that tend to charge inconveniently high prices as a premium for proximity.
Now, we may lose supermarket distribution while gaining more chain convenience stores selling a smaller selection of beer at higher prices. Which means higher costs for taxpayers and drinkers both.
We can't be certain how this will play out over the long term, but there is one absolute certainty over the short term: Ford could have hedged all that risk, avoided all that cost, eliminated all that exposure, by merely waiting out the original contract with the Beer Store until it lapsed in late 2026.
Net cost for waiting? Zero.
Why not wait? The premier has persuaded himself that a promise is a promise, that beer in corner stores is popular in the polls (as he boasted to reporters), and that delivering on distribution somehow clears the decks for an early election, possibly next spring, while he is riding high in public opinion surveys.
A premier who doesn't drink seems so hungry for power that he will spare no expense to make beer more expensive at the cash register and more costly for the treasury. Unfortunately this isn't about affordability, it's about electability.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces that beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Prices; Licenses; Beer; Convenience stores; Cost control; Public opinion surveys; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Cost control Public opinion surveys Supermarkets

Document 40 of 500

No need to rush booze to corner stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-need-rush-booze-corner-stores/docview/3062845339/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+need+to+rush+booze+to+corner+stores&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford appears seized by a sudden and injudicious haste to get beer, wine and cocktails into convenience stores ahead of schedule.
For a leader with a well-documented track record of rash acts and sheepish reversals, this new fervour can hardly help but raise alarms about potential costs and misplaced priorities.
Late last year, Ford announced plans to allow convenience stores to sell alcohol as of Jan. 1, 2026. His Progressive Conservatives had promised such a move in the election campaigns of 2018 and 2022, but were constrained by a master agreement signed with the Beer Store by the former Liberal government that ran until the end of 2025.
The impatient premier initially muttered about reneging on that agreement in order to get alcohol more widely available more quickly.
Until, that is, he was persuaded by cooler minds of the considerable legal costs and lamentable reputation for bad-faith dealing that such a move would cause.
Then last week came word that the premier's craving to get on with things had got the better of him.
The Ford government announced that it will pay the Beer Store - which is controlled by foreign-owned brewing giants - $225 million to allow "early implementation" of the expanded alcohol sales and make beer, wine and premixed cocktails available at more types of retail outlets by Oct. 31 this year.
The retailing of alcohol is no one's idea of the most pressing challenge facing Ontario. But from his earliest "buck-a-beer" days as Tory leader, Ford has regularly turned to it to burnish his populist image or distract from misjudgments and flip-flops.
Once again, there is a political subtext to his latest enthusiasm for booze sales.
The move would give Ford flexibility to call a provincial election sooner than the scheduled June 2026 vote by allowing him to claim he had delivered on another popular campaign pledge.
The motivation for an early election call is the belief in Ford circles that he would fare better running for a third term before the possible installation of a Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre in Ottawa.
All Ford would say about such a scenario was a portentous "stay tuned."
The premier insists an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets stocking alcohol will boost sales and bring in more in taxes to provincial revenues.
"We're going to see revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion," he told the legislature. "That's hundreds of millions of dollars that we can put towards health care."
But as could reasonably be said before many of the premier's giddy assertions, critics were soon yelling not so fast.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has said the complexities of the deal with brewing giants mean Ford's rush to reform could cost Ontarians hundreds of millions of dollars more, ending up closer to $1 billion than the province's estimated $225 million.
Her Liberals have asked Ontario's auditor general and the legislature's Financial Accountability Office to investigate the true cost of the deal, which seems reasonable.
Opposition Leader Marit Stiles asked Ford this week why he was "pouring money into the pockets of these big alcohol corporations while our emergency rooms are closing?"
Stiles makes a fair point. There is little justification for Ford's urgency.
Ontario was promised the sale of beer and wine in corner stores as long ago as 1985. Ontarians will benefit from the convenience and competition. But while we welcome the end of the Beer Store monopoly and applaud the direction, a change like this, with the risks and costs it entails, should be done with care rather than politically motivated haste.
It makes more sense to stick to the province's previous timeline and to spend the (at least) $225 million on truly urgent issues such as the province's crisis of primary health care or housing.
We have waited nearly 40 years for beer to reach convenience stores.
Given the price tag, we can wait one more.
CAPTION:

Document 41 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3062844839/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A poor use of taxpayers' money
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol expansion (May 24)
Once again, our premier has played the booze card. Remember buck-a-beer? Well, he just spent a cool $225 million to buy out Ontario's contract with the Beer Store, paving the way for beer and wine in corner stores. Could there be a quick election in the wind?
The Globe and Mail notes, "$225 million is roughly $100 million more than the Ontario government will spend to boost nursing enrolment over the next three years. It represents nearly a third of what Ontario spends annually on autism services ($720 million)." Meanwhile, others claim the actual cost is at least $1 billion.
Let's be conservative and use $600 million as a realistic cost of this transaction. Now consider the impact $600 million could have on Ontario's 16,000 homeless people. The mathematical answer is $37,500 per person, and it represents thousands of unbuilt tiny homes and a lost opportunity for a significant increase to ODSP support. It's all so tawdry. And scandalous. And shameful. It's not the premier's money.
James Newman, Hamilton
Invest in affordable housing
Re: Bill 185 will not provide more affordable housing (May 28)
This commentary was right on point and one of many published articles on the topic, in addition to the many letters to the editor echoing this stance. Doug Ford is ruining our beautiful province, piece by piece, with his greed (and making friends more wealthy). We need truly affordable homes and only these right now so that many young people are able to afford a small home or townhouse. We need intensification to provide homes to homeless people in the cities and those who choose to live in the cities.
This may sound simple because it is. Why not put the $225 million headed for more alcohol convenience (like we need that!) into this and our health system, which he has also ruined.
Melissa J. Langevin, Stoney Creek
Disparities are growing
Re: 'Difficulties' accessing Tim Hortons Field persist, school board says (May 30)
It's outrageous that the city and the school board can't give the kids at Bernie Custis more access to green space and playing time at the stadium. They messed up from Day 1 by putting too many buildings on too small a space, and making a completely unrealistic and unenforceable deal with a private entity - the Tiger-Cats.
There has been no "best effort" at any stage.
So now what? Level the final two mounds to create more playing space. Fence the entire area to keep balls in. Build a small set of bleachers at one end. Turn the far northwest corner into a decent, fenced, green outdoor space. And do it this summer.
Don't tell us it needs to go to committee, can't be done, is too complicated or whatever. Nonsense. Westdale had millions spent on its field only a few years ago. If money is needed, sell off one of the vast fields that surround Ancaster High. The disparities in our city are growing. It's beyond belief that nothing is done.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Find green space for students
When Scott Park was demolished and Bernie Custis built, there were promises that the students would have access to green space. Meanwhile, the former SJAM and Delta school properties still sit vacant, both of which have perfectly good green space.
Yet another example of the skewed priorities of the city and the school board. Let's find the students some green space.
Aha Blume, Hamilton
An exciting visit for all
I can't help but compare the visit by hundreds of area kids at the Canadian Open on Wednesday to the enthusiastic young hockey fans at some school day Bulldogs' games in the past. They cheered loudly when a golfer waved at them or made a good shot.
It was funny watching a young professional being mobbed by the elementary students looking for an autograph. I am sure it made his day. For the most part, they were well behaved and seemed interested in the staging of a professional golf tournament.
Kudos to the teachers, their parent volunteers and the pros who made this a special day for the students.
Don Finlayson, Hamilton
P3 is actually P6
Re: Who really benefits from public-private
partnerships? (May 30)
Paul Kahnert sums up the failures of public-private partnerships, called P3s, very well. If we more accurately labelled them P6s, "Public Partners Pay, Private Partners Profit," perhaps governments and voters would see past the catchy label to the boondoggles.
Jim Young, Burlington

Document 42 of 500

How my photos of a pick-up football game made a mark on history: It's said that a photo is worth a thousand words. Sometimes, it's worth your career

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-my-photos-pick-up-football-game-made-mark-on/docview/3062844621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+my+photos+of+a+pick-up+football+game+made+a+mark+on+history%3A+It%27s+said+that+a+photo+is+worth+a+thousand+words.+Sometimes%2C+it%27s+worth+your+career&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Hey, Doug - did you take that shot of Stanfield on the front of the Globe and Mail this morning?"
We were boarding the Progressive Conservative campaign bus outside the Hotel Vancouver early on May 31 during the 1974 federal election.
I was a photographer working for The Canadian Press out of Montreal, where I had been stationed the previous year.
Early the day before, we had flown out of Halifax heading for a rally in Saskatoon and then overnight in Vancouver.
We had a refuelling stop in North Bay and were allowed to deplane and stretch our legs on the tarmac.
Someone with the PC Party brought out a football and started throwing it around.
I asked Robert Stanfield, the then-Opposition leader, if he was going to join in - and he suggested he might.
I put my Nikon F2 camera aside and joined the game.
A few minutes in, I saw Stanfield take off his suit coat and prepare to enter the fray.
I quickly grabbed my camera and fired off the full roll of 36 frames of him catching the football, throwing it around - and missing it, too.
When I finished the roll, I ran into the terminal and sent the film back to my office in Toronto, where it would be developed, made into prints and transmitted to news clients.
After the evening rally in Saskatoon, I made a quick call to see how the photos had turned out.
All they said was: "It's on the front of the Globe and Mail!"
After waking up in Vancouver, I went to the door of my hotel room and saw two pictures on the front of the Vancouver Province newspaper - one of Stanfield dropping the football, another of him catching it.
Then I saw the Globe and Mail headline: "A Political Fumble?"
On the bus, while I waited to catch hell from Stanfield's political aides, Charles Lynch of Southam News sat down in front of me and asked the question - if it had been my photo. I said yes.
"Trudeau just won the election," he said.
Then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau had won a large majority in 1968, but Stanfield had given him a run for his money in 1972, cutting the Liberals down to minority control in Parliament.
Lynch wasn't wrong. Stanfield lost, and Trudeau formed another majority government.
Shortly after the election, Stanfield announced his intention to retire. He would go on to serve as the Opposition leader until 1976, when Joe Clark took the party helm.
When Stanfield spoke at the press gallery dinner in 1975, I wasn't in the room - but a friend told me that he gave the funniest speech, in which he called me out by name.
That year, I won the National Newspaper Award for best political photo.
More than a decade later, I got ahold of the ex-politician's phone number.
I dialed and expected a secretary to answer. But Stanfield picked up the phone.
I got nervous. I blurted out my name. I asked the question I'd been wondering about for years: would he sign a copy of the football photo?
It was the most pregnant pause I had ever heard.
Then: "Ah yes," he said. "You won an award for that, and I got nothing. Unbelievable!"
We met for lunch a few weeks later, and he signed a copy: "Doug - I should have taken off my tie."
After the meal, we headed out into a grey, drizzling day and took a few pictures together. Stanfield asked the photographer if we could take one more, on the count of three.
On three, Stanfield put the hook of his umbrella around my neck and gave it a playful tug, and we laughed.
Doug Ball is a retired
Canadian Press
photographer.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative leader Robert Stanfield grimaces as he drops a football May 30, 1974, while campaigning in North Bay, Ont.Robert Stanfield hooks Canadian Press photographer Doug Ball with an umbrella in an undated photo.DOUG BALL The Canadian Press file photoArt Lyon The Canadian Press file photo
