Document 43 of 500

Robert Pickton, one of Canada's most notorious serial killers, has died

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/robert-pickton-one-canadas-most-notorious-serial/docview/3062923833/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Robert+Pickton%2C+one+of+Canada%27s+most+notorious+serial+killers%2C+has+died&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of Canada's most notorious serial killers, whose arrest made international headlines and brought the issue of missing and murdered Indigenous women in this country into the collective consciousness, has died.
After 11 days on life-support and being placed in a medically induced coma by doctors at a Quebec hospital, Robert Pickton died on Friday, according to a release from Correctional Services Canada.
The 74-year-old suffered fatal wounds during an assault by another prisoner at Port-Cartier Institution in Quebec on May 19. He was reportedly speared by another prisoner in an unprovoked attack with a broom, although authorities have not confirmed details of the assault.
His alleged attacker, a 51-year-old prisoner who has not been publicly identified, remains in custody. As of Friday, he had not been charged.
"It probably won't happen for several weeks because there's no hurry to do that because he's already in jail," said Sgt. Hugues Beaulieu, a spokesperson for the Sûreté du Québec.
"We will finish our investigation, and then we will give it to the Crown prosecutor and then they will charge him."
Beaulieu said the inmate was in isolation after the attack, but confirmed he is now out.
Authorities had also been waiting to see if Pickton would recover from the wounds he sustained. Prison staff were not involved in the assault and Federal Minister of Public Safety Dominic LeBlanc said following the attack that Correctional Services Canada would be conducting its own investigation. Inquiries are usually held when an inmate dies in custody, but the findings are often not made public.
The release said that Pickton's next of kind has been notified, and that the investigation into how he died would continue.
"The safety and security of our institutions remains paramount for us. We are launching a Board of Investigation (BOI) into the assault that took place. The investigation will examine all of the facts and circumstances surrounding the assault, including whether policies and protocols were followed. It will also identify any recommendations and corrective measures, as needed," said the release.
It also noted the effect of the news, and what it might have on this victim's families.
"We are mindful that this offender's case has had a devastating impact on communities in British Columbia and across the country, including Indigenous peoples, victims and their families. Our thoughts are with them."
Robert William "Willie" Pickton was born Oct. 24, 1949, to a family of pig farmers in Port Coquitlam, B.C., just east of Vancouver. He, along with his brother, David, inherited the family business, and Robert eventually used the family property to dispose of his victims.
He is considered to be one of the the most prolific serial killers in Canadian history, and the farm became one of its largest crime scenes. Heavy equipment was brought in to search 380,000 cubic yards of land looking for human remains, with over 200,000 DNA samples collected. The investigation was estimated to have cost nearly $70 million by the end of 2003.
Pickton was initially arrested in 2002, after a worker informed police of illegal guns on the property. The subsequent search found weapons and evidence which led to a second warrant as part of B.C.'s missing women investigation.
Pickton was convicted of six counts of second-degree murder and sentenced to life in prison in 2007, with the maximum parole ineligibility period of 25 years, after being charged with the murders of 26 women.
The remains or DNA of 33 women, many who were taken from Vancouver's Downtown Eastside, were found on Pickton's pig farm in Port Coquitlam, B.C. He once bragged to an undercover police officer that he killed a total of 49.
Pickton's confirmed victims were Sereena Abotsway, Mona Wilson, Andrea Joesbury, Brenda Ann Wolfe, Georgina Papin and Marnie Frey.
Some family members of victims said they were pleased to hear news of the attack on Pickton.
"I don't think anybody that evil should be walking on earth, as far as I'm concerned," Cynthia Cardinal, 63, said on Tuesday to The Canadian Press. "I have happy tears. Very happy tears."
Pickton murdered her sister, Georgina Papin.
The families of Pickton's victims have pointed to botched police work during the investigation, and say that it was possible to have caught him earlier. In 1997, Pickton was arrested and charged with attempted murder, assault with a weapon and forcible confinement, but the charges were dropped, as the authorities didn't believe the victim was a reliable witness because she was a sex worker who used drugs. Vancouver police also maintained for years that there was not a serial killer preying on women in the city's Downtown Eastside.
Pickton's case spawned both provincial and federal government inquiries detailing failures by police. It also highlighted systemic inadequacies in dealing with cases of missing and murdered women. There are also several ongoing lawsuits against Robert and David Pickton.
"In my own view, the Pickton case represents the first glimmer of an awareness of the plight of murdered and missing women, and especially Indigenous women, sex workers and drug users, and how society's regulatory apparatus and policing apparatus can work together to marginalize those women and create conditions for exploitation by predatory members of public," said Jason Gratl, a Vancouver-based lawyer who represents 16 family members of victims in ongoing civil litigation against the Picktons.
Gratl said that Pickton's death changes nothing in his civil case which is expected to go to trial in the next few years. Currently, he and many other advocates are embroiled in a dispute with the RCMP, which is looking to destroy over 14,000 pieces of evidence that they gathered and have been stored at a warehouse. Gratl said there is a theory that Pickton may have had accomplices and want that evidence available to possibly hold others accountable. The next date for a hearing on that matter takes places on June 26th.
Angela Marie MacDougall, executive director of Vancouver-based Battered Women's Support Services, reflected on the weekend assault that left Pickton with what police called life-threatening injuries, telling The Canadian Press, "There's something to be said about jailhouse justice."
While that is the overwhelming sentiment of how many feel about Pickton dying from his assault injuries, it's also a warning about the rising numbers of inmate-on-inmate violence, something that a prison law advocate feels is being spurred along by poisonous rhetoric from politicians about prisoners.
"It's the legal obligation of Correctional Services Canada to keep all prisoners safe, and there must have been a serious failure on their part here," said Tom Engal, president of the Canadian Prison Law Association. "I am also concerned with the political climate that has been created by certain politicians, like (Pierre) Poilievre, Doug Ford and (BC) MP (Frank) Caputo. They are dehumanizing people in jail, saying things like, they should stay in jail and rot and only come out in a box," said Engal. "You've got politicians who are dehumanizing people like Pickton and (Paul) Bernardo, and openly advocating, hoping that they should die in jail and even that they should suffer violence. So, they're creating this atmosphere. Does that contribute to the attack on Pickton? Who knows? But it sure doesn't help."
With files from The Canadian Press

Document 44 of 500

Doug Ford unrepentant after furor over comments suggesting immigrants were behind school shooting: 'I stick with what I said'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-unrepentant-after-furor-over-comments/docview/3062923704/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+unrepentant+after+furor+over+comments+suggesting+immigrants+were+behind+school+shooting%3A+%27I+stick+with+what+I+said%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unrepentant Premier Doug Ford is doubling down despite criticism over his suggestion that immigrants were to blame for the shooting by unknown assailants at a Jewish girls' school in North York.
"You know something, I stick with what I said," he told reporters Friday at Toronto Metropolitan University's new medical school in Brampton.
"We're tired of lawlessness from ... anyone, it doesn't matter if you're here, you're new, whatever," the premier said.
"It's very, very clear. My phone is blowing up from every community saying you're bang on," said Ford, who publicly shares his cellphone number and says he answers scores of calls and texts daily.
"Ninety-nine point nine per cent of everyone gets along and that's what I believe in."
But he bristled at the notion his comment Thursday - that "you're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians" - was xenophobic.
"There's no premier ever that's (gone) on the news 10 million times (to say), 'I want more people coming to Canada.' We need the people, I love every single community equally," insisted Ford.
Critics have accused him of fanning the flames of division at a time when hate crimes reported to Toronto police have risen sharply in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war.
Police said Friday that antisemitic incidents jumped by 37 per cent last year, with anti-Muslim incidents up seven per cent.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have each called upon Ford to apologize for maligning immigrants.
Amira Elghawaby, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's special representative for combating Islamophobia, said "legitimizing irrational fears, scapegoating, or spreading unfounded conspiracies about immigrants, or any group, undermines social cohesion and safety."
"We can and should condemn antisemitism and the heinous acts against Jewish schools without fuelling further hate and division," said Elghawaby.
The controversy erupted after Ford and Trudeau had denounced the attack at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, which was shot up by two unidentified suspects around 5 a.m. last Saturday.
At a news conference just five kilometres from the school, the premier said, "Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."
"What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? Imagine a little kid in a school and because they're from the Jewish faith someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows? These guys need to be caught, they need to be punished, they need to be thrown in jail," he said Thursday.
"If you're a resident of Ontario, you get along with everyone, I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from," said Ford, emphasizing "diversity is our number one selling tool around the world."
After the initial outcry Thursday, the premier tried to clarify things later in the day.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us. While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province," he said in a statement.
Police have said it is still too early to determine if the Saturday shooting, which left no one injured but caused property damage, was hate-motivated.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has suggested immigrants were to blame for shooting at a Jewish school in Toronto last weekend despite police saying they have no information on the suspects. Ford, centre, delivers remarks while being flanked by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, and Mayor Olivia Chow, right, during a news conference in the facilities of vaccine producer Sanofi in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Ford suggests immigrants are behind T.O. school shooting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-suggests-immigrants-are-behind-t-o-school/docview/3062390427/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+suggests+immigrants+are+behind+T.O.+school+shooting&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford suggested immigrants are to blame for the shooting of an empty Jewish school in Toronto over the weekend, despite police saying they have little information on the suspects.
Opposition parties quickly denounced Ford's comments and demanded an apology.
Toronto police have said two suspects fired shots shortly before 5 a.m. last Saturday at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School and that no one was injured, though there was damage to the building.
Ford said the perpetrators must be caught and thrown in jail.
"You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing it to Ontario, and you're going after other Canadians, as the prime minister said: unacceptable," Ford said at an unrelated event in Toronto on Thursday.
"I got an idea: before you plan on moving to Canada, don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this. Simple as that. You want to come to Canada? You want to be a resident of Ontario? You get along with everyone."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford's comments were racist.
"At a time when we need political leaders of all stripes to do everything they can to bring people together and foster safety, the premier has chosen to fan the flames of xenophobia and hate," she said.
The Canadian Press

Document 46 of 500

May 30: We're finally No. 1, Ford's priorities are askew and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/may-30-were-finally-no-1-fords-priorities-are/docview/3061993452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=May+30%3A+We%27re+finally+No.+1%2C+Ford%27s+priorities+are+askew+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We're finally number 1
Re: CAA releases list of top 10 worst roads in Ontario (May 28)
Congratulations, Hamilton, for three consecutive years of having the worst road in Ontario! The city is rarely on anyone's top 10 list, but no one can beat us for poorly kept streets, unsafe expressways and confusing thoroughfares with unnecessary bike lanes (see Hatt Street in Dundas). It almost makes you giddy in anticipation of the mess the Main Street conversion and LRT construction chaos will deliver us!
David Shupe, Dundas
Progressively reduce nicotine levels
Re: Ford should put Big Tobacco out of business (May 28)
The authors make a good point. Tobacco fines are going to come from consumers who remain hooked on their products. This doesn't address the primary goal of eliminating nicotine addiction.
Some countries have instituted a progressive age limit for purchasing products. Each year the age moves forward one year until, many decades in the future, new smokers would be breaking the law by buying cigarettes.
There is a better way. Enact legislation that mandates a reduction in nicotine content, so that each year the level is reduced until tobacco products contain no nicotine at all. If manufacturers were forced to reduce nicotine content by 10 per cent each year, we could have nicotine-free cigarettes in 10 years.
This would effectively make smoking its own demise. Existing smokers will be weaned off nicotine as if they were using a nicotine patch; future smokers would be trying less addictive products and eventually non-addictive products.
Lee Fairbanks, former president, Hamilton Council on Smoking & Health
Ford's priorities are askew
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol expansion (May 24)
Premier Doug Ford has proclaimed that Ontarians will be pleased that there will be more locations available to buy alcohol. Do we really need this expansion of 8,500 new locations because we are disadvantaged when driving to our cottages and not being able to get alcohol along the way? There are far more pressing issues for Ontarians this government should be focusing on: a health-care system that is stretched to the limit; affordable housing being beyond the reach of so many; much-needed farmland and green spaces under threat of urban expansion; increasing homelessness; and the looming threats caused by climate change.
I, for one, do not have a cottage, and if I did, I would not have a problem getting my supplies, including alcohol, before heading out to it. Seems simplistic, but then there may be an election coming sooner than later.
Margaret Tremblay, Dundas
Spotlight on luxury homes offensive
Re: Ancaster home listed for $2.8M 'light-filled and lofty' (May 28)
Why does the Spectator have this preoccupation to showcase these ultra-expensive homes in the paper all the time? I find it offensive by comparison to the present reality of the housing situation here in Hamilton.
Why not showcase more of hovels, tents, simply constructed dwellings and squalid structures that our poor and marginalized have to resort to? Bring attention to the pandemic of poverty we seem to, here in Hamilton, not to be able to even begin to resolve.
Ross Prince, Hamilton
Grocer investigation is welcome
Re: Competition bureau probes Loblaws, Sobeys owners (May 24)
The competition bureau's investigation into anti-competitive conduct among grocers is a worthwhile undertaking. If indeed the big grocery chains utilize restrictive non-compete clauses in lease and sales agreements in order to stifle competition from other grocers, then we have a problem. Loblaws and Sobeys, of course, will argue in solemn tones that their behaviour in this matter, far from being anti-competitive, actually works to the benefit of the consumer.
I am reminded of Adam Smith's observation in "The Wealth of Nations" (1776) that "People of the same trade seldom meet together, even for merriment and diversion, but the conversation ends in a conspiracy against the public, or in some contrivance to raise prices." Some things never seem to change.
Jim Young, Dundas
CAPTION: Aberdeen Avenue in Hamilton is Ontario's worst road for 2024, the CAA said in a news release on Tuesday.

Document 47 of 500

Who really benefits from public-private partnerships?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-really-benefits-public-private-partnerships/docview/3061993133/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+really+benefits+from+public-private+partnerships%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Governments at every level have a big revenue problem.
This is largely a problem of their own making. For decades, Conservative and Liberal governments, under the cover of tax cuts for the general population, made massive tax cuts to the wealthy and their corporations. Now to solve their revenue problem to build critically needed infrastructure, they are turning to this very same corporate class, selling the myth that somehow free money is to be had from public-private partnerships known as P3s.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau introduced his P3 infrastructure bank in 2017 and is now going full steam ahead with a P3 high frequency rail (HFR) project between Quebec City and Toronto. Trying to solve revenue problems in Ontario, on Nov. 2 last year, Premier Doug Ford announced the creation of an infrastructure P3 bank here.
In whose interests are the proponents of public-private partnerships operating? Claims of "increased efficiencies," "innovation" and now "dynamism" are completely false. In the past, proponents have claimed they were off-loading risk onto the private partners. All completely bogus.
Make no mistake, P3s are privatization. There are three kinds of privatization. First is the outright sale of a public asset or service. The second is leasing public assets to the private sector. The third and most insidious form of privatization are P3s.
The record of P3 privatizations around the world is dismal. Before the HFR project gets underway, we should look at what happened when rail was privatized in the U.K. It is a national joke. In fact, the privatization of electricity, water, rail and hospital construction has been a complete failure there. New Zealand and Australia also had expensive experiences with P3 privatizations in school and hospital construction.
The most famous privatization gone bad was Cochabamba, Bolivia, when water was privatized. Martial law was declared there after two popular uprisings, then water was put back under public control.
In Europe, most water and electricity facilities leased or sold to the private sector were brought back under public control with the de-privatization trend in 2010.
One doesn't have to look that far for failed examples of privatization. The privatization of Highway 407 and hydro has left the people of Ontario with no appetite for anymore privatization of public assets.
The privatization of Connaught Labs by Brian Mulroney hurt us badly during the pandemic. The privatization of the Bruce nuclear plant in a long-term lease was one of the worst. The profits were privatized, but the debt risks and pollution remained public.
Governments did not privatize public assets during the First or Second World Wars, nor did they during the Great Depression. They wisely kept those public assets for the future. We worked our way out of those terrible times by co-operating in a robust public sector and non-profit public enterprise, and that is what we must do now.
We have a very good example of how to do that. In 1905, forward-looking Conservatives brought in public power in the province of Ontario with the motto "power at cost for the people." Rates dropped by half. The rest of the country quickly copied that model, making Canada's economy very successful.
In the end, there is no free money. P3s are a very expensive and inefficient way of financing infrastructure and services. They hide public borrowing while, at the same time, they provide long-term guarantees of profits to private companies.
It is a big mistake to bring in P3s. Close P3 infrastructure banks or we will pay and pay. Reversing tax cuts on the wealthy and their corporations is not raising taxes, it is restoring revenue to build a civil society.
CAPTION: The federal government's infrastructure bank is supporting a P3 high frequency rail project between Quebec City and Toronto, Paul Kahnert writes.
CREDIT: Paul Kahnert

Subject: Public private partnerships; Privatization; Infrastructure; Tax cuts

Business indexing term: Subject: Public private partnerships Privatization Infrastructure Tax cuts

Location: Quebec City Quebec Canada; Canada

Document 48 of 500

Doug Ford slammed for 'racist' remarks after suggesting immigrants to blame for shooting at Toronto Jewish school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-slammed-racist-remarks-after-suggesting/docview/3061993083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+slammed+for+%27racist%27+remarks+after+suggesting+immigrants+to+blame+for+shooting+at+Toronto+Jewish+school&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford was forced to do damage control after appearing to blame immigrants for the weekend shooting at a Jewish girls' school in North York.
Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had condemned the attack at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, which was shot up by two unidentified suspects around 5 a.m. Saturday.
Speaking to reporters just five kilometres from the school, the premier seemed to point the finger at newcomers to Canada - despite the fact police have not identified the shooters.
"You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians," he said, as Trudeau and Mayor Olivia Chow stood nearby, their eyebrows raised.
Under fire from many quarters, Ford later scrambled to clarify his statement.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us. While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province," he said.
Prior to Ford's comments, Trudeau said he was "disgusted by these vile and despicable acts of antisemitism."
"Just a short drive away, parents dropped off their daughters this morning at a Jewish girls' elementary school that was shot at over the weekend. And in Montreal, another Jewish school was targeted by an overnight shooting last night," he said.
"It needs to stop right now," the prime minister said at the opening of Sanofi's new $800-million vaccine manufacturing facility on Steeles Avenue West he attended with Ford and Chow.
"The Jewish parents and students across this country, we stand with you. We will always work to safeguard your right to live proudly Jewish lives in Canada," he said.
Ford expressed outrage at the increase in antisemitic hate crimes in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war that began last October.
"What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? Imagine a little kid in a school and because they're from the Jewish faith someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows? These guys need to be caught, they need to be punished, they need to be thrown in jail," said the premier.
But he sparked furor when he said "don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."
"If you're a resident of Ontario, you get along with everyone, I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from," he said.
Stressing that "diversity is our number one selling tool around the world," Ford said there is no place for racial discrimination in Ontario.
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she was "appalled by the premier's racist remarks" by suggesting new Canadians were to blame for the attack.
"Fighting hate with hate has never worked. Fighting antisemitism with xenophobia won't keep communities safe. He should apologize," Stiles said in a statement.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "dividing the people of Ontario isn't leadership - it further promotes hate and xenophobia."
"Our province deserves better from its premier. Doug Ford must apologize now," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it was "dangerous and unacceptable rhetoric from Premier Ford."
"What happened at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School was an alarming and unacceptable act of antisemitism," said Schreiner.
"But using it as an opportunity to stoke anti-immigrant sentiment only fans the flames of division and increases tension at a time when we as leaders should be focused on helping our community members come together and heal," he said.
Meanwhile, Chow noted she had visited the school on Monday and said city council has been working with the local community on safety measures.
She said that can include "shatterproof glass, fencing, lighting or trimming of bushes or cameras" to make vulnerable schools or places of worship feel more safe and secure.
Prior to the controversy over Ford's comments, federal Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks, who was also in attendance at Sanofi, praised the premier and prime minister for expressing support for the Jewish community.
"Allies matter. As a Jewish mother in this community we have the right to be safe and feel safe each and every day," said Saks.
Toronto police have said it is still too early to determine if the Saturday shooting was hate-motivated.
"It was pretty shocking," Rabbi Yaacov Vidal, the school principal, said Sunday.
"I think it's very sad that people target schools where children are innocent and just create this extra fear and chaos for no reason."
At a rally in support of the community on Monday, Daniel Held, chief program officer at the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, said the shooting "was a deliberate attempt to spread fear across our entire Jewish community, to make us cower and hide who we are, but as you can see here today, the attackers completely failed."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford attend an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, Ont. on April 25, 2024.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish schools; Community; Jewish life & ethics; Prime ministers; Elementary schools

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Doug Ford slammed for 'racist' remarks after suggesting immigrants to blame for shooting at Toronto Jewish school

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Document 49 of 500

Ford s priorities are askew Re Ontario speeds up alcohol ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-s-priorities-are-askew-re-ontario-speeds-up/docview/3061630610/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+s+priorities+are+askew+Re+Ontario+speeds+up+alcohol+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's priorities
are askew
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol
expansion (May 24)
Premier Doug Ford has proclaimed that Ontarians will be pleased that there will be more locations available to buy alcohol. Do we really need this expansion of 8,500 new locations because we are disadvantaged when driving to our cottages and not being able to get alcohol along the way?
There are far more pressing issues for Ontarians this government should be focusing on: a health-care system that is stretched to the limit; affordable housing being beyond the reach of so many; much-needed farmland and green spaces under threat of urban expansion; increasing homelessness; and the looming threats caused by climate change.
Margaret Tremblay, Dundas
Progressively drop nicotine levels
Re: Ford should put Big Tobacco out of business (May 28)
The authors make a good point. Tobacco fines are going to come from consumers who remain hooked on their products.
This doesn't address the primary goal of eliminating nicotine addiction.
Some countries have instituted a progressive age limit for purchasing products.
Each year the age moves forward one year until, many decades in the future, new smokers would be breaking the law by buying cigarettes.
There is a better way. Enact legislation that mandates a reduction in nicotine content, so that each year the level is reduced until tobacco products contain no nicotine at all.
If manufacturers were forced to reduce nicotine content by 10 per cent each year, we could have nicotine-free cigarettes in 10 years.
This would effectively make smoking its own demise.
Existing smokers will be weaned off nicotine as if they were using a nicotine patch; future smokers would be trying less addictive products and eventually non-addictive products.
Lee Fairbanks, former president, Hamilton Council on Smoking & Health
We're finally
number 1
Re: CAA releases list of top 10 worst roads in Ontario (May 28)
Congratulations, Hamilton, for three consecutive years of having the worst road in Ontario!
The city is rarely on anyone's top 10 list, but no one can beat us for poorly kept streets, unsafe expressways and confusing thoroughfares with unnecessary bike lanes (see Hatt Street in Dundas).
It almost makes you giddy in anticipation of the mess the Main Street conversion and LRT construction chaos will deliver us!
David Shupe, Dundas
Spotlight on luxury homes offensive
Re: Ancaster home listed
for $2.8M 'light-filled and lofty' (May 28)
Why does the Spectator have this preoccupation to showcase these ultra-expensive homes in the paper all the time?
I find it offensive by comparison to the present reality of the housing situation here in Hamilton.
Why not showcase more of hovels, tents, simply constructed dwellings and squalid structures that our poor and marginalized have to resort to?
Bring attention to the pandemic of poverty we seem to, here in Hamilton, not to be able to even begin to resolve.
Ross Prince, Hamilton

New Brunswick Higgs didn t see banned program Premier Blaine ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-brunswick-higgs-didn-t-see-banned-program/docview/3061629779/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Brunswick+Higgs+didn+t+see+banned+program+Premier+Blaine+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick
Higgs didn't see banned program
Premier Blaine Higgs says he saw only one slide of the sex education presentation that he banned from New Brunswick schools. In a Friday evening statement, Higgs called a presentation delivered to a dozen high schools "clearly inappropriate" and said the sex education group behind it would no longer be welcome in schools. While responding to reporters' questions this week, Higgs said he had seen the screenshot of the slide that he shared to social media but not the presentation in its entirety.
Canada
House Speaker survives vote
House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus has survived a vote put forward by the Conservatives to remove him from his position. The effort to oust Fergus failed by a vote of 142-168 after the NDP sided with the Liberals, with both accusing the Conservatives of undermining democracy and its independent institutions. This is the third time in less than eight months that the Conservatives have called on Fergus to step down.
Ontario
Arbitrator gives teachers raise
An arbitrator has awarded Ontario's elementary teachers a raise of nearly 12 per cent over the course of its four-year deal. The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario says the Arbitration Board awarded its 80,000 teachers increases of three per cent for the first two years of the agreement that begins from 2022 to 2023. That is followed by increases of 2.75 per cent and 2.5 per cent for the final two years, which comes out to an 11.73 per cent increase when compounded over the course of the deal.
News services

Document 51 of 500

Privatization by a different name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/privatization-different-name/docview/3061629771/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privatization+by+a+different+name&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Governments at every level have a big revenue problem.
This is largely a problem of their own making. For decades, Conservative and Liberal governments, under the cover of tax cuts for the general population, made massive tax cuts to the wealthy and their corporations. Now to solve their revenue problem to build critically needed infrastructure, they are turning to this very same corporate class, selling the myth that somehow free money is to be had from public-private partnerships known as P3s.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau introduced his P3 infrastructure bank in 2017 and is now going full steam ahead with a P3 high frequency rail (HFR) project between Quebec City and Toronto. Trying to solve revenue problems in Ontario, on Nov. 2 last year, Premier Doug Ford announced the creation of an infrastructure P3 bank here.
In whose interests are the proponents of public-private partnerships operating? Claims of "increased efficiencies," "innovation" and now "dynamism" are completely false. In the past, proponents have claimed they were off-loading risk onto the private partners. All completely bogus.
Make no mistake, P3s are privatization. There are three kinds of privatization. First is the outright sale of a public asset or service. The second is leasing public assets to the private sector. The third and most insidious form are P3s.
The record of P3 privatizations around the world is dismal. Before the HFR project gets underway, we should look at what happened when rail was privatized in the U.K. It is a national joke. In fact, the privatization of electricity, water, rail and hospital construction has been a complete failure there. New Zealand and Australia also had expensive experiences with P3 privatizations.
The most famous privatization gone bad was Cochabamba, Bolivia, when water was privatized. Martial law was declared there after two popular uprisings, then water was put back under public control.
In Europe, most water and electricity facilities leased or sold to the private sector were brought back under public control with the de-privatization trend in 2010.
One doesn't have to look that far for failed examples of privatization. The privatization of Highway 407 and hydro has left the people of Ontario with no appetite for anymore privatization of public assets.
The privatization of Connaught Labs by Brian Mulroney hurt us badly during the pandemic. The privatization of the Bruce nuclear plant in a long-term lease was one of the worst. The profits were privatized, but the debt risks and pollution remained public.
Governments did not privatize public assets during the world wars, nor the Depression. They wisely kept those public assets for the future. We worked our way out of those terrible times by co-operating in a robust public sector and non-profit public enterprise, and that is what we must do now.
We have a very good example of how to do that. In 1905, forward-looking Conservatives brought in public power in Ontario with the motto "power at cost for the people." Rates dropped by half. The rest of the country quickly copied that model, making Canada's economy very successful.
In the end, there is no free money. P3s are a very expensive and inefficient way of financing infrastructure and services. They hide public borrowing while, at the same time, they provide long-term guarantees of profits to private companies.
It is a big mistake to bring in P3s. Close P3 infrastructure banks or we will pay and pay. Reversing tax cuts on the wealthy and corporations is not raising taxes, it is restoring revenue to build a civil society.
Freelance writer Paul
Kahnert is a retired hydro worker. muckrack.com/paul-kahnert/articles.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Paul Kahnert Freelance writer;

Document 52 of 500

Seniors' lives have 'turned upside down': Ontario NDP calls for end of evictions in retirement homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/seniors-lives-have-turned-upside-down/docview/3061629470/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Seniors%27+lives+have+%27turned+upside+down%27%3A+Ontario+NDP+calls+for+end+of+evictions+in+retirement+homes&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario NDP is calling on Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives to amend the Residential Tenancies Act to prevent the eviction of Ontario retirement home residents.
The demand comes in the wake of the eviction of more than 200 residents of Heritage Glen Retirement Residence in Mississauga earlier this year. Owner Chartwell told the residents, many of whom are in their 80s and 90s, in March that they had until the end of July to vacate their units for "extensive" renovations.
The property is being sold to real estate giant Minto, which plans to redevelop the property into residential rental units for all ages.
"Seniors need safe, affordable places to live, but when the bulk of seniors' housing exists to make profits for investors, a real estate trust, like Chartwell, can sell their homes out from under them," said the NDP's seniors critic, MPP Lise Vaugeois, at a news conference Wednesday morning at Queen's Park, where she was joined by several relatives of Heritage Glen's evicted residents. "It is unconscionable for a senior on a fixed income to be suddenly uprooted and told to find another place to live. For many, it will be impossible to find comparable housing or any housing at all that they can afford."
Unlike seniors in long-term care, retirement home residents are treated as tenants under the province's Residential Tenancies Act, which means they can be evicted - as long as the landlord can demonstrate to the Landlord and Tenant Board that they intend to do the repair and renovation work described on the eviction notice and that such work requires the tenant to leave.
The Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing did not answer the Star's question about whether it would consider amending the Residential Tenancies Act to prevent the eviction of seniors from retirement homes.
Responding to the NDP during question period Wednesday, Minister for Seniors and Accessibility Raymond Cho said Heritage Glen has been working with the Retirement Homes Regulatory Authority to transition residents to a new home.
Chartwell says it has helped more than 170 Heritage Glen residents find alternative accommodations as part of an assistance package, which includes moving costs and compensation in the amount of three months' rent.
"The decision to close Heritage Glen was not made lightly," said Chartwell spokesperson Mary Perrone Lisi. "We have been operating at less than 60 per cent capacity for several years. That, combined with the aging infrastructure of the residence, made it unsustainable to continue operating as a retirement residence."
Such comments are cold comfort to Karen Santaguida, whose mother, Joyce Dodge, 92, who has lived at Heritage Glen for nearly 20 years and was shocked to learn on March 19 that she was being evicted.
"Our lives have been turned upside down," said Santaguida.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star

Subject: Retirement homes; Evictions

Document 53 of 500

We know politics can be done better

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-know-politics-can-be-done-better/docview/3061626278/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+know+politics+can+be+done+better&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I believe it is safe to say most Canadians are appalled and revolted at former American president Donald Trump's behaviour.
He appears entirely bereft of any form of human decency. He is mendacious, narcissistic, crude and due to him American politics have sunk to levels of coarseness and lunacy never seen before. Canadians are thankful that our politics have avoided sinking to those levels.
But we cannot become smug or "holier than thou" as that may be changing here. Recently we have seen Pierre Poilievre, leader of the Conservative Party of Canada (CPC), refer to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as a wacko, a Marxist and a racist among other insults. Instead of such vitriol, why doesn't Poilievre attack Trudeau's economic record and demand Trudeau release a plan as to how the budget will be balanced?
Trudeau is also certainly no angel. Who can forget him calling then-Conservative Environment Minister Peter Kent, a piece of s--- in December 2011 in the House of Commons? Really Mr. Trudeau? You'd expect a comment like that in a schoolyard, not from a high-profile political figure right in Parliament.
Trudeau and the Liberals have accused Poilievre of being connected to white supremacists, of admiring Trump and the MAGA movement and plotting to take away women's reproductive rights. Instead of making such outlandish accusations, how about pointing out that Poilievre and his party seem to have no policies or plan to govern?
On May 21, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland accused Poilievre of wearing more makeup than she did. Freeland is possibly Trudeau's most able and accomplished minister and considered to be a front-runner for the Liberal leadership when Trudeau steps down. This petty insult from her was quite beneath someone of her stature.
Canadians are not happy about this. Earlier this month Angus Reid released a poll showing that Canadians have never been as critical of the three major party leaders as they are today.
Is anyone surprised by these results? We must remember that political leaders represent all Canadians. And as such they must ensure their behaviour is not only above reproach at all times, but it must also always appear to be above reproach. And Trudeau and Poilievre have failed at this numerous times. New Democratic Party leader Jagmeet Singh has performed more admirably in so far as personal conduct is concerned compared to Trudeau and Poilievre. I believe his low rating in the Angus Reid poll is a result of Canadians not believing he is an effective leader.
You may ask why you should care about such a poll? You should care as these leaders make decisions that directly impact your life. Decisions as to how much taxes you will pay, how to deal with climate change, etc.
We need leaders we have confidence in and we cannot have confidence in leaders who act like unruly children. We have seen first-hand evidence of how effective a government led by someone incapable of acting like a dignified lady or gentleman is, with Trump. We all know how that turned out.
We have had wonderful examples in the past of successful highly refined leaders in Canada. Former Conservative prime minister Joe Clark was well known to be an extremely classy and dignified politician. Former Ontario premiers John Robarts and Bill Davis were renowned for their polished, respectable behaviour and for the extremely respectful manner in which they treated their opponents. We know politics can be done better as we have seen exactly that in the past.
Canadians need to reach out to their MPs and make it clear their tolerance for such juvenile and undignified behaviour is over. Let them know that politicians who behave in this manner will become unemployed after the next election. If enough of us do this, things will change.
Craig Wallace is a
Hamilton resident
and author of five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace;

Subject: Canadians; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Political parties

Title: We know politics can be done better

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.8

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 30, 2024

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 55 of 500

Doug Ford claims his $225M beer store deal will be a money-maker. Here's why critics say he's wrong - and how taxpayers could be on the hook for far more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-claims-his-225m-beer-store-deal-will-be/docview/3062239155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+claims+his+%24225M+beer+store+deal+will+be+a+money-maker.+Here%27s+why+critics+say+he%27s+wrong+-+and+how+taxpayers+could+be+on+the+hook+for+far+more&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is Premier Doug Ford's alcohol liberalization gambit a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" or a $225-million investment that will reap dividends for the treasury?
It depends who you ask.
The premier jolted Ontario's political landscape last Friday when he announced beer, wine and premixed cocktails would be more widely available at many different types of retail outlets by Oct. 31.
Ford said that's because the province is paying The Beer Store - which is controlled by foreign-owned brewing giants Labatt, Molson and Sleeman - $225 million to end their restrictive 10-year "master framework agreement" earlier than its slated Dec. 31, 2025 completion.
That move gives the premier the flexibility to hold a provincial election sooner than the scheduled June 2026 vote because he can be seen to have delivered on a key campaign pledge to voters.
While Ford is coy about a snap vote - "stay tuned" was all he would say after the Star revealed he's actively thinking about it - he insists his reforms will bring in more taxes.
"We're going to see new revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion. That's hundreds of millions of dollars that we can put towards health care," he told the legislature Tuesday, insisting an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets will translate into more sales.
But recent history has shown that while liberalization in other jurisdictions, such as Alberta, led to greater convenience for consumers, it did not necessarily mean increased government revenues.
In part, that's because beer prices spiked so high in Alberta that the government was forced to lower the tax it charged in order to offset price hikes that had drinkers fuming.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says the bar tab for Ford's changes could be three or four times higher than he claims.
"He's spending more than $1 billion to cater to wealthy insiders at the expense of our public services," Crombie said Monday.
"This billion-dollar booze boondoggle is the latest example of Doug Ford lining the pockets of the wealthy, well-connected few at the expense of the people of Ontario," she said, charging the only beneficiaries will be "Ford's friends at the major grocery chains, who are already making billions in record profits, and big-box American stores."
Crombie said the $225-million payout to The Beer Store - which the premier said is designed to "protect jobs," because scores of outlets will inevitably close since the company will no longer enjoy a monopoly on sales of 24-packs - is only the beginning.
As first reported by the Star, the rebate of service fees The Beer Store pays to the government-owned LCBO is worth about $250 million a year, so over the 18 months of the wind-down agreement, that could be $375 million.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office disputes that figure, but industry sources do not.
Additionally, there is a 10 per cent discount for the private companies that must buy their products from the LCBO, which could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
As well, the government is not charging any licensing fees for the 8,500 new retail outlets, which could mean foregone revenue of at least $300 million.
Finally, an amendment to the Ontario Deposit Return Program - the bottle recycling system run by the breweries and bankrolled by the government since 2007 - will see the subsidy paid for each recycled container jump from about 11 cents to more than 16 cents.
Industry sources say that could mean another $25 million for the brewing giants, who annually divert 445 million wine and spirits bottles from landfill. (The company also recycles 1.2 billion beer bottles and cans each year.)
In total, the tab could be as much as $1.075 billion - even though Ford and Bethlenfalvy insist taxpayers will only be on the hook for $225 million. If the master framework agreement had been allowed to expire on schedule at the end of next year, none of these costs would have been incurred by the provincial treasury.
"It's very complicated, but at the end of the day, we were losing hundreds of millions of dollars," the premier assured Newstalk 1010 host Jerry Agar on Tuesday.
"What it comes down to is making sure that we have convenience for people, treating them like adults and creating hundreds of millions of dollars of revenue."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at a media event in Toronto on May 24, 2024, where he announced that beer and wine sales will soon be allowed in Ontario's corner stores.

Subject: Recycling; Beer; Liquor; Liberalization; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Liberalization Breweries

Document 56 of 500

Decriminalization failures show half measures are not enough to address the opioid crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/decriminalization-failures-show-half-measures-are/docview/3061272479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Decriminalization+failures+show+half+measures+are+not+enough+to+address+the+opioid+crisis&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa's recent rejection of the City of Toronto's request to decriminalize possession of controlled drugs is the latest shoe to drop in the resurgence of conservative anti-drug sentiment sweeping the country, and backtracking on reforms achieved in other jurisdictions.
The announcement, citing concern for public safety, follows much the same script as that of the British Columbia government, which - after decriminalizing in 2023 - recently retreated to ban use in public places due to complaints of rampant drug use and continuing high rates of overdose.
In early April, the state of Oregon went further, recriminalizing drug possession, replacing small fines with probation and up to six months in prison. The state was forced to backtrack on decriminalization measures that were passed in 2020 due to backlash over spiking overdoses during the COVID-19 lockdown period.
Continued deaths due to drug use is all the evidence required to curb reform, despite the fact that these spikes occurred mostly during the pandemic.
Research shows that the pandemic exacerbated many of the factors underlying the drug crisis. Health disparities and a lack of resources in underserved communities, social isolation, economic burden, stress, a lack of access to treatment and barriers to care all contribute to acute health effects of drug dependence.
Regardless of these facts, drug policy reform is being blamed for these outcomes.
It's still all about the politics, of course
In Ontario, predictably, the Ford government's position on Toronto's proposal is staunch opposition. Yet there is a sense of new momentum in the same old rhetoric: because drug users continue to die and use drugs in public places, presumably this means the experts and "bleeding-heart liberals" who supported reform have been soundly proven wrong.
Most concerning, whereas conservatives and liberals may differ in their emphasis on punishment or treatment as solutions to drug problems, neither type of government seems prepared to fully implement a public health approach to substance use. That would mean addressing health disparities and deeper causes of substance use disorders, such as social inequality and social dislocation in Canadian society.
No North American jurisdiction has been able to achieve this, despite the depth of knowledge and increasingly articulate demonstration of progressive public servants. Bureaucrats and politicians speak the language used by experts and other knowledgeable observers, who in turn inform the public of the need for harm reduction and more comprehensive, meaningful social policy reform. Logically, this requires commitment to decriminalization to shift the emphasis from punishing addicts to rehabilitating them.
Increasingly, the discourse has gone further in establishing the need for more coherent public health policies addressing the root causes of addiction. To that end, important progress has been squandered by apparent bewilderment that addicts are still dying in large numbers, despite (and now because of) decriminalization measures that were never claimed to solve the problem of addiction, but rather to reduce known harms of criminalizing drugs.
Decriminalizing drugs is not intended to be a solution to drug problems. Rather, it is a critical first step that's necessary, but not sufficient, for replacing prohibition with a public health approach.
Although Canada has made this work for regulating cannabis, it still has far to go to implement a more progressive public health approach to other drugs. The dangers of drug use stem not so much from psychoactive substances themselves, but from the toxic and unsafe drug supply that is the result of criminalization. But most of these substances continue to be treated as dangerous enough to ban.
Yet there is scant consideration of the societal and legal circumstances and conditions that make drugs dangerous because they happen to be used in harmful ways by certain people. Whereas opiates may have higher risk of overdose and adverse health effects due to physical dependence all drugs, including alcohol, can be misused with risk to health and public safety.
Addiction and public health
Decriminalizing drugs can make them safer, but not always, and reduce some harms of using drugs. What cannot be resolved entirely by decriminalization are the societal conditions that make substance use attractive enough to override considerations of concern for the self or others.
Addiction thrives on hopelessness and feelings of despair, abandonment and disconnection, which are caused by homelessness, unemployment and discrimination, among other social problems. Despite the range of local addiction support services and treatment options called for in support of decriminalization proposals, so far decriminalization efforts have failed to go beyond half measures to implement a full array of integrated resources.
Further off course yet, there has been little indication of meaningful commitment to addressing deeper structural root causes of addiction in North American society. Canada's commitment to a public health approach has fared no better than in Oregon.
None of these jurisdictions have gone far enough to remedy the real causes of drug problems. The fate of Portugal's successful run as a global leader in tackling addiction, as opposed to punishing addicts, helps to clarify new lessons being learned in North America.
Adopted over 20 years ago, decriminalization measures in Portugal have been implemented more successfully because its social safety net is far more comprehensive and better integrated with the criminal justice system. In recent years, as cuts to funding decimated rehabilitation programs, rising rates of overdose soon followed, demonstrating that decriminalization is not a silver bullet; if you do that and nothing else, things will get worse.
Put otherwise, half measures are ineffective. Until we are prepared to go all in to solve the problem, people who use drugs will keep dying unnecessarily because we have not afforded them real hope.
Sadly, this is news to no one. Not just experts. The Liberal government went on the record years ago by recognizing that decriminalizing drug use is not a panacea. Rather than committing to the next step, then and now, the necessary first step has been taken off the table.
How about try giving it more time?
Another lesson learned in Portugal, that evidently has not been resonating here in North American society, is that the kind of change required takes time. Now that decriminalization has been tried (and deemed a failure) in B.C. and Oregon, the reigning logic appears to be that going back to doing nothing makes more sense than doing something.
Despite great patience shown during a century of misery inflicted on drug users in Canadian society, trying something new is scary. Cue the same old moral panic scapegoating addicts as dangerous people who those in charge prefer should not be seen.
Decriminalizing drugs can't eliminate people's struggles with addiction. To work well, it requires a considerable level of investment, similar to what has historically been devoted to policing and imprisonment of addicts. Redistributing resources to support the integration of services, housing and employment for more people is not, and has never been, prioritized over punishing drug users.
Until that shift occurs, we will keep falling back on failed policies consistent with a war-on-drugs mentality that has now prevailed for 50 years against all logic. In the meantime, be prepared for a new wave of antiquated arguments proclaiming more liberal policies a failure.
Commitment to half measures creates more problems than it solves.
CAPTION: Premier David Eby's government asked Health Canada to "urgently change" the decriminalization policy in B.C. to help stop public drug use.About 150 people gathered in downtown St. Catharines last year in recognition of International Overdose Awareness Day.People march in Vancouver in 2021 to remember those lost in the overdose crisis.
CREDIT: Andrew Hathaway

Subject: Drug overdose; Public safety; Pandemics; Society; Drug use; Health disparities; Public health; Decriminalization; Drug addiction; Substance abuse treatment

Five more Ontario school boards and two private schools file lawsuits against Snapchat, TikTok and Meta, alleging they're harming students

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/five-more-ontario-school-boards-two-private/docview/3061272474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+more+Ontario+school+boards+and+two+private+schools+file+lawsuits+against+Snapchat%2C+TikTok+and+Meta%2C+alleging+they%27re+harming+students&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Five more boards and two private religious schools in Ontario have joined multi-billion lawsuits targeting TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram, alleging the social media giants have hurt not only students' health and well-being but also their academic achievement, the Star has learned.
The most recent lawsuits, filed Tuesday, total almost $2.6 billion, on top of the $4.5 billion sought by the original four boards - Toronto public, Toronto Catholic, Peel public and Ottawa-Carleton public - that launched similar court action in March.
The new plaintiffs are the Catholic boards in Ottawa, Dufferin-Peel and York, the District School Board Niagara, Trillium Lakelands public, as well as Eitz Chaim Jewish day school in Toronto and Holy Name of Mary College School in Mississauga.
Although use of social media is an issue across his school board, Trillium Lakelands Director of Education Wes Hahn said it is complicated because he has schools in urban and also in rural areas of Muskoka, Kawartha and Haliburton, where kids are more isolated.
"We've been noticing the increased phone use, the bullying from that phone, the addictions to some of the gaming, the Instagram bullying, the Snapchat bullying - all of that certainly was happening prior, but post-COVID we started to see it regularly in our suspensions and expulsions," he said. There has also been a rise in student anxiety, which "we attribute to the use of social media and the addictive nature of being on their phones," he said.
Rabbi Mordechai Loiterman of Eitz Chaim said social media has had the same impact, but at his schools has also "interfered with the religious education" of students.
While phones are banned, "we did a survey with our own students and we asked them what's the first thing they do (when they get home) now that they are unplugged for the entire school day? ... And of course, the first thing is, 'We go online and check out YouTube videos or some other social media content to see what we missed.'
"And they get on it, and the obvious problems are - when are they doing their homework? When are they socializing? When are they exercising? And it goes from there, because the nature of the product is to keep them focused, and addicted."
The boards and two schools have filed their own separate, but similar cases in Ontario's Superior Court of Justice, alleging that the popular social media platforms "have rewired the way children think, behave, and learn" and are calling on the companies to make improvements, say their statements of claim.
Schools have had to bring in staff, resources and programming to address behavioural and learning issues, say the statements of claim.
None of the allegations has been tested in court, though the social media companies do plan to defend the claims.
A spokesperson for Snapchat said the platform "was intentionally designed to be different from traditional social media, with a focus on helping Snapchatters communicate with their close friends. Snapchat opens directly to a camera - rather than a feed of content - and has no traditional public likes or comments. While we will always have more work to do, we feel good about the role Snapchat plays in helping close friends feel connected, happy and prepared as they face the many challenges of adolescence."
A Meta spokesperson said "we want to reassure every parent that we have their interests at heart in the work we're doing to provide teens with safe, supportive experiences" on Facebook and Instagram.
The spokesperson noted it has "more than 30 tools to support teens and their families, including tools that allow parents to decide when, and for how long, their teens use Instagram, age verification technology, automatically setting accounts belonging to those under 16 to private when they join Instagram, and sending notifications encouraging teens to take regular breaks."
Meta says it has also "invested in technology that finds and removes content related to suicide, self-injury or eating disorders before anyone reports it to us. These are complex issues, but we will continue working with experts and listening to parents to develop new tools, features and policies that are effective and meet the needs of teens and their families."
As well, the default setting for both Instagram and Facebook content is the most restrictive.
The boards and two schools are represented by Neinstein LLP, and will not be out of pocket for legal costs as the firm will take a contingency fee.
About 500 school districts in the U.S. have launched similar cases, but these are believed to be the only ones in Canada.
Both Premier Doug Ford and Education Minister Stephen Lecce have chided the boards for taking the court action and wasting staff resources and time. In March, Ford said, "Let's focus on the core values of education - let's focus on math, reading and writing. That's what we need to do, put all the resources into the kids."
In new legislation to help curb kids' access to harmful online content, Lecce noted that he reached out to social media companies to work with the government to help find solutions, rather than turning to the courts.
CAPTION: The Catholic boards in Ottawa, Dufferin-Peel and York, the District School Board Niagara, Trillium Lakelands public, as well as Eitz Chaim Jewish day school in Toronto andHoly Name of Mary College School in Mississauga have joinedmulti-billion lawsuits targeting TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram.
