Document 58 of 500

May 29: Golf course in a sorry state, poor use of taxpayers' millions and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/may-29-golf-course-sorry-state-poor-use-taxpayers/docview/3061272120/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=May+29%3A+Golf+course+in+a+sorry+state%2C+poor+use+of+taxpayers%27+millions+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Golf course in sorry state
As a non-resident of Hamilton, I am a proud booster of all sports, arts and culture in the city. However it didn't take me long to recognize the city's own lack of commitment to its wonderful features.
A recent day at Chedoke golf course left me wondering if the city even cares about one of its oldest landmarks. My walk from the parking lot featured an overflowed garbage bin, three-inch weeds and at a terraced area leading to the Beddoes first tee, nothing but a disregarded bed of weeds. Sand traps have become weed traps and spotty, uneven tee boxes greeted us on many holes.
It wasn't until I read the small print on the scorecard that I realized the Chedoke golf course was established in 1924. Really? A 100th anniversary going unnoticed, unacknowledged and certainly uncelebrated. How do you ever expect to draw tourists and their money with such ill regard for your own? It's shameful.
If and when I might be fortunate enough to turn 100, I certainly hope my storied life and beauty are recognized in some small way and not overlooked. You can do so much better for your city and yes, golfers certainly deserve more for the cost of their green fees.
Perhaps a tee found on the course that day sums it up: "The older I get, the better I used to be."
Carole Bertuzzi Luciani, Oakville
Poor use of taxpayers' $225M
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol expansion; province will pay the Beer Store up to $225M (May 24)
Who would spend $225 million of hard-earned, and badly-needed taxpayer money, to break a Beer Store contract that will expire in only one-and-a-half years? Doug Ford would. The wasted money could go to health care, education, infrastructure, non-profit housing ... you name it. But my guess is that political expediency tops everything.
Soon after the new drinks appear in corner stores, I bet our premier will ignore the fixed election date law and take us to the polls. Will he be punished? I can only hope.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Why are we picking sides?
Re: Court's arrest warrants welcomed (May 27)
War is a failure of leadership. Ralph D'Angelo is right. Both leaders have enlisted people in their armies to kill both opposing combatants and any innocent civilians that get in the way. Is it too much to ask all western leaders to condemn the atrocities committed by both sides? Why do we pick sides here, since there is so much wrong in both camps? At least the International Criminal Court is trying to bring awareness of these crimes in a partial way to everyone.
Steve Penner, Hamilton
Give them a taste of freedom
Our government is concerned that China is meddling in our election process. I say let them: They might learn something about democracy. The Chinese state is so used to their citizens being docile that they do not appreciate the fact that you cannot manipulate a free people.
Dan Riga, Burlington
When is the right time?
Re: Canada's shifting position stops short of immediately recognizing Palestinian statehood (May 22)
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau claims Canada will not accept a Palestinian state at this time because "the timing is wrong." What time is Trudeau waiting for? The Palestinians have suffered occupation for more than 75 years. The majority of the world's countries are coming in line with acceptance of a Palestinian state and the number of countries are growing daily. Apparently 75 years is not enough time for Trudeau.
Roger Lambert, Hagersville
We are running out of time
Re: The massive climate failure of our federal political parties (May 25)
I was pleased to see the commentary by Grant Linney on Saturday.
The most recent target for net zero is 2050. I've read a number of reports indicating this will be impossible. Many countries are participating in fusion energy research as the only way to meet energy needs without fossil fuels. They have a plan to commission the world's first fusion energy power station in the 2040s. Already they know that date is unlikely to be met. Even when it is, it could well take another 30 years to build enough fusion stations to get to net zero.
Justin Trudeau's carbon tax rebate is little enough, but it is raising awareness. Even Doug Ford and Pierre Poilievre are helping by highlighting it, and influencing Trudeau to reinforce the message and do better at explaining the rebate. It will take time for that influence to take effect, time that we don't have. It has taken around 150 years of fossil fuel burning to cause today's climate challenge. We don't have another 150 to fix it, maybe not even 15.
But mankind has to do something. There may be other technologies that come along. Maybe there will be a miracle cure. The fatalist will say we can't fix it, what's the point of trying? Maybe we (the seniors' generation) should care about the world we are leaving to our children and grandchildren. One thing we can do is ensure we have leaders whose attitude is not "that's the problem for the next government, not us."
Roy Cheers, Dundas
Addiction care mired in politics
Re: The innocent victims of the opioid crisis: toddlers (May 24)
As an addiction doctor who looks after people with opioid use disorder (OUD), my heart shatters to read about so much untreated illness, with young families left fending for themselves.
We have effective, life-saving treatments and services to support people. Treatment helps people function - as parents, coworkers and valued members of society. The evidence of benefit is more than 60 years old. Yet our ability to provide a respectful, patient-centred model remains stymied, mired in politics. On one hand, we haverhetoric claiming supervised consumption and "safer" opioid tablets will solve the opioid crisis. The counter-rhetoric would have us believe we can arrest our way out of it by criminalizing the illness.
Opioid agonist treatment (OAT) is the only evidence-based, effective and safe option for opioid use disorder. We've known this a long time and yet continue to underfund these programs. "Rapid access addiction medicine (RAAM)" clinics across the province operate on skeletal budgets. Most community OAT clinics have been monetized with many owned by a Texas-based company. This has driven down the quality of treatment and de-incentivized patient-centred, compassionate care. The result is a valid health intervention that is stigmatized by society, by people with OUD, by the health and justice systems and even by harm-reduction advocates who should be directing patients to treatment.
Communities shatter when OUD is left untreated. Why are we neglecting these patients until a child dies? All of society is failing these families, not the untreated, unwell individuals.
Leonora Regenstreif, Hamilton
CAPTION: The "Chedoke Civic Golf Club" was established in 1924. A player says we should be doing more to mark its 100th anniversary.

Subject: Families & family life; Leadership; Addictions; Substance use disorder; Fusion; Arrest warrants; Narcotics; Anniversaries

Document 59 of 500

Ontario legislator makes history at Queen's Park with speech in Oji-Cree: 'I am speaking for ... for every Indigenous person in Ontario': Mamakwa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-legislator-makes-history-at-queens-park/docview/3060988503/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+legislator+makes+history+at+Queen%27s+Park+with+speech+in+Oji-Cree%3A+%27I+am+speaking+for+...+for+every+Indigenous+person+in+Ontario%27%3A+Mamakwa&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A First Nation legislator addressed Queen's Park in his own language Tuesday, marking the first time a language other than English and French has been allowed by officials in Ontario's legislative chamber.
In the process, New Democrat Sol Mamakwa, who spoke for 10 minutes in Anishininiimowin, or Oji-Cree, in the Ontario legislature, secured a pledge from the premier to build a long-term-care home in Sioux Lookout.
"I want to say thank you to everyone present. I'm very grateful, thankful for the opportunity to be able to speak my Anishininiimowin, in Indigenous Oji-Cree language in this legislature," Mamakwa said through an interpreter at the start of his speech.
"I am speaking for those that couldn't use our language and also for those people from Kiiwetinoong, not only those from Kiiwetinoong, but for every Indigenous person in Ontario."
Ontario's legislature had not previously allowed interpreting and transcribing a language other than English and French.
About 100 supporters gathered to watch the historic moment, including Mamakwa's mother, siblings, friends and First Nation leaders. It was a gift to his mom, Kezia Mamakwa, who turned 79 on Tuesday.
The politicians sang "Happy Birthday" to her and gave several standing ovations to Sol Mamakwa in an emotional question period.
"Today was monumental for me," Mamakwa said afterward.
He said he was nervous as he stood up to speak. In the back of his mind he briefly thought he still was not allowed to talk in his language.
"I thought I was breaking the laws and the rules of this house and I tried to remind myself that there's nothing wrong with this, 'You're allowed to speak,'" he said.
After his speech, Mamakwa took the lead questions, grilling the government in his language about elder care in the north. He demanded to know if the government would follow through on its commitment to build 76 more beds at a nursing home in Sioux Lookout.
"I'm committing today in the public: we will be building those beds. We'll be building a home in Sioux Lookout," Premier Doug Ford said.
"No one's ever done this, what you're doing today," Ford said, "and I just want to tell you how proud I am of you, how proud everyone here in the legislature is, how proud everyone is in the First Nations."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: NDP MPP Sol Mamakwa holds an eagle feather towards the government benches as he stands in the Ontario Legislature in Toronto, on Tuesday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 60 of 500

Ford's beer bet buys some time

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-beer-bet-buys-some-time/docview/3060988499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+beer+bet+buys+some+time&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has figured out the price of power. Too bad we're all paying for it.
To the premier, it's small beer - a mere $225 million in subsidies for suds. That's how much he's admitted to paying out in taxpayer funds to the big brewers as financial compensation for allowing beer into convenience stores roughly a year ahead of schedule. The political payoff? Ford wins bragging rights, with our money, for belatedly delivering on his 2018 campaign promise to make beer available just about anywhere, any time.
It also buys him the flexibility to hold an early election - not in 2026, as scheduled under Ontario's fixed election law, but a year sooner. At what price, precisely? By some counts, it is destined to be far higher than the purported low ball figure of $225 million - as much as $1 billion, according to opposition critics.
During his winning campaign, buck-a-beer was twinned with another slogan that was similarly oversold - "beer in corner stores." Ford knew perfectly well that the Beer Store's owners could sue the province for defaulting on a binding 10-year contract under which they'd first agreed to relax their grip on the market by phasing in supermarket beer sales.
That pioneering framework, negotiated by the previous Liberal government, broke the stranglehold of the big brewers. But it wasn't good enough for Ford - who always boasts of bettering what came before.
Mercifully, Ford's most influential cabinet ministers managed to talk him out of breaking the beer pact, persuading him that the province faced costly litigation and possible penalties exceeding $1 billion.
Now, all these years later, he has contented himself with an anticlimactic announcement about an antiquated issue. Belatedly, beer will become more convenient - a mere one year ahead of when it otherwise would have, and four decades after it first became a political issue.
Back in the 1980s, the Liberal leader of the day, David Peterson, first promised beer in corner stores but found it inconveniently complicated to deliver on it. Now, it's a non-issue that no one notices. They're not even called corner stores anymore. They're no longer on every corner and mostly owned by big chains, not Mom and Pop operators.
Which is why Ford's news fell flat on Friday - flatter than a stale beer washing down a nothing burger. Still, there is good reason to pay attention.
Beyond the stated subsidy of $225 million going to the Beer Store's owners, another $375 million in fees now paid by them to the LCBO will be rebated. As Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie points out, a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains - now enjoying record profits - could easily total more than $150 million in lost revenues. If you add in the foregone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores (charged by other jurisdictions), that's at least $300 million more money left on the table.
By Crombie's count, it adds up to a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" that is "lining the pockets" of the wealthy and well-connected - an echo of the campaign rhetoric Ford used against the Liberals in 2018. But beyond the opposition critiques, there's also the opportunity cost of spending so much time and money so utterly opportunistically on a faded, defanged oligopoly.
Imagine if Ford spent more time and money solving real problems rather than creating new ones - dealing with overflowing surgical wait-lists, say, or a housing and homelessness crisis.
The speculation is that Ford prefers to gear up for an early provincial campaign to get out ahead of a federal election later that same year likely to crown his Tory cousin Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as prime minister. The premier is understandably wary of how shabbily the federal Conservatives might treat Ontario as they slash spending.
An early election could also allow Ford to dodge the results of an RCMP probe into his other boondoggle (that'd be the Greenbelt imbroglio).
But it's a quid pro quo that's hard to quaff - or swallow: Ford's fantasy is that by keeping his stale promise on beer in corner stores, he is now free to break the promise of fixed election dates every four years codified in law.
Ford's billion-dollar bet may be one boondoggle too many.
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for
Torstar.
CAPTION:

Document 61 of 500

Tories try again to oust Speaker

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-try-again-oust-speaker/docview/3060987622/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+try+again+to+oust+Speaker&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Conservatives are demanding House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus be forced from his chair by next Monday and a non-confidence motion vote on the matter was scheduled to take place Tuesday night.
But the motion appears destined to fail as the Liberals and NDP accuse the Conservatives of undermining democracy and its independent institutions.
This is the third time in less than eight months that the Conservatives have called on him to step down.
They insist he is too partisan after several incidents including his decision last month to expel their leader Pierre Poilievre from the House. That came after Poilievre called the prime minister a "wacko" during
The Conservatives also asked Fergus to resign after a video tribute to the Ontario Liberals' departing interim leader in which Fergus wore his Speaker's robes was shown to a provincial party convention in December.
The most recent move comes a week after the Liberal party uploaded a video to its website that promoted a summer barbecue event featuring Fergus that used partisan language without getting Fergus's consent.
Manitoba Conservative MP James Bezan said Tuesday in the House that the Speaker has lost the good will and trust of the House.
"In baseball three strikes you're out, and we've got the Speaker on three different occasions, or actually this is the fourth occasion, that he has done partisan activities, and given partisan speeches," Bezan said.
The Tories urged the NDP to vote with them to oust Fergus but NDP House leader Peter Julian said that will not happen.

Document 62 of 500

Decriminalization failures show half measures are not enough: The reigning logic appears to be that going back to doing nothing makes more sense than doing something

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/decriminalization-failures-show-half-measures-are/docview/3060987425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Decriminalization+failures+show+half+measures+are+not+enough%3A+The+reigning+logic+appears+to+be+that+going+back+to+doing+nothing+makes+more+sense+than+doing+something&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa's recent rejection of the City of Toronto's request to decriminalize possession of controlled drugs is the latest shoe to drop in the resurgence of conservative anti-drug sentiment sweeping the country, and backtracking on reforms achieved in other jurisdictions.
The announcement, citing concern for public safety, follows much the same script as that of the British Columbia government, which - after decriminalizing in 2023 - recently retreated to ban use in public places due to complaints of rampant drug use and continuing high rates of overdose.
In early April, the state of Oregon went further, recriminalizing drug possession, replacing small fines with probation and up to six months in prison. The state was forced to backtrack on decriminalization measures that were passed in 2020 due to backlash over spiking overdoses during the COVID-19 lockdown period.
Continued deaths due to drug use is all the evidence required to curb reform, despite the fact that these spikes occurred mostly during the pandemic.
Research shows that the pandemic exacerbated many of the factors underlying the drug crisis. Health disparities and a lack of resources in underserved communities, social isolation, economic burden, stress, a lack of access to treatment and barriers to care all contribute to acute health effects of drug dependence.
Regardless of these facts, drug policy reform is being blamed for these outcomes.
It's still all about the politics
In Ontario, predictably, the Ford government's position on Toronto's proposal is staunch opposition. Yet there is a sense of new momentum in the same old rhetoric: because drug users continue to die and use drugs in public places, presumably this means the experts and "bleeding-heart liberals" who supported reform have been soundly proven wrong.
Most concerning, whereas conservatives and liberals may differ in their emphasis on punishment or treatment as solutions to drug problems, neither type of government seems prepared to fully implement a public health approach to substance use.
That would mean addressing health disparities and deeper causes of substance use disorders, such as social inequality and social dislocation in Canadian society.
Increasingly, the discourse has gone further in establishing the need for more coherent public health policies addressing the root causes of addiction. To that end, important progress has been squandered by apparent bewilderment that addicts are still dying in large numbers, despite (and now because of) decriminalization measures that were never claimed to solve the problem of addiction, but rather to reduce known harms of criminalizing drugs.
Decriminalizing drugs is not intended to be a solution to drug problems. Rather, it is a critical first step that's necessary, but not sufficient, for replacing prohibition with a public health approach.
Addiction and public health
Decriminalizing drugs can make them safer, but not always, and reduce some harms of using drugs. What cannot be resolved entirely by decriminalization are the societal conditions that make substance use attractive enough to override considerations of concern for the self or others.
Addiction thrives on hopelessness and feelings of despair, abandonment and disconnection, which are caused by homelessness, unemployment and discrimination, among other social problems. Despite the range of local addiction support services and treatment options called for in support of decriminalization proposals, so far decriminalization efforts have failed to go beyond half measures to implement a full array of integrated resources.
Further off course yet, there has been little indication of meaningful commitment to addressing deeper structural root causes of addiction in North American society. Canada's commitment to a public health approach has fared no better than in Oregon.
None of these jurisdictions have gone far enough to remedy the real causes of drug problems. The fate of Portugal's successful run as a global leader in tackling addiction, as opposed to punishing addicts, helps to clarify new lessons being learned in North America.
Adopted over 20 years ago, decriminalization measures in Portugal have been implemented more successfully because its social safety net is far more comprehensive and better integrated with the criminal justice system. In recent years, as cuts to funding decimated rehabilitation programs, rising rates of overdose soon followed, demonstrating that decriminalization is not a silver bullet; if you do that and nothing else, things will get worse.
Put otherwise, half measures are ineffective. Until we are prepared to go all in to solve the problem, people who use drugs will keep dying unnecessarily because we have not afforded them real hope.
Sadly, this is news to no one. Not just experts. The Liberal government went on the record years ago by recognizing that decriminalizing drug use is not a panacea. Rather than committing to the next step, then and now, the necessary first step has been taken off the table.
Try giving it more time?
Another lesson learned in Portugal, that evidently has not been resonating here in North American society, is that the kind of change required takes time. Now that decriminalization has been tried (and deemed a failure) in B.C. and Oregon, the reigning logic appears to be that going back to doing nothing makes more sense than doing something.
Despite great patience shown during a century of misery inflicted on drug users in Canadian society, trying something new is scary. Cue the same old moral panic scapegoating addicts as dangerous people who those in charge prefer should not be seen.
Decriminalizing drugs can't eliminate people's struggles with addiction. To work well, it requires a considerable level of investment, similar to what has historically been devoted to policing and imprisonment of addicts. Redistributing resources to support the integration of services, housing and employment for more people is not, and has never been, prioritized over punishing drug users.
Until that shift occurs, we will keep falling back on failed policies consistent with a war-on-drugs mentality that has now prevailed for 50 years against all logic. In the meantime, be prepared for a new wave of antiquated arguments proclaiming more liberal policies a failure.
Commitment to half measures creates more problems than it solves.
Andrew Hathaway is a professor in the Department of Sociology and Anthropology at the University of Guelph. This is republished from The Conversation.
CAPTION: About 150 people gathered in downtown St. Catharines last year in recognition of International Overdose Awareness Day.Allan Benner Torstar file photo

Golf course is in a sorry state As a non ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/golf-course-is-sorry-state-as-non-derived/docview/3060987412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Golf+course+is+in+a+sorry+state+As+a+non+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Golf course is
in a sorry state
As a non-resident of Hamilton, I am a proud booster of all sports, arts and culture in the city. However it didn't take me long to recognize the city's own lack of commitment to its wonderful features.
A recent day at Chedoke golf course left me wondering if the city even cares about one of its oldest landmarks. My walk from the parking lot featured an overflowed garbage bin, three-inch weeds and at a terraced area leading to the Beddoe's first tee, nothing but a disregarded bed of weeds. Sand traps have become weed traps and spotty, uneven tee boxes greeted us on many holes.
It wasn't until I read the small print on the scorecard that I realized the Chedoke golf course was established in 1924. Really?
A 100th anniversary going unnoticed, unacknowledged and certainly uncelebrated. How do you ever expect to draw tourists and their money with such ill regard for your own?
It's shameful.
If and when I might be fortunate enough to turn 100, I certainly hope my storied life and beauty are recognized in some small way and not overlooked. You can do so much better for your city and yes, golfers certainly deserve more for the cost of their green fees.
Perhaps a tee found on the course that day sums it up: "The older I get, the better I used to be."
Carole Bertuzzi Luciani, Oakville
A poor use of
taxpayers' money
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol expansion; province will pay The Beer Store up to $225M (May 24)
Who would spend $225 million of hard-earned, and badly-needed taxpayer money, to break a Beer Store contract that will expire in only one-and-a-half years? Doug Ford would. The wasted money could go to health care, education, infrastructure, non-profit housing ... you name it. But my guess is that political expediency tops everything.
Soon after the new drinks appear in corner stores, I bet our premier will ignore the fixed election date law and take us to the polls. Will he be punished? I can only hope.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Why are we
picking sides?
Re: Court's arrest warrants welcomed (May 27)
War is a failure of leadership. Ralph D'Angelo is right. Both leaders have enlisted people in their armies to kill both opposing combatants and any innocent civilians that get in the way. Is it too much to ask all western leaders to condemn the atrocities committed by both sides? Why do we pick sides here, since there is so much wrong in both camps? At least the International Criminal Court is trying to bring awareness of these crimes in a partial way to everyone.
Steve Penner, Hamilton
We are running out of time
Re: The massive climate
failure of our federal political parties (May 25)
I was pleased to see the commentary by Grant Linney on Saturday.
The most recent target for net zero is 2050. I've read a number of reports indicating this will be impossible. Many countries are participating in fusion energy research as the only way to meet energy needs without fossil fuels. They have a plan to commission the world's first fusion energy power station in the 2040s. Already they know that date is unlikely to be met. Even when it is, it could well take another 30 years to build enough fusion stations to get to net zero.
Justin Trudeau's carbon tax rebate is little enough, but it is raising awareness. Even Doug Ford and Pierre Poilievre are helping by highlighting it, and influencing Trudeau to reinforce the message and do better at explaining the rebate. It will take time for that influence to take effect, time that we don't have. It has taken around 150 years of fossil fuel burning to cause today's climate challenge. We don't have another 150 to fix it, maybe not even 15.
But mankind has to do something. There may be other technologies that come along. Maybe there will be a miracle cure. The fatalist will say we can't fix it, what's the point of trying? Maybe we (the seniors' generation) should care about the world we are leaving to our children and grandchildren. One thing we can do is ensure we have leaders whose attitude is not "that's the problem for the next government, not us."
Roy Cheers, Dundas
Give them a taste of freedom
Our government is concerned that China is meddling in our election process. I say let them: They might learn something about democracy. The Chinese state is so used to their citizens being docile that they do not appreciate the fact that you cannot manipulate a free people.
Dan Riga, Burlington
Addiction care mired in politics
Re: The innocent victims
of the opioid crisis: toddlers (May 24)
As an addiction doctor who looks after people with opioid use disorder (OUD), my heart shatters to read about so much untreated illness, with young families left fending for themselves.
We have effective, life-saving treatments and services to support people. Treatment helps people function - as parents, coworkers and valued members of society. The evidence of benefit is more than 60 years old. Yet our ability to provide a respectful, patient-centred model remains stymied, mired in politics. On one hand, we have rhetoric claiming supervised consumption and "safer" opioid tablets will solve the opioid crisis. The counter-rhetoric would have us believe we can arrest our way out of it by criminalizing the illness.
Opioid agonist treatment (OAT) is the only evidence-based, effective and safe option for opioid use disorder. We've known this a long time and yet continue to underfund these programs. "Rapid access addiction medicine (RAAM)" clinics across the province operate on skeletal budgets.
Most community OAT clinics have been monetized with many owned by a Texas-based company. This has driven down the quality of treatment and de-incentivized patient-centred, compassionate care. The result is a valid health intervention that is stigmatized by society, by people with OUD, by the health and justice systems and even by harm-reduction advocates who should be directing patients to treatment.
Communities shatter when OUD is left untreated. Why are we neglecting these patients until a child dies? All of society is failing these families, not the untreated, unwell individuals.
Leonora Regenstreif,
Hamilton

Document 64 of 500

Sarah Jama's court challenge of censure at legislature dismissed: Hamilton MPP prevented from asking question in session at Queen's Park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sarah-jamas-court-challenge-censure-at/docview/3060987128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sarah+Jama%27s+court+challenge+of+censure+at+legislature+dismissed%3A+Hamilton+MPP+prevented+from+asking+question+in+session+at+Queen%27s+Park&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A court has dismissed a push by Independent MPP Sarah Jama for a judicial review of her censure by a majority vote in the Ontario legislature last fall.
The censure - which prevents Jama (Hamilton Centre) from asking questions of Premier Doug Ford and his cabinet ministers or otherwise speaking in the legislature - was passed Oct. 23 by the Progressive Conservatives on the same day New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles ejected her from the party caucus for insubordination.
"The law is well-settled," says the nine-page decision by Ontario Superior Court of Justice Divisional Court. "Courts have no jurisdiction to review matters that fall within parliamentary privilege, including review under the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," adds the decision released Thursday.
Stiles booted Jama from the NDP over a statement about the Israel-Hamas war that prompted the censure motion by the PC government after two weeks of growing controversy surrounding the first-term MPP.
Jama's statement on her MPP letterhead did not condemn the Oct. 7 surprise attack on Israel by Hamas or the many lives lost, and focused on the plight of Palestinians under Israeli occupation.
That statement remained online even after she posted an apology following a 24-hour standoff with Stiles, who had demanded the statement be retracted.
Under the censure, Speaker Ted Arnott of the legislature said he would not call on Jama to speak unless she retracts and deletes her statement and apologizes in the house.
"The privilege is key to maintaining the separation of powers between the legislature and the courts," the divisional court decision continued.
"The applicant must pursue remedies before the legislative assembly, not the courts," the court decision said.
Jama was not in the legislature Tuesday.
Her office did not immediately reply to a request for comment.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama

CREDIT: Rob Ferguson

May 28: Doug Ford chooses booze over health care, glad to see deal reached at McMaster and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/may-28-doug-ford-chooses-booze-over-health-care/docview/3060797610/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=May+28%3A+Doug+Ford+chooses+booze+over+health+care%2C+glad+to+see+deal+reached+at+McMaster+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Booze vs. health care
Re: Ontario speeds up alcohol expansion; province will pay The Beer Store up to $225M (May 24)
The Ford government will now give $225 million to The Beer Store to provide convenience for Ontarians to get better access to alcohol. Most Ontarians would have preferred giving $225 million to health care to provide the convenience for Ontarians to get access to family doctors and hospital emergency services. Talk about "bread and circuses."
Jim Young, Burlington
Glad to see a deal reached
Re: McMaster encampment over after protesters vote to accept university's offer (May 25)
Kudos to McMaster University and the student protesters reaching an agreement to end the encampment. Unlike the heavy-handed actions of other universities in Canada and the U.S., McMaster chose to engage in a meaningful dialogue with its students. Both sides came to an understanding about how to address the students' concerns and move forward. That wasn't so hard, was it?
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
Movement is not about peace
Re: How much is the cost of peace? McMaster's response to protest disappointing (May 21)
How much is the cost of peace, the writer asks. Well, that depends on terror groups like Hamas, Islamic Jihad and Hezbollah. To them there will only be peace once Israel and the Jews are wiped off the map.
As for this BDS (boycott, divestment and sanctions), the tactics are utilized not in the name of peace between Israel and Arab countries, but against Israel's very existence. A more appropriate name for them would be "bully, defame and slander." The BDS movement started in Durban, South Africa. This is where the word "apartheid" originated from. Israel is not, and never was, an apartheid state. Most of these university students don't even know the history or even what "river to the sea" means.
Scott Camillo, Hannon
Animals are more than profit
Re: African Lion Safari fights bill that would end elephants in Canada (May 22)
I just wanted to say how much I appreciated your article on animals in captivity. I don't think animals in captivity can only have a bad life - especially older animals who need special care and attention. But my problem with zoos is that for all their talk of caring for the animals, really it is all about turning a profit for a business. This was made clear during the pandemic. The second the animals were not making money for zoos, they became dispensable, with even "high-end" zoos saying they were going to have to euthanize some of their animals. Imagine if the minute a museum became unprofitable we started burning the paintings. We would be outraged, as these things are important in and of themselves, not because of the money they bring in. None of the animals at these zoos wanted to be taken from the wild or to be bred for a life in captivity and then, when it comes to being there for the animals, the zoos abdicate any and all responsibility for the lives that depend on them. Zoos pretend they are there for the animals, when really it's the other way around.
Sara Crane, Toronto
Even-handed coverage appreciated
How wonderful to see the issue of elephant captivity explored so thoroughly and thoughtfully on the front page of The Hamilton Spectator. I run a non-profit organization that encourages media to cover animal issues well, so that people can make informed choices in line with their own values. With its even-handed coverage of the African Lion Safari park elephant issue, your paper did exactly that. Thank you!
Karen Dawn, founder and president of DawnWatch, California
Kudos for court coverage
Re: 'Difficult to image a more planned murder': Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li guilty (May 24)
Thanks to Nicole O'Reilly for an excellent series in covering the seven-week trial of Li and Karafa. Very thorough analysis and reporting. Thanks, Nicole. I look forward to future reporting.
Al Kruzins, Dundas
Dyer wrong about Gaza conflict
Re: Could money alone bring peace to the Middle East? (May 14)
London-based syndicated columnist Gwynne Dyer, who has published a litany of anti-Israel ramblings and conspiracy theories, sought to draw a moral equivalence between Israel and Hamas.
Dyer's column also disingenuously claimed that "religious hatreds" are at the core of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, failing to draw a distinction between Israel, a liberal democracy with equal rights for all citizens which is not governed by Jewish law, and Hamas, a genocidal Islamic terrorist organization which sees its war with Israel through an exclusively Islamic religious prism. Shame!
Milt Moskowitz, Toronto
Enjoy explosions somewhere else
Re: Fireworks bring people together (May 25)
On behalf of my dog and many other domesticated and other wild animal life that become terrified with mini-explosions (re: fireworks), we find recent comments of "just go away" both offensive and ridiculous. If the person is so enthused with "gunpowder explosions" there are probably many openings in European war zones that will probably be to your liking. Go for it.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces that beer and wine will be available at corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween- thanks to a $225-million payout to the privately run Beer Store.

Document 66 of 500

'We receive strength in speaking our language': Indigenous MPP makes history at Queen's Park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-receive-strength-speaking-our-language/docview/3060797475/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+receive+strength+in+speaking+our+language%27%3A+Indigenous+MPP+makes+history+at+Queen%27s+Park&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP Sol Mamakwa made history speaking in - and speaking of - his first language in the Ontario legislature on Tuesday.
The New Democrat - whose family and supporters packed the gallery, including his mother Kezia who was celebrating her 79th birthday - spoke for 10 minutes in Anishininiimowin at the start of daily question period, the first time that has been allowed in the province's history.
He also then asked the first question of the day in his language, which in English is known as Oji-Cree.
Mamakwa, who represents Kiwetinoong, noted the significance of the moment, saying it was his "great privilege and honour to speak in my First Nations, Indigenous language."
He said that in residential schools, young people - including himself - were punished for speaking in their language, and their mouths were washed out when they did.
There is a need for youth to learn the language, "to teach it to kids so they speak it, and be proud of it," Mamakwa said as his words were simultaneously translated into both English and French.
He then recalled how his mother "used to take me out into the wilderness ... teaching me the language. This is why I am able to speak my language."
"We receive strength in speaking our language," he said, and by doing so in the legislature, "it's a healing moment that we are in."
MPPs, family and visitors to the legislature stood and applauded Mamakwa - and his mother - several times in the house. Following his speech - and in an unprecedented show of support - Premier Doug Ford crossed the aisle to congratulate and embrace the MPP, as did Long-Term Care Minister Stan Cho and Energy Minister Todd Smith.
Ford noted he'd previously met with Mamakwa in his office, and said he was proud of him for "blazing a new trail."
The premier also promised the much-needed long-term-care beds in Sioux Lookout for which Mamakwa has been advocating - for which Ford received a standing ovation from his own MPPs as well as all opposition members.
Tuesday's historic moment came after Mamakwa commented during an event last March in the legislative building that he was not allowed to speak in his first language in the chamber. That caught the attention of Government House Leader Paul Calandra.
The two later worked to change the rules to allow MPPs to speak in their own Indigenous languages, instead of just in English and French.
CAPTION: New Democrat MPP Sol Mamakwa made history in the Ontario legislature on Tuesday when he asked aquestion in his first language, Anishininiimowin.

Subject: Language; Legislatures

Title: 'We receive strength in speaking our language': Indigenous MPP makes history at Queen's Park

MPP Sarah Jama's court challenge of her censure at Ontario legislature is dismissed

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+Sarah+Jama%27s+court+challenge+of+her+censure+at+Ontario+legislature+is+dismissed&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Jama was not in the legislature Tuesday. Her office did not immediately reply to a request for comment.
"I'm very, very happy that the courts ruled in our favour," said Government House Leader Paul Calandra, who noted a court also upheld parliamentary privilege in a 2022 ruling that upheld a rare speaker's warrant approved by MPPs seeking financial records from Laurentian University in Sudbury.

Document 68 of 500

Trudeau and Ford governments reach $357M deal in federal funds for affordable housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ford-governments-reach-357m-deal-federal/docview/3060797164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+and+Ford+governments+reach+%24357M+deal+in+federal+funds+for+affordable+housing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa and Queen's Park have reached a deal ensuring $357 million in federal funds will flow to the province to build affordable housing.
After months of negotiations - and an acrimonious letter made public in the Star - federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser and provincial Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra on Tuesday announced the impasse had been cleared.
"Solving the housing crisis requires a Team Canada approach. We will continue to work together, along with our municipal partners, to make sure the people of Ontario have the homes they need," Fraser and Calandra said in a joint statement.
"Canada and Ontario recognize that our collaboration is imperative to solving the housing crisis. That is why we are pleased to share that an agreement has been reached on a revised action plan from Ontario that will unlock $357 million of federal funding under the National Housing Strategy," they said.
Under the bilateral agreement between the governments of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, Queen's Park has submitted a revised "action plan" detailing the affordable housing projects that are receiving provincial funding.
The province's revamped 2022-25 plan forecasts at least 8,644 more affordable housing units will be built by March 31, 2025.
But Ford's government could actually meet its original federally mandated target of 19,660 new units by that deadline.
"Ontario has also included new measures in its action plan which better reflect Ontario's funding delivery model, as the only jurisdiction which flows the funds through municipal service managers," the joint statement says.
"These measures include: establishing provincial supply targets with service managers, directing funding toward new projects, setting annual goals, and implementing robust data collection and reporting mechanisms."
The province will also submit a 2025-28 "action plan" to Ottawa by the end of this year in order to secure additional federal funding for the final years of the decade-long $70-billion National Housing Strategy agreement.
In Ottawa, Fraser said "Ontario has charted a path that will allow it to meet its obligations under the original agreement.
At Queen's Park, Calandra told reporters his federal counterpart had "rightfully pointed out that it is a challenge to count Ontario's numbers given the fact that we fund our housing differently."
"Once we were able to fully explain how we fund affordable housing with our service partners and their municipal partners, then we were able to make progress on that," he said.
It's a marked shift in tone from two months ago when Fraser had warned Ford's failure to meet federal affordable housing targets was jeopardizing the $357 million in funding.
The province had argued Ontario wasn't being treated fairly because its affordable housing stock is the oldest in Canada.
That means necessary repairs to existing units have come at the expense of building new affordable housing.
"Should Ontario fail to provide a revised plan demonstrating how it intends to meet its housing targets under the agreement, the province will not receive $357 million intended for affordable housing from the federal government," Fraser chided Calandra in March.
"Speaking frankly, the proposed action plan is a disappointment. It shows almost no progress toward reaching the affordable housing expansion target, and proposes to achieve only 1,184 units of the 19,660 required by the end of 2024-25," he wrote.
"This leaves 94 per cent of the target to be achieved during the last three years of the agreement, which is not realistic."
While Fraser had insisted "no further extensions are possible" beyond March 31 -because Ottawa had already given Queen's Park an additional year to revise its plan in 2023 -he relented after Calandra pointed out that was punitive and would hurt the most vulnerable.
With files from Tonda MacCharles and Kristin Rushowy
CAPTION: Under the agreement between the governments, Queen's Park has submitted a revised "action plan" detailing the affordable housing projects that are receiving provincial funding.

Document 69 of 500

Doug Ford was asked about calling on early election. 'Stay tuned,' he says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-was-asked-about-calling-on-early/docview/3060797153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+was+asked+about+calling+on+early+election.+%27Stay+tuned%2C%27+he+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Stay tuned."
That's Premier Doug Ford's message when pressed on whether he will call an early election in the wake of his expedited liberalization of booze sales, which could cost taxpayers $1 billion.
After the Star reported Monday that Ford is considering holding a vote a year or more before the scheduled June 2026 election, the premier spoke with Newstalk 1010 Tuesday morning.
"Are we going to have an early election?" asked host Jerry Agar.
"Again, I can't answer that. I just can't right now," said a defensive Ford.
"Well, who else can?" replied Agar.
"Jerry, Jerry, as far as I'm concerned we're going to focus on our agenda, getting things done and the people are going to decide - that's what's beautiful about a democracy," said Ford.
"Stay tuned," the premier added.
Under fire over criticism of an agreement that will pay The Beer Store $225 million - though opposition rivals claim the tab could actually balloon to between $600 million and $1 billion - Ford scrambled to defend his move.
"That's going to bring in revenues of anywhere from $895 million to $1.16 billion that we were leaving on the table year after year after year," he said citing the 8,500 new points of sale that Ontarians should be able to shop at after Oct. 31.
"We're going to create more (tax) revenues. What it comes down to is making sure that we have convenience for people - treating them like adults."
Source say Ford rushed the agreement in a bid to clear the decks by fulfilling a key election promise that dates back to 2018, giving him the flexibility to call a snap election this fall or next spring.
Progressive Conservative insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the premier is concerned about budget cuts a future federal Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre might foist upon the province.
Ford, who works closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals on many shared priorities, is worried there could be reduced transfer payments to Ontario and a scrapping of the federal-provincial electric-vehicle subsidies strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario industrial policy.
At his announcement Friday to tout the sped-up beer-and-wine-in-corner-stores scheme, Ford repeatedly refused to commit to the June 2026 election date, leaving open the possibility of an early vote, which could cost more than $155 million.
"I just want to get our agenda through," he coyly told reporters that day.
Tory insiders say it is shrewd of Ford to keep his options open after the PCs won two byelections earlier this month and boast a double-digit poll lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," confided one loyalist.
As well, an early election might get the Tories ahead of any fallout from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under RCMP investigation.
But other Tories close to Ford oppose an early call, warning it would be seen as "cynical and self-serving" to have a vote so soon after the premier received a re-election mandate in June 2022.
They point to the 1990 election when popular Liberal premier David Peterson called an election a year earlier than required and was punished by voters, losing to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the biggest upsets in Canadian political history.
"Peterson was higher in the polls (50 per cent in summer of 1990) than Ford (at 39 per cent in the latest Abacus Data survey) is now and had won a larger majority (in 1987 than Ford did in 2022)," noted another Tory.
While Ford is aware of that perilous precedent, he is also mindful of what's known as the "Underhill balance theory" that posits that when Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they tend to choose Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the NDP - usually win provincially at Queen's Park.
The phenomenon has held true for 70 of the past 81 years in Ontario, so Ford fears a Poilievre victory in an expected October 2025 federal election would most likely help Crombie's Liberals in June 2026.
Peterson, now the chair of Torstar, parent company of the Toronto Star, was circumspect when asked Monday about the wisdom of going to the polls early.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, whose Liberals made history in 1985 by ending 42 years of PC rule in Ontario.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces that beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Privacy commissioner probes deleted Greenbelt emails

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/privacy-commissioner-probes-deleted-greenbelt/docview/3060711882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privacy+commissioner+probes+deleted+Greenbelt+emails&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's privacy commissioner says she will publish a special report about the use of non-government emails and deleted messages related to the Greenbelt.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles had asked Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario Patricia Kosseim to investigate the premier and government staffers' reported use of personal phones and emails on the controversial file.
In a letter from the commissioner released by the NDP, Kosseim says her office is working on 19 active access-to-information appeals that are similar to the concerns Stiles raised.
In late 2022, the Doug Ford government removed land from the protected Greenbelt to build 50,000 homes. After months of public outcry, and reports from both the auditor general and the integrity commissioner that found the process favoured certain developers, Ford reversed course.
The RCMP is now investigating the Greenbelt removal process.
The privacy commissioner did not provide a time line on when her special report would be published.

In a separate investigation, the auditor general found political staff used both personal and government emails on the Greenbelt file.
The auditor general also discovered political staff emails related to the Greenbelt land swap had been deleted. Both actions were inappropriate and should not have occurred, the investigation found.
CAPTION:

Document 71 of 500

Ford should put Big Tobacco out of business

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-should-put-big-tobacco-out-business/docview/3060710818/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+should+put+Big+Tobacco+out+of+business&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this month, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew did something that no other provincial leader has done.
He made public how the provincial lawsuits against tobacco companies will likely be settled. Manitoba would receive an initial payment of up to $500 million, he announced, and future payments are expected.
With Ontario's population more than 10 times that of Manitoba, Premier Doug Ford may similarly be anticipating an initial payment from tobacco companies of up to $5 billion and a commitment to future instalments.
However, there's a dark side to a settlement with Big Tobacco requiring payments that go beyond the $12 billion the companies currently have available: It would need to rely on future sales of addictive and harmful products.
Combined, Ontario and the other provinces have claimed more than $500 billion from tobacco companies to compensate for the health-care costs caused by decades of wrongful marketing and public deception. This amount is more than enough to drive the Canadian branches of multinational tobacco companies into bankruptcy, forcing them to shut down.
Any future compensation payments can only be generated by keeping alive these same companies whose products kill 17,000 Ontarians each year and who have hooked generations of young people through vaping.
In addition, using future sales revenues to fund ongoing compensation to the provinces and other creditors means that consumers - not companies - will finance a global settlement between the provinces and the companies. It means that the purchases, health and well-being of smokers, vapers and other nicotine users will underwrite compensation for past wrongdoing.
The vicious cycle of addiction, disease and health-care costs will continue. Meanwhile, corporate directors and others whose wrongful behaviours are detailed in the provinces' claim will go unpunished.
No premier should accept a settlement proposal that legitimizes and sustains the harmful tobacco industry behaviour that is at the root of these lawsuits. No government should seek compensation for past wrongdoing that results in a continuation of harm.
Instead of extending the viability of the tobacco business and prolonging the smoking and addiction epidemic, Ford and his colleagues should work together to impose an orderly wind-down of the commercial tobacco market. They should seize this historic opportunity to make the companies stop recruiting new customers and start phasing out this harmful business.
If these leaders prefer a monetary settlement to one focused on protecting health, they are out of step with public opinion. A poll conducted for our organizations by Leger in the fall of 2023 found that 70 per cent of Canadians supported their "province using these lawsuits to require tobacco manufacturers to phase out the commercial sale of cigarettes in Canada." Only 17 per cent of respondents opposed this outcome.
Kinew deserves credit for breaking the silence that Ford and others have imposed over the past five years onto these negotiations as a way to deny public input or insight about the future of the tobacco industry in Canada.
Secret settlement discussions are fundamentally undemocratic. The decisions on whether this industry is allowed to escape bankruptcy and how it will be held accountable for decades of wrongdoing should not be made through a backroom deal. They are important public policy issues that deserve transparency, open discussion and public engagement.
Now is the time for Ford and his legislative colleagues to consult with Ontarians about the future they want for the tobacco industry.
Such a listening exercise would not contravene any court-ordered confidentiality on insolvency discussions. It would instead help those talks by informing those at the negotiating table about the priorities of those they represent.
Without such transparency and engagement, the upcoming settlement looks very much like a sugar-coated money grab.
Cynthia Callard represents Physicians for a Smoke-Free Canada, Flory Doucas, the Quebec Coalition for Tobacco Control and Les Hagen, Action on Smoking & Health.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Cynthia Callard, Flory Doucas and Les Hagen;

Document 72 of 500

Doug Ford is betting that beer will help him win an early election - and taxpayers are stuck with the tab

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-betting-that-beer-will-help-him-win/docview/3060857354/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+betting+that+beer+will+help+him+win+an+early+election+-+and+taxpayers+are+stuck+with+the+tab&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has figured out the price of power. Too bad we're all paying for it.
To the premier, it's small beer - a mere $225 million in subsidies for suds. That's how much he's admitted to paying out in taxpayer funds to the big brewers as financial compensation for allowing beer into convenience stores roughly a year ahead of schedule.
The political payoff? Ford wins bragging rights, with our money, for belatedly delivering on his 2018 campaign promise to make beer available just about anywhere, anytime.
It also buys him the flexibility to hold an early election - not in 2026, as scheduled under Ontario's fixed election law, but a year sooner. At what price, precisely?
By some counts, it is destined be far higher than the purported low ball figure of $225 million - as much as $1 billion, according to opposition critics.
Remember "buck-a-beer," the other cynical slogan Ford dined out on in 2018? It fizzled out fast because that unsustainable price for a can of beer defied common sense and business sense.
During his winning campaign, buck-a-beer was twinned with another slogan that was similarly oversold - "beer in corner stores." Ford knew perfectly well that The Beer Store's owners could sue the province for defaulting on a binding 10-year contract under which they'd first agreed to relax their grip on the market by phasing in supermarket beer sales.
That pioneering framework, negotiated by the previous Liberal government, broke the stranglehold of the big brewers. But it wasn't good enough for Ford - who always boasts of bettering what came before.
Mercifully, Ford's most influential cabinet ministers managed to talk him out of breaking the beer pact, persuading him that the province faced costly litigation and possible penalties exceeding $1 billion. Yet it always rankled our teetotalling premier that he'd failed to deliver on a forgotten promise.
Now, all these years later, he has contented himself with an anticlimactic announcement about an antiquated issue. Belatedly, beer will become more convenient - a mere one year ahead of when it otherwise would have, and four decades after it first became a political issue.
Back in the 1980s, the Liberal leader of the day, David Peterson, first promised beer in corner stores but found it inconveniently complicated to deliver on it. Now, it's a non-issue that no one notices.
They're not even called corner stores anymore. They're no longer on every corner, not all that convenient, and mostly owned by big chains, not Mom and Pop operators.
Which is why Ford's news fell flat on Friday - flatter than a stale beer washing down a nothing burger. Still, there is good reason to pay attention, for we will potentially be paying far more than Ford pretends.
Beyond the stated subsidy of $225 million going to The Beer Store's owners, another $375 million in fees now paid by them to the LCBO will be rebated.
As Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie points out, a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains - now enjoying record profits - could easily total more than $150 million in lost revenues. If you add in the foregone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores (charged by other jurisdictions), that's at least $300 million more money left on the table.
By Crombie's count, it adds up to a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" that is "lining the pockets" of the wealthy and well-connected - an echo of the campaign rhetoric Ford used against the Liberals in 2018. Buy beyond the opposition critiques, there's also the opportunity cost of spending so much time and money so utterly opportunistically on a faded, defanged oligopoly.
Quite apart from financial resources, consider the governmental bandwidth being wasted by a premier who doesn't touch a drink deploying the best minds in the public service to bringing beer into convenience stores. Imagine if Ford spent more time and money solving real problems rather than creating new ones - dealing with overflowing surgical wait-lists, say, or a housing and homelessness crisis.
The speculation is that Ford prefers to gear up for an early provincial campaign to get out ahead of a federal election later that same year likely to crown his Tory cousin, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as prime minister. The premier is understandably wary of how shabbily the federal Conservatives might treat Ontario as they slash spending.
An early election could also allow Ford to dodge the results an RCMP probe into his other boondoggle (that'd be the Greenbelt imbroglio). He might also catch Crombie's Liberals off guard while deflating the NDP's Marit Stiles before either gains traction.
But it's a quid pro quo that's hard to quaff - or swallow: Ford's fantasy is that by keeping his stale promise on beer in corner stores, he is now free to break the promise of fixed election dates every four years codified in law.
Ford's billion-dollar bet may be one boondoggle too many.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford pretends to drink from a beer can and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy holds one at an announcement saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales, in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Beer; Convenience stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores

Document 73 of 500

Tory insiders say fear of a Pierre Poilievre victory has Doug Ford considering an early election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tory-insiders-say-fear-pierre-poilievre-victory/docview/3060857138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+insiders+say+fear+of+a+Pierre+Poilievre+victory+has+Doug+Ford+considering+an+early+election+call&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is considering an early election call before the scheduled 2026 vote over concerns about cuts a future federal Conservative government might impose, the Star has learned.
Sources say Ford is worried that if, as polls suggest, Pierre Poilievre wins an election expected in October 2025, there would be reduced transfer payments to the provinces, a scrapping of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy and other slashed spending that would hurt the Progressive Conservatives.
That's a major reason why the premier is paying The Beer Store $225 million to liberalize booze sales as of this fall - more than one year earlier than planned - in a deal critics warn could actually cost taxpayers between $600 million and $1 billion.
At his announcement Friday to tout the expedited beer-and-wine-in-corner-stores scheme, Ford repeatedly refused to commit to the planned June 2026 election and left open the possibility of a snap vote, which could cost more than $155 million, this fall or next spring.
"I just want to get our agenda through," the premier told reporters, noting the expansion of beer sales was long-standing PC campaign promise.
But Tory insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss ongoing internal deliberations, say Ford was giving himself options at a time when his party has just won two byelections and enjoys a big poll lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," said one insider, who is bullish on the plan.
Another said it would force the Tory caucus to focus on priorities like delivering on the 2022 campaign's "Get It Done" pledges, such as improved highways, public transit and hospital construction.
"We have to show results and (booze liberalization) is something we promised to deliver and the premier has done that," said the second official.
A third PC source conceded that an earlier than anticipated election could get the Tories ahead of any fallout from the ongoing RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
But some Tories fear a reprise of 1990 - when popular Liberal premier David Peterson called a snap vote, and then lost to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the most stunning upsets in Canadian political history.
"Peterson was higher in the polls (50 per cent in summer of 1990) than Ford (at 39 per cent in the latest Abacus Data survey) is now and had won a larger majority (in 1987 than Ford did in 2022)," said a fourth Tory.
"But voters were ornery and punished him for the early election."
A fifth Tory expressed alarm that voters would feel it was "cynical and self-serving" to have a vote so soon after Ford received such a resounding re-election mandate in June 2022.
"What would the rationale be for going early?" asked the Ford loyalist.
The premier's inner circle is mindful of the potential perils - but also aware of provincial political history.
According to what's known as the "Underhill balance theory," when Ontario voters elect Liberals federally, they tend to elect Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the NDP - usually win provincially.
That has been the case for 70 of the past 81 years in Ontario, so Ford's team believes a Poilievre victory in October 2025 would most likely help Crombie's Liberals in June 2026, which is why they may act pre-emptively.
For his part, Peterson, now the chair of Torstar, parent company of the Toronto Star, was circumspect when asked Monday about the wisdom of Ford going to the polls early.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, who governed Ontario from 1985 to 1990.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
Still, some on Ford's team are poring over "Not Without Cause," the searing 1991 post-mortem book on Peterson's gamble by former Liberal aides Georgette Gagnon and Dan Rath.
The book - billed as "a cautionary take for voters and politicians everywhere" - chronicled the mounting anger voters felt over an early election that cost $40 million at the time.
"Confident they knew what Ontarians were thinking in the summer of 1990, the Liberal strategists gave no thought to the possibility Peterson might actually be defeated in the election he was about to call," wrote Gagnon and Rath.
Elections Ontario said the 2022 election cost $145.3 million, which according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator, is the equivalent of $155.8 million this year.
Rivals charged Ford risks the wrath of voters if he holds an early vote for his own benefit.
"The people of this province would see it for what it is, which is an attempt to divert and hide what's coming or the RCMP criminal investigation, but I say also - bring it on," said Stiles.
Crombie said governments calling early elections are "typically punished" for wasting tens of millions of dollars.
"We know what the lesson has been in the past. It would be very disrespectful to the taxpayer. They have a majority government," she said, calling Ford's liberalization push "a billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
Under Ontario's election law, a premier does not have to abide by the fixed date every four years.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and his wife Karla celebrate after Ford led his Progressive Conservatives to a second election victory on June 2, 2022.
