Document 74 of 500

Report on personal emails in Greenbelt scandal will follow FOI appeals, privacy commissioner says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/report-on-personal-emails-greenbelt-scandal-will/docview/3060856984/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Report+on+personal+emails+in+Greenbelt+scandal+will+follow+FOI+appeals%2C+privacy+commissioner+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's privacy watchdog says a report on the use of personal email accounts by staff conducting government business related to the Greenbelt is "not imminent," and will only be issued after a number of freedom-of-information appeals are dealt with.
In a statement to the Star, the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario said it currently has "19 active appeal files concerning requests for records related to the Greenbelt. Upon conclusion of these appeals, we plan to publish a special report on our website encapsulating our findings and providing a coherent and thoughtful summary of our conclusions and insights resulting from all 19 appeals."
This "will not impact our normal appeal process," the statement said, adding that "while we cannot provide a specific release date for the special report at this time, we are committed to fully completing the appeals process for the 19 current appeals. The release of the special report, as such, is not imminent."
Privacy commissioner Patricia Kosseim confirmed that work is underway on the report in a May 21 letter to NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who has formally raised concerns about the use of private emails or the use of code words to evade freedom-of-information requests.
"Transparency in government actions and decisions is a fundamental principle that underpins the public's trust in government," Kosseim said.
"Ensuring such actions are open to external scrutiny is essential to maintaining this trust and upholding the values that guide our work at the IPC ... My office takes these matters seriously and is committed to a thorough, fair, and impartial examination of the issues arising from these various appeals, your letter and the auditor general's report."
The RCMP is already investigating the $8.28 billion Greenbelt scandal, although no updates have been provided. Reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both determined that staff were not involved in the selection of sites to be removed from the Greenbelt for housing - decisions later reversed by the Ford government as it restored the properties to their protected status - but did say well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
In the legislature Monday morning, Stiles said Premier Doug Ford should ask his chief of staff to resign in light of a Global News report that "revealed new evidence that the premier's chief of staff used his personal email account to conduct government business on dozens of occasions. That directly contradicts his sworn testimony to the integrity commissioner when he claimed, 'I do not conduct government business on my personal email." But guess what? He does.
"The premier's chief of staff appears to have repeatedly and directly contradicted his sworn testimony to the integrity commissioner under oath."
In response, Government House Leader Paul Calandra said the government" will continue to work with both the integrity commissioner and the information and privacy commissioner. Nothing has changed on that score.
"If the leader of the Opposition has any additional information that she would like to provide the integrity commissioner, I encourage her to do so."
In April, the Star reported that the province's integrity commissioner cleared Ford's chief of staff Patrick Sackville of wrongdoing after an email from his personal account came to light.
After looking into a complaint launched by Stiles, the integrity commissioner said he "accepted" Sackville's explanation that the messages sent in October 2022 were missed.
The NDP had also provided the information and privacy commissioner documents indicating that "G*" was being used instead of "Greenbelt" in emails, and that personal phones and email accounts were being used to conduct government business.
"We have been deeply concerned by revelations of deleted emails, the use of personal accounts, and other apparent attempts to cover up the government's actions as it schemed to give preferential treatment to well-connected Greenbelt speculators," Stiles said Monday.
Ford's office has repeatedly said it had no direct knowledge and played no role in selecting the lands to be removed from the Greenbelt.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said he welcomes the privacy commissioner's decision to report on "the Ford government's Greenbelt secrecy."
All along, he added, "we've known that the government's story doesn't add up. From deleted emails to misremembered dates, this government has gone out of its way to conceal the truth from the public. I hope this investigation sheds some much-needed light on the government's actions and delivers the honesty and accountability Ontarians deserve."
CAPTION: Patricia Kosseim is Ontario's information and privacy commissioner.

Document 75 of 500

Ontario's privacy commissioner to release special report on Greenbelt, NDP says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-privacy-commissioner-release-special/docview/3060279105/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+privacy+commissioner+to+release+special+report+on+Greenbelt%2C+NDP+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province's information and privacy commissioner will release a special report on Greenbelt-related emails, documents and the Ford government's record keeping on the matter, says the opposition NDP.
"We have been deeply concerned by revelations of deleted emails, the use of personal accounts, and other apparent attempts to coverup the government's actions as it schemed to give preferential treatment to well-connected Greenbelt speculators," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles. "This report will provide a clearer understanding of the government's conduct."
Stiles said "reports like these are rare and very serious," and added "the walls are closing in for this government. One way or another, the truth about Ford's Greenbelt dealings is coming out of the backrooms and into the light."
In April, the NDP gave documents to the Information and Privacy Commissioner indicating that in emails, "G*" was being used instead of Greenbelt, and that personal phones and email accounts were being used to conduct government business.
Stiles also says she provided examples of inconsistencies in testimony from government staffers.
The commissioner responded May 21, "revealing that they have 19 active files concerning requests for records related to the Greenbelt - so many that they have decided to dedicate an entire special report on access-to-information and record-keeping relating to changes to the Greenbelt," Stiles also said.
The RCMP is conducting an investigation into the $8.28 billion Greenbelt scandal and on Thursday Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie released a letter to the commissioner and assistant commissioner
"I am asking that you provide an update on the progress of your investigation, in light of these most recent revelations," including those in a Star story about a senior staffer in Premier Doug Ford's office who met with a developer involved in the scandal.
Premier Doug Ford's office has repeatedly said it had no direct knowledge and played no role in selecting the lands to be removed from the Greenbelt.
Reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both determined that staff were not involved in site selection, but did say well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Marit Stiles speaks to reporters outside the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

We all lose if Ford wages a war on drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-all-lose-if-ford-wages-war-on-drugs/docview/3060278967/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+all+lose+if+Ford+wages+a+war+on+drugs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's no great surprise that Ottawa has quashed Toronto's bid to decriminalize drugs. Yes, there is strong evidence that criminalization is a significant barrier to fighting addiction and reducing its many downstream costs; that people addicted to drugs won't seek the help they need if they feel they will be punished for doing so; that as long as they avoid such help, the cost will be not just to them, but also to the health-care system, the justice system and public safety.
But with a provincial government loudly opposed to decriminalization, and the federal Liberals' appetite for political risk at an all-time low, Toronto's timing could hardly have been worse.
For those with the power to decide, the example of British Columbia's experiment with decriminalization was surely impossible to ignore. While the precise effects of B.C.'s short-lived policy will take time to fully emerge, the costs - both human and political - were clear. As drug use became increasingly evident in the streets, and public overdoses spiked, many British Columbians complained of a sense of unease and disorder in their communities, pushing the province's NDP government to modify its approach.
It may well be true, as many advocates have argued, that British Columbia did not give the policy its best chance to succeed by, for instance, making the necessary investments in housing and anti-poverty measures. But in any case, if Toronto's push to decriminalize ever had a chance, B.C.'s experience no doubt killed it.
Ottawa and Queen's Park decided the risks were too big to ignore. The question now is whether the province will take as seriously the well-documented risks of over-criminalization or whether it will once again wage a misguided war on drugs.
It can be hard to tell where Doug Ford stands on this. Over the years he has intermixed punitive bluster with notes of compassion. While running for office in 2018, he said he was "dead against" supervised consumption sites, but added he believed "in supporting people, getting them help."
Stressing the need for treatment is common among governments that oppose decriminalization and other harm reduction measures. Less common, however, is actually providing that help. Ontario is a case in point. Six years after Ford pledged to help those struggling with addictions, that help remains elusive for many.
With decriminalization off the table, how will the province ensure adequate treatment services? How will it eliminate barriers that impede access to those services? In a pre-decriminalization survey in British Columbia, illicit drug users cited fear of criminal sanctions as the No. 1 reason they were hesitant to seek help. Obviously, one goal of decriminalization is to allay that fear. In the absence of such a policy, other harm-reduction measures like supervised consumption sites and safer supply become even more important.
Several studies, in both B.C. and Ontario, have shown supervised-injection sites and safe supply of drugs are the most effective ways of addressing addiction and its diverse harms. Despite this evidence, Ford has been less than enthusiastic about harm reduction. In keeping with his 2018 comments about supervised consumption facilities, Ford paused approval of new sites pending review. On the heels of Ottawa's rejection of decriminalization, Ford wrote to the feds asking "to immediately stop approving new (safer supply) sites, and conduct a formal review of existing ones in the province."
We have seen some of the concerns around decriminalization also apply to supervised-injection sites. These have in some cases created a sense of unease in communities that must be managed, and certainly not glibly dismissed. But nor should fear alone dictate the government's direction. Nor should the province dismiss the ample evidence that, despite the trade-offs, harm reduction is an important tool in fighting addiction, reducing the burden it puts on society and public coffers and enhancing public safety.
Ford got his way on decriminalization. But he and his province will lose if he lurches in the other direction. If Ford is truly interested in getting people help, if he truly wants to take on the epidemic of drug addiction and the resulting unease in the streets, and if he truly wants a drug policy that promotes public safety, he should remember that drug users won't seek the treatment they need if they think doing so will get them in trouble.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Document 77 of 500

How an Indigenous MPP won the right to speak in his own voice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-indigenous-mpp-won-right-speak-his-own-voice/docview/3059926649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+an+Indigenous+MPP+won+the+right+to+speak+in+his+own+voice&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Sol Mamakwa speaks, politicians listen.
A sudden hush falls over the legislative chamber that is eerily respectful. The heckling stops, the chatter ceases, the clacking of keyboards is suspended.
A silence so strong, in a place riven by rancour and resentment, that it has a special power. How can it be so, in a place so often boisterous and poisonous?
Mamakwa is a charismatic First Nations MPP representing a majority-Indigenous riding. When he rises, recognized by the Speaker, no one else speaks - for in that moment, all partisan rivalry is suddenly suspended.
And yet, for all his commanding rhetoric and political gravitas in the legislature, Mamakwa's voice has always been subdued, his countenance constrained. He could speak, yes, but the crown's legacy and the legislature's rules would permit him to speak only in English (or French, Canada's other official language).
Mamakwa could not say what he was thinking - in his head, in his heart, in his mother tongue, Anishinaabemowin. He might be empowered by his Indigenous voters, yet he could not give them voice - in their own voice.
Now, six years after winning his far-north riding of Kiiwetinoong, and winning over so much of the legislature, Mamakwa has finally prevailed. He will soon have his say, in his own way.
On Tuesday, his mother Kezia Mamakwa and Indigenous dignitaries will descend on the legislature from Northern Ontario to hear him speak to them - and all of us - in his first language for the first time.
"All languages matter, especially Indigenous languages that are on the brink of extinction," Mamakwa tells me.
"Language connects us to our history, language connects us to who we are, it connects us to the land where we came from. It is the language of our people."
His victory is a testament to the power of persuasion, political negotiation, and artful performance. How did it happen, after all these years?
The seeds were planted when Mamakwa was asked to speak at a public event inside the legislature earlier this year, alongside the powerful PC cabinet minister, Paul Calandra, who serves as government house leader. Calandra had the microphone first, bringing greetings from the government.
But when it was Mamakwa's turn to speak on behalf of the NDP, he made his move - speaking first in Anishinaabemowin. He then translated for himself, explaining that his mother had always taught him to speak his own language lest he lose it - as so many other Indigenous people already have.
Lingering and listening in the audience was Calandra, who couldn't believe that his language was forbidden. Bumping into the New Democrat in the legislative cafeteria the next morning, Calandra suggested it was surely a misunderstanding of the rules.
But it wasn't. In fact, Mamakwa been ruled out of order by the Speaker within a few seconds of trying to speak his language.
Calandra's staff quickly confirmed that Mamakwa was correct. Incredulous about the archaic rules, the cabinet minister told Mamakwa that together, they would right this wrong.
"I was dumbfounded," Calandra recalls. "Over breakfast, we figured it out."
He credits Mamakwa for forcing the rule change - the rare opposition MPP who wins respect and gets results.
"Honestly, had Sol not been there to say that, nobody else would have brought it up, frankly," Calandra told me. "It struck me that it should just be done."
Which is not often said by Tories to New Democrats (or vice-versa). But Mamakwa knew what he was doing by speaking out while Calandra was within earshot.
"That was me putting a bug in his ear when I spoke my language," he confesses. "I did that on purpose. Sometimes you says things that will make people think later."
Within a week - warp speed by legislative standards - changes to the rules had been circulated by the government and approved in a vote. Looking back, Calandra says Mamakwa has a gift for connecting with people and deploying humour to make a serious point.
I've written before about his fondness for social media - "As a First Nations person, humour is part of who I am - and his penchant for sly protests in the legislature. Mamakwa once poked fun at the solemn procession that marks the start of legislative sittings by smiling and hissing, "Colonizers!"
Mamakwa muses that he is rarely on the same page with Calandra, but "I think he understood that they cannot continue the old ways of the colonial institutions that did not allow First Nations people to speak."
He gives the sometimes cantankerous Calandra full marks for listening - and acting: "Even though it seems like a small change, it's a monumental change to me."
It's a change Mamakwa could not have fathomed when, as a boy, he attended a residential school in the far north. The Mennonite teachers prohibited his mother tongue and the punishment included hard manual labour, with students sent out in the bitter cold to cut wood without any gloves.
All these years later, Mamakwa is about to make his first official remarks in Anishinaabemowin and then pose a question to the government on behalf of the NDP. The topic will likely be long-term care for Indigenous people in his riding, because he wants to mark the event as both substantive and symbolic.
Language matters, Mamakwa says, turning sombre.
"It's going to be a serious question - it's not just for show."
CAPTION: MPP Sol Mamakwa speaks with media inside the Ontario legislature at Queens Park in Toronto on April 22, 2024.

Subject: Language; Legislatures; Native North Americans

Document 78 of 500

Housing strategy won't solve crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/housing-strategy-wont-solve-crisis/docview/3059816047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Housing+strategy+won%27t+solve+crisis&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is about to undo the protections we have for the province's watersheds, wilderness and farmland. It's also taking away the public's ability to speak out - claiming it's the only way to get affordable housing.
The government is wrong. The consequences of two new housing laws introduced by Premier Doug Ford will remove remaining guardrails to unbridled, costly and unaffordable low-density sprawl with no guarantee affordable homes will ever be built.
Ford's proposed laws run contrary to the advice from his own Housing Task Force as well as the Blueprint for More and Better Housing. Both reports call for housing to be built within cities and communities - not in fields and forests - to save money, combat climate change and facilitate rental housing.
The proposals also put the province directly at odds with the federal government's plans to promote new housing within existing cities and communities. For the first time in decades, Ottawa has signaled clearly to Canadians it is back in the housing business, committing $8.5 billion in new spending. This focuses on building badly needed rental housing and a recommitment to affordable co-op and non-profit housing, including on publicly owned lands, which typically happens in existing towns and cities.
The province is headed in the opposite direction. Shortly before the federal housing plan was released, Ford's government introduced Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build more Homes Act, along with proposed changes to the Provincial Planning Statement, Ontario's land use master plan. If passed, these will give developers a free hand to open up farmland and natural areas to build expensive single-family homes in new subdivisions. This won't help the housing crisis.
The premier's stated preference to focus on single family units is at odds with the fact that Ontario's median household income is about $75,000 and that one- and two-person households now comprise over half of all households.
No surprise that eight out of 10 housing starts in 2023 were multi-residential units - that's what's needed.
The proposed provincial laws remove legal requirements that direct growth to existing communities rather than greenspace. They also remove any requirements for even minimum density on farmland approved for housing - so there's no guarantee that affordable units would be built. And they take away any municipal powers to stop urban expansion onto farmland.
At the same time, the public will lose its voice in the planning process. The proposed legislation removes the ability of any person, landowner, non-government organizations, or upper tier municipality to challenge an urban expansion at the Ontario Land tribunal.
It's true that pedantic legal challenges can sometimes slow up planning and housing, but rather than trying to streamline the process, Ford's proposals simply take away peoples' rights. And that's wrong.
Even after being stung by the Greenbelt scandals and while still tainted with the whiff of corruption, the Ford government is moving forward on the removal of Ontario's farmland and environmental protections with this proposed legislation. It's also needlessly stripping the rights of elected municipal councillors and ratepayers to participate in the planning of their communities.
We call upon Queen's Park to join with Ottawa to ensure we build the housing we need in the right places within our existing towns and cities.
David Crombie is the former mayor of Toronto and
federal cabinet minister. Anne Golden is the former president of the Conference Board of Canada. Both are founders of the Alliance for a Liveable Ontario.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: David Crombie and Anne Golden;

National briefs: Protesters must clear by Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3059815664/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Protesters+must+clear+by+Monday&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Saskatchewan
House leader resigns over
gun incident
Saskatchewan's government house leader has resigned his position and says he took a long gun into the legislature building a decade ago. Jeremy Harrison says in a statement that he has apologized to Premier Scott Moe. He says the gun was properly cased, and he took it into the legislature before going on a weekend hunting trip. The Opposition NDP has been calling for days for Harrison to explain himself, after Speaker Randy Weekes accused him in the house of bringing a hunting rifle into the legislature.
Ontario
Ford fuels early Ontario election speculation
Premier Doug Ford is fuelling early election speculation by declining to commit to sticking to the planned June 2026 date. Ford announced Friday that his plan to expand sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails is being sped up. He was asked if he is speeding up the timeline to set the stage for an early election call. He said that is not the reason, but then when asked several more times if he is committed to the June 2026 election date, he did not directly answer.
Protesters must clear by Monday
The University of Toronto issued a trespass notice Friday to a pro-Palestinian encampment on campus, vowing to take "all necessary legal steps" if protesters did not clear out by Monday morning. Despite the notice, encampment organizers said university administrators had also agreed to meet on Sunday at 5 p.m. for continued negotiations. The notice comes after the university made an offer Thursday, with a 24-hour deadline, to end the weeks-long protest.
News services

Document 80 of 500

Expansion of sales to begin Aug. 1: Licensed grocery stores OK to carry ready-to-drink cocktails, larger cases

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/expansion-sales-begin-aug-1/docview/3059815154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Expansion+of+sales+to+begin+Aug.+1%3A+Licensed+grocery+stores+OK+to+carry+ready-to-drink+cocktails%2C+larger+cases&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is speeding up its expansion of alcohol sales in the province, and the move comes with up to $225 million in public money for The Beer Store.
Premier Doug Ford announced late last year that sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails would be allowed in convenience stores and all grocery stores by 2026.
He announced Friday that the phased expansion will now start on Aug. 1 of this year, with licensed grocery stores that currently sell beer, cider and wine able to sell ready-to-drink cocktails and sell large-pack sizes like 30 packs.
After Sept. 5, eligible convenience stores will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails, and after Oct. 31, all grocery stores and big box stores could sell those products, including large-pack sizes. Over time, the changes will add up to 8,500 new places where people in Ontario could buy alcohol, the government said.
"People are excited they're going to enjoy the same choice and convenience as other Canadians and other people right across the world to buy a case of beer or a bottle of wine on the way up to the cottage," Ford said at an announcement Friday.
"Or maybe you're going to start up the grill in the backyard or (have a) summer barbecue. They're excited to hear that they're finally being treated as adults."
An agreement the former Liberal government signed with The Beer Store in 2015, which gave the company exclusive rights to sell 12- and 24-packs of beer as the province expanded sales of beer and wine to grocery stores, was set to expire at the end of 2025.
As part of this new, earlier rollout of Ford's plan to expand alcohol sales, an "early implementation agreement" signed with The Beer Store involves the province paying the company up to $225 million to support the transition, including to help it keep stores open and workers employed.
Ford disputed a characterization that the money is going to the companies that jointly own The Beer Store, though the agreement specifies "the province shall reimburse TBS" for added costs up to that amount.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the money will "help maintain perhaps unprofitable Beer Stores."
Part of the new agreement with The Beer Store includes keeping at least 386 stores open until July 2025 and at least 300 until Dec. 31, 2025, Bethlenfalvy's office said.
The Beer Store, which is owned by three international companies, will remain the province's primary beer distributor along with its very successful recycling program. That program will continue until at least 2031, the company wrote in a statement.
About 6,500 people work at The Beer Store across the province.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy hold cans as they announce the expansion of alcohol sales in Toronto on Friday. The government says over time, the changes will add up to 8,500 new places where Ontarians can buy alcohol.Christopher Katsarov The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 81 of 500

Unpredictability is key for Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unpredictability-is-key-ford/docview/3059815117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unpredictability+is+key+for+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's enduring popularity is a peculiarity.
Despite the disdain of so many, Ontario's premier dominates opinion polls and defies expectations in byelections.
Why is Ford so immune to the midterm decline so typical and predictable for any politician - let alone a leader savouring his second term in power? How does a premier who keeps losing face and executing about-faces still stay in the good graces of his supporters?
Much of Ford's re-election victory in 2022 can be attributed to the ebbing popularity of his political rivals. Yet both opposition parties now have new, able leaders in place - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - with little apparent traction to date.
Consider the premier's latest byelection triumphs, in which the Tories won two out of two. By way of disclaimer, I'd normally ignore the polls or byelections in between general elections, because they typically attract protest votes without necessarily forcing a change in government.
What's more, both of this month's byelections could be seen as signs of decline in the ruling party, triggered by two cabinet ministers who bailed early. Voters often punish parties for early departures, but happily for the Tories, one of the two seats - vacated by Monte McNaughton, the former minister of labour - was in a largely rural stronghold.
But the race in Milton was too close for comfort. Parm Gill, the incumbent cabinet minister, bizarrely and unexpectedly defected to the federal Tories.
So how did Ford's Tories hold the riding against all odds? Turns out that Milton's newest MPP is not just from the governing party. He is also new to the party - having defected from the Liberals before the byelection.
Zee Hamid ran federally for the Liberals a decade ago, has donated to the party ever since, and actively supported the former provincial leader Steven Del Duca when he campaigned for the post in 2020. The Karachi-born tech entrepreneur, with degrees in math and computer sciences and a CV that includes Microsoft and Amazon, embodies the kind of business-savvy, multicultural candidate most parties covet in 905 ridings.
When I caught up with him at a Queen's Park event, I asked him why he'd rather switch to Ford's Tories than fight for the Liberals he once loved. He chuckled and talked about feeling comfortable running for a Tory party with broad appeal, after serving as a councillor in Milton.
That's an interesting admission and evolution (regardless of whether one believes Ford's claim that he too has "evolved"). As a recent graduate of municipal politics, Hamid understands how partisan affiliation remains in the background - a mindset that also animates much of Ford's politics at the provincial level, where the premier plays up his personal brand more than party loyalty.
The long-standing recipe for Ford's popularity is populism, not partisanship. That's why his loose rhetoric is so often aimed at pleasing people, rather than appeasing policy experts; at short-term tactics over long-term strategy; at political positioning rather than dispassionate deliberating.
The impulse for quick fixes - and fast flips - can be seen in Ford's fondness for seductive slogans over enduring solutions. Hence his resort to privately run medical clinics as an antidote to hospital wait-lists, as if these pilot programs can somehow be scaled up quickly to save the system; similarly with Ford's Greenbelt fiasco, he pretended that lifting the protections on a few parcels of land would make a dent in a much bigger housing shortage.
That's the problem with Ford's short-term outlook and midterm run of luck - long term, his luck is bound to run out when the bad news catches up to him.
That day may come after next year's federal election, if Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre becomes prime minister - putting Ford under the gun should a new government in Ottawa misfire badly. Or Ford may yet dodge that bullet by burying the Ontario Tory brand, pivoting even more to the centre, and pushing back against Poilievre's anticipated antics.
In politics there is no predictability, just uncertainty. Which is why Ford keeps defying expectations.
Martin Regg Cohn covers politics and international affairs for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION;

Document 82 of 500

'NEW MARKETPLACE': Ontario Premier Doug Ford says every grocery store, convenience store and big-box store can sell beer, cider, wine and other alcohol by November

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-marketplace-ontario-premier-doug-ford-says/docview/3059407694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27NEW+MARKETPLACE%27%3A+Ontario+Premier+Doug+Ford+says+every+grocery+store%2C+convenience+store+and+big-box+store+can+sell+beer%2C+cider%2C+wine+and+other+alcohol+by+November&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some big changes are coming to grocery stores, big-box stores and variety stores in Ontario, according to Premier Doug Ford.
Starting in August, shoppers can purchase new products like coolers and other ready-to-drink beverages alongside more pack sizes at grocery stores that currently sell wine or beer, the government said Friday (May 24), followed by new retailers being able to sell an increased selection of local, domestic and international alcohol products.
By the end of October, every convenience, grocery and big-box store in Ontario will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages if they choose, Ford said.
"We are delivering on our commitment to give consumers in Ontario the choice and convenience every other Canadian enjoys and we're doing so even sooner than we had originally promised," said Ford in a statement. "In the coming weeks and months, people in Ontario, like many Canadians across the country, will have the option to responsibly and conveniently purchase a case of beer or a bottle of wine on their way up to the cottage or to a summer barbecue, all while having even more opportunity to support local Ontario breweries and wineries."
Here's a timeline of the phased rollout:
 On Aug. 1, the up to 450 grocery stores that are currently licensed to sell beer, cider or wine will also be able to sell ready-to-drink beverages. These grocery stores will also be able to start offering consumers large-pack sizes like the 30-pack that is popular in Quebec, the government said;
 After Sept. 5, all eligible convenience stores will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages.
 After Oct. 31, all eligible grocery and big-box stores will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages, including in large pack sizes, the government said.
This plan comes much earlier than expected, as the Ontario government hinted late last year that beer, wine, cider, coolers, seltzers and other low-alcohol ready-to-drink beverages wouldn't be available at all participating convenience, grocery and big box stores across the province until 2026.
"Through this phased rollout, the government is creating the conditions for a more open and convenient alcohol beverage marketplace. Individual retailers and businesses will make their own decisions about how they participate and when," the release states.
Ontario Minister of Finance Peter Bethlenfalvy says the new approach "treats Ontario consumers like adults" by giving them more choice and convenience, while also supporting Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry, adding the "new marketplace" will see government officials continue to meet, consult and work closely with industry partners, local beverage alcohol producers and other stakeholders.
CAPTION: Doug Ford.

Subject: Liquor; Convenience stores; Grocery stores; Breweries

Document 83 of 500

Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-beer-wine-are-coming-ontario/docview/3059407322/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+beer+and+wine+are+coming+to+Ontario+corner+stores&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians will drink to that.
There will finally be beer and wine in corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's long-awaited liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween - thanks to a $225-million payout to the privately run Beer Store.
"We are delivering on our commitment to give consumers in Ontario the choice and convenience every other Canadian enjoys, and we're doing so even sooner than we had originally promised," Premier Doug Ford said Friday at an Etobicoke convenience store.
The surprise move would give the premier the flexibility to call a snap provincial election in 2025 - a year earlier than the scheduled June 2026 vote - because he will have kept a key campaign pledge.
When Ford unveiled the plan to give additional choice for buyers of beer, wine, cider, hard seltzer and premixed cocktails on Dec. 14, he conceded it might take until 2026 to implement due to long-standing agreements with the beer and wine industry.
But taxpayer-funded subsidies of up to $225 million to The Beer Store, which is majority-owned by breweries Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, over the next 19 months expedited the process.
That cash is designed to "protect jobs" and maintain the foreign-owned company's retail "footprint" as liberalization unfolds.
The move was denounced by a "disappointed" Retail Council of Canada president and CEO Diane Brisebois, who represents major grocers, as a "sweetheart deal for the big multinational beer companies" that won't help supermarkets.
The Beer Store will continue to be paid to oversee recycling and bottle returns for all alcohol beverage containers until 2031, and the publicly owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) will maintain its monopoly on spirits sales such as whisky, vodka, gin and rum.
Private retailers, who can begin applying for licensing online on June 17, will buy their booze from the government monopoly. They will get an interim wholesale discount of 10 per cent off LCBO retail prices until 2026.
Under the new timetable, up to 450 supermarkets that were licenced to sell beer, wine and cider by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 will be allowed to sell premixed cocktails - like NÜTRL, Aperol Spritz, White Claw and Dillon's Small Batch - on Aug. 1.
As well, those grocery outlets, which are currently restricted to selling beer in six-packs, will be able to offer cases of 24 that only The Beer Store has been permitted to sell.
After Sept. 5, all eligible convenience stores will be allowed to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages.
And after Oct. 31, eligible grocery and big-box stores - such as Walmart and Costco - will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and premixed booze.
The government will continue to impose minimum price restrictions to thwart any attempts at cut-price sales, although "private-label" products could be available in 2026.
The province's Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario (AGCO) will police the liberalized system.
"In the coming weeks and months, people in Ontario, like many Canadians across the country, will have the option to responsibly and conveniently purchase a case of beer or a bottle of wine on their way up to the cottage or to a summer barbecue, all while having even more opportunity to support local Ontario breweries and wineries," the premier said.
Ford - a teetotaller whose government last week announced stiffer penalties for those who drink and drive and has previously pledged $10 million over five years to promote social responsibility - emphasized it was time Ontarians were treated like grown-ups.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, who counts on the LCBO to generate around $2 billion a year in profits to provincial coffers, echoed that.
"Our responsible and balanced approach treats Ontario consumers like adults by giving them more choice and convenience, while also supporting Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry," said Bethlenfalvy.
"As we launch this new marketplace, we will continue to meet, consult and work closely with industry partners, local beverage alcohol producers and other stakeholders."
When liberalization is finally completed, there will be an additional 8,500 booze retailers in Ontario.
Currently, there are about 2,935 outlets - including the LCBO's 685 locations plus 389 agency stores, 422 Beer Stores, 449 supermarkets, 526 wine retailers, 373 craft brewery shops and 75 distillery stores - licensed to sell booze in the province.
As well, about 19,000 bars and restaurants have been permitted sales of alcohol to go since 2020 - thanks to pandemic-era changes implemented by Ford's former treasurer Rod Phillips.
Phillips's move, which was intended to help small businesses cope with COVID-19 restrictions that prevented them from operating normally, has led to the opening of hundreds of new boutique bottle shops.
Last Christmas, Ford and Bethlenfalvy announced Queen's Park would not renew the 10-year "master framework agreement" with the beer and wine industry when it expires on Dec. 31, 2025.
The winding down that accord, signed by Wynne, means the Progressive Conservatives can finally keep their 2018 campaign pledge of "beer in corner stores" in time for the next election.
After a series of stories by the Star's Martin Regg Cohn on The Beer Store's private monopoly, Wynne inked the 2015 agreement with the brewing giants and vintners in order to allow sales of beer and wine in up to 450 supermarkets across Ontario.
In a miscalculation that generated international headlines, the newly elected Ford unilaterally tried to scuttle her deal with legislation in 2019.
But the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, strongly warned of the grave consequences of scrapping such a contract and outlawing compensation payments for the multinational companies involved.
With Ontario at risk of being accused of being anti-business, Ford's Tories retreated and agreed to let the master framework agreement run its course.
It is unclear how much demand exists for additional points of sale - some supermarkets that are already licensed and equipped with trained staff to sell booze, are abandoning the practice due to shoplifting concerns and thin profit margins.
CAPTION: Beer and wine will be sold in corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's long-awaited liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween, Premier Doug Ford announced Friday.

Document 84 of 500

Tanenbaum trumpets 'Canada's team': Owner of soon-to-be WNBA expansion franchise in Toronto has city abuzz with announcement

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tanenbaum-trumpets-canadas-team/docview/3059209167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tanenbaum+trumpets+%27Canada%27s+team%27%3A+Owner+of+soon-to-be+WNBA+expansion+franchise+in+Toronto+has+city+abuzz+with+announcement&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The WNBA isn't just coming to Toronto, it's coming to Canada.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, rapper Drake, Toronto Raptors star Scottie Barnes and former Raptors great Kyle Lowry were all in attendance at a downtown hotel on Thursday morning as the WNBA made the new franchise official. Larry Tanenbaum's Kilmer Sports Ventures are the owners of the as-yet unnamed team that he hopes will be adopted across the country.
"This franchise will be Canada's team," Tanenbaum said. "While our home base will be at Exhibition Place in Toronto, we will play games in Vancouver and Montreal throughout the season, uniting the country behind our franchise and inspiring pride and passion in fans from coast to coast."
Tanenbaum is the chair and chief executive of Kilmer Group and the chair of the board for Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment, which owns the Raptors, the National Hockey League's Maple Leafs and other sports properties in Toronto. Kilmer Group also has a 25 per cent ownership stake in MLSE.
Kilmer Sports Ventures paid $115 million (U.S.) for the WNBA team. Tanenbaum was also responsible for bringing the NBA to Toronto, with the Raptors joining the league 30 years ago.
"This is an incredibly important occasion and a historic one," said Tanenbaum after being presented with a WNBA basketball by commissioner Cathy Engelbert. "I'm thrilled so many of you are here to celebrate this game-changing day for not only women's basketball, but for sports in Canada."
Trudeau, Ford and Chow all praised Tanenbaum's efforts to bring the WNBA to Toronto. The three political leaders noted that bringing a professional women's basketball team to the city was good for gender equality, but that it was a savvy business opportunity.
"I don't think Toronto and indeed Canada can thank you enough for all your contributions to sport but this one, even a Montrealer can get way behind," said Trudeau to Tanenbaum on the dais at Hotel X. "As a Montrealer, it does pain me to say that Toronto is such an incredible sports city.
"Through thick and thin, through heartbreak and triumph and heartbreak, Torontonians consistently demonstrate their passion, their commitment and their love for professional sports."
The WNBA team will play out of the 8,700-seat Coca-Cola Coliseum, an arena in downtown Toronto at Exhibition Place and will have the ability to move up to the 19,800-seat Scotiabank Arena on occasion. The 103-year-old arena is also home to the American Hockey League's Toronto Marlies and hosted the first Professional Women's Hockey League's Toronto team during its first playoff run.
Kilmer Sports said upgrades to the arena will be made so it can be used by the WNBA team for training and practices, as well.
Teresa Resch was named the WNBA team's first president during the announcement ceremony Thursday. She had spent the past 10 years with the Raptors, helping to design and build the NBA team's practice facility the OVO Athletic Centre and launching the Raptors 905, Toronto's NBA G-League affiliate based in Mississauga.
Resch said that she'll put her more than decade of experience as a sports executive to use building the franchise.
"We're gonna put all those things to use because we've made mistakes in the past and we won't have to make those in the WNBA because we know those things now," she said. "It's an untapped market.
"I think the reason that (Kyle Lowry) is here in the audience today is because of the impact that Toronto had on him and his experience, and I think a lot of his teammates would say the same thing and we look forward to doing that in the W."
Toronto's WNBA team will start playing in May 2026.
It will be the WNBA's 14th franchise, with the expansion Golden State Valkyries set to start play next year.
"We're excited to expand outside the United States and this is our first (team outside the U.S.) as we continue to work to bring in new audiences, new fans," said Engelbert. "It creates new opportunities for players.
"The depth of talent in this league is amazing and so to be able to offer a deeper pool of talent with a team here in Canada is great."
Canada has hosted sold-out WNBA pre-season games at Scotiabank Arena in 2023 and Edmonton's Rogers Place on May 4. Engelbert said that the success of those exhibitions helped sell Toronto as a landing spot for a new expansion team.
"I'll never forget last year at Scotiabank Arena when I attended our Toronto game and lots of people coming up to me and saying 'Thank you for making my dreams come true,' by bringing a WNBA game here," said Engelbert.
"That's when I knew this is the right place."
There are four Canadians in the WNBA this season - Aaliyah Edwards, of Kingston, Ont. (Washington Mystics), Bridget Carleton, of Chatham, Ont. (Minnesota Lynx), Laeticia Amihere, of Milton (Atlanta Dream), and Hamilton's Kia Nurse (Los Angeles Sparks).
The Canadian Press, with files from The Associated Press

Document 85 of 500

Bill would expand citizenship rights: Canadians would now be allowed to pass citizenship rights down to children born abroad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bill-would-expand-citizenship-rights/docview/3059208258/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bill+would+expand+citizenship+rights%3A+Canadians+would+now+be+allowed+to+pass+citizenship+rights+down+to+children+born+abroad&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new government bill tabled in the House of Commons on Thursday would allow Canadians to pass citizenship rights down to their children born outside the country - a move that would add an unknown number of new citizens.
In 2009, former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper's government changed the law so that Canadian parents who were born abroad could not pass down their citizenship, unless their child was born in Canada.
Those who've not had access to citizenship rights as a result of the amendments are known as "Lost Canadians."
The new bill looks to undo that change, which was struck down by a recent court challenge, and extend citizenship by descent beyond the first generation born outside of Canada.
The legislation would automatically confer citizenship rights to children born since 2009 who were affected by the Conservatives' changes. It would also create a new test for children born after the legislation comes into force.
Parents who were born outside of Canada will need to have spent at least three years in Canada before the birth or adoption of their child to pass on their citizenship.
The government has no idea how many people will be automatically granted citizenship if the legislation is passed.
"We're a country that supports human rights, equality, and respect for all people," Immigration Minister Marc Miller said in the foyer outside of the House of Commons after he tabled the bill.
"There's no doubt that Canadian citizenship is highly valued and recognized around the world. We want a citizenship to be fair, accessible, with clear and transparent rules."
Last year, the Ontario Superior Court found the current system unconstitutionally creates two classes of Canadians, and gave Ottawa until June 19 to fix the problem.
The implications of the change made in 2009 were hugely significant for families, NDP immigration critic Jenny Kwan said. She helped to draft the new legislation alongside the Liberals.
"I've talked to family members who've been separated from their loved ones because of this unjust law that Conservatives brought in 15 years ago," she said.
In a statement on Thursday, Conservative MP Tom Kmiec, who serves as his party's critic of immigration, refugees and citizenship, said Canada's immigration system is "falling apart."
"Common sense Conservatives will fix our immigration system that the Liberals have broken," the statement reads.
CAPTION:

Document 86 of 500

TTC workers' right to strike upheld

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ttc-workers-right-strike-upheld/docview/3059207668/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+workers%27+right+to+strike+upheld&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's top court has upheld the right of Toronto Transit Commission workers to strike, a decision that comes just days before potential job action.
The Court of Appeal for Ontario dismissed an appeal Thursday by the provincial government of a lower-court ruling that had declared a law eliminating the workers' right to strike unconstitutional.
In 2011, the Ontario government, under the Liberals, enacted a law banning unionized TTC workers from striking, which government lawyers in this appeal said came after "unusually frequent strike action and immediate ad hoc back-to-work legislation."
A Superior Court judge found last year that the law interfered with workers' collective bargaining rights, and the Appeal Court agreed in a split decision. The lower court judge did make some errors in his analysis, the Appeal Court wrote, but none of them "fatally undermined" his conclusion that the law breached TTC workers' collective bargaining rights and that breach could not be justified under the section of the charter that allows reasonable limits.
"The main thrust of the application judge's reasons is that Ontario's evidence did not persuade him that a full pre-emptive ban on TTC strikes was reasonably needed to protect public health and safety, the environment, and the economy," the Appeal Court majority wrote.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 87 of 500

Pierre Poilievre says he'll 'axe the carbon tax' for consumers - but when it comes to the pricing system for Canadian industries, he won't say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-says-hell-axe-carbon-tax/docview/3059533762/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+says+he%27ll+%27axe+the+carbon+tax%27+for+consumers+-+but+when+it+comes+to+the+pricing+system+for+Canadian+industries%2C+he+won%27t+say&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-He would repeal incoming federal electricity regulations. He would scrap Liberal clean fuel standards. And, yes, he wants to "axe the tax." But it's less clear where Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stands on another major piece of federal climate policy: the national requirement for industrial carbon pricing.
It's a significant question mark in his campaign against the federal levy on consumer fuel, which Poilievre denounces as a tax that unfairly imposes costs on Canadians. That levy, formally called a "fuel charge" on things like gasoline, is just one piece of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon pricing regime. The other part is the legislated need for all provinces and territories to have an industrial pricing system for sectors like oil and gas, cement and steel production, or adopt one that was made in Ottawa.
The murkiness has some industry players, lobbyists, and climate policy advocates trying to decipher what the Conservative leader thinks, with some urging him to come clean so businesses don't delay spending that will be crucial to meeting Canada's emissions reduction commitments.
Numerous political and business insiders say Poilievre's office is devoting time and resources to thinking through their environmental policy, and despite his ongoing criticisms of the lobbying sector, remain open to hearing from industry leaders about how existing programs and policies help or hurt their sector's own environmental ambitions.
But few expect clarity on industrial pricing program anytime soon, arguing that if industry wants certainty their questions should be directed not to Ottawa, but to provinces like Alberta and Ontario that have their own industrial pricing systems meeting federal requirements.
Poilievre has not held a press conference on Parliament Hill in more than a month. On Thursday, his office provided a statement to the Star that lambasted Trudeau's climate policies but didn't say whether a future Conservative government would keep, change or ditch the current rules for industrial pricing.
The head-scratching only got more vigorous after the new head of the Pathways Alliance, an oil industry lobby group devoted to reducing the sector's Canada-leading greenhouse gas pollution, told the CBC that "it would be very helpful for him to provide greater clarity" on industrial pricing.
The group declined an interview request from the Star this week.
The Liberal government quickly took note of the demand. Last weekend, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault posted on social media: "When will Pierre Poilievre come clean to Canadians about his plan to help his big polluter friends off the hook?"
Guilbeault also told a parliamentary committee on Wednesday that industrial pricing helped spur projects like Dow Chemical's $11.5 billion upgrade to make an Edmonton-area facility produce no emissions, and suggested such investments could be in jeopardy if these pricing systems were dismantled.
But some say uncertainty around future climate policies - including what the Conservatives would do with industrial pricing - is also dragging potential investments to reduce emissions from heavy industry. Catherine Cobden, the president and chief executive officer of the Canadian Steel Producers Association, said "big questions" are hanging about the future of the industrial pricing system is "putting a chill" on private spending to slash emissions that cause climate change.
She also cited questions around Liberal government policies, including over supports for heavy industries as the minimum carbon price is scheduled to keep increasing every year, from the current $85 per tonne to $170 per tonne in 2030.
"From the government, we are still seeking clarification regarding the future carbon pricing regime and associated supports for our industry," Cobden said in a statement to the Star.
"From the opposition parties, we are looking for more detail on their climate policies should they form a future government."
Other industry groups, like the Chemical Industry Association of Canada (CIAC), say they want industrial pricing to stay in place, but also want to see additional support like the promised tax credits for green investments that the government says will be worth more than $90 billion over the next 11 years.
"Our message is to continue with carbon pricing," said CIAC president Bob Masterson. Like Guilbeault, he pointed to investments like the Dow Chemical project near Edmonton as evidence the industrial systems can help spur green spending.
But he said more support will be needed if the price keeps going up as the Liberals have planned, since a heavier price could risk them losing business to competitors in places without carbon pricing. "You have to be concerned about the outlook of companies," he said.
For Michael Bernstein, executive director of the pro-carbon pricing think tank, Clean Prosperity, significant emissions reductions from heavy industries are critical for Canada to hit its 2030 target. The country has pledged to slash emissions to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by that year, while the most recent government data shows Canada was 7.1 per cent below 2005 levels in 2022.
With an unprecedented amount of reductions needed in a span of less than a decade, Bernstein said clarity from Poilievre on industrial pricing would be welcome, since there are questions about whether the system will remain in place, whether the minimum price under federal law would be changed, and what provinces would do if the federal requirements were removed or weakened.
One recent report, from the Canadian Climate Institute, estimated that industrial pricing could be responsible for a big chunk - between 20 to 48 per cent - of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030.
Poilievre has previously expressed support for Alberta's version of industrial carbon pricing, and that the Conservative leader told reporters last year that he is not proposing to withdraw Canada from the international Paris Agreement.
But if Poilievre kept Canada's current emissions targets and scrapped industrial pricing, that would raise questions about how provinces would respond, and how the country would turn to alternative policies - like government regulations or taxpayer subsidies - to reduce industrial emissions.
"Industry is not going to decarbonize, at least in a systematic way, without some policy push," he said. "You currently need the industrial pricing system, or you have to rely on one - or both - of regulations or subsidies."
There is also a political question of whether, in keeping a form of industrial pricing at the federal level, Poilievre would take hits for supporting a form of carbon pricing - something that hurt his predecessor as Conservative leader, Erin O'Toole, within his own party.
Ken Boessenkool, the executive director of Conservatives for Clean Growth, said politically, Poilievre has no need to fear attacks for keeping the industrial system even if he goes ahead and scraps the consumer carbon levy, because voters simply don't see them as the same. Former Alberta premier Jason Kenney, he noted, removed the provincial consumer price on carbon yet embraced an industrial program, named TIER, for Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction.
"If you go up and down Alberta, 95 per cent of people would agree that Kenney got rid of the carbon tax," Boessenkool said. "They would be wrong. But they would also be right."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre during question period, in Ottawa on May 23, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Climate policy; Emissions; Carbon; Subsidies; Climate change; Steel production; Environmental tax

Document 88 of 500

Pickering mayor asks Ford government to revoke MZO to build housing near airport lands, but some are on the Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pickering-mayor-asks-ford-government-revoke-mzo/docview/3059525386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pickering+mayor+asks+Ford+government+to+revoke+MZO+to+build+housing+near+airport+lands%2C+but+some+are+on+the+Greenbelt&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The mayor of Pickering wants Premier Doug Ford's help to have land set aside in the 1970s for a never-built international airport eventually opened up for housing.
Kevin Ashe has written to provincial Housing Minister Paul Calandra formally requesting he revoke a 1972 minister's zoning order (MZO) that prevents construction of any houses or apartment towers on properties adjacent to the federal-owned Pickering Airport Lands.
But some of the lands in question are now part of the protected Greenbelt.
Former premier Bill Davis's government issued the MZO as part of an agreement with then prime minister Pierre Trudeau to ensure the flight paths of jetliners using the planned Pickering international airport wouldn't be hindered by tall buildings.
Ashe's request was quietly posted to the Environmental Registry of Ontario (ERO) late Wednesday and would affect 3,563 hectares, or 8,804 acres, of land.
In the wake of Ford's Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP, Queen's Park will only consider an amendment to the MZO rather than revoking it.
That's because the provincial government, still stinging from the $8.28-billion land debacle, does not want anything built on the Greenbelt.
"This posting is being undertaken in response to a request from the City of Pickering to revoke Ontario Regulation 102/72 to remove restrictions on future urban development, on a portion of the lands subject to the zoning order," the ERO post said.
"This future urban development is proposed in the Region of Durham Official Plan, which is currently before the minister for a decision," it continued.
"The city's request sought full revocation of the zoning order; however, the ministry is consulting on a proposal to amend the zoning order to only remove a portion of the lands that are outside of the Greenbelt Plan Area, which would ensure continued application of the MZO in the Greenbelt area."
In a letter to Calandra sent last Friday, Ashe wrote that he was "respectfully requesting that the Government of Ontario revoke O. Reg. 102/72, a provincial MZO" in northeast Pickering, which would enable the construction of homes for 40,000 people, creating 10,000 jobs.
"Restrictions regarding a potential airport are outlined in the Regional Official Plan, which makes it clear that constructing an airport and developing northeast Pickering are separate and independent matters," the mayor wrote.
"The Regional Official Plan does not limit an airport nor depend on one. In fact, planning and developing northeast Pickering will help inform the federal government's decision making on whether or not to site an airport in Pickering," he continued, noting he is using his "strong mayor powers" to achieve provincially mandated housing construction targets.
More than a half century ago, Ottawa expropriated about 7,500 hectares (18,600 acres) of farm land for a new airport 56 kilometres from Toronto because what is now known as Pearson International Airport was at the time considered to be at full capacity. Pearson has since been expanded several times and is now the largest and busiest airport in Canada.
While the Pickering airport scheme has been scaled back over the years, the project remains in a holding pattern and has never been scrapped.
In 2013, Ottawa created the Rouge National Urban Park from a significant chunk of the land, reserving 3,400 hectares (8,400 acres) for a "potential future aviation development."
Thirteen months ago, the federal government announced a new study "analyze current and future airport supply and demand" in southern Ontario that could determine what, if anything, is built in Pickering.
A spokesperson for federal Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez would only say the work is ongoing.
"Our government will consider the results of this study among many other factors to determine next steps on the Pickering Lands," Laurent de Casanove said.
Opponents, such as community group Land Over Landings that worry about the impact on agricultural land, hope the airport plan will crash and burn once and for all.
After Ford's election in 2018, MZOs have become a far morecommonly used tool to expedite development.
But the orders have sparked concerns from municipalities, opposition parties, community groups and environmentalists that the Progressive Conservatives were invoking too many of them.
Since taking over the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing last September in the wake of the Greenbelt fiasco, Calandra has made sweeping reforms to curb the use of MZOs.
Last August, separate reports by the auditor general and integrity commissioner concluded certain well-connected developers with Tory connections were "favoured" when Ford opened up 3,000 hectares (7,400 acres) of protected Greenbelt land to build housing.
After a public outcry, plunging poll numbers and resignations of key ministers and aides, the premier cancelled the land swap last Sept. 21.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt," he said at the time.
"I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes."
With files from Mark Ramzy
