Document 89 of 500

There's a new book about Pierre Poilievre. Here are five things we learned about the Conservative leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-new-book-about-pierre-poilievre-here-are/docview/3058772588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+a+new+book+about+Pierre+Poilievre.+Here+are+five+things+we+learned+about+the+Conservative+leader&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-"It's just Pierre being Pierre."
That's the answer many in Conservative circles give these days when they're quizzed about why party leader Pierre Poilievre does or says something that observers struggle to understand.
But over eight months and through more than 60 interviews, journalist Andrew Lawton sought to probe more into what that actually means: Who is Poilievre? What drives him?
"By explaining who Poilievre is, where he came from, and how he fits into the changing landscape of Canadian conservative politics, we get a sense of what the future may hold as his political life unfolds," Lawton writes in the introduction to "Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life", his newly-released book obtained ahead of its publication by the Star.
Here are some key takeaways from the first book-length look at a man polls suggest could be well on the road to becoming Canada's next prime minister.
Why is he sometimes called Jeff?
That Poilievre is the adopted son of two schoolteachers is well known; he references it in his regular rally speeches and elsewhere all the time.
Lawton reveals that one of the reasons Poilievre is sometimes referred to as "Jeff" is because that's the name his birth mother would have given him, if she'd raised him on her own. His adoptive parents also adopted his biological half-brother Patrick. The brothers forged a relationship with their biological mother when they were in their 20s.
His adoptive parents separated when he was 12, and he remains close to his mother Marlene, as well as his father Don and Don's long-time partner Ross McWhirter.
Lawton also shoots down a rumour that has circulated for years that Poilievre's father was sitting in the House of Commons when Poilievre voted against legalizing same-sex marriage and also for a broad reopening of the debate itself.
He eloped to Portugal
Poilievre grew up in Calgary and got involved in politics early - he was so involved that he ultimately dropped out of his bachelor's degree program at the University of Calgary in 2002 to take a job with then-foreign affairs critic Stockwell Day. He was three courses shy at the time, Lawton writes, and eventually took online courses through Athabasca University to finish off his degree in 2008.
'One of the biggest mistakes of his political career': New book details what happened when Pierre Poilievre crossed Stephen Harper
His affection for his wife Anaida Poilievre - and the political force she is in her own right - also comes across at campaign rallies, but Lawton's book reveals the early days of their courtship.
She dodged him initially and was miffed he unilaterally set the terms for their very first date in 2012 - at a downtown Ottawa coffee shop Poilievre was able to have access to before it opened to the public. Their first date went so long Poilievre ended up with a parking ticket.
He told her he loved her after only two weeks of dating, and two weeks later wrote her a poem about their first month together. They moved in together shortly after, and would later elope to Portugal in 2017 to get married.
The duo have two children, and Lawton notes that their older daughter, Valentina, has been diagnosed with autism and is non-verbal. She receives therapy, though what kind and from who is not specified, but Lawton reports it has benefitted her.
Known as a prankster
Lawton reports Poilievre likes playing pranks on his own staff and other colleagues, often using foreign accents or mimicking other politicians.
He's posed as a foreign diplomat to call the office of then-Conservative Foreign Affairs Minister John Baird claiming Baird had promised something during a trip abroad and demanding he deliver.
Poilievre, Lawton writes, also once had a woman call his own office to pretend she was pregnant after a one-night stand with him and wanted to track him down to tell him about it.
Poilievre was in a relationship with longtime Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne at the time.
Byrne did speak to Lawton about their relationship for the book - the pair dated for 12 years - but declined to delve into it in detail. The relationship ended, she told Lawton, because they had simply grown apart.
Lawton also attempted to interview people who worked with Poilievre during his time as a junior cabinet minister, but many did not return his calls.
One unnamed source said Poilievre was "insufferable" as a minister, though has grown out of it, while others told Lawton that if people chafe under Poilievre's leadership, it means they simply aren't cut out to work on the Hill. He once told his staff "this is not a self-esteem factory," a phrase that was then turned into a poster and put on the wall of his office.
As party leader, Lawton reports "Poilievre and his team have run a tight ship, with high expectations for attendance and performance in question period. Non-essential absenteeism is forbidden," but Poilievre also ensures caucus has a chance to speak their mind behind closed doors so they have their say, Lawton writes.
He is often his own harshest critic; colleagues recount how he was "sullen and withdrawn" for some time after stepping on his government's 2008 apology for residential schools.
Where did he cut his political teeth?
Lawton traces a long arc for Poilievre's political ideas and ideals all the way back to his childhood - his first political outing, Lawton says was a provincial Progressive Conservative riding association meeting he attended with his mother.
"He enjoyed debate already and sometimes his teachers didn't like that very much," his mother Marlene told Lawton. "Some teachers told me he sat on the edge of his desk and couldn't wait to debate."
As a teen, among his go-to books? The dictionary, Lawton writes. He also began reading about politics and economics.
He tried to join the board of a Calgary riding association, but was rebuffed because it was already full - so they signed him on as a fundraiser, and he would go on to impress with his ability to rake in cash.
He also, Lawton writes, showcased an interest in mastering procedural rules for debates at the Model United Nations, and teaching his team lessons about where to put their hands or how to use body language to make a point.
"Pierre hasn't changed," Lawton quotes one of his schoolmates - someone who works today on Parliament Hill though not for the Tories.
"I think some people think this is a persona, but he is identical to the Pierre I remember from the speech and debate club, model Parliament, and Speakers' Corner."
He's written a book
As an adult, he also once worked on a book - ahead of the 2021 campaign, Poilievre had also been writing something tentatively called "Debtonation," which Lawton describes as a "a meticulously researched, chart-heavy manuscript" meant to explore Canada's current fiscal situation and what needed to happen to - in Poilievre's view - fix it.
Lawton's book includes an excerpt from the never-published manuscript that references the same themes Poilievre hammers on now - removing the gatekeepers and giving people back their freedom.
Lawton also writes about the tensions between Poilievre and the media, quoting Byrne as saying whatever people may think it's not strategy but just "Pierre being Pierre."
But Lawton also quotes current House Leader and former Conservative leader Andrew Scheer as reflecting on why it should be a strategy: Scheer believes the media "concoct and contrive" narratives to hurt Conservatives and help Liberals.
"So at the very least, if we can show that bias to Canadians, then come campaign time, we'll have a fighting chance to get our message through."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and his wife Anaida walk from a news conference in Quebec City on Sept. 6, 2023.

Subject: Political leadership

Location: Portugal; Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: There's a new book about Pierre Poilievre. Here are five things we learned about the Conservative leader

Document 90 of 500

Bonnie Crombie asks RCMP for update on Greenbelt investigation of Ford government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-asks-rcmp-update-on-greenbelt/docview/3058772529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+asks+RCMP+for+update+on+Greenbelt+investigation+of+Ford+government&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is asking the RCMP for an update on its investigation into the Greenbelt scandal, saying one is needed given new information that has been uncovered.
In a letter sent to RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme and Assistant Commissioner Matt Peggs on Thursday, Crombie said, "I am asking that you provide an update on the progress of your investigation, in light of these most recent revelations," including those in a Star story about a senior staffer in Premier Doug Ford's office.
"Mr. Carlo Oliviero attended at least one meeting with a developer involved in the scandal," according to the Star, Crombie wrote.
"Further, records show that correspondence on this meeting was sent to Mr. Oliviero's personal email. This meeting calls into question the premier's assertion that his office was 'not involved in specific-site selection.'
"I urge you to closely review this new information, to assess whether further investigations are warranted into the conduct of Premier Ford and his staff and provide the public with a progress update on the current investigation."
Crombie also raises concerns about what she calls the "unacceptable revolving door between the premier's office and corporate lobbyists connected to the Greenbelt scandal. Mr. Oliviero, who we now know was directly involved, joins a growing list of registered lobbyists who appear to be using their close ties to the premier's office to advance private interests."
As reported by the Star's Sheila Wang and Noor Javed, Oliviero - who at the time worked for Ford - was invited to a meeting to discuss Greenbelt lands in Hamilton that were later released from their protected status. Oliviero has said that after the initial meeting, he "referred the issue to the appropriate ministry."
The meeting was uncovered in documents released by the province after a recent freedom of information request by the Ontario NDP about the $8.28 billion scandal, documents they say have raised even more questions about the involvement of the premier's office in removing land from the Greenbelt.
Ford's office has said it had no direct knowledge or role in the changes. The province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both said staff were not involved in site selection, but have said well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
Last October, the RCMP began a criminal investigation into the Greenbelt plan, but there have been no updates on it.
A spokesperson for Ford directed all inquiries to the RCMP.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to the 2024 Ontario budget at Queens Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

Document 91 of 500

City of Burlington asking province to deny quarry proposal before 2025 appeal hearing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-burlington-asking-province-deny-quarry/docview/3058772521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+of+Burlington+asking+province+to+deny+quarry+proposal+before+2025+appeal+hearing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two local environmental organizations are pleased with a City of Burlington resolution, passed unanimously at Tuesday's city council meeting, asking the Province of Ontario to deny a proposed quarry expansion before an appeal hearing next year and ensure long-term protection of Mount Nemo Plateau.
A 60-day Ontario Land Tribunal hearing of Nelson Aggregate's proposal to expand its existing quarry at Cedar Springs Road and No. 2 Side Road is scheduled for March 4 to May 30, 2025.
Burlington Ward 3 Coun, Rory Nisan, who moved the council resolution with Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, said the province "can save a ton of money" by rejecting the application and cancelling the scheduled tribunal hearing.
The city will ask the province to deny the application to expand the Mount Nemo open-pit quarry and establish long-term protection through redesignation of the Mount Nemo Plateau to "Escarpment Natural and Escarpment Protection."
The resolution will be forwarded to local members of provincial parliament, Premier Doug Ford, Attorney General Doug Downey, Minister of Environment, Conservation and Parks Andrea Khanjin, Minister of Natural Resources and Forestry Graydon Smith and OLT chair Michael Kraljevic.
In a Wednesday news release, the volunteer groups Conserving Our Rural Ecosystems Burlington (CORE) and Protecting Escarpment Rural Land (PERL) stated they are pleased the city is joining them in asking the province "to use common sense and stop this unwanted Nelson quarry application."
The release states the Niagara Escarpment Commission recommended the new protective Niagara Escarpment Plan designations be adopted in 2016. That came after a 2012 decision of the Consolidated Hearing Board to deny Nelson Aggregate its application to redesignate its Niagara Escarpment property to "Escarpment Extraction."
That quarry application was opposed by PERL, the City of Burlington, Halton Region, Conservation Halton and the Niagara Escarpment Commission.
"It is a complete waste of taxpayer funds to be reconsidering this application when it was denied in 2012," said Gord Pinard of CORE.
Roger Goulet, a director of PERL, stated in the release: "We are very happy to see the mayor and council unanimously pick up the torch from previous councils and staff, and work to secure the proper protections for the Mount Nemo plateau."
The Burlington council resolution also notes the 2012 denial and 2016 recommendation for redesignation of the area within the Niagara Escarpment Plan.
The resolution states Mount Nemo Plateau is located within Ontario's Greenbelt, is an integral part of the Niagara Escarpment UNESCO Biosphere Reserve and "sustains fragile and interconnected ecosystem featuring over 20 headwater tributaries, provincially significant wetlands, areas of natural and scientific interest, regionally significant woodland, and the significant habitat of rare and endangered species."
The resolution states the balance of the Mount Nemo Plateau is composed of prime agricultural land under threat of development for aggregate extraction now and in the future.
"The extraction of aggregate will permanently disrupt the fragile balance of ecology, natural habitat, water and woodland systems, rural and community land use of the Mount Nemo Plateau and supporting area," the resolution said. "The extraction of aggregate is not necessary in the region as the aggregate industry has the licensed capacity in Ontario to produce 13 times the annual average demand for aggregate in Ontario, with 22 active quarries already in operation in Halton Region."
Nelson Aggregate applied to Halton Region and the city in 2020 for regional and local official plan amendments permitting expansion of extraction areas of its Burlington quarry to the south and west. In 2022, the applicant appealed the lack of decision by both governments - putting both applications before the tribunal.
In October 2023, the City of Burlington announced it would oppose Nelson Aggregate's appeal at the tribunal hearing.
CAPTION: File photo of the Nelson Quarry site on No. 2 Sideroad in Burlington.

Document 92 of 500

Unpredictability has been the key to Doug Ford's popularity, but it won't carry him forever

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/unpredictability-has-been-key-doug-fords/docview/3058532346/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unpredictability+has+been+the+key+to+Doug+Ford%27s+popularity%2C+but+it+won%27t+carry+him+forever&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's enduring popularity is a peculiarity.
Despite the disdain of so many, Ontario's premier dominates the public opinion polls and defies expectations in byelections.
Why is Ford so strangely immune to the midterm decline so typical and predictable for any politician - let alone a leader savouring his second term in power? How does a premier who keeps losing face and executing about-faces still stay in the good graces of his supporters?
A survey published in the Star this week shows his Progressive Conservative government still well out in front. In fact, the numbers from Abacus Data show higher positive ratings for Ford than for his opposition rivals (albeit higher negatives, too).
Much of Ford's re-election victory in 2022 can surely be attributed to the ebbing popularity of his political rivals. Yet both opposition parties now have new, able leaders in place - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - with little apparent traction to date.
Beyond the numbers, consider the premier's latest byelection triumphs, in which the Tories won two out of two. By way of disclaimer, I'd normally ignore the polls or byelections in between Ontario's general elections, because they typically attract protest votes from people who want to punish the party in power without necessarily forcing a change in government.
What's more, both of this month's byelections could be seen as signs of decline in the ruling party, triggered by two cabinet ministers who bailed out early. Voters often punish parties for these early departures, but happily for the Tories, one of the two seats - vacated by Monte McNaughton, the former minister of labour - was in a relatively safe, largely rural stronghold beyond the grasp of the opposition.
But the race in Milton was too close for comfort. Parm Gill, the incumbent cabinet minister, bizarrely and unexpectedly defected to the federal Tories - word is that he feared losing his provincial seat in the next general election for 2026.
So how did Ford's Tories hold the riding against all odds?
Turns out that Milton's newest MPP is not just from the governing party. He is also new to the party - having defected from the Liberals before the byelection.
Zee Hamid ran federally for the Liberals a decade ago, has donated to the party ever since, and actively supported the former provincial leader, Steven Del Duca when he campaigned for the post in 2020. The Karachi-born tech entrepreneur, with degrees in math and computer sciences and a CV than includes Microsoft and Amazon, embodies the kind of business-savvy, multicultural candidate most parties covet in 905 ridings.
Why would he flip?
When I caught up with him at a Queen's Park event, mingling easily with MPPs from other parties, I asked him why he'd rather switch to Ford's Tories than fight for the Liberals he once loved. He chuckled and talked about feeling comfortable running for a Tory party with broad appeal, after serving as a councillor in Milton over the years.
That's an interesting admission and evolution (regardless of whether one believes Ford's claim that he too has "evolved"). As a recent graduate of municipal politics, Hamid understands how partisan affiliation remains in the background - a mindset that also animates much of Ford's politics at the provincial level, where the premier plays up his personal brand more than party loyalty.
The long-standing recipe for Ford's popularity is populism, not partisanship. That's why his loose rhetoric - like his undisciplined spending - is so often aimed at pleasing people, rather than appeasing policy experts; at short term tactics over long term strategy; at political positioning rather than dispassionate deliberating.
The impulse for quick fixes - and fast flips - can be seen in Ford's fondness for seductive slogans over enduring solutions. Hence his resort to privately run medical clinics as an antidote to hospital wait-lists, as if these pilot programs can somehow be scaled up quickly to save the system; similarly with Ford's Greenbelt fiasco, he pretended that lifting the protections on a few parcels of land would make a dent in a much bigger housing shortage that defies Band-Aids and bulldozers.
That's the problem with Ford's short-term outlook and midterm run of luck - long term, his luck is bound to run out when the bad news catches up to him. Hospital wait-lists, housing shortages, LRT delays, deficit growth - sometimes the stars are in alignment, but over time the bad news cumulatively comes together in a perfect storm.
That day may come after next year's federal election, if (as the polls suggest) Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre becomes prime minister - putting Ford under the gun should a new government in Ottawa misfire badly. Or Ford may yet dodge that bullet by burying the Ontario Tory brand, pivoting even more to the centre, and pushing back against Poilievre's anticipated antics.
In politics there is no predictability, just uncertainty. Which is why Ford keeps defying expectations.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative candidate Zee Hamid celebrates with Premier Doug Ford after winning a provincial byelection in Milton on May 2, 2024.

Subject: Partisanship; Politics; Elections

Title: Unpredictability has been the key to Doug Ford's popularity, but it won't carry him forever

Document 93 of 500

Woeful energy policies create a triple whammy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/woeful-energy-policies-create-triple-whammy/docview/3058532158/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Woeful+energy+policies+create+a+triple+whammy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fire smoke season 2024 has begun. Fort McMurray, Alta., was again evacuated because of the smoke and its proximity to a massive out-of-control fire. Oh, the irony - the fire is a threat to the oilsands.
Last summer was the hottest in 2,000 years. Yet the Ford government continues its wilful ignorance of climate change, recently with a $2.2-billion contract to build a new gas "peaker" plant in Napanee, and more recently with the passing of Bill 165 that locks in gas-powered new home builds, subsidized by the buyers.
Please take a minute to support the Ontario Clean Air Alliance with their request for a full value-for-money audit by Ontario's auditor general.
It's a triple whammy - increased emissions, wasted money, and wasted energy.
The Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory flow chart shows a whopping 67.5 per cent of the energy produced by burning natural gas, petroleum, coal, and biomass is wasted or rejected.
Ontario has devolved from the 2014 high when the Liberal government made Ontario the first North American jurisdiction to shut down coal plants, to a disgraceful tripling of power by natural gas by today's Conservative government. Do not be bamboozled by claims that natural gas is clean energy. It is mostly methane, which is a far more potent greenhouse gas than carbon dioxide.
We'd all love to have non-polluting, perpetual motion machines to power our world, but that defies the laws of physics - although there are some curious anomalies. The Oxford Electric Bell has rung almost continuously since 1840 powered by a Zamboni pile or electrostatic battery, and New Zealand's Beverly Clock has not been wound since 1864 and is powered by atmospheric pressure and temperature changes.
But we do have electric motors that, by all reports, are way more energy efficient than gas engines. Natural Resources Canada writes, "The efficiency of energy conversion from on-board storage to turning the wheels is nearly five times greater for electricity than gasoline, at approximately 76 per cent and 16 per cent, respectively."
Decarbonizing our buildings and vehicles requires new building codes such as the Toronto Green Standard, replacing gas heating and aging air conditioners with heat pumps that both heat and cool, and making it cheaper and faster to replace internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles with battery electric vehicles (BEVs). The long wait times for BEVs are unacceptable, as well as the higher initial cost. The reduced maintenance and energy costs to run BEVs mean nothing when people can't afford to switch.
We must push back against Ontario's regressive and expensive energy policies. It doesn't matter how big or small a part Ontario plays in the global emissions picture because every jurisdiction must decarbonize. If you're as unhappy as so many of us are with the direction Ontario has been going to address climate change, tell Doug Ford. He left me the following contact information on a recent pre-recorded voice message: 1-800-913-6453 and info@ontariopc.com.
Susan Koswan is a freelance contributing columnist for the Waterloo Region Record. Reach her at greyandstillgreen@gmail.com.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Susan Koswan OPINION Susan Koswan is a freelance contributing columnist for the Waterloo Region Record. Reach her at greyandstillgreen@gmail.com.;

Document 94 of 500

Five things you didn't know about Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/five-things-you-didnt-know-about-pierre-poilievre/docview/3058963171/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+things+you+didn%27t+know+about+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-"It's just Pierre being Pierre."
That's the answer many in Conservative circles give these days when they're quizzed about why party leader Pierre Poilievre does or says something that observers struggle to understand.
But over eight months and through more than 60 interviews, journalist Andrew Lawton sought to probe more into what that actually means: Who is Poilievre? What drives him?
"By explaining who Poilievre is, where he came from, and how he fits into the changing landscape of Canadian conservative politics, we get a sense of what the future may hold as his political life unfolds," Lawton writes in the introduction to "Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life", his newly-released book obtained ahead of its publication by the Star.
Here are some key takeaways from the first book-length look at a man polls suggest could be well on the road to becoming Canada's next prime minister.
Why is he sometimes called Jeff?
That Poilievre is the adopted son of two schoolteachers is well known; he references it in his regular rally speeches and elsewhere all the time.
Lawton reveals that one of the reasons Poilievre is sometimes referred to as "Jeff" is because that's the name his birth mother would have given him, if she'd raised him on her own. His adoptive parents also adopted his biological half-brother Patrick. The brothers forged a relationship with their biological mother when they were in their 20s.
His adoptive parents separated when he was 12, and he remains close to his mother Marlene, as well as his father Don and Don's long-time partner Ross McWhirter.
Lawton also shoots down a rumour that has circulated for years that Poilievre's father was sitting in the House of Commons when Poilievre voted against legalizing same-sex marriage and also for a broad reopening of the debate itself.
He eloped to Portugal
Poilievre grew up in Calgary and got involved in politics early - he was so involved that he ultimately dropped out of his bachelor's degree program at the University of Calgary in 2002 to take a job with then-foreign affairs critic Stockwell Day. He was three courses shy at the time, Lawton writes, and eventually took online courses through Athabasca University to finish off his degree in 2008.
His affection for his wife Anaida Poilievre - and the political force she is in her own right - also comes across at campaign rallies, but Lawton's book reveals the early days of their courtship.
She dodged him initially and was miffed he unilaterally set the terms for their very first date in 2012 - at a downtown Ottawa coffee shop Poilievre was able to have access to before it opened to the public. Their first date went so long Poilievre ended up with a parking ticket.
He told her he loved her after only two weeks of dating, and two weeks later wrote her a poem about their first month together. They moved in together shortly after, and would later elope to Portugal in 2017 to get married.
The duo have two children, and Lawton notes that their older daughter, Valentina, has been diagnosed with autism and is non-verbal. She receives therapy, though what kind and from who is not specified, but Lawton reports it has benefitted her.
Known as a prankster
Lawton reports Poilievre likes playing pranks on his own staff and other colleagues, often using foreign accents or mimicking other politicians.
He's posed as a foreign diplomat to call the office of then-Conservative Foreign Affairs Minister John Baird claiming Baird had promised something during a trip abroad and demanding he deliver.
Poilievre, Lawton writes, also once had a woman call his own office to pretend she was pregnant after a one-night stand with him and wanted to track him down to tell him about it.
Poilievre was in a relationship with longtime Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne at the time.
Byrne did speak to Lawton about their relationship for the book - the pair dated for 12 years - but declined to delve into it in detail. The relationship ended, she told Lawton, because they had simply grown apart.
Lawton also attempted to interview people who worked with Poilievre during his time as a junior cabinet minister, but many did not return his calls.
One unnamed source said Poilievre was "insufferable" as a minister, though has grown out of it, while others told Lawton that if people chafe under Poilievre's leadership, it means they simply aren't cut out to work on the Hill. He once told his staff "this is not a self-esteem factory," a phrase that was then turned into a poster and put on the wall of his office.
As party leader, Lawton reports "Poilievre and his team have run a tight ship, with high expectations for attendance and performance in question period. Non-essential absenteeism is forbidden," but Poilievre also ensures caucus has a chance to speak their mind behind closed doors so they have their say, Lawton writes.
He is often his own harshest critic; colleagues recount how he was "sullen and withdrawn" for some time after stepping on his government's 2008 apology for residential schools.
Where did he cut his political teeth?
Lawton traces a long arc for Poilievre's political ideas and ideals all the way back to his childhood - his first political outing, Lawton says was a provincial Progressive Conservative riding association meeting he attended with his mother.
"He enjoyed debate already and sometimes his teachers didn't like that very much," his mother Marlene told Lawton. "Some teachers told me he sat on the edge of his desk and couldn't wait to debate."
As a teen, among his go-to books? The dictionary, Lawton writes. He also began reading about politics and economics.
He tried to join the board of a Calgary riding association, but was rebuffed because it was already full - so they signed him on as a fundraiser, and he would go on to impress with his ability to rake in cash.
He also, Lawton writes, showcased an interest in mastering procedural rules for debates at the Model United Nations, and teaching his team lessons about where to put their hands or how to use body language to make a point.
"Pierre hasn't changed," Lawton quotes one of his schoolmates - someone who works today on Parliament Hill though not for the Tories.
"I think some people think this is a persona, but he is identical to the Pierre I remember from the speech and debate club, model Parliament, and Speakers' Corner."
He's written a book
As an adult, he also once worked on a book - ahead of the 2021 campaign, Poilievre had also been writing something tentatively called "Debtonation," which Lawton describes as a "a meticulously researched, chart-heavy manuscript" meant to explore Canada's current fiscal situation and what needed to happen to - in Poilievre's view - fix it.
Lawton's book includes an excerpt from the never-published manuscript that references the same themes Poilievre hammers on now - removing the gatekeepers and giving people back their freedom.
Lawton also writes about the tensions between Poilievre and the media, quoting Byrne as saying whatever people may think it's not strategy but just "Pierre being Pierre."
But Lawton also quotes current House Leader and former Conservative leader Andrew Scheer as reflecting on why it should be a strategy: Scheer believes the media "concoct and contrive" narratives to hurt Conservatives and help Liberals.
"So at the very least, if we can show that bias to Canadians, then come campaign time, we'll have a fighting chance to get our message through."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and his wife Anaida walk from a news conference in Quebec City on Sept. 6, 2023.

Subject: Political leadership

Location: Portugal; Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Five things you didn't know about Pierre Poilievre

Publication year: 2024

Document 95 of 500

Canadian history gets a tip of the hat as a struggling British prime minister calls an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-history-gets-tip-hat-as-struggling/docview/3058962927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+history+gets+a+tip+of+the+hat+as+a+struggling+British+prime+minister+calls+an+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tired Conservative party with an unelected leader who also happens to be a barrier-smashing political pioneer.
A right-wing Reform party threatening to siphon votes from the Tories in key constituencies around the country that could turn the election into a landslide for the main opposition party.
A grey and not particularly inspiring lawyer poised to become prime minister, returning his party to power after years on the opposition benches.
Sound familiar?
No, not Canada in 1993, but the United Kingdom in 2024.
British Prime Minister Rishi Sunak on Wednesday called a July 4 election, after months of speculation that has included a surprising amount of interest in the 1993 Canadian election that ushered out prime minister Kim Campbell's Tories and elected Jean Chrétien's Liberals.
That's because Sunak's Conservatives - in power for 14 years and five prime ministers, having presided over the economically disastrous Brexit that saw Britons vote to leave the European Union in a 2016 referendum - trail Sir Keir Starmer's Labour by more than 20 percentage points in every major public-opinion poll.
Andrew MacDougall, former director of communications to prime minister Stephen Harper and now a director of London-based Trafalgar Strategy, said the Canadian comparison is an imperfect one.
Even though it has been cited in Fleet Street newspapers and hit podcasts like The News Agents, which featured dramatic clips from the CBC's Oct. 25 1993 election special, MacDougall emphasized Wednesday that "it's oversold" in the UK.
"Everybody got a little excited about that parallel," he said in an interview from London, which was abuzz with election fever.
Like Campbell, the first (and still only) woman to be Canadian PM, Sunak, the first person of colour to govern Britain, made domestic political history after taking over from an unpopular and controversial predecessor.
But MacDougall stressed that Campbell inheriting Brian Mulroney's legacy of constitutional wrangling over the Meech Lake and Charlottetown Accords and imposing the Goods and Services Tax is not quite the same as Sunak taking the reins from Liz Truss, who served just seven weeks as British PM in 2022 after tanking the British economy with unfunded tax cut promises the markets strongly rejected.
Truss had briefly succeeded the scandal-plagued Boris Johnson, who had been ousted after Sunak and other cabinet members resigned en masse earlier that summer.
"It's different in the causes (of an anticipated Tory defeat), but the outcome could be similar (to 1993)," he said.
"Though it would be nothing like the two seats (Campbell's party won.) A terrible outcome here would be around 80 seats for the Tories of the 650 in the Commons."
Unlike the Canadian PCs - who lost votes nationwide to the breakaway Alberta-based Reform party, which helped Chrétien's Liberals win a stunning 98 of the 99 seats in Ontario and 31 of 32 in Atlantic Canada in 1993 - MacDougall notes Reform UK has no geographical stronghold.
"Canadian Reform had split away from the Progressive Conservatives," he said, pointing out Reform UK is a protest movement that probably won't even win a seat.
However one similarity is that Starmer, like Chrétien 31 years ago, does not get many voters excited.
A famed lawyer knighted by Queen Elizabeth II for his work as head of the Crown Prosecution Services and director of public prosecutions, the Labour leader is seen more as a safe set of hands than a dynamic agent of change.
"He's beige, he's dull, people just aren't that convinced about him," said MacDougall, joking the party leaders, including the Liberal Democrats' Ed Davey, will be like a group of "accountants debating on stage."
Indeed, the wealthy Sunak - his wife is the Infosys heiress Akshata Murty - also has the public image of a dreary technocrat.
But Mother Nature appears to be voting for Starmer's party - a miserable-looking Sunak was drenched by pelting rain as he stood outside 10 Downing Street to announce the election, his voice at times drowned out by a protester cranking out former Labour PM Tony Blair's campaign song, "Things Can Only Get Better."
"Things can only get wetter," quipped more than one British pundit.
MacDougall, a veteran of many elections and an expert in political communications, said it was a cringe-inducing launch.
"We're an hour into this campaign and he's already a meme. It's not a great look," he said.
"I mean, if the (Tory) premise is 'it's a dangerous world out there and only he can protect us,' mate, you can't even get an umbrella."
CAPTION: Britain's Prime Minister Rishi Sunak, soaked by rain, pauses as he delivers a speech to announce July 4 as the date of the UK's next general election, at 10 Downing Street in London, on May 22, 2024.

Ford government must provide better heat protections for workers, critics say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-must-provide-better-heat/docview/3057803918/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+must+provide+better+heat+protections+for+workers%2C+critics+say&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The early arrival of hot, sticky weather has the NDP and Ontario Federation of Labour pressing Premier Doug Ford's government for stronger "heat stress" protections for workers.
"Ontario summers are getting hotter and hotter leading to unsafe working conditions for many workers," New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) said Wednesday, noting the trend will worsen as climate change accelerates.
Tabuns and OFL president Laura Walton said improved protections are needed for both outdoor and indoor workers in jobs where they are subject to high temperatures, such as in schools without air conditioning or factories where air conditioning is not feasible.
Measures could include access to cooling rooms, cold water and shade, more breaks, and regulated or legislated standards for when workers are entitled to get out of the heat to avoid medical problems that can lead to serious health consequences, such as heat stroke and possibly death.
"This isn't something that can be put off," said Walton, whose federation last year called for heat stress to be recognized as a workplace hazard under the Occupational Health and Safety Act.
She and Tabuns said the Progressive Conservative government conducted a 30-day consultation on heat stress regulation with employers, unions and other stakeholders last August.
"Nothing's happened since," Tabuns said.
In a statement to the Star, the office of Labour Minister David Piccini did not explain why no major new policies on the issue have been developed in the nine months since the consultation.
The minister's spokeswoman pointed to voluntary guidelines issued to employers three years ago.
"Under the Occupational Health and Safety Act (OSHA), employers and supervisors have a duty to take every precaution reasonable in the circumstances for the protection of a worker, including protection in hot environments," said Zoe Knowles.
"The employer should have established a heat stress control plan for the summer to manage job tasks in high temperatures and humidity," she added.
"Any worker in Ontario who feels unsafe should report a violation for the ministry to investigate their complaint, and issue orders if necessary to ensure worker health and safety."
Walton said the government has missed "a really good opportunity over the winter to deal with a situation like this" and develop "real legislation that will protect workers."
In the consultation, the province proposed regulations to protect workers from heat-related illnesses that would require employers to comply with heat stress exposure limits for various types of jobs, from light workloads to heavy ones.
The plan was also to require employers to implement measures to control heat exposures and provide information to workers on how to recognize signs of heat-related health problems and illnesses.
Some unions have been able to negotiate heat stress protections with employers, such as cooling stations and more air-conditioned lunch rooms, said Walton.
Heat stress has garnered attention in recent weeks after Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signed a law preventing cities and counties in a southern state known for its hot, humid weather from enacting heat protections for outdoor workers.
California, Oregon and Washington have adopted requirements that employers provide shade and breaks for workers exposed to high temperatures.
CAPTION: NDP MPP Peter Tabuns is shown in the Ontario legislature on July 23, 2018.

Subject: Heat; Employers; Air conditioning; Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

Business indexing term: Subject: Employers Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

Document 97 of 500

Separation is back on the boil

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/separation-is-back-on-boil/docview/3057619116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Separation+is+back+on+the+boil&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not a topic of conversation in English Canada. Mention "separation" in a political context and, just like Google search results, the discussion is likely to be about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's split from his spouse Sophie Grégoire or, as the Edmonton Journal recently put it, Alberta's "slow-motion" separation from Canada. Quebec separation? Well, that matter was placed on the far back burner - you know, the one you forget about until it starts to burn?
Quebec may not be ignoring the federal carbon pricing law like Saskatchewan (it doesn't need to since it has its own cap-and-trade system) or threatening to pull out of the Canada Pension Plan like Alberta (Quebec already has its own pension plan), but unrest is brewing and it could play a crucial role in the next federal election.
Last month, the Parti Québécois's youthful and charismatic leader, Paul St-Pierre Plamondon, promised a third referendum on Quebec sovereignty before the end of the decade.
It's a real threat. With Premier François Legault's Coalition Avenir Quebec (CAQ) cratering in public opinion surveys, the Parti Québécois is poised to win a majority mandate when Quebecers next head to the polls in 2026.
In a fiery speech to party supporters in April, St-Pierre Plamondon - or "PSPP," as he is known in the province - laid out the case for Quebec sovereignty. He claimed Trudeau has encroached on provincial jurisdiction more than any other prime minister, and that he has refused to hand over immigration powers to the province in order to "destabilize Quebec," to "weaken us."
"It is a regime that will not change," PSPP told the crowd. "Either we remain a minority that keeps declining in Canada until we disappear, or we become the majority at home with a guarantee that our language and culture survives."
Outside of Quebec, this may seem like hyperbole. The Trudeau government has given child-care funding to Quebec with opt-out provisions and no strings attached. It has revamped language laws to protect French in the province that are so deferential to Quebec's own language laws that Montreal Liberal MP Anthony Housefather voted against them.
But there are real concerns, shared in newspapers, on the airwaves and at kitchen tables, that Canada's current trajectory of large population increases through immigration will inevitably lead to the decline of French and Quebec's influence in Canada.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, for example, could easily win (not that he wants to) a majority mandate without any seats in Quebec - despite the province being home to 22 per cent of Canada's population. Even with a grandfather clause that provides Quebec with seven extra seats in the House of Commons, its weight in the chamber will decline over the next decade after more seats were awarded to British Columbia, Alberta and Ontario to reflect their growing populations - populations whose second language isn't French.
The primary duty of Canada's prime minister is to keep its citizens safe and the country united. If the PQ continues to lead in the polls, voters will need to ask themselves which federal leader can best keep the country together.
Poilievre was part of a government that recognized the Québécois as a nation within a united Canada. His Conservatives are less likely to trample on provincial jurisdiction with new social programs than the Liberals or the NDP.
But will Quebecers see themselves reflected in a Conservative leader whose list of spending priorities ("roads, bridges, police, military, border security and a safety net for those who can't provide for themselves") makes no mention of culture?
Quebec remains the only province where the federal Liberals aren't currently faring too badly in public support - they closely trail the Bloc Québécois in second place. Quebec, according to a recent Leger survey, is the only province where voters believe Trudeau would be the best prime minister.
As a francophone Montreal MP, does Trudeau maintain home-field advantage? Do Liberal values best reflect Quebecers' values? Is Trudeau too toxic outside of Quebec to make the case for Canada?
These are questions that all Canadian voters - not just Quebecers - would do well to contemplate.
Altha Raj is a national columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION Altha Raj is a national columnist with Torstar.;

Document 98 of 500

Ford's Tories dip slightly but still lead Liberals and NDP, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-tories-dip-slightly-still-lead-liberals-ndp/docview/3058298671/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Tories+dip+slightly+but+still+lead+Liberals+and+NDP%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have dipped slightly in the latest Abacus Data poll, but still enjoy a healthy lead over the Liberals and New Democrats.
In the Abacus monthly tracking survey for the Star, Ford's Tories were at 39 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and nine per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
"The new normal is they're hovering around the high 30s and low 40s," Abacus president David Coletto said Tuesday.
Last month, the Tories were at 41 per cent, the Liberals 25 per cent, the NDP 21 per cent and Greens seven per cent.
"Ford's ability to deflect and to dodge and to change positions so easily is allowing him to continue to do well despite what other incumbent governments are facing," said Coletto, referring to recent polling results for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals who trail Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives.
Unlike some other politicians who are pilloried for policy U-turns, voters appear to give Ford a pass when he alters course - as was the case with the aborted Greenbelt land swap fiasco now being investigated by the RCMP.
"That tag doesn't stick to him. It's not seen as flip-flopping, it's seen as being responsive."
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from May 10 until last Wednesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll found a jump in the undecided vote.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 28 per cent of poll respondents said Tory, 19 per cent Liberal, 16 per cent NDP, seven per cent Green, three per cent another party while 28 per cent were undecided.
That compares with an undecided vote of 21 per cent last month.
"It shows a lot of people in the province aren't thinking that much about who their preference is," the pollster said.
Coletto pointed out that the governing party's byelection wins in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex on May 2 support the poll's findings.
"The byelection results took all of the air out of any momentum Bonnie Crombie may have had after the (Dec. 2 Liberal) leadership," he said.
An onslaught of PC attack ads criticizing Crombie and burnishing Ford also appears to be helping the Tories and hurting the Liberals.
Asked for their impressions of the four party leaders, 31 per cent of respondents said they had a positive impression of Ford while 39 per cent had a negative impression with 24 per cent neutral and five per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus eight per cent.
Crombie was 27 per cent positive, 27 per cent negative, 27 per cent neutral and 10 per cent unsure for a zero net rating.
Stiles had a 27 per cent positive, 21 per cent negative, 31 per cent neutral and 22 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus six per cent.
Schreiner had a 23 per cent positive, 18 per cent negative, 35 per cent neutral and 24 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus five per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to press at the Ontario legislature in Toronto, on May 6, 2024.

Conservatives, BQ demand resignation of House Speaker over 'partisan behaviour'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/conservatives-bq-demand-resignation-house-speaker/docview/3057221128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservatives%2C+BQ+demand+resignation+of+House+Speaker+over+%27partisan+behaviour%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Opposition Conservatives and the Bloc Québécois are renewing calls for Liberal MP Greg Fergus to resign as Speaker of the House of Commons, alleging he has engaged in a pattern of partisan behaviour that taints his decisions as the arbiter of debate in Canada's seat of democracy.
Chris Warkentin, a Conservative MP from Alberta, rose in the House on Tuesday morning to raise concerns over the online description of a partisan Liberal event Fergus is slated to host on June 4 near Parliament Hill.
Alain Therrien, the Bloc's House Leader, echoed Warkentin in arguing Fergus "doesn't understand" how to keep out of the partisan fray, and that he should resign as Speaker.
The original notice advertising the event included allegations about Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's "reckless policies," which Warkentin argued were inappropriate in light of how Fergus recently ejected Poilievre from the House for called Prime Minister Justin Trudeau a "wacko" and refusing to unequivocally withdraw the comment.
As of late Tuesday morning, the Liberal party's website was still advertising the event, but without the partisan language that Warkentin had quoted.
In an email to the Star, Fergus's office said it didn't know the Liberal party had advertised the event using "generic text" that had not been approved by the Speaker's team. His office also consulted the Clerk of the House of Commons about the event, and the clerk raised "no concerns" since it's within the rules for the Hull-Aylmer MP riding to organize events to help him get re-elected, wrote Mathieu Gravel, a spokesperson in Fergus's office.
The Speaker's team asked the Liberal party to remove the post, Gravel said, while Fergus's riding association used non-partisan language in its own notice to invite his constituents to the "community BBQ," Gravel said.
Asked about the issue on Tuesday, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said the Liberal government still has confidence in Fergus as Speaker.
But for the Conservatives, it was another troubling example of what they allege is Fergus's inappropriate partisanship.
In a letter to Fergus dated Tuesday, Warkentin said it was just the latest example of Fergus's questionable "partisan behaviour," which also included a tribute video in which he appeared in his Speaker's robes and praised the outgoing interim leader of the Ontario Liberals. That incident sparked calls for his resignation last year, with the Conservatives and Bloc Québécois declaring they no longer had confidence in his ability to serve as Speaker.
Fergus apologized for the video and repaid the parliamentary resources used to make it.
Warkentin argued Tuesday that the latest event advertisement provides another reason why Fergus should step down, and he called on the NDP to reverse its support for Fergus and join the Tories and BQ in declaring non-confidence in him if he does not resign. He cited House of Commons rules that say the Speaker should abstain from "all partisan political activity" and be careful, when running for re-election under a party banner, to avoid statements that "might prejudice their perceived impartiality.
"A Speaker cannot credibly claim to be objectively policing debate in the House of Commons by day while holding partisan attack rallies against the Leader of the Opposition by night," said the letter, which was obtained by the Star.
"Every ruling you give now - and, with hindsight, every ruling you have given - will now all be assumed to have been delivered with a red tint."
Heather McPherson, an NDP MP from Edmonton, said her party is indeed "very concerned" with the situation, but that it would "reserve" judgment "until some point in the future.
"It's important for us to take the time to look at this very clearly," she said.
Therrien, the Bloc House Leader, told the chamber Tuesday that debates are becoming more "acrimonious," meaning the House needs a Speaker in whom all parties can have confidence.
He called it "bad casting" to put Fergus - with his years of devoted Liberal partisanship - in the Speaker's chair.
"We must understand that he doesn't have his place on that seat," Therrien said in French.
With files from Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: Speaker Greg Fergus is shown in the House of Commons on April 17, 2024.

Should Hamilton reverse decision to push for drug decriminalization?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/should-hamilton-reverse-decision-push-drug/docview/3056207418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+Hamilton+reverse+decision+to+push+for+drug+decriminalization%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nearly two years after Hamilton city councillors voted to ask for an exemption in law to decriminalize small amounts of illicit drugs for personal use, the request has never been made to Health Canada.
"It is a little bit unusual that directions pass and it's not actioned right away," said Coun. John-Paul Danko. "In hindsight, I think perhaps that's fortunate because there's been quite a few developments on the issue of decriminalization since then."
Danko was one of 10 councillors who voted at a board of health meeting on Aug. 10, 2022 to ask the federal government to remove criminal penalties for the possession of 2.5 grams or less of certain illicit substances for personal use. Instead of being arrested, charged or having their drugs seized, adults would be given information and help with referral for health and social supports.
While Danko was in favour at that time of Hamilton joining the decriminalization experiment launching in British Columbia in January 2023, he says he'd no longer vote for it now.
"I certainly wouldn't support it," said Danko. "It's pretty clear from what we've seen out of B.C. and also out of the U.S. jurisdictions that it's been an abject failure and not something that we should aspire to in Hamilton."
B.C. has reversed course after just 16 months of what was supposed to be a three-year pilot project amid concern for community safety and a record number of drug-related deaths in 2023. The federal government approved the province's request on May 7 to exempt public spaces from the decriminalization policy, so police have the authority to seize drugs and make arrests again.
In addition, Danko looks warily at Toronto, which is pushing ahead with its request for a decriminalization exemption despite staunch opposition from Ontario's Progressive Conservative government.
The warnings from Premier Doug Ford's government against decriminalization have been enough to make Danko question whether it's time to bring this matter before Hamilton city councillors again.
"I just saw the reaction from the Ford government to Toronto's request and I was considering whether it would be worthwhile bringing forward reconsideration on our request," said Danko. "Obviously, we would have the same reaction that the Ford government is having to the City of Toronto."
Hamilton Public Health Services is also "continuing to monitor the changing political landscape around decriminalization," epidemiology and wellness director Julie Prieto said in a statement.
She explained that the two-year delay in making the request is related to the fact that Health Canada requires the city to conduct extensive analysis and consultation to make an application for decriminalization. Public health must provide an examination of the local context, potential benefits and risks, local addiction support services and different options for a specific decriminalization approach, including evidence.
Consultation also must be done with local organizations, a variety of police forces, health care providers, mental health and substance use experts, people with lived experiences and a broad range of Hamiltonians.
Lastly, the application requires a robust evaluation plan.
"Undertaking this work requires significant staff time and resources," Prieto said.
Discussions with the federal government and other municipalities have taken place and some of the analysis is underway. But ultimately it was decided that the development and implementation of the Hamilton Opioid Action Plan that was approved in June 2023 would take priority instead.
"The opioid crisis and increasingly toxic drug supply was the most pressing issue," Prieto said.
An update on the action plan is expected be presented in late spring or early summer to the city's public health committee (which was previously the board of health).
There has been a municipal election since the vote to join the decriminalization experiment, so the makeup of the committee has significantly changed. At that time, just two councillors voted against decriminalization, but Danko questions whether more would vote 'no' now.
"The fact is this is not an issue that we can address as a municipality," said Danko. "Hamilton, like every city in Canada, desperately needs more provincial support."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com

The toxic drug supply has been a pressing issue for public health, said epidemiology and wellness director Julie Prieto.SPC-0208-DRUG-STRATEGY Harm-reduction Uploaded by: Percy-Beauregard, Celeste

Document 101 of 500

EV transition stalls despite government mandates and billion-dollar handouts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ev-transition-stalls-despite-government-mandates/docview/3056207301/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EV+transition+stalls+despite+government+mandates+and+billion-dollar+handouts&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Both Canada and the United States have set ambitious mandates to accelerate the transition from combustion vehicles to zero-emission vehicles.
According to the Trudeau government, all new passenger vehicles and light trucks sold in Canada must be zero-emission vehicles by 2035, with interim targets of 20 per cent by 2026 and 60 per cent by 2030. Similarly, the Biden administration has mandated that two-thirds of new vehicles sold in the U.S. must be electric by 2032.
But despite massive taxpayer-funded subsidies for the electric vehicle (EV) sector, storm clouds are growing for the industry.
In April, Tesla laid off 10 per cent of its global workforce as it grapples with slow EV demand and falling sales. Similarly, Ford recently announced it would delay the start of EV production at the Oakville, Ont., plant by two years to let the consumer market develop and allow for further development of EV battery technology. Car rental giant Hertz earlier this year announced plans to sell one-third of its U.S. electric vehicle fleet and reinvest in gas-powered cars due to high repair costs and weak demand for its battery-powered cars. General Motors has abandoned the goal of producing 400,000 EVs by mid-2024 due to lower-than-expected sales.
The sluggish demand for EVs and the response from automakers should raise red flags for both the Trudeau government and Biden administration, given the massive subsidies (a.k.a. corporate welfare) injected into the EV and battery production industry. For instance, in Ontario, the Trudeau government and the Ford government have given $28.2 billion to the Stellantis EV battery plant in Windsor and the Volkswagen plant in St. Thomas. According to the Parliamentary Budget Officer, it will take 20 years for the federal and Ontario governments to break even on the $28 billion pledged for those two plants. And this doesn't include the $5-billion subsidy to Honda for a new EV manufacturing plant in the province.
Similarly, in Quebec, federal and provincial governments have pledged to spend $2.7 billion in subsidies for a new EV battery manufacturing plant and give $644 million to help Ford build a plant to produce EV battery materials.
But in reality, the EV transition faces major hurdles despite the massive amounts of taxpayer money being thrown at the industry.
Firstly, we lack adequate power grid infrastructure to meet the electricity demands of EV mandates. According to a recent study, meeting Canada's EV mandate by 2035 could increase electricity demand by up to 15.3 per cent nationwide, necessitating substantial investments in new generation capacity and transmission infrastructure. Specifically, Canada would need to construct 10 new mega hydroelectric dams, comparable to British Columbia's Site C, or alternatively, 13 new gas plants of 500-megawatt (MW) capacity to accommodate the surge in electricity demand from EVs.
Yet the timelines and costs associated with such projects are daunting. Drawing from recent experience with B.C.'s Site C dam, it took more than a decade to plan and comply with environmental regulations and approximately another decade to construct. To date, Site C, which remains under construction, is expected to cost $16 billion.
Secondly, there's a shortage of mineral supply for EV batteries, with projections indicating the need for numerous new mines to meet EV adoption mandates. According to a recent study, to meet international EV adoption mandates (including mandates in Canada and the U.S.) by 2030, the world would need 50 new lithium mines, 60 new nickel mines, 17 new cobalt mines, 50 new mines for cathode production, 40 new mines for anode materials, 90 new mines for battery cells, and 81 new mines for EV bodies and motors, for a total of 388 new mines worldwide. For context, in 2021 there were only 340 metal mines operating in Canada and the U.S.
Historically, the development of mining and refining facilities has been sluggish. Production timelines range from six to nine years for lithium and 13 to 18 years for nickel - two elements critical for EV batteries. The aggressive government timelines for EV adoption clash with historically sluggish metal and mineral production, raising the risk of EV manufacturers falling short of needed minerals.
The EV transition faces major obstacles, and the recent scaling back or delays in EV production by automakers should serve as a warning to governments about the feasibility of their forced transition policies, which clearly put Canadian taxpayers at risk.
CAPTION: Koshiro Kudo, president and representative director of Asahi Kasei Corporation, speaks Tuesday as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford announced Asahi Kasei's investment of about $1.6 billion to build an EV battery separator plant in Port Colborne, Ont.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, Honda executive Toshihiro Mibe and Premier Doug Ford walk along an assembly line at an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, Ont. Despite massive taxpayer-funded subsidies for the electric vehicle (EV) sector, storm clouds are growing for the industry.
CREDIT: Elmira Aliakbari and Julio Mejia

Document 102 of 500

Can you be conservative and 'pro-labour'? Pierre Poilievre takes a page from Doug Ford's playbook

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/can-you-be-conservative-pro-labour-pierre/docview/3056207300/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+you+be+conservative+and+%27pro-labour%27%3F+Pierre+Poilievre+takes+a+page+from+Doug+Ford%27s+playbook&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tories have traditionally been the party of big business with a simple subtext: labour be damned.
Today, it's not so simple. Right-wing politicians who once bashed union leaders as "bosses" are rebranding themselves as friends of the working man.
Doug Ford famously reinvented himself as the premier of the proletariat after taking power in 2018, winning the loyalty of major construction unions that had long opposed Ontario's Progressive Conservatives.
Now, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is taking a page from Ford Nation's gains in Ontario, quietly repositioning himself as a supposedly pro-labour politician ahead of the next election due in 2025.
Yes, you heard right. If you think right-wing politicians don't know the playbook for pivoting - ideologically, politically, economically - you're wrong.
Ford did it by cosying up to key labour leaders with a major charm offensive and a modest boost in the minimum wage - after first freezing the hourly rate for years. Swallowing his visceral disdain, Ford has been steadily eating the lunch of rival New Democrats and Liberals in lunch-bucket territory.
How can Poilievre repeat that feat? No, not a charm offensive - not in his nature.
Just watch him.
"Conservatives are 100 per cent onside with workers - union and non-union - who are fighting for pay hikes," he said earnestly late last year.
Poilievre startled his critics last February by supporting an "anti-scab" bill that bans the use of replacement workers during labour disputes in federally regulated workplaces, put forward by the Liberal government at the behest of the NDP in a minority Parliament.
No one saw that one coming. Not from a Tory party leader who made his name bashing unions while promoting a controversial U.S.-style "right-to-work" law long before he became party leader.
Labour leaders have seen this movie before - a political conversion on the road to the campaign trail. As Poilievre tries to rewrite the script, his union adversaries want people to hear the full story.
The Canadian Labour Congress has launched a campaign to hold politicians to account for their latest words and past actions. Top of the list for CLC president Bea Bruske are the federal and Ontario Tory leaders.
"I look at some of the pretty words ... that politicians like Ford and his party have used - saying they stand up for workers - but then when you peel back the layers of that onion and you look at the policies, they don't align," Bruske told me. "We see that the pretty words ... don't match the history in the Doug Ford situation."
She points to Ford's controversial plan to override collective bargaining rights by invoking the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" against striking CUPE workers in late 2022, triggering threats of a major labour confrontation until he quickly backed down.
Yet as Bruske readily conceded in our interview, Ford's contradictions have not yet cut into his strong voter support after nearly six years in power, according to the latest polling. Poilievre's federal Conservatives are enjoying stratospheric levels of support.
How to explain? Bruske says the CLC wants to better explain Poilievre's past record of union-bashing - and why shouldn't be airbrushed.
"Workers are busy ... they're not reading the newspaper," she argues. "So it's going to be up to labour to remind folks of what that voting record means."
Poilievre's track record is a matter of public record. As he told the Star more than a decade ago, he supported "workers freedom" that makes membership optional even when a union local has negotiated contract increases for everyone's benefit (so-called free riders).
"I believe in free choice for workers and I am going to do my part to see that happens at the federal level," he vowed. "Over time, I believe I can convince people of its merits. And hope springs eternal that one day we will have free choice for workers in Canada."
Fighting words, way back then. All these years later, never mind.
That said, Ontario's Tories embraced a similar "right-to-work" ideology in opposition, led by MPP Monte McNaughton. But when he became Ford's labour minister, McNaughton pivoted adroitly, shedding the anti-bosses baggage and dropping the minimum wage freeze.
Alongside McNaughton, Ford showed up at pre-election photo-ops with leaders of the construction trades, and even coaxed the then-leaders of Ontario's biggest unions - Unifor and OPSEU - to appear alongside the premier. It was a remarkable reinvention, obscuring the fact that Ford had axed many of the labour reforms enacted by the previous Liberal government (notably on legislated sick days).
Bruske says labour leaders and voters must now be vigilant about Poilievre's makeover. She points to a new poll for the CLC by GQR Canada showing nearly two-thirds of Canadians are pro-union, and 70 per cent want governments to lower barriers to joining unions.
Perhaps Poilievre can read those polls. Or perhaps he can just pivot.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre delivers a speech at a Canada's Building Trades Unions conference in Gatineau, Que. onApril 30, 2024.
