Document 103 of 500

If you thought Canada was over its separation anxiety, there's an unpleasant surprise brewing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-you-thought-canada-was-over-separation-anxiety/docview/3056207295/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+you+thought+Canada+was+over+its+separation+anxiety%2C+there%27s+an+unpleasant+surprise+brewing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not a topic of conversation in English Canada. Mention "separation" in a political context and, just like Google search results, the discussion is likely to be about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's split from his spouse Sophie Grégoire or, as the Edmonton Journal recently put it, Alberta's "slow-motion" separation from Canada. Quebec separation? Well, that matter was placed on the far back burner - you know, the one you forget about until it starts to burn?
Quebec may not be ignoring the federal carbon pricing law like Saskatchewan (it doesn't need to since it has its own cap-and-trade system) or threatening to pull out of the Canada Pension Plan like Alberta (Quebec already has its own pension plan), but unrest is brewing in the province and it could play a crucial role in the next federal election.
Last month, the Parti Québécois's youthful and charismatic leader, Paul St-Pierre Plamondon, promised a third referendum on Quebec sovereignty before the end of the decade.
It's a real threat. With Premier François Legault's Coalition Avenir Québec (CAQ) cratering in public opinion surveys, the Parti Québécois is poised to win a majority mandate - a feat last accomplished in 1998 - when Quebecers next head to the polls in 2026.
In a fiery speech to party supporters in April, St-Pierre Plamondon - or "PSPP," as he is known in the province - laid out the case for Quebec sovereignty. He claimed Trudeau had encroached on provincial jurisdiction more than any other prime minister, and that he has refused to hand over immigration powers to the province in order to "destabilize Quebec," to "weaken us."
"It is a regime that will not change," PSPP told the crowd. "Either we remain a minority that keeps declining in Canada until we disappear, or we become the majority at home with a guarantee that our language and culture survives."
Outside of Quebec, this may seem like hyperbole. The Trudeau government has given child-care funding to Quebec with opt-out provisions and no strings attached. It has revamped language laws to protect French in the province that are so deferential to Quebec's own language laws that Montreal Liberal MP Anthony Housefather voted against them.
But there are real concerns, shared in newspapers, on the airwaves and at kitchen tables, that Canada's current trajectory of large population increases through immigration will inevitably lead to the decline of French and Quebec's influence in Canada.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, for example, could easily win (not that he wants to) a majority mandate without any seats in Quebec - despite the province being home to 22 per cent of Canada's population. Even with a grandfather clause that provides Quebec with seven extra seats in the House of Commons, its weight in the chamber will decline over the next decade after more seats were awarded to British Columbia, Alberta and Ontario to reflect their growing populations - populations whose second language isn't French.
The primary duty of Canada's prime minister is to keep its citizens safe and the country united. If the PQ continues to lead in the polls, voters will need to ask themselves which federal leader can best keep the country together. Who can win the hearts and minds of Quebecers, making the case that their interests and aspirations are better served within Canada than outside of it?
Poilievre was part of a government that recognized the Québécois as a nation within a united Canada. His Conservatives are less likely to trample on provincial jurisdiction with new social programs than the Liberals or the NDP. But will Quebecers see themselves reflected in a Conservative leader whose list of spending priorities ("roads, bridges, police, military, border security and a safety net for those who can't provide for themselves") makes no mention of culture? Will environmentally conscious Quebecers see themselves in a leader whose priorities include building more pipelines and green-lighting big oil and gas projects? Is that the Canada they want to be a part of?
Unfortunately for the NDP, Quebecers have not warmed to Jagmeet Singh. Since 2019, the party has held just one seat in the province, making it hard to see Singh take on the role of Captain Canada.
Quebec remains the only province where the federal Liberals aren't currently faring too badly in public support - they closely trail the Bloc Québécois in second place. Quebec, according to a recent Leger survey, is the only province where voters believe Trudeau would be the best prime minister. Despite this, PSPP's criticisms stand, and a recent incident involving a Liberal Franco-Ontarian MP telling a Quebec witness at a Commons committee that he was "full of s - t" did the Liberals no favours.
As a francophone Montreal MP, does Trudeau maintain home-field advantage? Do Liberal values best reflect Quebecers' values? Is Trudeau too toxic outside of Quebec to make the case for Canada? And if Trudeau goes, will the next Liberal leader be chosen based at least in part on their appeal to Quebecers?
These are questions that all Canadian voters - not just Quebecers - would do well to contemplate.
CAPTION: Parti Quebecois Leader Paul St-Pierre Plamondon speaks at the opening of a Parti Quebecois national council meeting in Drummondville, Que. on April 13, 2024.

Subject: Jurisdiction; Voters; Prime ministers; Public opinion surveys; Immigration; Sovereignty; Pension plans

Business indexing term: Subject: Public opinion surveys Pension plans

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

Company / organization: Name: Parti Quebecois; NAICS: 813940

Document 104 of 500

Should city change course on drug decriminalization?: Councillor questions Hamilton's stance in light of issues faced in B.C. and U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/should-city-change-course-on-drug/docview/3056155965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+city+change+course+on+drug+decriminalization%3F%3A+Councillor+questions+Hamilton%27s+stance+in+light+of+issues+faced+in+B.C.+and+U.S.&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nearly two years after Hamilton city councillors voted to ask for an exemption in law to decriminalize small amounts of illicit drugs for personal use, the request has never been made to Health Canada.
"It is a little bit unusual that directions pass and it's not actioned right away," said Coun. John-Paul Danko. "In hindsight, I think perhaps that's fortunate because there's been quite a few developments on the issue of decriminalization since then."
Danko was one of 10 councillors who voted at a board of health meeting on Aug. 10, 2022, to ask the federal government to remove criminal penalties for the possession of 2.5 grams or less of certain illicit substances for personal use. Instead of being arrested, charged or having their drugs seized, adults would be given information and help with referral for health and social supports.
While Danko was in favour at that time of Hamilton joining the decriminalization experiment launching in British Columbia in January 2023, he says he'd no longer vote for it now.
"I certainly wouldn't support it," said Danko. "It's pretty clear from what we've seen out of B.C. and also out of the U.S. jurisdictions that it's been an abject failure and not something that we should aspire to in Hamilton."
B.C. has reversed course after just 16 months of what was supposed to be a three-year pilot project amid concern for community safety and a record number of drug-related deaths in 2023. The federal government approved the province's request on May 7 to exempt public spaces from the decriminalization policy, so police have the authority to seize drugs and make arrests again.
In addition, Danko looks warily at Toronto, which is pushing ahead with its request for a decriminalization exemption despite staunch opposition from Ontario's Progressive Conservative government.
The warnings from Premier Doug Ford's government against decriminalization have been enough to make Danko question whether it's time to bring this matter before Hamilton city councillors again.
"I just saw the reaction from the Ford government to Toronto's request and I was considering whether it would be worthwhile bringing forward reconsideration on our request," said Danko. "Obviously, we would have the same reaction that the Ford government is having to the City of Toronto."
Hamilton Public Health Services is also "continuing to monitor the changing political landscape around decriminalization," epidemiology and wellness director Julie Prieto said in a statement.
She explained that the two-year delay in making the request is related to the fact that Health Canada requires the city to conduct extensive analysis and consultation to make an application for decriminalization. Public health must provide an examination of the local context, potential benefits and risks, local addiction support services and different options for a specific decriminalization approach, including evidence.
Consultation also must be done with local organizations, a variety of police forces, health-care providers, mental health and substance use experts, people with lived experiences and a broad range of Hamiltonians.
Lastly, the application requires a robust evaluation plan.
"Undertaking this work requires significant staff time and resources," Prieto said.
Discussions with the federal government and other municipalities have taken place and some of the analysis is underway. But ultimately it was decided that the development and implementation of the Hamilton Opioid Action Plan that was approved in June 2023 would take priority instead.
"The opioid crisis and increasingly toxic drug supply was the most pressing issue," Prieto said.
An update on the action plan is expected be presented in late spring or early summer to the city's public health committee (which was previously the board of health).
There has been a municipal election since the vote to join the decriminalization experiment, so the makeup of the committee has significantly changed. At that time, just two councillors voted against decriminalization, but Danko questions whether more would vote 'no' now.
"The fact is this is not an issue that we can address as a municipality," said Danko. "Hamilton, like every city in Canada, desperately needs more provincial support."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. John Paul Danko no longer supports Hamilton asking to be a part of an illicit drug decriminalization experiment.A harm reduction kit available from Hamilton Public Health. The toxic drug supply has been a pressing issue for public health, said epidemiology and wellness director Julie Prieto.Hamilton Spectator file photoJohn Rennison The Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 105 of 500

EV transition faces major obstacles

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ev-transition-faces-major-obstacles/docview/3056155821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EV+transition+faces+major+obstacles&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Both Canada and the United States have set ambitious mandates to accelerate the transition from combustion vehicles to zero-emission vehicles.
According to the Trudeau government, all new passenger vehicles and light trucks sold in Canada must be zero-emission vehicles by 2035, with interim targets of 20 per cent by 2026 and 60 per cent by 2030. Similarly, the Biden administration has mandated that two-thirds of new vehicles sold in the U.S. must be electric by 2032.
But despite massive taxpayer-funded subsidies for the electric vehicle (EV) sector, storm clouds are growing for the industry.
In April, Tesla laid off 10 per cent of its global workforce as it grapples with slow EV demand and falling sales. Similarly, Ford recently announced it would delay the start of EV production at the Oakville, Ont., plant by two years to let the consumer market develop and allow for further development of EV battery technology.
Car rental giant Hertz earlier this year announced plans to sell one-third of its U.S. electric vehicle fleet and reinvest in gas-powered cars due to high repair costs and weak demand for its battery-powered cars. General Motors has abandoned the goal of producing 400,000 EVs by mid-2024 due to lower-than-expected sales.
The sluggish demand for EVs and the response from automakers should raise red flags for both the Trudeau government and Biden administration, given the massive subsidies (a.k.a. corporate welfare) injected into the EV and battery production industry. For instance, in Ontario, the Trudeau government and the Ford government have given $28.2 billion to the Stellantis EV battery plant in Windsor and the Volkswagen plant in St. Thomas.
According to the Parliamentary Budget Officer, it will take 20 years for the federal and Ontario governments to break even on the $28 billion pledged for those two plants. And this doesn't include the $5-billion subsidy to Honda for a new EV manufacturing plant in the province.
Similarly, in Quebec, federal and provincial governments have pledged to spend $2.7 billion in subsidies for a new EV battery manufacturing plant and give $644 million to help Ford build a plant to produce EV battery materials.
But in reality, the EV transition faces major hurdles despite the massive amounts of taxpayer money being thrown at the industry.
Firstly, we lack adequate power grid infrastructure to meet the electricity demands of EV mandates. According to a recent study, meeting Canada's EV mandate by 2035 could increase electricity demand by up to 15.3 per cent nationwide, necessitating substantial investments in new generation capacity and transmission infrastructure.
Specifically, Canada would need to construct 10 new mega hydroelectric dams, comparable to British Columbia's Site C, or alternatively, 13 new gas plants of 500-megawatt (MW) capacity to accommodate the surge in electricity demand from EVs.
Yet the timelines and costs associated with such projects are daunting. Drawing from recent experience with B.C.'s Site C dam, it took more than a decade to plan and comply with environmental regulations and approximately another decade to construct. To date, Site C, which remains under construction, is expected to cost $16 billion.
Secondly, there's a shortage of mineral supply for EV batteries, with projections indicating the need for numerous new mines to meet EV adoption mandates.
According to a recent study, to meet international EV adoption mandates (including mandates in Canada and the U.S.) by 2030, the world would need 50 new lithium mines, 60 new nickel mines, 17 new cobalt mines, 50 new mines for cathode production, 40 new mines for anode materials, 90 new mines for battery cells, and 81 new mines for EV bodies and motors, for a total of 388 new mines worldwide. For context, in 2021 there were only 340 metal mines operating in Canada and the U.S.
Historically, the development of mining and refining facilities has been sluggish. Production timelines range from six to nine years for lithium and 13 to 18 years for nickel - two elements critical for EV batteries. The aggressive government timelines for EV adoption clash with historically sluggish metal and mineral production, raising the risk of EV manufacturers falling short of needed minerals.
The EV transition faces major obstacles, and the recent scaling back or delays in EV production by automakers should serve as a warning to governments about the feasibility of their forced transition policies, which clearly put Canadian taxpayers at risk.
Elmira Aliakbari and Julio Mejia are analysts at the Fraser
Institute.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, Honda executive Toshihiro Mibe and Premier Doug Ford announce an electric vehicle battery plant for Alliston, Ont. Despite massive taxpayer-funded subsidies for the electric vehicle sector, storm clouds are growing for the industry, Elmira Aliakbari and Julio Mejia write.Nathan Denette The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Elmira Aliakbari and Julio Mejia;

Ford government issues 'enhanced' minister's zoning order to fast-track Ontario Place development

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-issues-enhanced-ministers-zoning/docview/3056241714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+issues+%27enhanced%27+minister%27s+zoning+order+to+fast-track+Ontario+Place+development&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government is using an "enhanced" minister's zoning order to speed up construction as it moves ahead with changes to Ontario Place.
The zoning order, called an eMZO, will "provide development permissions for new and existing land uses at Ontario Place," said a written statement from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
"The eMZO is a land use planning tool that will allow the province to maintain site plan control for portions of the site and make much-needed improvements to the publicly accessible open space on the East Island as part of the rebuild of Ontario Place. The eMZO will also allow for expanded public spaces and parks on the West Island."
Ash Milton, Surma's press secretary, said an "enhanced ministers zoning order gives the minister additional powers as it relates to site plan. In this case, the eMZO will exempt certain parts of the Ontario Place site from the municipal site plan process."
Infrastructure Ontario has already put out a "request for qualifications" as it looks to hire a team to design, build and finance the new Ontario Science Centre, which is to be located at Ontario Place's waterfront site.
Surma's announcement also noted that the government is "working with Live Nation to create an updated, year-round concert venue at Ontario Place" for a crowd of 20,000.
Ontario Place will also have 50 acres of green space and beach, as well as a privately run spa.
Late last year, Premier Doug Ford announced a $9 billion deal with the city, with the province uploading responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway and providing funding for housing and new subway trains.
At the same time, Mayor Olivia Chow agreed to the controversial development at Ontario Place.
Surma has previously said "we just have to make a decision and move on" and noted that dozens of environmental assessments and other related studies have already been done on the site.
The NDP has said it opposes giving a 95-year lease to Therme for the spa, and also moving the Ontario Science Centre to the site.
Enhanced MZOs, introduced in 2020, are a relatively new tool for the minister. This one allows the government to "establish land use permissions for the entire site; establish detailed development permissions (i.e. height, gross floor area, and setback requirements); exempt some parts of the site from municipal site plan control; and direct that the City of Toronto's Official Plan cannot prevent the issuance of any licence, permits, or approvals for Ontario Place."
CAPTION: The Ford government wants to create an updated, year-round concert venue at Ontario Place along with 50 acres of green space and beach, as well as have aprivately run spa.

Document 108 of 500

More ServiceOntario locations could move into private retail stores after $10M Staples expansion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-serviceontario-locations-could-move-into/docview/3055933568/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+ServiceOntario+locations+could+move+into+private+retail+stores+after+%2410M+Staples+expansion&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial government is looking for more private retail partners to host ServiceOntario locations, after its pilot program moved six ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores at the beginning of the year.
A request for qualifications was issued by the government on Wednesday, "to develop a roster of qualified retail partners willing to offer lease space across Ontario to host future ServieOntario centres," Matthew D'Amico, a spokesperson for the minister of public and business service delivery, said in a statement.
The Ford government earlier in the year announced a $10.2 million partnership with the stationary big box store, a move opposition parties said was inching public services toward privatization, while the government contended that the new partnership would lead to savings for taxpayers.
D'Amico emphasized that there are currently 193 privately run ServiceOntario locations (in addition to 80 that are publicly run) and said, "our partnerships with retailers have no impact on publicly run ServiceOntario centres."
The new call for retailers to pitch their case to host ServiceOntario locations requires the retailer to have convenient locations that meet the government's service coverage criteria and provide a "cost-effective lease space," according to the proposal.
Global News previously reported that other major retailers - like Ikea, Costco, Loblaws and Rexall - were all considered as potential landing spots for ServiceOntario, while Staples Canada is set to receive $8.29 million over three years to host its new ServiceOntario locations.
The Staples partnership - worth $10.2 million for operating, labour and retrofit costs - will lead to an estimated $1 million in savings for the government, according to D'Amico, but "the exact savings will be better understood in the future as we further analyze the impacts of this pilot."
Why are there ServiceOntario locations in Staples?
In January, the Ontario government made the move to shutter the doors of six ServiceOntario locations and replace them with new counters within Staples stores.
The decision to move ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores drew widespread attention after initial reporting from CityNews. The continued media attention on the move, at one point had Premier Doug Ford telling CityNews reporter Richard Southern at a news conference, "Buddy, you gotta get a new story."
ServiceOntario is already operating in private retail stores even before the Staples deal, with branches within Canadian Tire stores, including at its Yonge-Davenport and St. Clair-Old Stock Yards locations. The partnership began in 2010 under then-premier Dalton McGuinty.
NDP MPP Catherine Fife said, in January, that the ServiceOntario-Staples partnership was "another attempt for Ford to quietly hand over our public services to private corporations," and lamented the "uncertainty" that some public servants would face if ServiceOntario locations were to close.
In the same vein, Green Leader Mike Schreiner took aim the "back room deals" that he alleges the Ford government has prioritized over everyday Ontarians, and questioned why Staples was chosen as the government's partner.
Previous governments had also tried introducing automated ServiceOntario kiosks, but those were permanently shut down in 2012 due to security concerns.
But, the human element hasn't been without its issues.
In December 2023, several ServiceOntario employees were charged in Toronto police's Project Safari investigation and were alleged to have provided criminals with vehicle and driver information.
CAPTION: The government is exploring more partnerships with private retailers to host ServiceOntario locations, similar to the deal it inked with Staples Canada earlier this year.

Document 109 of 500

Two dreaded words: colonoscopy prep

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/two-dreaded-words-colonoscopy-prep/docview/3055845239/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+dreaded+words%3A+colonoscopy+prep&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nine years ago, I had my first colonoscopy.
I wrote about it at the time (this paper, March 30, 2015) because I write about most things, especially things I believe readers could also benefit from. Well, you can't benefit from my colonoscopy, but you can definitely benefit from your own. Consider this a public service announcement.
Usually screening for colon cancer starts at age 50 and then you endure it every 10 years. The gene I have that causes breast cancer also puts me in the crosshairs for colon cancer; I've had a double mastectomy and a hysterectomy, but I'd like to hang onto my pooper.
My interval is every five years, just enough time to let one almost forget what the procedure entails.
Almost.
The woman at the clinic called me to schedule the appointment. I was hoping it would be long in the future.
"We had a cancellation for Monday. Do you want that?"
"Oh, hell no."
"How about the following Saturday?"
"You guys work on Saturday? Is this a so-many-butts-so-little-time thing?"
I threw away a perfectly good Friday night to make the Saturday appointment. Though I've done this twice before, I carefully read all the instructions.
You can't eat nuts or seeds the preceding five days; one time I hadn't opened up the instructions until the day before and found out too late peanuts were illegal. You can only have clear broths and liquids and Jell-O. I scoured the cupboard for an out-of-date box of Jell-O I knew I had, and made it. I hate Jell-O. I also realized it was strawberry and you can't consume anything purple or red, but only things you can see through.
At the store, I decided lime Jell-O would be OK. I also wondered if I could see through a potato chip. Back home over a sad bowl of chicken broth, I waited for my unwanted Jell-O to jell. I made the litres of prep juice I would have to swallow, crossing off each glass like a prisoner etching on a cell wall. It's a good thing I'm not much of a candy person because a package of Skittles the day before the prep for a colonoscopy would make people wonder if Jackson Pollock had been reincarnated in a toilet.
A few months ago, my occasional lactose intolerance kicked into high gear. It took me a month to wrangle it under control, and I finally accepted that our bodies alter as we get older and you can't just wait things out.
Sometimes, you have to make big, permanent changes. I said goodbye to cheese and milk and entertained feeling confident enough to leave the house. I also booked my colonoscopy a year too early because I didn't want to discover I'd been a year too late.
Colon cancer is a big deal, but we have great screening that could save your life. The prep is gross but bearable, and the procedure itself is nothing. They knock you out. I'm a big fan of anesthetic, even if it's only for a 20-minute power nap.
Remember back in 2019, when Doug Ford proposed chopping the anesthetic for the procedure? All that told me was that our premier was overdue for his first colonoscopy.
I went from fishnets and Duran Duran to probiotics and Metamucil in the blink of an eye.
That tells me I've had a great long life so far, and I'd like to keep it going. I told my sons they have to get genetic testing to determine if I've passed the dastardly gene onto them. Both nodded and asked how to go about it.
This is where the conversation has to be. Please get over the ick factor. They can't save your life if you don't take the first steps, so talk to your doctor.
contact@lorraineonline.ca
CAPTION: Colon cancer is a big deal, but we have great screening that could save your life. The prep is gross but bearable, and the procedure itself is nothing, Lorraine Sommerfeld writes.

Subject: Colonoscopy; Colorectal cancer

Title: Two dreaded words: colonoscopy prep

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 17, 2024

Section: life

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Two dreaded words: colonoscopy prep: 'I went from fishnets and Duran Duran to probiotics and Metamucil in the blink of an eye'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-dreaded-words-colonoscopy-prep/docview/3055843454/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 111 of 500

Loblaw boycott organizers 'excited' as stance continues through May, while university polls Canadians on boycott

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/loblaw-boycott-organizers-excited-as-stance/docview/3056012154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Loblaw+boycott+organizers+%27excited%27+as+stance+continues+through+May%2C+while+university+polls+Canadians+on+boycott&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Organizers of the May-long Loblaw boycott are feeling good about how things are going so far.
"We're really excited about it. I never dreamed this could happen in a million years," said organizer Emily Johnson of Milton.
She has seen recent news that a number of suppliers stated Loblaw-affiliated stores are actually selling more food than before the boycott, but she doesn't buy it.
"My personal opinion is that it is just cherry-picking data," she said.
"We've seen a lot of posts on the sub from people who work at other grocery stores talking about how they've had increased volumes. We've seen lots of posts of people screenshotting their Google traffic data during the busy times of the day and the traffic is half of what it usually is."
The grassroots boycott movement began on Reddit, in the r/loblawsisoutofcontrol subreddit.
At first it began as a fun place where members could post memes and photos of groceries with high prices, Johnson said, but when Loblaw changed its 50 per cent off sticker to 30 per cent off in early 2024, people grew really angry and thousands came to the subreddit group. Loblaw later brought back the 50 per cent sticker.
"We started to see some natural conversations popping about how we should be doing more as consumers," Johnson said.
That's how the movement to organize the boycott began.
We reached out to Loblaw Companies Ltd. for comment, but received no new response.
The company previously stated: "As a business, we are acutely aware of the fact that we have to win our customers' business each and every day. The last few years have been tough for Canadians, and we continue to do what we can to combat inflation at our stores."
Sylvain Charlebois, a researcher and professor in food distribution and policy at Dalhousie University, said the university is polling Canadians to see if the boycott is actually happening, but that data is not available yet.
Even so, he doesn't doubt that people are boycotting Loblaw-affiliated stores. He's just not sure it's an effective strategy.
"I've always believed that a boycott is extremely dangerous because what will likely happen, let's say the boycott is actually successful and the group puts pressure on the top line of Loblaw, so affecting their revenues. It won't affect their margins because they'll just turn around and put pressure on the manufacturers," he said.
"The manufacturers are the ones who are going to be hit by the boycott."
He said a boycott would be more effective if it targeted all big box stores and if people spent their money at independent grocers, and that it should have happened months ago as prices have already started coming down.
He also said that while anger at grocery prices is warranted, directing it all at Loblaw may be misguided as prices have shot up everywhere.
But he does agree with organizers calling for a grocery code of conduct, although he wishes organizers had jumped on that sooner and would educate people about it.
Organizers hope the boycott can lead to lower grocery prices, but also to show people that they can avoid Canada's major grocery chains.
Johnson, who lives in an area where she has access to ethnic grocery stores, local farms and a farmers market, says she's been able to avoid Loblaw stores, but understands not everyone can.
For people who can't boycott Loblaw stores, she suggests people write their local member of Parliament, use their PC Optimum points to get discounts and sign the group's petition, supported by Hamilton Centre NDP MP Matthew Green, asking for a number of measures to protect consumers, including mandating Loblaw and Walmart to sign a grocery code of conduct.
She also recommends people visit altgrocery.ca, started by a member of the group's subreddit, which maps out non-big-box grocery stores across Canada.
She also recommends people try ethnic grocery stores. As a single mother of two who has to watch every penny she spends, she's found the ethnic supermarket near her saves her money.
"I can pretty much find everything that I want, plus a few snacks that I can't find at other grocery stores," she said.
The group's petition is available on the House of Commons website.
CAPTION: Organizers of a Loblaw boycott say they're "excited" with how things are going so far.

Document 112 of 500

Ford government warns Toronto to drop request for drug decriminalization

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-warns-toronto-drop-request-drug/docview/3055508122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+warns+Toronto+to+drop+request+for+drug+decriminalization&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is warning Toronto to scrap its "dangerous" push to have Health Canada decriminalize illegal drugs - or face the consequences.
"If Toronto Public Health fails to rescind its misguided application, we will be forced to explore all options available to us," Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said in a letter to Toronto's medical officer of health, Dr. Eileen de Villa.
In the meantime, Ontario will "begin enacting enhanced accountability measures for existing consumption and treatment services site to ensure that the safety and the well-being of the public is protected," the cabinet ministers pledged.
Officials said details will be worked out in the coming weeks.
The blunt two-page letter, released Thursday, reiterates that the province is "100 per cent opposed" to the Toronto proposal to decriminalize drugs for personal use.
"We are frankly surprised that, in the aftermath of British Columbia's and other jurisdictions that have attempted this experiment are just the latest examples that show decriminalization does not work," the letter continues.
"Instead, it encourages dangerous behaviour in public spaces, victimizes innocent people and undermines law enforcement's ability to protect our communities."
"Under no circumstances will our government ever support your request, which would only add to crime and public drug use while doing nothing to support people struggling with addiction."
The letter adds the province will make its opposition "clear" to the federal government.
Health Canada is reviewing Toronto's application, which it made in 2022. The city maintains that criminalizing drug possession makes it more difficult for drug users to get the aid and supports they need, such as improved mental health service and harm reduction strategies.
Last fall, Ontario halted approvals of any new supervised consumption and treatments sites while a review of their effectiveness was underway.
Mayor Olivia Chow and de Villa, who announced her pending resignation earlier this week, have called on the province to provide free or affordable and accessible drug rehabilitation, which is beyond the city's scope.
The province's letter is expected to be discussed at a city hall news conference this afternoon.
De Villa said in a statement that "decriminalization is one tool, supported by the best available evidence, to address the drug toxicity epidemic" that kills more than 500 Torontonians a year.
Toronto's exemption request was made following consultation with health-care experts, police, people with lived experience of drug use and service providers, at the request of Toronto's Board of Health. De Villa said the Board of Health would have to order the request to be rescinded.
"Given the current state of the epidemic, Toronto Public Health prioritizes saving lives ... I support increased access to treatment and harm reduction services, which are life-saving and serve as the front door to health care and other supports," she said. "I agree with the province on the need for increased investment in prevention, increased access to health-care interventions, and whole-of-society solutions including housing and supportive housing. All community members should feel safe and supported as we work to address this epidemic."
With files from Alyshah Hasham
CAPTION: Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Premier Doug Ford talk in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 26, 2024.

Subject: Decriminalization

Document 113 of 500

Tougher penalties for impaired driving won't end the problem, critics warn

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tougher-penalties-impaired-driving-wont-end/docview/3055570603/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tougher+penalties+for+impaired+driving+won%27t+end+the+problem%2C+critics+warn&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is hoping to scare impaired drivers straight by proposing a tough new road safety law aimed at taking offending motorists off the roads longer or for good.
The legislation would require the installation of ignition interlock devices in the vehicles of first offenders, longer roadside suspensions for driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, and automatic lifetime driving bans for those convicted of impaired driving causing death.
"There is never an excuse for getting behind the wheel impaired," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Wednesday.
"It's about time we stopped making excuses for those who do," he added. "There's always a choice ... plan ahead on a friend, call a cab or take public transit."
But critics and civil libertarians said the approach is too black and white, and likely won't have the impact the Ford government hopes because logic doesn't work on people under the influence.
They also warned the new approach could result in police pulling over drivers indiscriminately, perhaps leading to racial profiling.
"There's a real risk here," warned Noa Mendelsohn Aviv, executive director of the Canadian Council of Civil Liberties.
"We are deeply concerned about the tragedy, of the loss of life in relation to impaired driving, but that kind of sadness doesn't mean there are easy solutions."
Ontario Provincial Police laid more than 10,000 impaired driving charges last year. There were about 50 deaths caused by impaired driving.
Sarkaria, who will introduce the legislation Thursday, said the government also plans a public-relations campaign on the dangers and consequences of drug-impaired driving, as police report a rising number of cases of motorists using cannabis.
Police will be given more resources and drivers convicted of impaired driving would face mandatory remedial education and treatment.
"If you think there are no consequences for drinking and driving, my advice would be to start planning for life without your car," added Sarkaria, who on Tuesday proposed tougher penalties for car thieves as part of the same package of legislation.
Immediate roadside suspensions for a first impaired driving offence would increase to seven days from three, and to 14 days from seven for second offences, Sarkaria said.
The proposed changes follow public outrage at cases like the one involving convicted drunk driver Marco Muzzo.
He was sentenced to 10 years in prison in 2016 and saw his driver's licence suspended for 12 years after he blew through a stop sign on the way home from the airport after an alcohol-fuelled bachelor party in Miami in 2015, killing three children from the Neville-Lake family and their grandfather.
Muzzo was released on full parole after five years in prison and will be eligible to drive again in 2028. If passed, the new legislation would not retroactively apply to him.
The proposed changes from Premier Doug Ford's government were applauded by Mothers Against Drunk Driving.
"Despite progress, too many people are still making the choice to drive impaired," said chief executive Steve Sullivan.
Carolyn Swinson, a MADD volunteer counsellor whose father and son were killed by impaired drivers in separate accidents, said families whose lives have been tragically changed forever typically want the offending motorists to lose their driver's licences permanently.
"We need to keep people safe," Swinson said at a news conference with Sarkaria.
Prominent defence lawyer Calvin Barry, a former Crown attorney, said he considers automatic lifetime driver's licence suspensions "a little draconian" because they do not give judges any leeway for exceptional circumstances, or the prospect of rehabilitation or recovery from addiction.
There could be situations where a fatal accident was inevitable because of traffic, weather or other conditions, and automatic lifetime driving bans can make it impossible for some people, such as truckers or people who live in remote areas, to earn a living.
"There should be some discretion for the judge," said Barry. "But the best defence is take an Uber."
He cautioned that ignition interlock systems can be defeated if impaired motorists have someone who is sober blow into them.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said some of the changes are "a step in the right direction" but cautioned "a lot of victims, a lot of survivors, are not getting their day in court because our courts are so overwhelmed and underfunded."
CAPTION: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria is shownin St. Catharines on April 5, 2024.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Criminal sentences; Fatalities; Legislation; Automobile drivers; Racial profiling; Breath tests; Drunk driving

Title: Tougher penalties for impaired driving won't end the problem, critics warn

Document 114 of 500

NDP blasts Ford government for 'shameful record' on sex assault prosecutions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-blasts-ford-government-shameful-record-on-sex/docview/3055570598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+blasts+Ford+government+for+%27shameful+record%27+on+sex+assault+prosecutions&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The NDP says it will "hold this government to account" when it comes to tracking and reporting the number of sex assault cases thrown out of court each year due to trial delays - something MPP Catherine Fife calls a "shameful record" in the province.
Fife, who represents Waterloo, said the Ford government used its majority to shuffle her private member's bill - "Lydia's Law" - to be studied by a committee, adding that New Democrats "have to keep the pressure on" to ensure it doesn't languish there.
"We're going to develop a strategy not to let this government run away from their own disastrous courtroom system," Fife told reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday.
She said 1,326 sex assault cases were thrown out of Ontario courts in 2022 because of delays in coming to trial or a shortage of judges, and that another 1,119 cases were thrown out last year.
"This government has had opportunities to be transparent and be accountable and they've chosen not to ... so yes I am worried that this is just a way to avoid accountability and transparency," Fife said. "They've shown us who they really are."
Both the Ontario Court of Justice and the Superior Court of Justice can handle sex assault cases, typically depending on the severity.
The federal government has recently announced four new judge appointments for the Superior Court, although a handful of vacancies remain and staff shortages continue at both levels.
The Star has reported on a number of sexual assault, child abuse and human trafficking cases that were stayed due to a lack of judges, or because the the constitutional rights of an accused were violated because they took too long to come to trial.
Government House Leader Paul Calandra said the bill was sent "directly to a committee of the legislature... so that it could be part of the study that the justice committee is doing on intimate partner violence" that will lead to "comprehensive recommendations on how we can provide better services to victims of intimate partner violence."
Fife's bill is based on recommendations from the province's auditor general five years ago. It stipulates that the attorney general should keep tabs on criminal cases that have been in the system for eight months and not heard, and report on why.
The bill would also ensure that survivors of sexual assault are informed about the province's independent legal advice program.
Lydia's Law is named after a young sexual assault survivor who told Fife of a two-year experience with the courts that included many delays. "She told me that she did not want any other survivor to go through what she went through," Fife said.
Cait Alexander, a sexual assault survivor who used to live in Toronto and is now an outspoken advocate for changes in the justice system, said "we don't need more studies, we don't need more committees, we need the governments who listen and pass the bills," The Canadian Press's Liam Casey reported.
Fife's bill had the support of the Liberals, with MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood) saying "delays in bringing these cases to trial are excruciating to victims, and often result in the charges being dropped as these delays can mean the constitutional right to a speedy trial is violated."
Hazell added that it should "be a non-partisan issue. The government committed five years ago to addressing this issue, but it has only gotten worse."
CAPTION: Cait Alexander, an advocate for survivors of sexual assault, is shown at Queen's Park in Toronto on May 15, 2024.NDP MPP Catherine Fife speaks to reporters at Queen's Park in Toronto on May 15, 2024.

Subject: Domestic violence; Sex crimes; Constitutional amendments; Trials

May 15: Bike lane rejection questioned, time to retire fireworks and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/may-15-bike-lane-rejection-questioned-time-retire/docview/3054978832/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=May+15%3A+Bike+lane+rejection+questioned%2C+time+to+retire+fireworks+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bike lane rejection questioned
Re: Protected bike lanes on Dewitt Road scrapped after council debate (May 13)
I read with some understanding about the Dewitt Road issue. If the street was recently rebuilt, why was there not a separated cycling system included during that? Same with the speed bumps?
From the image presented, it would appear that creating a multi-use pathway beside the roadway - with the roadway also getting a diet - would have been a far better solution.
I agree that paint on a roadway does not make a cycling lane any safer for the cyclists. Even a curb is not ideal.
Considering there are two schools and a goal of increasing active activity, this multi-use version of solution would have achieved both.
As it stands, it appears that driving a child to school will remain the norm, even with bumps installed.
Ilpo Lehto, Hamilton
Thanks for touching column
Re: Call your mother. She'll be dead one day (May 11)
Anne Bokma's heartfelt Mother's Day piece on the trauma of losing a mother really resonated with me. Thank you for helping me to feel that I am not alone in my grief.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Time to retire fireworks
A long weekend is on the horizon and while wildfires ravage the West, we will be setting off fireworks.
Guaranteed to frighten most animals, wild or domesticated, there will be lots of garbage as well through our parks as I rarely see anyone cleaning up afterwards.
Why are we still having fireworks? Is it worth it to entertained for five minutes? Why not lasers or drones like they do in other countries? We can do better.
Robin Magder, Dundas
A timely 'common sense' reminder
Re: We've seen claims of 'Common Sense' before and it wasn't pretty (May 11)
What an excellent editorial outlining and reminding us of the brutally destructive wrath that swept through Ontario under the Progressive Conservatives and Mike Harris. You see this pattern again in Doug Ford's approach to governing and certainly get the feeling that Pierre Poilievre would have these aspirations and more with his right-wing views.
What we need more than ever is political leadership of mature, professional people willing to govern for the good of the people, not themselves.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Know when it's time to go
Justin Trudeau should take a page from the many athletes (Gordie Howe, Roger Clemens, George Foreman, Brett Favre) who tried to keep going past their prime. While they may have had some admirable motivations other than money, it's time for him to move on and give the Liberals and the country a better choice.
Terry Johnson, Burlington
Rex Murphy became increasingly caustic
Re: Rex Murphy, keen-eyed observer of Canadian life and politics, dies at 77 (May 9)
Rex Murphy was a Canadian icon, admired for his sharp and forthright views on the Canadian political landscape and Canadian culture. But for all his wit and way with words, Murphy's commentary in recent years became less about enlightenment and more about nursing long-held grievances expressed in right-wing conservative media.
Over time, his opinions became increasingly caustic and decidedly divisive. Unfortunately, Murphy's writings appear to have devolved into the current views held by many Conservatives who often come across as negative, angry and mean-spirited.
As a result, we are left with a diminished view of a great Canadian.
Carolyn Evans, Burlington

Document 116 of 500

Pierre Poilievre hints he'd like to strip Canadians of some rights. There's something to think about when it's time to vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-hints-hed-like-strip-canadians/docview/3054978827/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+hints+he%27d+like+to+strip+Canadians+of+some+rights.+There%27s+something+to+think+about+when+it%27s+time+to+vote&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No one knows what the ballot-box question will be in the next election, whenever it does come.
But at least one legal scholar in Canada believes voters should be asking some hard questions about the Constitution - specifically, whether a future prime minister would be willing to opt out of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Pierre Poilievre has dropped some broad hints in the past couple of weeks that he could be ready to blaze a trail here as prime minister. No federal government has ever used the notwithstanding clause in the 40-plus years it has been part of the Constitution. But the Conservative leader told a police chiefs' gathering he might go down that road to get tougher on criminals.
"All of my proposals are constitutional, and we will make sure we will make them constitutional. We're using whatever tools the Constitution allows me to use to make them constitutional," Poilievre said in his Apr. 29 speech.
"I think you know exactly what I mean."
Jeffrey Meyers, a B.C. lawyer and legal instructor, says even hinting about it should worry citizens. He stresses that his concerns have nothing to do with partisanship - he's not a fan of how the Liberals have been trying to politicize this debate in recent days, for instance.
But Meyers says that Poilievre's hints cannot be seen in isolation from what's happening in the United States or the increasing willingness of conservative provinces in Canada to talk about opting out of federal legislation they don't like.
He points also to Doug Ford's efforts to use the notwithstanding clause on political financing and the right to strike, as well as the Ontario premier's comments about wanting "like-minded" judges on the provincial bench.
"The bottom line is that it's getting normalized at the provincial level," Meyers said, and he fears it's only a matter of time before some prime minister, quite possibly Poilievre, will try to do the same at the federal level.
That's how the law and the courts got increasingly politicized in the United States, he argues - to the point where former president Donald Trump is openly badmouthing judges, jurors and the courts, and where a lineup of Republicans appeared this week outside a New York court to describe the legal process as a sham. Not so long ago, this would be seen as outrageous. Now it's just another day in the Trump bid for re-election.
Meyers says there's already a playbook for politicizing the justice system. It starts with taking an established political party, Republicans in the United States, for instance, and then "reshuffling the deck in terms of what the established norms are."
Trump now boasts about how he has made the U.S. Supreme Court more friendly to Republicans and the anti-abortion crowd in the States. Meyers says imagine what Trump would have done - or could do in future - if he had something like an opt-out clause from the American constitution.
"That's a shortcut alarmingly available here."
Meyers, who wrote a column in The Conversation last fall on this very issue, says this is why Poilievre's most recent remarks to the police chiefs deeply worried him; that the Conservative leader is tilling the ground to make opting-out of the Charter no big deal.
"It's a norm that hasn't yet been broken. He breaks that norm, though it undermines the Charter so profoundly. And I just think it really, really changes the direction of the country and in unbelievably significant ways and opens the door also to eventually increasing politicization of the courts ... I don't think that's alarmism. I think that's just real."
Meyers, no big fan of Justin Trudeau and the Liberals either, fears the current governing party will "demagogue" the whole debate in ways that citizens will dismiss as the usual political fare. It's not, he says.
What he would like to see is an election campaign in which citizens demand that politicians commit to never using the notwithstanding clause, or, better yet, to getting it out of the Constitution altogether (which wouldn't be easy.)
Former prime minister Paul Martin made that very promise in the dying days of the 2005-06 campaign, which knocked the Liberals out of power. Some Conservatives in the Commons last week were taunting Liberals that all this talk about the notwithstanding clause is a mark of the same desperation that made Martin issue the eleventh-hour promise not to use it.
Canadians should already be braced for an election campaign in which Liberals will accuse Poilievre of trying to import Trumpism to Canada, which Conservatives will frame similarly as desperation or an exaggeration.
Meyers, for his part, doesn't think Canada is immune to the Trump style of politicizing the courts and the basic laws of the land. One clear way to protect ourselves, he says, is to demand that any future government keep its hands off the notwithstanding clause.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has dropped some broad hints in the past couple of weeks that he could be ready to use the notwithstanding clause to override the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Susan Delacourt writes.

Subject: Conservatism; Political campaigns; Charters; Constitutional law; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Charters

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Israeli flag raised with little fanfare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/israeli-flag-raised-with-little-fanfare/docview/3054754888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Israeli+flag+raised+with+little+fanfare&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Israel's flag was flying at Ottawa City Hall early Tuesday morning in honour of the country's national day, but there was no public ceremony to mark the occasion.
Instead, the city said the Jewish Federation of Ottawa organized a private event, but officials refused to say when or where it was happening.
City officials said they had information suggesting that holding a public event posed a risk to public safety, but they gave no details.
The decision to forgo a flag-raising ceremony sparked backlash from the Jewish community, including federal politicians such as Liberal MP Anthony Housefather and Conservative MP Melissa Lantsman, who urged the city to go ahead with the event.
Last Friday, Mayor Mark Sutcliffe posted on social media that he had asked police and the city to come up with a way to hold the event safely, but city officials said Monday that would not happen.
Pro-Palestinian demonstrators who gathered outside city hall on Tuesday claimed victory, saying they had forced the cancellation of what they called a "genocidal flag-raising" ceremony. A masked woman leading the group of about 50 people told the crowd "the shameful Zionists have gone home."
She called Sutcliffe's stance "disgraceful" but praised Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow for her decision not to participate in a flag-raising in that city.
Chow's office released a statement Tuesday, saying the mayor would not attend the ceremony because "she believes raising (the flag) is divisive at this time, and understands the deep pain and anguish felt by many in the community."
Meanwhile, Premier Doug Ford released a statement celebrating 76 years since the establishment of Israel as an "independent and democratic state."
"Today, we also recognize the many challenges Israel has faced through its existence, including the attacks of Oct. 7, 2023," Ford said. "I continue to pray for the safe return of the hostages who were taken in the attacks and condemn hatred and antisemitism in all its forms, wherever it is found."
On Oct. 7, Hamas fighters from the Gaza Strip broke into southern Israel and killed 1,200 people, mostly Israelis, sparking the conflict. Israel responded by bombarding and invading Gaza, killing more than 35,000 Palestinians from the enclave, according to the Hamas-run Health Ministry. More than 600 Israeli soldiers have been killed since the fighting erupted.
With files from The Associated Press
CAPTION: Supporters of Israel celebrate the country's Independence Day in Montreal on Tuesday.Christinne Muschi The Canadian Press

Full text: It's time to
retire fireworks
A long weekend is on the horizon and while wildfires ravage the West, we will be setting off fireworks.
Guaranteed to frighten most animals, wild or domesticated, there will be lots of garbage as well through our parks as I rarely see anyone cleaning up afterwards.
Why are we still having fireworks? Is it worth it to be entertained for five minutes? Why not lasers or drones like they do in other countries? We can do better.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Bike lane rejection questioned
Re: Protected bike lanes on
Dewitt Road scrapped after
council debate (May 13)
I read with some understanding about the Dewitt Road issue. If the street was recently rebuilt, why was there not a separated cycling system included during that? Same with the speed bumps?
From the image presented, it would appear that creating a multi-use pathway beside the roadway - with the roadway also getting a diet - would have been a far better solution.
I agree that paint on a roadway does not make a cycling lane any safer for the cyclists. Even a curb is not ideal.
Considering there are two schools and a goal of increasing active activity, this multi-use version of solution would have achieved both.
As it stands, it appears that driving a child to school will remain the norm, even with bumps installed.
Ilpo Lehto, Hamilton

Andrea Rado, Hamilton

What an excellent editorial outlining and reminding us of the brutally destructive wrath that swept through Ontario under the Progressive Conservatives and Mike Harris. You see this pattern again in Doug Ford's approach to governing and certainly get the feeling that Pierre Poilievre would have these aspirations and more with his right-wing views.
What we need more than ever is political leadership of mature, professional people willing to govern for the good of the people, not themselves.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington

Justin Trudeau should take a page from the many athletes (Gordie Howe, Roger Clemens, George Foreman, Brett Favre) who tried to keep going past their prime. While they may have had some admirable motivations other than money, it's time for him to move on and give the Liberals and the country a better choice.
Terry Johnson, Burlington

'We have a lot of work to do': Bonnie Crombie says Liberals re-evaluating strategy after byelection losses to Doug Ford's PCs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-have-lot-work-do-bonnie-crombie-says-liberals/docview/3055174763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+have+a+lot+of+work+to+do%27%3A+Bonnie+Crombie+says+Liberals+re-evaluating+strategy+after+byelection+losses+to+Doug+Ford%27s+PCs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Liberals are taking a closer look at their strategy after losing two byelections to Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives earlier this month, says Bonnie Crombie.
"I'm not going to deny that we have a lot of work to do and we're going to go back and see how we can improve our numbers," the party's leader said Tuesday.
In her first news conference at Queen's Park since the May 2 defeats in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, the former Mississauga mayor said a blitz of feel-good and attack ads from Ford and the Progressive Conservatives in addition to government advertising are proving difficult to overcome.
"It certainly is a challenge for us, but we're all up for the challenge," Crombie said as she unveiled the promise of a tax cut that would save small businesses about $450 million a year.
"We'll be going back and taking a look at where we could have made changes, where we'll do better next time and where to go forward."
In Milton, the PCs won with candidate Zee Hamid, a former Liberal, who defeated Liberal hopeful Galen Naidoo-Harris, the son of the riding's former Liberal MPP and cabinet minister Indira Naidoo-Harris.
Despite an RCMP investigation into the Ford government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap, the Progressive Conservative margin of victory was 2,407 votes - higher than the 1,680 former PC cabinet minister Parm Gill won by in 2022 as Ford was elected to a second term.
"I would say that was certainly one of the issues in the mix," Crombie said of Hamid's Liberal history.
Hamid's nomination had rankled some Tories given his Liberal past, but as the Star's Robert Benzie reported, Ford was convinced that would help the PCs in a close race. Liberal sources have also said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's slide in the polls is not helping the provincial party.
In the meantime, Crombie said she will continue travelling the province, meeting with Liberals and local media to become better known in advance of the next provincial election campaign two years from now.
The party will soon open nominations for candidates in ridings across Ontario for the 2026 election.
"I've spent the last four or five months building capacity with our team, putting organizational resources in place, building up a fundraising team that is going to help us raise the kind of money we need to be competitive in the next election," said Crombie, who became leader in December.
In the most recent monthly tracking poll of 995 Ontarians for the Star by Abacus Data on April 20, the PCs were at 41 per cent support, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the NDP 21 and the Greens at 7. The Liberals were down two points from the previous month while the other parties were unchanged.
While the Abacus opt-in poll cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie addresses the provincial budget at Queen's Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Provincial elections; Nominations; Negative campaigning; Political advertising; Elections

Document 120 of 500

$1.56-billion EV battery plant a 'game-changer for Niagara'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/1-56-billion-ev-battery-plant-game-changer/docview/3055174558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%241.56-billion+EV+battery+plant+a+%27game-changer+for+Niagara%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Game-changer. Massive. Transformational.
To say the excitement was palpable at a gathering of municipal, regional, provincial and federal dignitaries for the announcement a $1.56-billion electric vehicle battery separator plant will be built in Port Colborne would be an understatement.
"This is a game-changer for Niagara," said Mishka Balsom, chief executive officer of the Greater Niagara Chamber of Commerce.
"We will look back on this particular day and remember the announcement that has been made here. What's good for Port Colborne is good for all of Niagara."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford were joined Tuesday at Port Colborne's engineering and operations centre by several key ministers, along with officials from across Niagara, to welcome Asahi Kasei Corporation's massive investment that attendees said will lead to significant spinoff benefits for the entire peninsula.
With medium- to long-term growth forecasted in the North American electric vehicle market, the Japanese company expects to make additional multibillion-dollar investments through multiple phases, said the federal government, adding details about direct jobs are being finalized and will be announced at a later date.
Both Trudeau and Ford said Asahi's decision to build its facility in Port Colborne is a testament to Canada and Ontario's competitive business environment, highly skilled workforce and dependable supply chains.
The project will benefit from federal support through the Clean Technology Manufacturing Investment tax credit. Ontario will support the project with both direct and indirect incentives.
"This is probably the most important announcement made in the last 25 years in the Niagara region," said Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley.
"For Port Colborne, for the Niagara region, this really paints a good picture of the future. It puts us in the right place for the green economy. It means that we're a key player here."
He said Port Colborne is attractive, in part, due to its large and suitable available land.
"There's going to be a lot of spinoffs because this company is going to have to be serviced by local people when it's up and established, so this will produce a lot of good jobs, a lot of economic activity."
Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele said it was a "Team Niagara" approach that helped bring the investment to fruition.
"We've been meeting for nine months. Every week, we have a call that has approximately 45 people on it. We talk about the project, we talk about infrastructure, we talk about what we can do for this company to come to Niagara - the incentives that we have, both at the regional level and the city level. This is what's made them come here."
He said Niagara is on the "cusp" of bringing "lots of new businesses, lots of new people here."
"You're going to see the resurgence of all kinds of new industry that will tie in not just with the EV battery trade but, quite frankly, our marine trade."
Japanese battery maker Asahi Kasei building $1.56B plant in Port Colborne
Welland Mayor Frank Campion said Niagara is "starting to turn into a bit of an EV centre," pointing to the Linamar manufacturing facility expected to start producing parts for electric vehicles in his city next year.
"It's massive for Niagara. We don't really care where the jobs are in Niagara, as long as the jobs are being created and (there is) prosperity and economic spinoffs. It's good for every municipality in Niagara."
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati used the analogy of a "high tide is going to bring up all the boats of Niagara."
"This is going to be as impactful as General Motors was when they came to Niagara a long time ago," he said.
"Even though it was in St. Catharines, so many people in Niagara Falls and Welland and around the region worked at General Motors and this is going to have the same impact, except it's forward-leaning into a new industry."
Wainfleet Mayor Brian Grant said the new investment will benefit his township, which is next door to Port Colborne.
"I think this is an amazing opportunity for Port Colborne and Niagara, especially southern Niagara because generally we see a lot more development like this happening more in the north end - Niagara Falls, St. Catharines areas," he said.
"There's going to be other opportunities that are going to come up. It's going to bring more investors and more business that's ultimately good for all around Niagara."
Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff said the investment is "great news" for local jobs.
"This is going to be transformational for our local workers," he said.
"It's really about having that anchor corporation ... that's going to be a facility hundreds of acres in sizes, with potential for thousands of jobs ... and we know that there are a lot of suppliers that are going to be feeding into not just building this site, but then a lot of the transformational manufacturing that's going to be happening around this site."
While the mood inside the centre garage bay on Killaly Street West was positive, the same couldn't be said about a group of protesters gathered on the other side of a fence.
People waving Canadian flags and signs, including one calling for an end to the carbon tax, chanted against Trudeau throughout Tuesday's announcement.
With one of the chants being "Trudeau must go," the protesters blamed the prime minister for a plethora of issues, such as cost of living and homelessness, while calling for him to come out and address their concerns.
CAPTION: With Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to his right, Premier Doug Ford speaks about Asahi Kasei Corp.'s $1.56-billion investment in Port Colborne, bringing the first Hipore wet-process lithium-ion separator manufacturing facility to Canada.Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley (front, left) says Tuesday's announcementof a $1.56-billion investment that will see an electric vehicle battery separator plant built in Port Colborne is "probably the most important announcement made in the last 25 years here in the Niagara region."Wainfleet Mayor Brian Grant speaks to the media ahead of Japanese manufacturer Asahi Kasei Corp.'s announcement of a $1.56-billion investment in Port Colborne.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford on Tuesday announce Asahi Kasei Corporation's investment of $1.56 billion to build an electric vehicle battery separator plant in Port Colborne.
