Document 287 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3035427744/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We aren't that gullible
Re: Ford forces LCBO to reinstate
paper bags for customers (April 8)
Does the Ford government think the people of Ontario are that gullible? First, LCBO paper bags were taken away due to preserving our trees, now they are being reinstated because families have enough costs without spending their money on reusable bags? If it is reusable, how much money are they spending?
Mr. Ford is just trying to buy votes. Don't kid yourselves.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Paper bag reversal hilarious
I find it hilarious that Premier Doug Ford wants paper bags reintroduced at the LCBO because some people can't afford to purchase reusable bags. If they can't afford a reusable bag, they shouldn't be able to afford to buy liquor. Does Ford have an interest in LCBO profits? I think he must.
Sandra Carpenter, Ancaster
Raising the cost of booze next?
Perhaps Premier Doug Ford should just raise the cost of alcohol so that "poor" people cannot afford it, rather than worry about them affording the cost of reusable bags, just like the rest of us.
Frances Cormier, Stoney Creek
LCBO bag decision just a stunt
I am appalled that Premier Doug Ford would pull an idiotic stunt and announce that he is bringing back paper bags to the LCBO. Why not do something constructive like repeal the fact that police automatically get paid while under suspension, costing taxpayers millions of dollars. If the people shopping at the LCBO cannot afford a reusable shopping bag, then they cannot afford to buy liquor.
Peter Rathbone, Hamilton
Customers can't afford bag?
Mr. Ford, are you kidding? If you can afford to shop at the LCBO, you can surely afford a reusable bag that you can reuse anywhere you shop.
Irene Hough, Hamilton
Eclipse lives up to the hype
Re: Not buying the hype (April 7)
I was nearly dissuaded from watching the eclipse after George Torok's strongly worded letter. As he pointed out, I am a resident of Earth, and I do see the sun every day. However, I threw caution to the wind and solar glasses onto my face and braved the seven-minute drive to the RBG Arboretum to take part.
I am pleased to report that the solar eclipse was worth the hype. The clouds parted just in time to see the once-in-the-lifetime event. A chill came over the air. The birds stopped chirping. We reached totality. I hugged my family and felt connected to the cosmos - a reminder that we are a part of something larger and spectacular, of which humans still have relatively little understanding. And the best part is, I didn't even have to ask the universe for my money back.
Rachael Collier, Hamilton
Thanks for the eclipse glasses
Just a shout-out to McMaster University that kindly provided eclipse glasses to our community free of charge. It helped make Monday's eclipse a memorable event for many. Bravo to the individual or individuals responsible.
Janet Leach, Stoney Creek
A magical, surreal experience
Re: Total eclipse 'an almost spiritual experience'
for Hamilton viewers (April 8)
What an ultimate experience for everyone who had the chance to watch the total eclipse this week. I would say it went beyond "almost" and was a "spiritual experience." As Hasit Shukla's said in Fallon Hewitt's article, it sure was "magical and surreal." Millions of us, in one go, made a connection with nature and with each other, something we humans crave.
Sure, NASA and other scientists used this opportunity to gather information, which is great. Identifying something like the eclipse as a spiritual experience is even greater. No religion required.
Loretta Jaunzarins, Hamilton
Event brought us together
People coming together, almost total positives from the many persons interviewed, diverse crowds smiling and cheering together: Wow, what a day. Along the path of Monday's total solar eclipse the world seemed to come together for a few hours.
I just wonder if such a natural phenomena could influence people along a path that followed the Russia/Ukraine and Israel/Gaza borders and maybe even lots more of the Middle East.
James White, Nanticoke

Province appoints Don Robertson to Hamilton police board: Local realtor started his three-year term on April 1

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-appoints-don-robertson-hamilton-police/docview/3035427678/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+appoints+Don+Robertson+to+Hamilton+police+board%3A+Local+realtor+started+his+three-year+term+on+April+1&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province has chosen a local realtor as the newest member of Hamilton's police board.
Don Robertson began a three-year term on the oversight body on April 1, the board said in a release Wednesday. He takes over the provincial appointment role from Pat Mandy, who served six years on the board and announced her departure as chair last month.
A lifelong Hamiltonian, Robertson has been a realtor since 1974.
The board touted Robertson in its release as a man who believes in giving back to his community.
He's the past president of the Lynden Lions Club, a former vice-president of the Ontario Liberal Party and was once voted Dundas' Citizen of the Year.
He currently serves as owner and general manager of the Allan Cup-winning Dundas Real McCoys hockey team, and sits on the board of the Ontario Hockey Association.
Sebastian Bron is a reporter
at The Spectator. s
bron@thespec.com
CAPTION: Don Robertson is the owner of the Dundas Real McCoys hockey team.

Document 289 of 500

Pressure mounts on Justin Trudeau's Liberals as NDP joins Conservatives in calling for national meeting on carbon levy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pressure-mounts-on-justin-trudeaus-liberals-as/docview/3037133542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pressure+mounts+on+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals+as+NDP+joins+Conservatives+in+calling+for+national+meeting+on+carbon+levy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Opposition parties banded together Wednesday to call on the Liberal government to stage a televised, federal-provincial summit on the carbon price within five weeks, the latest shift in the long-running political battle over Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's signature climate policy.
The demand came through a motion in the House of Commons that was sponsored by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, and received support from the Bloc Québécois and New Democratic Party. It passed 172 to 150, with Liberal MPs opposing.
A spokesperson for the Prime Minister's Office declined to comment.
Although the motion was non-binding, the joint demand by opposition parties with a majority of seats in the House adds to the mounting pressure over concerns the federal carbon price - which requires provinces to have pricing systems to discourage the use of fuels that are causing the climate crisis - is straining Canadians who are already struggling with inflation and high interest rates.
NDP MP Laurel Collins, whose party is propping up Trudeau's minority government through a parliamentary alliance, claimed the Liberal government has made consumer carbon pricing the "be all, end all" of climate policies, and suggested the government should strengthen the carbon price for heavy industries while taking serious action to curb emissions. New Democrats are also open to premiers presenting alternative plans to the federal carbon price on consumers, she said.
The NDP premier of Manitoba, Wab Kinew, is pushing for an "exemption" from the federal carbon price because of the province's abundant clean power and plans to slash greenhouse gas emissions.
In recent weeks, several provincial premiers, including the Liberal leader of Newfoundland and Labrador, failed to prevent Ottawa from raising the national minimum carbon price on April 1. The move added another three cents to the price of gasoline, according to federal statistics, but the Liberal government has noted that quarterly rebates to provinces where the federal carbon price applies - worth $1,130 for an Ontario family of four this year - also went up.
Calls have since shifted to hosting a summit on the carbon price, with Poilievre leading the pressure campaign in the House of Commons this week, with similar demands coming from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, a fellow staunch critic of the policy.
On Tuesday, former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney also endorsed the idea, but said provincial opponents of carbon pricing should show up with alternative climate policies that are at least similarly effective.
A study published in March by the Canadian Climate Institute concluded that less controversial industrial carbon pricing systems could contribute between 20 and 48 per cent of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030, while the consumer levy on fuel could contribute between eight and 14 per cent.
Poilievre's Conservatives have pledged to "axe the tax" on consumer fuel, along with the quarterly rebates to households that pay it, but haven't said whether they would also scrap the federal requirement for industrial carbon pricing.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre talks to reporters on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Tuesday, April 9, 2024.

Subject: Opposition parties; Climate policy; Emissions; Carbon; Prime ministers

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 290 of 500

April 10: Ford is no model for law and order, listen to local voices and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/april-10-ford-is-no-model-law-order-listen-local/docview/3035151300/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=April+10%3A+Ford+is+no+model+for+law+and+order%2C+listen+to+local+voices+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford is no model for law and order
Premier Doug Ford said in the legislature that he is going to double, triple or even quadruple down on people who break the law. This apparently does not apply to his own actions as premier breaking the most fundamental law of the country: The Constitution.
1. He tried to force gas station owners to post his anti-carbon-tax stickers: Struck down by the courts as interfering with free speech.
2. He passed a law trying to micromanage how university student unions were run. Struck down as violating university independence.
3. He tried to legislate away public service collective bargaining rights by restricting them to one per cent pay raises. Struck down as interfering with the constitutional right of freedom of association.
All this does not even include many examples of favouritism and cronyism such as his attempt to get his good friend appointed as OPP commissioner and letting his wealthy buddies in the home-building industry dominate the office of the housing minister.
If he wants to lead by example, he needs to realize that he was elected to serve all the people of Ontario not just the minority who voted for him. It is time for him to triple down on ethical behaviour.
John Roy, Hamilton
We need more than criticism
Every day in The Spec there are one or two letters to the editor that contain some form of political complaint. And yes, I have written those types of letters myself be it civic, provincial or federal politicians. My question is, why do the letter writers constantly bash one party only? There are writers who constantly rant about the prime minister but at the same time never complain about the premier. What I don't see or read are letters offering alternatives, just criticism. If you don't like the job someone is doing but aren't willing to do the job yourself you don't have the right to complain. There are many ways to contact all levels of government, so if you disagree with what is going on put your effort into getting your voice heard instead of complaining.
Mike Lacroix, Hamilton
Looking for someone to blame
Re: Kagame blames the world's inaction as Rwanda commemorates the 1994 genocide (April 7)
Rwanda's President Paul Kagame blames the inaction of the international community for the Rwandan genocide. No doubt outsiders should have done more, and earlier. But domestic conflicts are usually triggered by domestic tensions, in this case between Hutus and Tutsis. If I were Kagame, looking for "blame," I would look closer to home.
Richard Harris, professor emeritus, McMaster University
Listen to local voices first
I do not think Mayor Andrea Horwath should use the "strong mayor" powers given to her by the province. She once said it was undemocratic and she wouldn't; until she did. It is undemocratic.
The Stoney Creek residents and the Hamilton council have voted no. Coun. Matt Francis has suggested another site at Riverdale. Would it not be better to investigate that site? When I use the Lake and Mountain street parking lots I find them full of cars. They are needed for the medical buildings around them and for downtown Stoney Creek to thrive. Before ramming through a plan, listening to residents and the councillor for Ward 5 might be in order.
Sigrid Smith, Hamilton
Willingness to change needed
Re: Fire, EMS, library face $418M funding gap: city report (April 5)
Coun. Maureen Wilson rightly notes that decades of council inaction have created our infrastructure maintenance problem. Here are some possible action steps.
We need to reduce infrastructure by selling underused assets, maximize the use of existing assets and only build critically needed items. We also must reduce the cost of existing services and maintenance by implementing improvements and cost reductions. This will fund desperately needed maintenance items. We do not need consultants for this - director Patricia Leishman has experience in implementing these ideas and many trained, retired professionals are available for support. We do not need more taxes or staff, just the will of council to change!
Scott Innes, Hamilton
Food banks are lifesavers
Re: Why do we love food banks? (April 4)
Dennis Raphael's commentary is dead wrong. He is sadly confused. Today's food banks are vital to every community. They fill a need for all patrons, notwithstanding their place in life. Food banks for the most part are funded by donations from generous individuals and responsible corporations who recognize the need to give. Raphael should open his eyes to the world around him and look beyond his many references to European culture which I doubt is so different from ours. Worldwide hunger is the prime root and cause of today's strife. Helping to fill empty bellies is only the beginning.
Dan Levy, Hamilton
How big things get done
People who have an interest in the ongoing LRT debate will want to read "How Big Things Get Done: The Surprising Factors That Determine the Fate of Every Project, from Home Renovations to Space Exploration and Everything In Between" by Bent Fluvberg, (first published February 2023). The author is an expert on megaprojects and it is a cautionary tale about delays and cost overruns, due to overly hasty commitments and inadequate planning. It also outlines timely and successful projects and how to achieve them. For councillors, civil servants and taxpayers, this book is a must read.
Colleen Kurtz, Hamilton
Who are MPs voting for in the House?
The NDP seems to have discovered that inflation produces not only higher prices but also higher profits. Just as they have forgotten that as of April 1 MPs' salaries are at record levels making our MPs the second-highest paid legislators in the world. In addition, they also delayed the scheduled date for the next election by a week so that any MPs who lose their seats qualify for their gilded pensions. Makes you wonder, when they vote to support a motion, if they are voting for our benefit or for their pension benefit. Perhaps the NDP could propose an excessive benefits tax on the excessive pension benefits they receive. It's all eerily similar to George Orwell's "Animal Farm."
Paul Tavares, Hamilton
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says he is going to crack down on people who break the law. This apparently does not apply to his own actions, John Roy writes.

Subject: Food programs; Councils; Law enforcement; Letters to the editor; Genocide

Province appoints Don Robertson to Hamilton police board

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-appoints-don-robertson-hamilton-police/docview/3035151296/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+appoints+Don+Robertson+to+Hamilton+police+board&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province has chosen a local realtor as the newest member of Hamilton's police board.
Don Robertson began a three-year term on the oversight body on April 1, the board said in a release Wednesday. He takes over the provincial appointment role from Pat Mandy, who served six years on the board and announced her departure as chair last month.
A lifelong Hamiltonian, Roberston lives with his wife in rural Ancaster and has been a realtor since 1974.
The board touted Robertson in its release as a man who believes in giving back to his community.
He's the past president of the Lynden Lions Club, a former vice-president of the Ontario Liberal Party and was once voted Dundas' Citizen of the Year, the board said. He currently serves as owner and general manager of the Allan Cup-winning Dundas Real McCoys hockey team, and sits on the board of the Ontario Hockey Association.
Sebastian Bron is a reporter with The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Don Robertson is the newest member of Hamilton's police board. He is a longtime realtor and owner of the cup-winning Dundas Real McCoys hockey team.

Document 292 of 500

Ford government attempts to speed new housing starts with sweeping bill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-attempts-speed-new-housing-starts/docview/3035150845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+attempts+to+speed+new+housing+starts+with+sweeping+bill&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Desperate to build housing, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have introduced sweeping legislation that throws in nearly everything but the kitchen sink.
Housing Minister Paul Calandra tabled an omnibus bill Wednesday designed to streamline government permit processes, make it easier for universities to build student dorms, improve transparency around the controversial minister's zoning orders (MZO) framework and eliminate parking requirements for higher density housing developments near transit stations to lower costs.
"These measures recognize the struggles that our municipal partners have faced in building homes and are targeted at removing those obstacles," said Calandra of a law that amends 39 statutes and regulations.
The Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act - so named even though Ford hasn't had a red tape reduction minister since Parm Gill resigned suddenly Jan. 25 to run federally - also formally breaks the premier's promise to the late Hazel McCallion that he would dissolve Peel Region to make Mississauga, Brampton and Caledon stand-alone cities.
McCallion, the legendary Mississauga mayor, had extracted that pledge from Ford shortly before she died last year at 101.
The Tories announced they were abandoning dissolution in December over concerns about municipal job losses and service cuts.
As well, Calandra's bill enshrines "use it or lose it" provisions to allow cities to withdraw permits for stalled housing developments - and prioritizing new infrastructure such as roads and waterworks for ready-to-go projects - and further streamline approvals for garden suites, laneway housing and basement apartments.
However, the Tories will not mandate as-of-right fourplexes across Ontario despite calls from Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie to do so in the name of "gentle density."
Crombie blasted the legislation as "a random grab-bag of small-ball measures the Ford government could have done years ago if they were serious about building housing."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said it was "a weak bill from a government lacking in the bold vision and leadership that is needed in order to do what they should have done years ago: build at least 1.5 million homes by 2031."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "with this bill, the Ford government is effectively admitting defeat after five years of housing failures."
It includes amendments to the provincial building code to promote mass timber buildings, which are faster and cheaper to build, and modernize Ontario's standards to be more in line with national construction codes. Currently, only 12-storey "advanced wood" buildings are permitted, but that will change to 18 floors.
The province will also allow cities to revamp development charges on condos and many other projects by eliminating a previous five-year phase-in of hikes, but as of June 1 there will finally be exemptions from or discounts of such fees for builders of affordable housing.
That's designed to spur construction and increase revenues to municipalities that had been concerned about previous Tory moves.
Calandra told reporters the government has "listened to our municipal partners" and will ensure civic revenues aren't affected by the changes to development charges.
In a bid to curb NIMBYism, the government will be "limiting third-party appeals to the Ontario Land Tribunal" to get quicker approvals for housing. Some 67,000 housing units, which began being built between 2021 and last year, are being thwarted by third-party appeals of official plans and rezoning.
"The proposed changes would also allow proponents to appeal to the Ontario Land Tribunal when a municipality refuses an application or does not make a decision on a settlement boundary change outside the Greenbelt area, ensuring that decisions over boundary changes are subject to an independent and neutral process," the government said.
Ontario's 23 publicly funded universities will join colleges in being exempted from Planning Act restrictions when it comes to building student housing on campuses and on land they own elsewhere. All universities and colleges will be required to improve accessibility to student housing.
Calandra's legislation - tabled on the six-month anniversary of the RCMP launching a criminal probe of the $8.28-building Greenbelt land swap scandal that has hobbled housing construction - comes as the Tories risk missing their target of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
While the government needs to build an average of 150,000 new homes annually to meet its goal, only 110,000 were built in 2023 - just 73 per cent of what's needed each year.
It is the 12th "red tape reduction bill" introduced by the Tories since they took office in 2018.
The bill contains changes that have nothing to do with housing, including renaming the Province of Ontario Council for the Arts to the Ontario Arts Council, the Ontario Tourism Marketing Partnership Corporation to Destination Ontario, and the Ontario Media Development Corporation to Ontario Creates to "support brand awareness and remove needless complications by matching their legal names with their common, publicly recognized names."
It makes automatic the licence plate renewal for drivers with no outstanding fines after the Star revealed in February there were one million expired plates in Ontario because motorists forget to renew them since there's no longer any fee.
Finally, the threshold of motor vehicle collision damage that must be reported to police will be raised from $2,000 to $5,000. But all crashes where motorists, cyclists or pedestrians are hurt still must be reported to police.
"Raising the amount for damages for reporting collisions where no one is injured to the police will help reduce the administrative burden on drivers, commercial vehicle operators, and police services," the government said.
However, the Tories are not yet reversing their 2019 changes to the building code that zapped mandatory electric vehicle chargers in new homes after property developers objected to the $500 cost.
"It is not my intention at this point to be mandating that recharging," said Calandra.
That's despite the fact the government is spending billions to subsidize EV battery manufacturing in Ontario, including cash for Stellantis and Volkswagen to build factories here.
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks to reporters at the Ontario legislature in Toronto on March 7, 2024.

Subject: Automobile drivers; Colleges & universities; Housing developments; Housing starts; Student housing

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities Housing developments Housing starts

Ford is no model for law and order Premier Doug ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-is-no-model-law-order-premier-doug-derived/docview/3034973202/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+is+no+model+for+law+and+order+Premier+Doug+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford is no model
for law and order
Premier Doug Ford said in the legislature that he is going to double, triple or even quadruple down on people who break the law. This apparently does not apply to his own actions as premier breaking the most fundamental law of the country, the Constitution.
1. He tried to force gas station owners to post his anti-carbon-tax stickers: Struck down by the courts as interfering with free speech.
2. He passed a law trying to micromanage how university student unions were run. Struck down as violating university independence.
3. He tried to legislate away public service collective bargaining rights by restricting them to one per cent pay raises. Struck down as interfering with the constitutional right of freedom of association.
All this does not even include many examples of favouritism and cronyism such as his attempt to get his good friend appointed as OPP commissioner and letting his wealthy buddies in the home-building industry dominate the office of the housing minister.
If he wants to lead by example, he needs to realize that he was elected to serve all the people of Ontario not just the minority who voted for him. It is time for him to triple down on ethical behaviour.
John Roy, Hamilton
We need more
than criticism
Every day in The Spec there are one or two letters to the editor that contain some form of political complaint. And yes, I have written those types of letters myself, be it civic, provincial or federal politicians. My question is, why do the letter writers constantly bash one party only? There are writers who constantly rant about the prime minister but at the same time never complain about the premier. What I don't see or read are letters offering alternatives, just criticism. There are many ways to contact all levels of government, so if you disagree with what is going on put your effort into getting your voice heard instead of complaining.
Mike Lacroix, Hamilton
Ticketing eclipse viewers wrong
It was a spectacular event on Monday. It is too bad the City of Hamilton saw the event as a cash cow. Ticketing people near parks. Yes, they were forced to park on streets where normally there is no parking due to parking lots being filled. Some of the people were from Toronto and presumably other catchment areas who came here just for this event. What kind of impression are they taking away from our city now? It is too bad bylaw officers couldn't have turned a blind eye for 20 minutes.
Mary Hickey, Burlington

Document 294 of 500

Will premiers back Ottawa's housing plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-premiers-back-ottawas-housing-plan/docview/3034973175/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+premiers+back+Ottawa%27s+housing+plan%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Throw stuff at the wall and see what sticks. This appears to be Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's communications strategy in the lead-up to the federal government's budget rollout next week. In fact, nowhere is this mad dash, campaign-style, policy push more pronounced than on the housing file.
In recent weeks the federal government has launched a $6-billion infrastructure fund to speed up housing construction; it has promised a renters' "bill of rights," that would establish a national standard lease agreement; it has announced $15 million in funding for provincial legal aid for tenants; it has announced $600 million in loans, in part to fast track the construction of prefabricated homes; it has announced a $15-billion top-up to its Apartment Construction Loan Program.
For it is plainly obvious that these announcements have not come out of the blue. The prime minister is trying to court young voters who are struggling amid a national housing crisis and lending their support to federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as a result (the Conservative leader hasn't been shy about his plans to "build homes fast").
Yet despite the rather obvious political calculation inherent in the announcements, many of Trudeau's policy planks - particularly where renters are concerned - are positive and welcome news. Additional funding for legal aid for renters - an often overlooked, demographic - is helpful and necessary. So is the fact the federal government clearly views housing as a national priority. After all, Canada's housing woes intensified when the federal government exited the affordable housing business.
It is a great thing it wants back in. The problem though is not the prime minister's motivation, but his methods, pumping out piecemeal policy announcements in quick succession to maximize publicity ahead of the release of the federal budget.
Indeed, the stream of announcements perfectly summarizes much of what is wrong with Canada's approach to the housing crisis. Fragmented policy will not get us out of this crisis. The only way forward is through holistic collaboration and problem solving with provincial and municipal governments at every step.
And the fact remains that while many of the federal government's policies could benefit Canadians, they require buy-in from other levels of government that are not always eager to collaborate. A prime example of this conflict is playing out right now in Ontario where Premier Doug Ford has declared his opposition to "forcing" municipalities in the province to allow fourplexes, residential buildings with four units inside. The federal government's infrastructure fund announcement comes with conditions for provinces and territories, one of them being fourplex legalization across municipalities.
Ford, however, is adamant he won't play along; he told reporters communities in Ontario would "lose their minds" if fourplexes were allowed as a right. In other words, though we may perceive the issue as hyperlocal, the sheer possibility that voters will cry "Not in my backyard" in the face of new development has the power to impact policy at a provincial and national level.
Trudeau insists he won't let provincial intransigence get in the way. "If a province decides it doesn't want to be ambitious on housing, that's their decision. We will work with the municipalities within that province that are ambitious."
But it isn't just Ford who is objecting to what he perceives to be federal government overreach. According to reporting by the Toronto Star's Tonda MacCharles, the prime minister's recent announcements on several files, from health care to housing, has angered some of Canada's premiers; one provincial source described the situation as "a risk for national unity."
However, at the end of the day the risk is not to national unity but to a Canadian public that is at the mercy of leaders who refuse to co-operate with one another.
The housing crisis in Canada is often described as intractable and complex. And it is. But there is one truth about the crisis that is straight forward: it will take all three levels of government working together in good faith to make a difference. Until that happens Canada's housing crisis will rage on unabated, no matter how many news conferences the prime minister calls.
CAPTION:

Document 295 of 500

'It's misleading': Ford government ridiculed for including student and retirement units in new homes target

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/misleading-ford-government-ridiculed-including/docview/3035279901/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+misleading%27%3A+Ford+government+ridiculed+for+including+student+and+retirement+units+in+new+homes+target&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Scrambling to improve slow progress on new housing starts, Premier Doug Ford's government will include student and retirement units toward its goal of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
But opposition parties are crying foul, saying those types of accommodation don't make the grade because Ontarians struggling to buy homes in a supply and affordability crisis can't raise a family in them.
"What are they going to count next ... jail cells?" New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said Tuesday as she criticized Ford for fighting fourplexes as a way to improve the housing supply.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra - who on Wednesday will announce new measures to "cut red tape and help municipalities build" - pushed back at critics with his own argument.
"Obviously, student housing is very important," he said.
"Every time we build new housing, or a college or university campus builds new housing, that is more housing that is available in the community. I don't think that's a very difficult concept to follow."
In a recent letter to acting Mississauga Mayor Joe Horneck, Calandra said the province is figuring out how to get statistics on student and retirement housing starts.
"We will continue to explore data sources for tracking the numbers of other institutional types of housing such as student residences and retirement homes for future program years and commit to engaging municipalities on the same," he wrote March 27.
To meet its 1.5-million target, the province needs to build an average of 150,000 new homes annually.
But the last two years, the actual numbers were well below that, with 109,111 in 2023 and 80,300 in 2022. Soaring interest rates and higher building costs driven by inflation were factors.
"What we're going to do is double down on removing red tape, putting sewer and water infrastructure in the ground, working with our municipal partners to build not only hundreds of homes but millions of homes," Calandra said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, a former three-term mayor of Mississauga, accused the government of "trying to prop up their numbers with dorms and retirement units because they're not meeting their (housing) targets."
"It's misleading, and it's just a shell game," she told a news conference at Queen's Park.
"You can't even have a microwave in a dorm room. My goodness, that is not a home," Stiles said in an exchange with Calandra in the legislature's daily question period.
"We absolutely need to build more housing options for students. But this government's attempt to pad the numbers by including dorms as part of the housing targets, I think Ontarians will see through that."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner mused whether the government might go further.
"At this point, the government is going to start counting tents," he quipped.
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks to reporters outside the Ontario Legislature in Toronto on March 7, 2024.

Subject: Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts

Title: 'It's misleading': Ford government ridiculed for including student and retirement units in new homes target

Document 296 of 500

'It's very, very strange': Key figures in Doug Ford's $8.28B Greenbelt land swap are still waiting for RCMP questions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/very-strange-key-figures-doug-fords-8-28b/docview/3035279837/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+very%2C+very+strange%27%3A+Key+figures+in+Doug+Ford%27s+%248.28B+Greenbelt+land+swap+are+still+waiting+for+RCMP+questions&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It has been six months since the Mounties began investigating the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, but they have yet to interview the key players.
While the cloud of a criminal investigation has loomed over Premier Doug Ford's government since Oct. 10, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police are tight-lipped on how things are progressing.
The RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, will only confirm the probe is ongoing.
Two cabinet ministers and two top aides were forced to part ways with Ford over the imbroglio.
"I haven't heard a word from anyone - it's very, very strange," former municipal affairs and housing minister Steve Clark said Tuesday.
Kaleed Rasheed - who quit as Ford's business minister and no longer sits as a Progressive Conservative MPP after revelations about a 2020 Las Vegas trip with a Greenbelt developer pal - declined to comment.
But friends confided Rasheed has not been contacted by the Mounties and said the Independent Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP feels he's been "treated unfairly since he did nothing" except get the date of the Las Vegas jaunt wrong. He is hoping to return to the Tory caucus.
Ford's office said the premier has not heard from the RCMP investigators.
Clark, who stepped down from cabinet on Labour Day, reiterated his willingness to co-operate with the Mounties and clear the air.
"I'm not aware of anyone who has been contacted," said the veteran Tory MPP for Leeds-Grenville-Thousand Islands and Rideau Lakes.
"I've talked to both the auditor general and the integrity commissioner. Neither of them indicated they have forwarded anything to them that is in regard to me in any way," he said.
"But, hey, if they want to talk to me, I'm here."
The Mounties swept in after Ford initially allowed development on 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land that is part of the Greenbelt, a two-million-acre swath around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
Last August, then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake released separate reports revealing certain developers with Tory ties were "favoured" in an unusual process led by Clark's chief of staff Ryan Amato.
Amato, who has maintained he did nothing wrong, resigned that month.
Another key aide, Jae Truesdell - who was in Las Vegas with Rasheed, the premier's then-principal secretary Amin Massoudi and developer Shakir Rehmatullah - stepped down in September as Ford's housing adviser.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss an ongoing police investigation, said the Mounties haven't reached out to any of them.
With the start of the June 2026 election campaign only about two years away, the Tories are watching the calendar closely, mindful that any developments on the investigation could have political ramifications.
"The RCMP isn't going fast, but they're not going away," conceded a government insider.
But the Tories are encouraged public attention seems to have moved on from a debacle that roiled Ford for months.
According to Abacus Data's monthly polling for the Star, the governing party has recovered from its tailspin last summer.
At that time, the Tories' support plunged from 41 per cent in July to 38 per cent in August to 34 per cent in September.
Last month's Abacus tracking poll found the Tories back at 41 per cent support, to 27 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 21 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and seven per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
Abacus president and CEO David Coletto has said the premier's Sept. 21 cancellation of the Greenbelt land swap and his public apology for the fiasco boosted the party's standing.
On that day, Ford said "it was a mistake to open the Greenbelt."
"I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," he said at the time.
"As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives."
The RCMP took over a review of the matter on Aug. 23 after the Ontario Provincial Police, which is funded by Queen's Park and staffs the premier's security detail, recused itself "to avoid any potential perceived conflict of interest."
But the actual investigation wasn't launched for another seven weeks.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as Steve Clark, thenOntario's minister of housing,speaks during a news conference in Mississauga on Aug. 11, 2023.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals; Mounted police

Location: United States--US; Las Vegas Nevada

Company / organization: Name: Royal Canadian Mounted Police; NAICS: 922120

Document 297 of 500

Doug Ford can fix your LCBO bag woes - just don't expect him to do the important part of his job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-can-fix-your-lcbo-bag-woes-just-dont/docview/3035279628/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+can+fix+your+LCBO+bag+woes+-+just+don%27t+expect+him+to+do+the+important+part+of+his+job&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's no problem too small for the premier to solve. Personally.
LCBO stopped giving out free paper bags to customers?
No problem - this week, the premier persuaded it to reverse course. Now he's bragging about bringing back bagging.
From buck-a-beer to bags for booze, you have to admire Ford's talent for salesmanship and showmanship on the small. Just not his leadership on the big issues, like higher education.
Last week, you may have heard Ford proudly announcing a new medical school for York University to be built in Vaughan. Another day, another victory lap:
"I can't wipe the smile from my face," he crowed.
On paper, the new medical school is a good idea in bad times - 2.3 million Ontarians still need a family doctor. But the fine print and minuscule numbers tell a different story.
Sure, there's $9 million for initial planning, spread over four years (which works out to about $2.2 million a year), and a promise to cover capital and operating spending when the time comes - count on it.
But Ford is deeply conflicted about the campuses of Ontario. He can't decide whether he wants to build them up or tear them down.
York is promised a new medical school - money to come. Yet it is drowning in $600 million of red ink, while other campuses sink into chaos.
At least eight universities across Ontario are looking at deficits in 2023-24, for a combined total of $152 million. And some 12 universities are projecting operating deficits in 2024-25, adding up to $293 million.
This is a crisis created entirely by Ford's Progressive Conservative government. Playing the populist card in 2019, the premier decreed that universities and colleges must cut tuition by 10 per cent, followed by a freeze every year thereafter, through 2026.
Ford is playing Robin Hood in reverse - making universities ever more affordable for affluent families, while neglecting student aid for the poor who still afford the fees, with or without a freeze. (Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University and the Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy.)
A government cannot in good faith compel universities to cut fees without making up the difference in lost revenues. Instead, while bleeding campuses all those years, the Tories goaded and incentivized them to tap into seemingly limitless foreign student revenues.
The government gave them carte blanche to raise tuition for overseas students at sometimes triple the rate charged for domestic students - fostering greed and gouging. Today, as my Star colleague Kristin Rushowy has documented, foreign students make up more than half of the student body at many colleges, and as much as one-quarter at some universities.
That's too much of a good thing. The result of this overreach - and the strain on housing - was a recent federal cap on foreign student visas and work permits that has hit post-secondary schools in Ontario harder than anywhere else in Canada.
Searching for a fix last year, the government appointed a blue-ribbon panel that called for a compromise - a five per cent tuition increase and a $2.5-billion funding infusion to help campuses catch up.
But Ford rejected the tuition increase outright, refusing to unfreeze revenues. His government grudgingly allocated barely $700 million (or roughly $233 million a year) to be divvied up among 47 campuses in the post-secondary - a fraction of what the panel had suggested.
So how did the premier personally react to these recommendations from a panel of his own creation, dealing with a mess of his own making?
Speaking at the York medical school announcement, Ford lashed out at foreign students taking up valuable spots in Ontario, displacing locals and upending the student mix in medical schools.
"My number one pet peeve ... I just want to support Ontario students. And God bless everyone else coming to our country ... (who) pays a little more," he began.
All this from a premier who has made foreign students - and foreign revenues - a pillar of his post-secondary strategy in Ontario while freezing tuition domestically and starving schools of funding.
"I understand that money pays for some of the local students right now ... what is the percentage, it's 18 per cent?" he mused, muddling the numbers. "Get rid of the 18 per cent. I'm not being mean, but I'm taking care of our students - our kids - first."
Our kids first?
"I want 100 per cent Ontario students going to these universities," Ford vowed.
And so the premier who engineered the massive expansion of foreign students throughout the system now complains about the imbalance in our medical schools.
He promises another quick fix - just like he fixed the disappearing bags at the LCBO. Except that Ford had his chance to repair the damage when his own blue ribbon panel recommended a road map to stabilizing Ontario's colleges and universities.
Rejecting that blueprint, Ford is slowly dismantling the province's post-secondary system, while bankrolling the construction of a new medical school. Count on it.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks during a funding announcement on April 5, 2024 in Ottawa.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding; College campuses; Medical schools

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Document 298 of 500

April 9: Plasma centre should be welcomed, premier provides a poison pill and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/april-9-plasma-centre-should-be-welcomed-premier/docview/3034751171/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=April+9%3A+Plasma+centre+should+be+welcomed%2C+premier+provides+a+poison+pill+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Plasma centre should be welcomed
Re: Mayor looks to declare Hamilton a 'paid-plasma-free zone' (April 5)
Mayor Andrea Horwath gets it exactly backward when she says paying for plasma donations will "prey on folks who are in need of resources and not in the best financial shape." In fact, the opposite is true. It offers people in need a way to boost their income and have more resources.
I give plasma regularly. It is safe and easy. It doesn't exploit me or take anything from me except what my body replaces. And then I give again. I do it as a volunteer with Canadian Blood Services. My plasma goes to patients whose lives depend on it.
Canada needs additional sources of plasma. It is required to produce immunoglobulins needed by some Canadians to maintain their health. The current supply is inadequate. This proposed way to increase the supply of plasma has the benefit of also providing additional income for people who need it - it doesn't exploit them. It would not take anything from them that their bodies wouldn't replace effortlessly. It is welcomed by the CBS. It is safe and causes no harm, only positive benefits.
Rather than being something sinister that "preys upon the most vulnerable," as Horwath said, this is a win-win proposition. It should be welcomed.
David Mivasair, Hamilton
Rising costs no surprise
My nominee for least surprising headline of the year is from Saturday's Page A1: "Costs for big transit projects like LRT are spiking: MTO docs." And in the story, take note that the "overview document" has redacted the current estimate. So we're not even allowed to see their current guess as to how much of our money this white-elephant-to-be will swallow.
And here I thought four years to rebuild a park washroom was ridiculous.
Tony Carlson, Waterdown
LRT not a priority right now
The LRT saga continues. I hope I'm not the only one scratching my head and questioning the reasoning behind implementing this ludicrous project.
With so many other social problems going on in this city, along with provincially and federally, how can this sitting government in good conscience allocate billions of dollars to a project that will not benefit the city as a whole? Use this money to shelter those that cannot afford a roof over their heads because of astronomical rents and interest rates.
These are the important things, not a train that will take one from Point A to Point B with nothing in between but boarded up businesses and empty lots.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Premier provides poison pill
Judging by the critical reaction to Hamilton's Andrea Horwath flexing her "strong mayor" power, it seems that Premier Doug Ford provided his former Queen's Park rival a poison pill. The legislation may turn out to be more like kryptonite than a super power for Howarth come the next civic election, especially among Stoney Creek residents.
Doug Foley, Hamilton
Discount not seen in store
Re: The Loblaw saga: Corporate missteps in the grocery aisles (March 19)
In the commentary by Sylvain Charlebois, he mentions Loblaws had to backtrack on its decision to end the 50-per-cent discount on expiring food. Unfortunately in Burlington, the Fortinos stores are still using 30 per cent off stickers on many expiring products. When I asked employees why, I was told they were just doing what they were told to do. When I asked to speak to the manager, I was told he was busy.
I waited to see if things would change, but when I went into the Aldershot store at the end of last week, I found that they were still using the 30 per cent stickers.
Shame on Loblaws for not following through on what it had promised.
Linda Palichuk, Burlington
Put parking lot to good use
A recent letter writer correctly suggests that the mayor use her "super powers" to fix the city's dilapidated housing stock. Examples being the Jamesville housing project, the vacant Vrancor property on King Street and there is also city housing boarded up on Upper Wentworth.
While this is slowly progressing through the bureaucracy, I have a temporary suggestion. There is a huge parking lot located beside St. Mary's high school near the bicycle path. It is all securely fenced. Every time I walk past, there is not one car in the lot.
How about renting the lot and putting some trailers into it, one of them with a nurse and security? This would take care of many of the homeless and could be a temporary solution while "affordable housing" gets sorted out. It would also help the medical teams keep track of the health and welfare of the underprivileged.
Just a suggestion, but it seems more than what is coming out of our council.
Andy Price, Ancaster,
Keep the satire coming
Re: The vital role of satire in upholding democratic values (April 6)
I always look forward to Graeme MacKay's editorial cartoons. Graeme, you shouldn't have to apologize for satire. I love the thought provoking themes while making fun. Of course, you will upset some of the readers, it goes with the territory. There will always be readers offended by your cartoons. They get offended by a lot of things The Spectator does, but thank God we still have a viable newspaper. Keep on keeping on.
Eleanor Blackborow, Flamborough
CAPTION: Mayor Andrea Horwath is looking to have Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone" and prohibit private for-profit blood collection companies from operating in the city.

Subject: Plasma; Editorial cartoons; Affordable housing

Document 299 of 500

National briefs: Paramedic among dead

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3034629338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Paramedic+among+dead&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Agency knew China interfered

Canada's spy agency knew China "clandestinely and deceptively interfered" in the past two federal votes, says a briefing document that emerged Monday at the public inquiry into foreign interference. The heavily redacted, six-page document is dated February 2023 and carries the title, "Briefing to the Prime Minister's Office on foreign interference threats to Canada's democratic institutions." It was prepared by the Canadian Security and Intelligence Service, the inquiry heard. Earlier Monday, government officials said the information they received about interference activities did not meet the high threshold for warning Canadians.
Ontario
Premier tells LCBO to bring back paper bags
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has directed the Liquor Control Board of Ontario to immediately reverse its decision to get rid of paper bags. The LCBO announced in April 2023 that it would start phasing out the bags,. Ford has told the LCBO families are already struggling to make ends meet and they should not face the additional cost of having to buy reusable bags.
Paramedic among dead
An Ontario county has confirmed that a local paramedic was among the Canadians killed after being caught in an avalanche while on vacation in Switzerland. Officials in Perth County say Nikky Nagy was "a bright, caring, amazing person." The Canadian military has said Capt. Sean Thomas is presumed dead after going missing on April 1. Local authorities had reported three people were killed.
News services

Document 300 of 500

When African Lion Safari meets Germany's Oktoberfest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/when-african-lion-safari-meets-germanys/docview/3034627890/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+African+Lion+Safari+meets+Germany%27s+Oktoberfest&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It would be strange to see an elephant at Yonge-Dundas Square.
Luckily, Yonge-Dundas Square is not in Germany and Canada has not annoyed Botswana. I feel like I'm in high school and working on a geography paper. Or a project on animal diplomacy. Or an essay on bizarre international threats.
A CNN headline this week: "Botswana Threatens to Send 20,000 Elephants to Germany ..."
It seems Germany has recently proposed stricter limits on hunting trophy imports over fears of poaching. This has annoyed Mokgweetsi Masisi, Botswana's president.
As he told the German newspaper Bild: "It is very easy to sit in Berlin and have an opinion about our affairs in Botswana. We are paying the price for preserving these animals for the world."
Let me preface this by stating I am an animal lover. I drove my daughters to school this morning and spotted a poodle out for a walk in hot pink boots and matching coat. I couldn't stop laughing. Animals give us our humanity. They should always be protected.
That said, President Masisi makes a valid point.
Thanks to conservation efforts and a military crackdown on big game hunting, Botswana's elephant population has almost tripled to 130,000 since 1984. If my high school math is right, that's about 65 million kilograms of elephants in a territory of 581,000 square kilometres.
That's a lot of per capita elephant. Living in parts of Botswana is like driving a Honda Odyssey with a camel in the back. There is no room for other passengers.
So what is the human leader supposed to do? Elephant herds are destroying property. They are trampling residents. They are creating food shortages by feasting on crops. They are wreaking havoc on infrastructure. A telephone pole to an elephant is a toothpick to us.
We whine about raccoons in Toronto. Imagine if you looked out your patio doors and a five-ton, tusked behemoth was chomping your maple tree after crushing your patio sectional from Canadian Tire. You think traffic is a mess now? Imagine if the DVP was shut down in rush hour because 40 elephants were sunbathing and playing with groundhogs.
Botswana doesn't want to hurt its elephants. Botswana loves its elephants. It just doesn't know what to do with its elephants because it has too many elephants. Angola may take 8,000. Mozambique is pondering 500.
But that's still not enough.
A few years ago, Zimbabwe basically had a global wildlife sale of exotic animals. All ecosystems were strained by overpopulation and drought. The country made $2.7 million by just selling 90 elephants to China and Dubai. That revenue was poured back into conservation programs. Everyone was happy, including the travelling elephants spared an execution.
The planet needs biodiversity. We should do everything we can to protect endangered species. But it's not really fair to expect certain countries to shoulder this burden if the quality of human life plummets as a consequence.
Mr. Masisi is not threatening to send 20,000 elephants to Germany this week because he wants to ignite chaos at the next Oktoberfest. (As an aside, remind me to tell you the story about how a very nice and possibly colour-blind white supremacist once showed me around Munich. I learned quite a lot about the Rathaus-Glockenspiel that afternoon.)
Masisi is frustrated. He's got elephants coming out the wazoo.
It's easy for the rest of us to care about animals when we are darting in and out of condos and offices on land once roamed by mammoths and mastodons, dire wolves and short-faced bears. We have the luxury of championing animals we do not live among.
Neighbourhood cats terrorize my bird feeders. It would be even more stressful if I had to be on the lookout for sabre-toothed tigers. Rhinos are majestic beasts. But all things considered, I'd prefer to not see one charging my car in the parking lot at Best Buy.
Elephants, the largest land animal, are truly amazing. According to the WWF, they have 150,000 muscles in their trunks. Newborn calves can walk an hour after birth - my daughters were still face-planting into ottomans after 14 months. Elephants have empathy and photo memory.
But elephants also take up a lot of space and resources.
So if the world wants elephants, the world should help find suitable habitats for elephants when their home countries are at max occupancy.
If Ontario can have one African Lion Safari, why can't we have 10 more? Hey, Botswana, send a few elephants to Cambridge. Doug Ford will sort it out.
If every country framed animal conservation as global and not local, Botswana would not need to fantasize about putting 20,000 elephants on Lufthansa flights between Gaborone and Hamburg. Botswana would get the help it deserves.
Elephants do not want to emigrate. But it's preferable to getting killed.
CAPTION: So if the world wants elephants, the world should help find suitable habitats for elephants when their home countries are at max occupancy. If Ontario can have one African Lion Safari, why can't we have 10 more? Hey, Botswana, send a few elephants to Cambridge. Doug Ford will sort it out, writes Vinay Menon.MONIRUL BHUIYANAFP VIA GETTY IMAGES file photo

CREDIT: Vinay Menon OPINION

Full text: Lets welcome plasma centre
Re: Mayor looks to declare Hamilton
a 'paid-plasma-free zone' (April 5)
Mayor Andrea Horwath gets it exactly backward when she says paying for plasma donations will "prey on folks who are in need of resources and not in the best financial shape." In fact, the opposite is true. It offers people in need a way to boost their income and have more resources.
I give plasma regularly. It is safe and easy. It doesn't exploit me or take anything from me except what my body replaces. And then I give again. I do it as a volunteer with Canadian Blood Services. My plasma goes to patients whose lives depend on it.
Canada needs additional sources of plasma. It is required to produce immunoglobulins needed by some Canadians to maintain their health. The current supply is inadequate. This proposed way to increase the supply of plasma has the benefit of also providing additional income for people who need it - it doesn't exploit them. It would not take anything from them that their bodies wouldn't replace effortlessly. It is welcomed by the CBS. It is safe and causes no harm, only positive benefits.
Rather than being something sinister that "preys upon the most vulnerable," as Horwath said, this is a win-win proposition. It should be welcomed.
David Mivasair, Hamilton

Discount not seen in store
Re: The Loblaw saga: Corporate
missteps in the grocery aisles (March 19)
In the commentary by Sylvain Charlebois, he mentions Loblaws had to backtrack on its decision to end the 50 per cent discount on expiring food. Shame on Loblaws for not following through on what it had promised.
Linda Palichuk, Burlington
Keep the satire coming
Re: The vital role of satire
in upholding democratic values (April 6)
I always look forward to Graeme MacKay's editorial cartoons. Graeme, you shouldn't have to apologize for satire. I love the thought provoking themes while making fun.
Of course, you will upset some of the readers, it goes with the territory. There will always be readers offended by your cartoons. They get offended by a lot of things The Spectator does, but thank God we still have a viable newspaper. Keep on keeping on.
Eleanor Blackborow, Flamborough

Document 302 of 500

Doug Ford bristles at questions about $100K salaries in his office

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-bristles-at-questions-about-100k/docview/3034824021/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+bristles+at+questions+about+%24100K+salaries+in+his+office&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is firing back at critics after the number of premier's office staff making at least $100,000 a year has more than doubled to 48 people.
"The gravy train has pulled into the premier's office," Liberal MPP John Fraser (Ottawa South) charged Monday as the legislature resumed sitting for the first time since the annual "Sunshine List" of $100,000 earners was released.
"At a time when Ontario families are struggling, how does this premier justify increasing the staff?"
Ford, elected in 2018 on a mantra of "respect for taxpayers," bristled at the question in a testy exchange with Fraser.
"The nerve of the member, to say about saving taxpayers' money," the premier shot back.
"Your leader, Bonnie Crombie, called the tax break that we gave on gas a gimmick," Ford added in a bid to deflect attention from the issue.
Crombie said last week that Ford is jeopardizing jobs of the future in Ontario with "reckless" underfunding of colleges and universities as several run deficits.
She questioned whether the gas tax cut, which costs the provincial treasury $1.2 billion a year as the government notched a $9.8-billion deficit, could be better spent on improving health care and education with 2.3 million Ontarians unable to find a family doctor.
Ford did not explain why the number of premier's office staff has grown so dramatically under his Progressive Conservatives.
In 2019, his first full year in government, there were 20 staff in the premier's office earning at least $100,000 - two more than former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne.
But by last year, there were 48 staff on the list, for a total of $6.9 million in annual salaries, up from $2.9 million in 2019.
Fraser noted later that Ford did not try to defend or explain the increase at a time when Ontario's annual deficit has soared to $9.8 billion.
"He couldn't say it, because he knows it's wrong."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at the Ottawa Board of Trade on March 28, 2024.

Document 303 of 500

Doug Ford forces LCBO to reinstate paper bags for customers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-forces-lcbo-reinstate-paper-bags/docview/3034468517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+forces+LCBO+to+reinstate+paper+bags+for+customers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The LCBO will resume giving paper bags to customers after the ban was sacked by Premier Doug Ford.
In an unusual intervention with the operations of the Crown-owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario, Ford ordered the booze giant to rethink its bag policy that took effect last September.
"I'm requesting that you take immediate steps to reverse the decision to remove paper bags from the LCBO's retail locations," the premier wrote Sunday in a terse letter to George Soleas, president and CEO of the company.
"At a time when many Ontario families are already struggling to make ends meet, every additional expense counts. That includes charging customers for reusable bags instead of the free paper bags that the LCBO previously offered," wrote Ford.
"This change has left people stuck openly carrying alcohol in public when leaving a LCBO store," added the premier, a teetotaler.
The LCBO, which first announced the phasing out of the paper bags last spring, confirmed Monday it "has received direction from the provincial government to take steps to reintroduce single-use paper bags" at its more than 680 outlets.
"While we are not able to confirm an availability date at this time, we will share more details with our valued customers in the coming weeks," the company said.
Last April 27, Soleas said the scheme would remove about 135 million paper bags from circulation, diverting 2,665 tonnes of waste from landfill.
The company maintained at the time that it would save the equivalent of more than 188,000 trees annually.
It followed the decision to stop providing customers plastic bags in 2008.
But Ford said Sunday that "the environmental merits of this decision are questionable at best."
"Paper bags are an easily recyclable alternative to single-use plastic, which is why the LCBO adopted them in the first place," he reminded Soleas.
"As a government, we are focused on making life easier, more convenient, and more affordable for the people of Ontario. The decision to remove paper bags has had the opposite effect."
CAPTION: File photo: Premier Doug Ford.
