Document 316 of 500

Mayor looks to declare Hamilton a 'paid-plasma-free zone' to stop private Spanish clinic from opening

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mayor-looks-declare-hamilton-paid-plasma-free/docview/3033041372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+looks+to+declare+Hamilton+a+%27paid-plasma-free+zone%27+to+stop+private+Spanish+clinic+from+opening&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Andrea Horwath is looking to have Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone" to stop a private, for-profit Spanish company from opening a blood collection centre in the city that she says will "prey on folks who are in need of resources and not in the best financial shape."
"By taking a stand like this it sends a pretty clear message," Horwath told The Spectator. "Anything that preys upon the most vulnerable is hideous and doesn't belong in Hamilton."
A notice of motion was put forward on the mayor's behalf at Tuesday's public health committee meeting that would ban operation of private, for-profit clinics paying donors for plasma in the city.
"It's an important conversation to have in our community," said Horwath. "To take a stand to say, 'No this is not Hamilton's values.'"
The mayor said she expects the city's ban would be respected if it passed when the motion is debated and voted on at a future committee meeting.
However, it's not clear if Canadian Blood Services (CBS) would change its plan. CBS did not answer The Spectator's questions about whether the Hamilton clinic would still open next year if city council voted to block it. Instead, CBS said the only applicable legislation was the province's Voluntary Blood Donations Act.
That law prohibits paying for plasma donation in Ontario but CBS has declared itself exempt from the act as the charity that runs the blood system nationally. Therefore, CBS says the health-care company from Spain is also exempt because Grifols is acting as its agent when it opens up pay-for-plasma collection sites in Hamilton, Cambridge, Whitby and two other yet-to-be-determined Ontario locations.
A spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones confirmed to The Spectator in March that the centres legally conform to the legislation.
Horwath questioned why more isn't being done to enforce the law that she called for in 2014 as the leader of the NDP at that time. It was passed by the Liberal government to shut down a pay-for-plasma clinic built in Hamilton at 370 Main St. E. - and two more in downtown Toronto - by private company Canadian Plasma Resources.
"The best path is to close the loophole, not to allow these private companies to come in here and start paying for plasma donations," said Horwath. "We're going backwards if we allow this to happen."
At issue is a controversial plan by CBS to reduce the country's reliance on the global market for plasma, which is a component of blood. The plan also aims to ensure Canadians have access to immunoglobulins, which is a medication made from plasma.
"There is a global shortage of life-saving immunoglobulins and the plasma needed to make them," CBS said in a statement to The Spectator. "Plasma donors who are paid are a significant part of the global industry."
The plan involves CBS opening 11 of its own unpaid centres while Grifols opens 17 pay-for-plasma sites nationally. Grifols would also manufacture the plasma collected at the sites into immunoglobulins exclusively for Canadian use at its Montreal plant once it opens in 2026.
"This approach protects the blood system, addresses a global shortage of critical medications, and secures a domestic supply chain of life-saving immunoglobulins for patients in Canada," stated CBS. "This is a necessary and positive outcome."
But Horwath questions the safety of the plan, pointing to the recommendations against paid donation in the 1997 report of the Krever Commission. The commission of inquiry was in response to the tainted blood scandal in the 1980s that left more than 30,000 Canadians infected with HIV and hepatitis C through blood transfusions and plasma products.
"We've learned really difficult and tragic lessons from the private involvement in blood donation and plasma," said Horwath. "Why go down the same dangerous path again? We have put in place public policy that speaks to the national values around donations of free will - not out of economic desperation."
CBS argues "advancements in technology and strict standards of regulatory compliance have made plasma products from paid donors as safe as those that use plasma from unpaid donors."
"All collections centres in Canada are licensed and regulated by Health Canada," stated CBS. "This includes requirements on donor eligibility criteria and how frequently plasma can be collected from each donor, in addition to donor centre inspections."
All sites - including the one planned for Hamilton - were chosen "after careful analysis," said CBS. However, Horwath says that to her knowledge city council was never consulted. CBS did not answer The Spectator's questions about whether it has had any talks with the city.
"Site selection criteria ... include factors such as population size, proximity to post-secondary institutions, and access to public transit," stated CBS.
The pay-for-plasma sites were only approved in areas where they would not interfere with unpaid centres, said CBS, and where there was "no obvious donor or employee safety or security concerns, and no obvious shelters or unsavoury businesses adjacent to the location."
CBS has not provided the exact location for the centre but documents obtained by BloodWatch.org suggest it will be located at 2255 Barton St. E., near the Red Hill Valley Parkway and Nash Road North.
The freedom-of-information documents suggest the provincial Ministry of Health doesn't know how much Grifols plans to pay donors. However, ministry staff found the company's existing centre in Winnipeg gives out $75 to $100 per donation and adds an extra $50 for referring friends.
If Horwath is successful in getting Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone," the resolution would be sent to CBS, the federal and provincial ministers of health, Grifols and all Ontario municipalities.
"That is a major step to stop Grifols from opening in Hamilton," said Kat Lanteigne, executive director of BloodWatch.org, a not-for-profit organization that champions a voluntary, public blood system. "We will be advocating for the motion to pass."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Canadian Blood Services (CBS) has declared itself exempt from Ontario's ban on playing for plasma.Mayor Andrea Horwath is looking to have Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone" and prohibit private for-profit blood collection companies from operating in the city.Mayor Andrea Horwath is looking to have Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone" and prohibit private for-profit blood collection companies from operating in the city.

Document 317 of 500

Fire, EMS, library face $418M funding gap: city report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fire-ems-library-face-418m-funding-gap-city/docview/3033041313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fire%2C+EMS%2C+library+face+%24418M+funding+gap%3A+city+report&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More sticker shock is on the horizon as Hamilton continues to take stock of its municipal infrastructure.
This time, it's an estimated $418-million "funding gap" over 10 years for firefighting, paramedic and transit services.
That assessment is the latest in an ongoing provincially mandated exercise for municipalities to draft asset management plans for everything that's on their books.
In 2022, staff pegged the 10-year funding gulf for "core assets" - roads, bridges, water, wastewater and stormwater pipes and plants - at $1.95 billion.
"Without attention to that gap and without a strategy of how to close the gap, it will just get worse and we could find ourselves in quite a situation," Patricia Leishman, director of corporate asset management, told council Wednesday.
The infrastructure hole has deepened over decades of underspending, due, in part, to a lack of funding from upper levels of government, but also big jumps in population, a staff report explained.
"Throwing money at it is obviously the easiest way to go," Leishman said. But not the only way, she added, pointing to other strategies.
"Are we providing a level of service that's maybe too high? ... Are we over-maintaining our assets? Are we giving too much in areas where maybe we don't need to?
"So that funding gap is not necessarily 100 per cent looked at from the need for dollars. It can be looked at in terms of the need to reassess what level we're providing."
Understanding life-cycle costs is also crucial, senior program analyst Amber Dewar emphasized.
It's often the case that the acquisition represents 10 to 20 per cent of the overall cost of an asset, Dewar said.
"But actually, 70 to 80 per cent of the cost of an asset's whole life are the operations and maintenance phase."
So while the purchase might be steeper for one model, it could end up costing less to maintain down the road, and last longer than the cheaper model.
"If we don't understand the whole list cost of assets when we acquire them, how can we budget to maintain levels of service?"
Here's what the latest phase of analysis found:
In particular, the HSR faces a $163-million gap over 10 years, driven mostly by the cost of new shelters and roughly 15 buses added to the fleet annually, as well as the facility costs, Leishman noted.The fire department faces a narrower gap of $37 million, which is primarily rooted in facility needs, Leishman said.For paramedics, the gulf is $118 million, with the renewal of Station 30 on Victoria Avenue North and additional staffing costs to keep up with population growth the main factors.Two end-of-life Bookmobiles, facility maintenance work and future acquisitions are behind the public library's $100-million shortfall, Leishman noted.
Corporate real estate was also included in the latest phase of study, but the division doesn't have a funding gap, a distinction related to the fact that it owns municipal land, like the airport property, but not necessarily the assets on it.
The quality of data used to study the state of assets is a weak point, which can greatly influence the analysis of funding gaps, Leishman advised.
Transit, for instance, has "pockets of reasonable data" but also significant voids, notably when it comes to the purchase price of tools in its maintenance shop, she said.
"It's hard to go back historically and gather that information for previous assets at times."
The funding-gap discussion follows budget talks that saw city officials reduce a preliminary 14.2 per cent tax hike to 7.9 per cent and eventually 5.8 per cent, in part, by relying more heavily on reserves.
As of late, the city has been "left holding the bag" to pay for future growth, thanks to the provincial government's breaks on development charges, Coun. Brad Clark remarked Wednesday.
"What's going to happen to the lowly resident who's paying taxes, either in their rent or their home ownership?"
Provincial legislation, Bill 23, in particular, has reduced the city's proceeds from development charges, which are used to pay for growth-related infrastructure to the tune of $34 million, noted Mike Zegarac, general manager of finance and corporate services.
Premier Doug Ford recently announced $17.5 million for Hamilton from the Ontario Building Faster Fund for meeting housing targets, but there's still a gap of about 50 per cent when it comes to development charge revenue, Zegarac noted.
Meanwhile, the problem of mounting costs is the product of years of inaction, Coun. Maureen Wilson suggested.
To illustrate this, Wilson alluded to the housing and climate crises.
"The solutions were there decades ago, and the cumulative impact of not acting and not thinking about future generations has created the place where we are now."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: HSR faces a funding gap of $163 million over 10 years, mostly driven by the cost of new buses and shelters.Coun. Maureen Wilson says years of inaction has saddled future generations with mounting infrastructure costs.Two end-of-life Bookmobiles, facility maintenance work and future acquisitions are behind the public library's $100-million shortfall.For EMS, the gulf is $118 million, with the renewal of Station 30 on Victoria Avenue North a driving factor.

April 5: Mayor should delegate her powers, Poilievre simply better at listening and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/april-5-mayor-should-delegate-her-powers/docview/3033041255/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=April+5%3A+Mayor+should+delegate+her+powers%2C+Poilievre+simply+better+at+listening+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What is an affordable home?
What is affordable housing? Will unhoused people be able to afford one of Mayor Andrea Horwath's affordable houses? Will those addicted to drugs and people with mental health problems and unable to work be able to afford these homes? Are the people who have lost their jobs and therefore lost their homes being retrained so they can afford your affordable homes?
Jacquie Reid, Hamilton
Mayor should delegate powers
Re: Strong mayors, weakened democracy (April 3)
It was refreshing to read Burlington Coun. Rory Nisan explain why he and two of his fellow councillors tabled a motion asking Mayor Marianne Meed Ward to delegate her "strong-mayor" powers back to council and the city manager. The powers never should have been granted by Premier Doug Ford, never should have been accepted by the mayor, and should absolutely be returned to council, just as several other municipalities have now done.
That Meed Ward continues to "need more time" to think about whether she'll agree to support democracy is ridiculous (after trying and failing to keep the motion off the agenda, trying and failing to get a lengthy delay and have it go straight to committee, then at last week's meeting refusing to answer whether she will delegate the powers back).
There has been a huge turnover of senior staff in the City of Burlington. Nisan's words on strong-mayor powers' potential to "wreak havoc on a municipal administration," whether those powers be "used or sheathed" and referring to them as a "weapon" are particularly apt and deeply concerning. All at last week's meeting supported the motion. There is a petition on change.org with more than 200 signatures and counting asking the mayor to stand for democracy and return the strong-mayor powers. We are watching.
Lynn Crosby, Burlington
We need to lead by example
Re: Ford pleads with Trudeau to ditch the carbon levy (April 2)
Naturally, we hate taxes and they evoke strong negative emotions. That, however, creates a problem, because when people have negative emotions, they stop thinking. If you have doubts about that point, just look back on your own experiences with negative emotions like anger, anxiety or frustration; out of that gloom was there ever a single action or decision made that was constructive, positive or beneficial to anyone? I've done this exercise and I cannot come up with one, not a single one.
Looking solely at the financials, yes we incur the cost of this tax, but to the great shame of so many of our leaders who badmouth the tax, barely any mention is made of the incentive payment we also receive. An individual gets $488 per year and a family of four would get $976. For 80 per cent of Ontarians this cash amount exceeds what we pay out, so we end up ahead of the game. What's to complain about there?
A valid point has been made that Canada's emissions are so small that reducing them cannot make a difference to the world's environment. No dispute there, except that, to make a coarse analogy, with a neighbour who allows weeds and litter to accumulate on their lawn, does that mean we should not maintain ours? There is a point of pride in doing the right thing and proving ourselves to be leaders by example. This creates hope that others will clean up their act, too.
So yeah, I hate paying taxes. But this one I can do.
Emil Andrew Sekerak, Beamsville
Poilievre is better at listening
Re: What will a Poilievre victory cost? (April 1)
This letter writer talks about angry Canadians, but neglects to mention why. Canadians are speaking out about the mess this country is in - and it's not Pierre Poilievre's fault. What Poilievre is doing is a better job of listening to what normal average Canadian are facing.
Fearing that Poilievre will introduce "Trump-inspired policies," I wonder which ones the letter writer is referring to. Are they the ones that gave America low unemployment rates, the ones that raised more people out of poverty, the one that initiated the most promising Middle East peace deal in decades or the ones that kept America out of a major war?
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Shurvin was a man of vision
Re: Flamborough's last mayor Mark Shurvin was a 'plain-spoken man' (April 1)
Condolences to the family of Mark Shurvin. He was a man of vision and understanding. He knew what was best for Flamborough, Ancaster and Dundas. Sadly, others in power did not. Amalgamation at its worst.
Gord Baker, Carlisle
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford should never have granted "strong-mayor" powers, they never should have been accepted by the mayor, and should absolutely be returned to council, a Burlington reader writes.

Document 319 of 500

Justin Trudeau is angering Canada's premiers with his legacy projects. Inside the fight between provinces and the prime minister

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-angering-canadas-premiers-with/docview/3032922596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+angering+Canada%27s+premiers+with+his+legacy+projects.+Inside+the+fight+between+provinces+and+the+prime+minister&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau came to power promising a new era of federal-provincial co-operation, but now the Liberal leader finds himself locked in battles with friends and foes alike.It's not just with "Conservative premiers" who Trudeau says are unserious about tackling climate change because they oppose the federal Liberal consumer carbon-levy-and-rebate scheme. And it's not just about the carbon price.Trudeau's battles are across the country and on a range of files, where the prime minister claims the federal Liberals are more "ambitious." They show just how much pandemic-era goodwill between him and the premiers has eroded, reveal a federal government urgently trying to win some political credit at a time when the Liberals are vulnerable, and lay bare contradictions in Trudeau's arguments.Trudeau is right that Conservative premiers have opposed his climate action plan alongside federal Conservatives who long ago won the shorthand contest, branding it a "carbon tax" even if it's technically a levy.However, the Conservative premiers are now joined by people who should be Trudeau's political allies: Newfoundland and Labrador Liberal Premier Andrew Furey, Manitoba's newly elected Premier Wab Kinew, and Ontario Liberal party leader Bonnie Crombie.All have distanced themselves from Trudeau's consumer carbon price-and-rebate program.Seven premiers, including Furey, asked Trudeau to pause the rate increase that just took effect - the April carbon price went up by $15 a tonne or about 3.3 cents on a litre of gasoline - at the very least. Some want to ditch the carbon price altogether and look at other ways to incentivize change. Only two - B.C.'s David Eby and Yukon's Ranj Pillai - are still standing behind the federal program, which neither B.C. nor Quebec are subject to because they have their own carbon cap-and-trade pricing system aimed at emissions reductions.Furey penned an open letter to Trudeau calling for a first ministers' meeting. He said Ottawa should look at the U.S. where there is no carbon tax, and the Biden administration has gone all out in using the power of the federal treasury to offer public subsidies to leverage a "green economy" transition and prime its electric vehicle industry. On top of carbon pricing concerns, the premiers are fuming over Trudeau's latest moves to stomp all over provincial jurisdiction, announcing about $25 billion in pre-budget measures in the past week to expand housing, child care, and school lunch and breakfast programs - all provincial areas.Those are on top of Ottawa's forays into health care with the Liberal government's offer of free diabetes meds and birth control as first steps toward a universal drug coverage plan, and with the rollout of a dental care plan.They are all complex programs to put in place and administer. They all require a measure of provincial buy-in, or at least co-ordination . And on none, according to two senior government sources in different provinces, did Trudeau consult with the provinces in advance. There was obviously some co-ordination with NDP premiers in B.C. and Manitoba, who were persuaded to join Trudeau for a pair of housing photo-ops in the past week for the Liberal pre-budget tour that's underway.Yet on one particularly provocative measure - Tuesday's promise of $5 billion in new infrastructure spending to boost new housing construction that came with strings attached - it was Canada's biggest city mayor, Toronto's Olivia Chow, who stood by and applauded, not Premier Doug Ford.Several premiers talked about it privately that night on a phone call. The next day, several went public, calling it an overreach.One provincial source privy to discussions among the provinces said they had never seen this level of frustration among premiers, calling it a risk for national unity.Trudeau shrugged off questions about intruding on provincial jurisdiction Thursday. He said Canadians want to see action.Ford, too, publicly downplayed jurisdictional squabbles, saying that everyone just wants to get housing built. But the Ontario premier made clear he doesn't think Ottawa should be dictating to other levels of government, including municipalities, whether they must allow fourplexes to be built without municipal permits (or "as of right").Ford has long opposed Trudeau on carbon pricing, but the reality is on many other files he has been an ally of the Trudeau government.So it seems curious Trudeau saw the need to pick the fourplex battle with him.Yet there's irony in the fact that Trudeau - who said in August "housing isn't a primary federal responsibility" - is a prime minister now prepared to dictate conditions for housing funds to the provinces or bypass them altogether. Trudeau says he will give the money directly to municipalities willing to accept those conditions and match the federal "ambition" to move faster on housing.Last week, it was "Conservative premiers" who weren't ambitious enough on child care. They were deliberately "slow-walking" the rollout of the Liberals' $10-a-day daycare plan because, Trudeau said, they were taking their cue from federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.On Thursday, standing next to Premier Kinew, the prime minister insisted he remains "open" to working with provinces, and to hearing Manitoba's carbon tax exemption plan right after their housing photo-op."Provinces that want to price pollution in their own way can do so as long as it is equivalent in impact to what's going on elsewhere in the country, for matters of fairness and for matters of shared ambition," he said.So when he asserts federal jurisdiction, Trudeau insists the provinces have to follow his lead. And when he flexes federal spending power in areas of provincial jurisdiction, they still have to follow his lead. If it's a matter of winning political credit, it may also come at a price of losing political allies.As for that request for a first ministers' meeting to revisit carbon pricing, Trudeau seems cool to the idea.He said Thursday he met with all premiers in 2016 and decided on the "pan-Canadian" framework for addressing climate change.Still, several of those premiers have changed and a post-pandemic affordability crisis could factor into any thinking about the need for another such meeting.Although Alberta, Saskatchewan, Ontario, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador all asked Trudeau to pause the 23-per-cent carbon price increase this month, Trudeau says it comes with a matching rebate, and he's not budging.For all of Trudeau's determination to stay the course, he is aware of how acutely Canadians are feeling the pain of inflation and high interest rates, yet appears unwilling to take his own advice for how to decrease polarization and division in the nation. He told CBC host Matt Galloway Thursday it behooves everyone "to start talking and listening to each other. And we need to start working together to build stronger communities."For sure, Trudeau's right. In an era of social media and echo chambers, complex discussions are harder to have. All the more reason to have them.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talks to media during an affordable housing press conference at a public housing development in Winnipeg on April 4, 2024.

Document 320 of 500

Fire, EMS, library face $418M funding gap, city report says: Ten-year shortfall will only get worse without strategy, director warns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fire-ems-library-face-418m-funding-gap-city/docview/3032921457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fire%2C+EMS%2C+library+face+%24418M+funding+gap%2C+city+report+says%3A+Ten-year+shortfall+will+only+get+worse+without+strategy%2C+director+warns&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More sticker shock is on the horizon as Hamilton continues to take stock of its municipal infrastructure.
This time, it's an estimated $418-million "funding gap" over 10 years for firefighting, paramedic and transit services.
That assessment is the latest in an ongoing provincially mandated exercise for municipalities to draft asset management plans for everything that's on their books.
In 2022, staff pegged the 10-year funding gulf for "core assets" - roads, bridges, water, wastewater and stormwater pipes and plants - at $1.95 billion.
"Without attention to that gap and without a strategy of how to close the gap, it will just get worse and we could find ourselves in quite a situation," Patricia Leishman, director of corporate asset management, told council Wednesday.
The infrastructure hole has deepened over decades of underspending, due, in part, to a lack of funding from upper levels of government, but also big jumps in population, a staff report explained.
"Throwing money at it is obviously the easiest way to go," Leishman said. But not the only way, she added, pointing to other strategies.
"Are we providing a level of service that's maybe too high? ... Are we over-maintaining our assets? Are we giving too much in areas where maybe we don't need to?
"So that funding gap is not necessarily 100 per cent looked at from the need for dollars. It can be looked at in terms of the need to reassess what level we're providing."
Understanding life-cycle costs is also crucial, senior program analyst Amber Dewar emphasized.
It's often the case that the acquisition represents 10 to 20 per cent of the overall cost of an asset, Dewar said.
"But actually, 70 to 80 per cent of the cost of an asset's whole life are the operations and maintenance phase."
So while the purchase might be steeper for one model, it could end up costing less to maintain down the road, and last longer than the cheaper model.
"If we don't understand the whole list cost of assets when we acquire them, how can we budget to maintain levels of service?"
Here's what the latest phase of analysis found:
In particular, the HSR faces a $163-million gap over 10 years, driven mostly by the cost of new shelters and roughly 15 buses added to the fleet annually, as well as the facility costs, Leishman noted.
The fire department faces a narrower gap of $37 million, which is primarily rooted in facility needs, Leishman said.
For paramedics, the gulf is $118 million, with the renewal of Station 30 on Victoria Avenue North and additional staffing costs to keep up with population growth the main factors.
Two end-of-life Bookmobiles, facility maintenance work and future acquisitions are behind the public library's $100-million shortfall, Leishman noted.
Corporate real estate was also included in the latest phase of study, but the division doesn't have a funding gap, a distinction related to the fact that it owns municipal land, like the airport property, but not necessarily the assets on it.
The quality of data used to study the state of assets is a weak point, which can greatly influence the analysis of funding gaps, Leishman advised.
Transit, for instance, has "pockets of reasonable data" but also significant voids, notably when it comes to the purchase price of tools in its maintenance shop, she said.
"It's hard to go back historically and gather that information for previous assets at times."
The funding-gap discussion follows budget talks that saw city officials reduce a preliminary 14.2 per cent tax hike to 7.9 per cent and eventually 5.8 per cent, in part, by relying more heavily on reserves.
As of late, the city has been "left holding the bag" to pay for future growth, thanks to the provincial government's breaks on development charges, Coun. Brad Clark remarked Wednesday.
"What's going to happen to the lowly resident who's paying taxes, either in their rent or their home ownership?"
Provincial legislation, Bill 23, in particular, has reduced the city's proceeds from development charges, which are used to pay for growth-related infrastructure to the tune of $34 million, noted Mike Zegarac, general manager of finance and corporate services.
Premier Doug Ford recently announced $17.5 million for Hamilton from the Ontario Building Faster Fund for meeting housing targets, but there's still a gap of about 50 per cent when it comes to development charge revenue, Zegarac noted.
Meanwhile, the problem of mounting costs is the product of years of inaction, Coun. Maureen Wilson suggested.
To illustrate this, Wilson alluded to the housing and climate crises.
"The solutions were there decades ago, and the cumulative impact of not acting and not thinking about future generations has created the place where we are now."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at
The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. Maureen Wilson says years of inaction have saddled future generations with mounting infrastructure costs.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Teviah Moro Reporter

Document 321 of 500

Mayor looks to declare Hamilton a 'paid-plasma-free zone'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mayor-looks-declare-hamilton-paid-plasma-free/docview/3032921455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+looks+to+declare+Hamilton+a+%27paid-plasma-free+zone%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Andrea Horwath is looking to have Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone" to stop a private, for-profit Spanish company from opening a blood collection centre in the city that she says will "prey on folks who are in need of resources and not in the best financial shape."
"By taking a stand like this it sends a pretty clear message," Horwath told The Spectator. "Anything that preys upon the most vulnerable is hideous and doesn't belong in Hamilton."
A notice of motion was put forward on the mayor's behalf at Tuesday's public health committee meeting that would ban operation of private, for-profit clinics paying donors for plasma in the city.
"It's an important conversation to have in our community," said Horwath. "To take a stand to say, 'No this is not Hamilton's values.'"
The mayor said she expects the city's ban would be respected if it passed when the motion is debated and voted on at a future committee meeting.
However, it's not clear if Canadian Blood Services (CBS) would change its plan. CBS did not answer The Spectator's questions about whether the Hamilton clinic would still open next year if city council voted to block it. Instead, CBS said the only applicable legislation was the province's Voluntary Blood Donations Act.
That law prohibits paying for plasma donation in Ontario, but CBS has declared itself exempt from the act as the charity that runs the blood system nationally. Therefore, CBS says the health-care company from Spain is also exempt because Grifols is acting as its agent when it opens up pay-for-plasma collection sites in Hamilton, Cambridge, Whitby and two other yet-to-be-determined Ontario locations.
A spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones confirmed to The Spectator in March that the centres legally conform to the legislation.
Horwath questioned why more isn't being done to enforce the law that she called for in 2014 as the leader of the NDP at that time. It was passed by the Liberal government to shut down a pay-for-plasma clinic built in Hamilton at 370 Main St. E. - and two more in downtown Toronto - by private company Canadian Plasma Resources.
At issue is a controversial plan by CBS to reduce the country's reliance on the global market for plasma, which is a component of blood. The plan also aims to ensure Canadians have access to immunoglobulins, which is a medication made from plasma.
"There is a global shortage of life-saving immunoglobulins and the plasma needed to make them," CBS said in a statement to The Spectator. "Plasma donors who are paid are a significant part of the global industry."
The plan involves CBS opening 11 of its own unpaid centres while Grifols opens 17 pay-for-plasma sites nationally. Grifols would also manufacture the plasma collected at the sites into immunoglobulins exclusively for Canadian use at its Montreal plant once it opens in 2026.
"This approach protects the blood system, addresses a global shortage of critical medications, and secures a domestic supply chain of life-saving immunoglobulins for patients in Canada," stated CBS. "This is a necessary and positive outcome."
But Horwath questions the safety of the plan, pointing to the recommendations against paid donation in the 1997 report of the Krever Commission. The commission of inquiry was in response to the tainted blood scandal in the 1980s that left more than 30,000 Canadians infected with HIV and hepatitis C through blood transfusions and plasma products.
"We've learned really difficult and tragic lessons from the private involvement in blood donation and plasma," said Horwath. "Why go down the same dangerous path again?"
CBS argues "advancements in technology and strict standards of regulatory compliance have made plasma products from paid donors as safe as those that use plasma from unpaid donors."
"All collections centres in Canada are licensed and regulated by Health Canada," stated CBS. "This includes requirements on donor eligibility criteria and how frequently plasma can be collected from each donor, in addition to donor centre inspections."
All sites - including the one planned for Hamilton - were chosen "after careful analysis," said CBS. However, Horwath says that to her knowledge city council was never consulted. CBS did not answer The Spectator's questions about whether it has had any talks with the city.
"Site selection criteria ... include factors such as population size, proximity to post-secondary institutions, and access to public transit," stated CBS.
The pay-for-plasma sites were only approved in areas where they would not interfere with unpaid centres, said CBS, and where there was "no obvious donor or employee safety or security concerns, and no obvious shelters or unsavoury businesses adjacent to the location."
CBS has not provided the exact location for the centre but documents obtained by BloodWatch.org suggest it will be located at 2255 Barton St. E., near the Red Hill Valley Parkway and Nash Road North.
The freedom-of-information documents suggest the provincial Ministry of Health doesn't know how much Grifols plans to pay donors.
If Horwath is successful in getting Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone," the resolution would be sent to CBS, the federal and provincial ministers of health, Grifols and all Ontario municipalities.
"That is a major step to stop Grifols from opening in Hamilton," said Kat Lanteigne, executive director of BloodWatch.org, a not-for-profit organization that champions a voluntary, public blood system. "We will be advocating for the motion to pass."
jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mayor Andrea Horwath is looking to have Hamilton declared a "paid-plasma-free zone" and prohibit private for-profit blood collection companies from operating in the city.Graham Paine Metroland

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3032921166/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What is an affordable home?
What is affordable housing? Will unhoused people be able to afford one of Mayor Andrea Horwath's affordable houses? Will those addicted to drugs and people with mental health problems and unable to work be able to afford these homes? Are the people who have lost their jobs and therefore lost their homes be retrained so they can afford your affordable homes?
Jacquie Reid, Hamilton
Mayor should delegate powers
Strong mayors, weakened democracy (April 3)
It was refreshing to read Burlington Coun. Rory Nisan explain why he and two of his fellow councillors tabled a motion asking Mayor Marianne Meed Ward to delegate her "strong-mayor" powers back to council and the city manager.
The powers never should have been granted by Premier Doug Ford, never should have been accepted by the mayor and should absolutely be returned to council, just as several other municipalities have now done.
That Meed Ward continues to "need more time" to think about whether she'll agree to support democracy is ridiculous (after trying and failing to keep the motion off the agenda, trying and failing to get a lengthy delay and have it go straight to committee, then at last week's meeting refusing to answer whether she will delegate the powers back).
There has been a huge turnover of senior staff in the City of Burlington. Nisan's words on strong-mayor powers' potential to "wreak havoc on a municipal administration," whether those powers be "used or sheathed" and referring to them as a "weapon" are particularly apt and deeply concerning.
All at last week's meeting supported the motion. There is a petition on change.org with more than 200 signatures and counting asking the mayor to stand for democracy and return the strong-mayor powers. We are watching.
Lynn Crosby, Burlington
We need to lead by example
Ford pleads with Trudeau to ditch the carbon levy (April 2)
Naturally, we hate taxes and they evoke strong negative emotions. That, however, creates a problem because, when people have negative emotions, they stop thinking. If you have doubts about that point, just look back on your own experiences with negative emotions like anger, anxiety or frustration; out of that gloom was there ever a single action or decision made that was constructive, positive or beneficial to anyone? I've done this exercise and I cannot come up with one, not a single one.
Looking solely at the financials, yes we incur the cost of this tax, but to the great shame of so many of our leaders who badmouth the tax, barely any mention is made of the incentive payment we also receive. An individual gets $488 per year and a family of four would get $976. For 80 per cent of Ontarians, this cash amount exceeds what we pay out, so we end up ahead of the game. What's to complain about there?
A valid point has been made that Canada's emissions are so small that reducing them cannot make a difference to the world's environment. No dispute there, except that, to make a coarse analogy, with a neighbour who allows weeds and litter to accumulate on their lawn, does that mean we should not maintain ours? There is a point of pride in doing the right thing and proving ourselves to be leaders by example. This creates hope that others will clean up their act, too.
So, yeah, I hate paying taxes. But this one I can do.
Emil Andrew Sekerak, Beamsville
Poilievre is better at listening
What will a Poilievre victory cost? (April 1)
This letter writer talks about angry Canadians, but neglects to mention why. Canadians are speaking out about the mess this country is in - and it's not Pierre Poilievre's fault. What Poilievre is doing is a better job of listening to what normal, average Canadians are facing.
Fearing that Poilievre will introduce "Trump-inspired policies," I wonder which ones the letter writer is referring to. Are they the ones that gave America low unemployment rates, the ones that raised more people out of poverty, the ones that initiated the most promising Middle East peace deal in decades or the ones that kept America out of a major war?
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Shurvin was a man of vision
Flamborough's last mayor Mark Shurvin
was a 'plain-spoken man' (April 1)
Condolences to the family of Mark Shurvin. He was a man of vision and understanding. He knew what was best for Flamborough, Ancaster and Dundas. Sadly, others in power did not. Amalgamation at its worst.
Gord Baker, Carlisle

Document 323 of 500

Ethics watchdog clears Doug Ford's chief of staff after he 'overlooked' Greenbelt email

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ethics-watchdog-clears-doug-fords-chief-staff/docview/3033624735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ethics+watchdog+clears+Doug+Ford%27s+chief+of+staff+after+he+%27overlooked%27+Greenbelt+email&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's ethics watchdog has cleared Premier Doug Ford's chief of staff of wrongdoing after the discovery of an "overlooked" email linked to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
In response to a complaint from NDP Leader Marit Stiles, integrity commissioner J. David Wake said he has "accepted" Patrick Sackville's explanation of how his Oct. 17, 2022 email exchange - on private accounts instead of government servers - with Ryan Amato, at the time chief of staff to then-municipal affairs minister Steve Clark, was missed.
"I have concluded that the Oct. 17 email sent from Mr. Amato to Mr. Sackville's personal email in the circumstances I have outlined does not support a finding that Patrick Sackville knew about the specific removal criteria for lands from the Greenbelt or the properties selected for removal until he was briefed with others, as he testified, on Oct. 27," Wake wrote Thursday.
While the integrity commissioner said "it is unfortunate that this email was not retrieved in accordance with the request for documents I made to Mr. Sackville ... I have accepted his explanation as to how it was overlooked."
Ford's chief of staff told Wake it "was an oversight on my part" that the missive fell through the cracks because it was to his personal account rather than his government email.
"When you previously sought documents relevant to the issues you are investigating, I conducted a diligent search of my records that would be responsive to your request. However, I did not locate the email from Mr. Amato in making that search," said Sackville.
"In conducting some due diligence related to freedom of information requests, I later became aware of this email and, consistent with my obligations, provided it to you expeditiously," he said, referring to the message being found last December.
"I do not conduct government business on my personal email, and would not have expected or anticipated such an email."
Wake said "it is entirely plausible that he would have given primary attention to the hundreds of daily emails he receives to his government email address and that he may have neglected to be attentive to his personal emails."
The opposition New Democrats, who obtained the documents through a freedom of information request, argued the email contradicted testimony given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
In the message from Amato to Sackville, the ministerial chief of staff shared a "list of criteria for removals" of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
These include "infrastructure services," such as proximity to roads, gas and hydro lines and water mains, police and fire services.
The email also listed "potential offsets" such as the Paris Galt Moraine, "green space our government has decided not to move on" as well as "options to go larger depending on executive interest."
At issue is the timing of the email, which is at odds with testimony made under oath to Wake for his Aug. 30 report on the Greenbelt affair.
"Members of the premier's staff also told us they were not involved in the selection of properties," the integrity commissioner wrote last summer.
"Mr. Sackville said he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on Oct. 27, 2022," he noted at the time.
"Mr. Sackville recalled being first briefed about the Greenbelt project at a meeting facilitated by the cabinet office on Oct. 27, 2022. He told me that he recalled the intentions of the policy were discussed and that it should be 'programmatic,' meaning that it should be something that could be repeated and made standard."
However, that was 10 days after the Oct. 17 email in which criteria for removal of the lands from the Greenbelt were addressed. The changes were finally announced publicly on Nov. 4, 2022.
Sackville declined to comment when contacted by the Star on Thursday, as did Amato.
Ford has maintained he knew nothing about the removal of 15 parcels of land from the Greenbelt, which were estimated to be worth $8.28 billion to property owners.
In separate reports last summer, the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain developers with Tory connections were "favoured."
Under pressure, Ford cancelled the land swap on Sept. 21. The deal is now being investigated by the RCMP.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at the Ottawa Board of Trade on March 28, 2024.

Document 324 of 500

Bonnie Crombie slams Doug Ford for 'reckless' underfunding of colleges and universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-slams-doug-ford-reckless/docview/3033624365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+slams+Doug+Ford+for+%27reckless%27+underfunding+of+colleges+and+universities&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Setting the stage for her plan to defeat Doug Ford in 2026, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says the premier is jeopardizing jobs of the future with "reckless" underfinancing of colleges and universities.
"It is imperiling our economy," Crombie said Thursday in a 20-minute luncheon speech to the Empire Club, her first since taking the party helm in December.
"If I were premier today, I'd spend less time on pocketbook gimmicks and more time on the fundamental differentiator for our economy - education," she added in a shot at Ford measures including a 5.7-cent-per-litre gasoline tax cut and scrapping of license plate renewal fees.
While Ford's Progressive Conservatives recently announced a $1-billion, three-year funding boost for post-secondary education, struggling colleges and universities warned it's not enough to put them on solid financial footing given the government's extended tuition freeze, rising costs and a looming federal cap on international students.
Eight of Ontario's 23 universities are running deficits, and colleges are expected to lose $3 billion in revenue from international students over the next three years.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop has defended the $1-billion increase as the biggest "in over a decade" and noted tuition can be raised five per cent next fall for students from other provinces.
But Crombie said limiting revenue for colleges and universities will stifle their ability to research and innovate, and pointed to how University of Waterloo graduates helped spawn a "global revolution" with the BlackBerry smartphone two decades ago.
"Between 2025 and 2030, our economy will undergo a massive disruption. Technologies like (artificial intelligence) will continue to change how we live, work and learn. Retirements will soar, shrinking our workforce," she noted in a reference to baby boomers.
Crombie, whose party is a distant third in the legislature's standings with nine MPPs, did not say how much a Liberal government would boost post-secondary support other than to say "we need to fund them properly."
However, she noted in a question-and-answer session following her speech that "pocketbook gimmicks" like the gas tax break are "really reducing the fiscal capacity" of the provincial treasury.
"I keep thinking, would people really miss that or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?"
The gas tax cut, for example, costs $1.2 billion annually. Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's budget last week included a $9.8-billion annual deficit.
Crombie, a former three-term mayor of Mississauga, said she passed on a chance to run in the upcoming May 2 byelection in nearby Milton to travel the province, meet voters and revive the Liberal party after stunning election defeats in 2018 and 2022.
"If a Mississauga seat comes open, we could have another conversation," she said.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to reporters at Queen's Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Baby boomers; Tax cuts; Colleges & universities; Gasoline taxes; Education

Three NDP MPs say they won't seek re-election - creating openings for Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/three-ndp-mps-say-they-wont-seek-re-election/docview/3031739932/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Three+NDP+MPs+say+they+won%27t+seek+re-election+-+creating+openings+for+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Three New Democrats, including one of the party's longest-serving MPs, have announced they won't be running for re-election in battleground regions where Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives hope to make gains.Charlie Angus, who has represented Timmins-James Bay for two decades, said Thursday he has decided not to seek re-election in the northern Ontario riding that is of particular interest to Poilievre.Following Angus's announcement, the NDP said British Columbia MP Rachel Blaney, who has fielded stiff competition from the Conservatives in her riding of North Island-Powell River, and Carol Hughes, who has held Angus's neighbouring riding of Algoma-Manitoulin-Kapuskasing since 2008, had also decided not to run again."On behalf of all New Democrats, I want to thank Rachel, Charlie and Carol for their years of advocacy and dedication to serving working people," NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said in a written statement."They've all accomplished so much for Canadians and worked tirelessly to make our country a fairer, better place. They've added so much to our party and, while we'll miss their contributions around the caucus table, I want to wish my friends the very best when each of them begins their next chapter."The departures are likely to put pressure on a party that is fighting to expand its electoral footprint at a time when the NDP is grappling with other caucus exits, its ties to an increasingly unpopular Liberal government, and a Conservative party eager to boost their seat count at the New Democrats' expense. Thursday's announcements mean six MPs out of the NDP's 25-member caucus will have vacated their seats by the time Canadians next head to the polls. Blaney, who was first elected in 2015, is the third NDP MP in B.C. who has said they won't run again. She joins fellow longtime MPs Randall Garrison and Richard Cannings, the latter of whom also represents a blue-orange battleground.Manitoba MP Daniel Blaikie left federal politics at the end of March to work for Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew.Angus told the Star it was time for new blood to revitalize the party."We've seen great MPs step aside in the past and I didn't know how we were going to carry on, and then other great MPs step up," said Angus, who has garnered a reputation as a scrappy and outspoken New Democrat who once ran against Singh for leadership of the NDP. "Every caucus needs renewal, and I think renewal is happening."As the party's natural resources critic, Angus worked closely with his government counterpart Jonathan Wilkinson on Ottawa's sustainable jobs bill as part of the Liberal-NDP parliamentary alliance."I will continue to find ways to advocate for Indigenous and Northern issues. The climate crisis is an all-hands-on-deck moment. I started in grassroots activism and am excited to return to those roots. And yes, there will be more gigs, more books, more historical projects, and more s--t disturbing," Angus wrote on Facebook.Angus partly pinned his decision on significant boundary changes to Canada's ridings that came into effect this month."The Federal Electoral Boundary Commission has ruled that this constituency, already bigger than the U.K., will undergo dramatic changes. The new riding of Mushkegowuk-Kapuskasing-Timmins will be even larger, with the addition of many new communities spread out over an additional 20,000 square kilometres of territory," Angus wrote in his post.That redrawing of the electoral map also means Hughes's riding is set to be dissolved. The departure of both Angus and Hughes creates a timely opening for the Conservatives, who have the region - an NDP stronghold - in their sights. Poilievre has visited the area on at least four occasions since announcing his intent to run for the Conservative leadership in 2022, often hammering his party's tough-on-crime message to a region wrestling with public safety concerns.For his part, Angus said he feels the NDP's northern Ontario base is thriving and secure, but conceded Thursday the coming fight will be a "battle." He acknowledged that Poilievre is "playing to a rage machine" - a small group of vocal voters he says are receptive to the Conservative leader's rhetoric.Poilievre on Thursday accused the trio of MPs on X, formerly Twitter, of "jumping off Jagmeet's ship before going down in defeat." Hughes, who is also the House of Commons assistant deputy speaker, has criticized the riding changes in Ontario's north, arguing that a large swath of constituents will be underserved with only one MP to represent them. Hughes said in a written statement that she was proud of her tenure and looked forward to "helping elect that next generation of progressive MPs."Blaney, the NDP's whip and critic for veterans affairs, seniors and rural economic development, said in her own statement that the decision not to seek re-election was "incredibly difficult."All three MPs said they were keen to spend more time with their families after spending years toiling on Parliament Hill.
CAPTION: NDP MP Charlie Angus attends a news conference in Ottawa on Oct. 17, 2023.

Ford wants '100 per cent' Ontario students at med schools

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-wants-100-per-cent-ontario-students-at-med/docview/3031566877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+wants+%27100+per+cent%27+Ontario+students+at+med+schools&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says he wants all spots in Ontario medical schools to be reserved for students from the province.
Ford said Wednesday about 18 per cent of students are from foreign countries.
"In my opinion, and we will continue working with the ministry, get rid of the 18 per cent," he said at an announcement for a new medical school at York University. "I'm not being mean, but I'm taking care of our students, our kids first."
Ford then lamented the fact that some kids and parents have said some Ontario students study abroad and then do not return home after they meet someone.
"I want 100 per cent of Ontario students going to these universities," he said.
His office said later Wednesday that "it's medical seats he wants filled with Ontario students."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles criticized Ford for his comments.
"The Premier's comments were wildly disrespectful to the thousands of students and internationally trained physicians with experience from across the world stuck waiting for a residency spot so they can finally practice in our province," she wrote in a statement.
"He is telling skilled physicians from around the world looking to build a life in Ontario that they're simply not welcome here."
A group representing migrant workers also took issue.
"The Migrant Workers Alliance for Change is outraged at Premier Ford distracting from his chronic underfunding of public education by blowing a racist dog-whistle that casts international students as outsiders, which will worsen the already pervasive anti-migrant climate," said Sarom Rho, a spokesperson for the organization.
Ford's comments came as post-secondary institutions grapple with a growing reliance on international students. Post-secondary institutions, especially colleges, in Ontario turned increasingly to international students after Ford's government cut tuition by 10 per cent in 2019 and froze it there.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Doug Ford hints at Greenbelt flip-flop in new ad: 'You can't always get it right'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hints-at-greenbelt-flip-flop-new-ad-you/docview/3032768475/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hints+at+Greenbelt+flip-flop+in+new+ad%3A+%27You+can%27t+always+get+it+right%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Changing gears from ads attacking Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Premier Doug Ford has a new TV spot acknowledging his flip-flop on the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap now under RCMP investigation.
The one-minute advertisement paid for by his Progressive Conservatives comes two years before the next provincial election campaign, and takes a softer approach as the party hopes to hold the ridings of Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex in May 2 byelections.
It shows Ford getting ready for work in the morning, returning phone calls, meeting people and listening to their concerns in a folksy bid to turn a political vulnerability into a strength.
"You can't always get it right. Let me tell you, when I don't, I hear from the people," the premier says, hinting at the Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"I always ask my team, what are we doing to make life better and easier for people out there?" he adds amid shots of him tying his necktie, talking to a family in their upscale home and meeting patrons in a restaurant.
"That's what it's all about. Listening and working hard to make things better for every single person in Ontario, getting it done for the people."
Ford has also reversed his position on the dissolution of Peel Region, which was to have taken effect next year and would have made Mississauga, Brampton and Caledon single-tier municipalities with no shared regional services such as police.
The most recent monthly tracking poll from Abacus Data shows Ford has rebounded from last fall's Greenbelt debacle with 41 per cent support, compared to Crombie's Liberals at 27 per cent, the NDP under Marit Stiles at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from March 14 to 21 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford has recently stepped up attacks on the federal government's consumer carbon levy - which was foisted on the province after he axed a cap-and-trade system to reduce carbon emissions in the fight against climate change - as a diversion from a budget last week with a soaring deficit of $9.8 billion.
The gauzy new spot is in contrast to the hard-punching PC radio, television and social media ads attacking Crombie, which were launched shortly after she was elected Liberal leader on Dec. 2. They portrayed her as an out-of-touch elitist eager to raise taxes.
"It's good to see the Conservatives use Conservative party funds for their blatantly partisan ads instead of Ontarians' hard-earned money," said Stiles, who has been critical of recent feel-good government ads touting the province.
CAPTION: Doug Ford speaks at the Ottawa Board of Trade on March 28, 2024.

Subject: Scandals; Political advertising

Document 328 of 500

April 3: Bookstore will be missed, parking lot is vital and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/april-3-bookstore-will-be-missed-parking-lot-is/docview/3031291202/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=April+3%3A+Bookstore+will+be+missed%2C+parking+lot+is+vital+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bookstore will be missed
Re: Coles to close after more than 50 years in Jackson Square (March 28)
Any book lover in downtown or central Hamilton will suffer a big loss on Saturday when Coles in Jackson Square closes its doors for the last time. Contrary to what the article says on the second line, it is not closing "later this spring."
It will be a tremendous loss for our community, and I for one will have a difficult time filling the void it will leave in my life. It has been my go-to happy place since moving to Hamilton in 2014. The staff have been friendly, knowledgeable, helpful and above all top notch.
Many residents who use this store are not suburban shoppers and the options of Lime Ridge Mall, Ancaster or Stoney Creek means that it will limit the in-person visits since these places are not close by. I wish all the staff the best of luck in their future.
Linda Miller, Hamilton
This parking lot is vital
Re: Why I am using strong mayor powers to build affordable housing in Stoney Creek (March 30)
I read our mayor's opinion piece in last Saturday's paper and cannot believe her ridiculous decision to remove vital parking spots for affordable housing. I agree we need to solve the housing crisis, but definitely not eliminate small-town parking, which she termed surplus lands.
This parking lot is vital to the survival of the businesses in the downtown core of Stoney Creek and it's full every day. Street parking cannot handle it all and that is why the lot was developed. If there is no parking for customers, they will not shop and consequently the businesses will eventually close.
Therefore, use your power to change condos to affordable housing at locations like the Sherwood Lanes lot, the Upper Sherman Walmart lot, or Limeridge Mall Sear's lot, or Jamesville, which are empty.
John Day, Hamilton
Where am I supposed to park?
Once again, the residents of Stoney Creek are labelled as being against affordable housing. Easy to say when you live in Ancaster or Oakville! Affordable housing is needed indeed and we, in Stoney Creek, know that as well as everybody else.
However, what we are against is the removal of the only public parking lot in downtown Stoney Creek. We have numerous small businesses that depend on it, as does the medical building. As a case in point, I had a medical appointment at 10 a.m. last Tuesday and I had to drive around the lot twice before being able to park.
By all means, go ahead and use this spot for a new building, but, please tell me where I will be able to park next time I have a medical appointment.
Anne-Marie Kniehl, Stoney Creek
Checking back on predictions
On a beautiful fall day in 2015, Bob Brattina, campaigning as the Liberal candidate for the federal seat in Hamilton East, approached my home with a small but enthusiastic entourage. He asked for my vote to help Justin Trudeau defeat Stephen Harper. I told him it would be a frosty day in hell before that ever happened.
After taking a moment to regain his composure, he asked why. I told him that, based on my experience, a Liberal government would only do three things well, and these included increasing government spending and taxes on a grand scale, increasing the size and cost of the federal government payroll and increasing the country's debt and the cost to carry it. Realizing that I would not be drinking the Kool-Aid, Bob stormed off.
Today is April 1, 2024, and it was recently reported that the federal payroll has grown by more than 30 per cent since 2015. The hot news of the day includes the latest increase in the carbon tax, another annual increase in the excise tax on alcohol and a substantial increase to already generous MP salaries.
In addition, Trudeau is delaying the date of next year's federal election by one week to ensure 80 or so MPs will reach the minimum requirement of six years of service in order to qualify for their gold-plated pensions, resulting in a further fleecing of taxpayers to the tune of $120 million.
All I can say at this point is check, check and check. Oh yes, just one more thing, when exactly is that budget going to balance itself?
Cameron Stringer, Hamilton
Premier has a dismal record
Oh Doug, what have you done to my province? And what do you continue doing? You did not run on a platform of privatization and yet here we are.
Short-funding health care and then paying for private agencies. Nurses who fled the public system due to overwork and underpayment. Starving the education system to produce uneducated voters. Cutting inspections in long-term care. Destroying any protection for renters so we have a heartbreaking housing crisis. Gutting the conservation authorities to find any loophole to build.
How can you claim to be premier of Ontario when the only people receiving "premier" treatment are the one per cent? Take your party and go.
Martha Howatt, Hamilton
Oil a necessity, not addiction
Re: Anxiety in the time of climate change (April 2)
I just read a ridiculous, nonsensical article in The Spec comparing the use of fossil fuel as an addiction to drugs. It's the most absurd comparison I have ever heard. The writer seems to think we are all addicted to the use of oil and gas. Really? I drive a gas-powered vehicle to do my job, I don't have time to sit and charge an expensive EV during the day. Has the writer researched the environmental impact of manufacturing lithium batteries? Where do they go at the end of their life? Landfill right now.
I heat my house with natural gas which is clean and efficient. Heat pumps have a 15- to 20-year break even point even with the rebate, so not even worth the expense. Has the writer taken a drive on the 400 series highways to see the amount of diesel-powered transport trucks shipping food and goods that we need to live on?
Oil is used to manufacture almost everything we use. Stop calling it an addiction, it's necessary for living. Yes there is climate change, but we need to stop the over-the-top fear mongering when Canada contributes 1.5 per cent of the world's carbon emissions.
The transition to greener energy is happening (hydrogen fuel cells), but we need to stop yelling "The sky is falling."
Gary Brown, Hamilton
Properly fund health, education
Premier Doug Ford can spend millions of dollars on partisan advertising, but can't afford to give the health and education sectors the money they need to provide us all with good service? Multiple times we have needed the court system to reverse Ford decisions and each one costs more tax dollars. It is very clear that we cannot afFORD this government.
Geoff Johnson, Hamilton
Alcohol pushed in the media
Recently I watched some old clips of "The Tonight Show with Johnny Carson." I could not believe the amount of guests who, along with Johnny, smoked on the set. It was truly disgusting knowing that every puff taken was shortening their lives. This was also the norm for movies at the time.
Fast forward to now and I suggest you note that on each and every TV series or movie, there is the preverbal glass of wine brought into the storyline. This is usually directed at women who at times are out on the night or home alone. You will never see them open the fridge and pour a glass of juice or water. It's always wine that Hollywood is implying.
As we all know, alcohol consumption is not good for anyone, just like we realized about smoking years ago. Unfortunately monkey see, monkey do.
Ralph Corning, Stoney Creek
What happened to common sense?
This weekend, someone took out our community mailboxes at a high rate of speed. I am so thankful that my neighbours weren't out walking their dogs or pushing strollers. What can be done to avoid this happening again?
If we use the logic the city is using with Main Street, we need to reconfigure the road. Really? Rarely do roads cause collisions - selfish and careless driving cause collisions. We need drivers to understand the enormous consequences that can happen when they drive poorly or selfishly.
Perhaps we can take some of the millions slated to reconfigure Main Street and instead start a program for new drivers, where a police officer does a ride along with them. They could visit sites of past horrific crashes and the officer could explain what happened and show the new driver photos. Then they could make a visit to a victim's family and hear first hand about the impact someone's careless or selfish driving has had on real people's lives.
This is not a cure all for all the bad driving out there, but to me it is a good start and makes more sense than wasting money changing roads that have worked for decades. City council needs to make better decisions. Don't get me started on the LRT!
Casey Duffy, Hamilton
CAPTION: The Coles book store in Jackson Square is closing Saturday. Coles has had a store in Jackson Square for 51 years. It was one of the original tenants in the mall.
