Pierre Poilievre's popularity is giving Doug Ford's Tories a boost, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-popularity-is-giving-doug-fords/docview/2973439236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+popularity+is+giving+Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+a+boost%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's current popularity appears to be having a halo effect on Premier Doug Ford, a new Abacus Data poll suggests.
At the same time, embattled Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is proving to be a drag on provincial Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
In the Abacus monthly tracking survey for the Star, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 27 per cent for Crombie's Liberals, 21 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and seven per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
By comparison, Poilievre's Tories were at 44 per cent in Ontario to 28 per cent for Trudeau's Liberals, 19 per cent for Jagmeet Singh's NDP and four per cent for the Greens led by Elizabeth May.
Despite their private differences - Ford skipped a recent Poilievre rally in Etobicoke that was a nine-minute drive from his home - the federal Tory leader appears to be giving the provincial Progressive Conservative premier a boost.
"Poilievre currently gets more attention in Ontario than the premier of Ontario does, so that's probably a factor," Abacus president David Coletto said Friday.
"A big part of the Poilievre halo is among younger voters in Ontario. They are very motivated," noted Coletto.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from March 14 until Thursday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll found the honeymoon for Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor who was elected party leader on Dec. 2, seems to have been brief.
"There's no question it is a very hard thing to be a Liberal leader anywhere in the country now and Ontario is no exception," said Coletto.
"As long as Trudeau is around - and the federal Liberal brand is as weak as it is - it's bad news for Bonnie Crombie," he said.
"Justin Trudeau's unpopularity is the best thing that could happen to the PCs."
But the pollster said if there is any silver lining for Crombie, it's that "it gives her an excuse until she faces voters" in the June 2026 Ontario election.
Coletto said the survey's findings are why it was politically "smart for her to go against the carbon tax."
That's a reference to Crombie on Monday announcing there would be no provincial carbon levy if her Liberals win the next Ontario election.
Abacus, meanwhile, found 44 per cent of the Tory respondents identified themselves as a "Poilievre Conservative."
Just 20 per cent considered themselves a "Ford Conservative" while 33 per cent said they were "equally both" Poilievre and Ford Tories and two per cent were neither.
"They're more likely to identify as a 'Poilievre Conservative' than a 'Ford Conservative,' but Doug Ford is not as unpopular as he was in the 2019 election when the federal Conservatives asked him to disappear," said Coletto.
"So I don't think Ford is depressing federal Conservative support," he said.
In that 2019 campaign, the Tory leader Andrew Scheer refused to even mention the premier by name while Trudeau sometimes cited Ford more than a dozen times in a single stump speech.
Although the Liberal prime minister and the Tory premier have since mended fences - and work closely on a range of issues, including massive subsidies for electric vehicle battery factories in Windsor and St. Thomas - 56 per cent of respondents believed Ford wants Poilievre to win a federal election expected by October 2025.
Only 17 per cent felt the premier wants Trudeau to win a fourth term and 27 per cent didn't know.
But Coletto noted if a newly elected federal Tory government in the fall of 2025 begins making deep spending cuts, including reducing transfer payments to provinces, that could have an impact on the provincial PCs' popularity.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on March 20, 2024.

Brian Mulroney's state funeral: Trudeau, Poilievre, Ryan Reynolds, Wayne Gretzky among those attending

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brian-mulroneys-state-funeral-trudeau-poilievre/docview/2973439216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brian+Mulroney%27s+state+funeral%3A+Trudeau%2C+Poilievre%2C+Ryan+Reynolds%2C+Wayne+Gretzky+among+those+attending&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MONTREAL-Governors general, prime ministers, ambassadors, premiers, too many boldface names to count. And Ryan Reynolds and Wayne Gretzky, to boot.
It will be a Montreal summit the likes of which Brian Mulroney would have loved.
All of Mulroney's worlds collide today at a state funeral here where notable figures who were his contemporaries and others who counted him as a friend, ally or influence are gathering to honour the former prime minister.
Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister, died Feb. 29 at age 84. After four days of formal public mourning in Ottawa and Montreal, his state funeral begins after a formal cortege escorts his casket from Saint Patrick's Basilica to the majestic Notre-Dame Basilica in old Montreal.
Although Mulroney left office more than 30 years ago, he had a profound impact in shaping modern Canada through his negotiation of North American free trade, economic and tax reforms including the GST, leading international opposition to apartheid, crafting major environmental protections to combat acid rain and ozone-depleting chemicals, and even through ultimately unsuccessful efforts at constitutional reform.
The guest list for his final farewell draws on a deep and wide list of contacts Mulroney cultivated during and after his career in government, reaching into the worlds of politics, business, law, sports, entertainment, media and beyond.
On the celebrity guest list are Canadian actor Ryan Reynolds and hockey legend Wayne Gretzky who will deliver a eulogy, and Sarah Ferguson, Duchess of York.
Former Conservative British prime minister John Major, a former White House chief of staff to Ronald Reagan, and President Joe Biden's ambassador to Canada, David Cohen, are expected, as are other ambassadors.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will speak at the funeral, which a third of his cabinet will attend. Also attending are four governors general, four former prime ministers - Kim Campbell, Jean Chrétien, Joe Clark and Stephen Harper - 12 of 13 provincial and territorial leaders, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, and former PC leaders Peter MacKay, Erin O'Toole, and interim leader Rona Ambrose, along with Inuit leader Natan Obed, and Métis national council head Cassidy Caron.
Former premiers David Peterson from Ontario, Jason Kenney of Alberta, Frank McKenna of New Brunswick, British Columbia's Christy Clark, Manitoba's Heather Stefanson (who was a former staffer in Mila Mulroney's office), New Brunswick's Bernard Lord, Newfoundland and Labrador's Brian Tobin, will attend, along with and a raft of former Mulroney cabinet ministers.
Quebec's political class is well represented with Premier François Legault, and six other former premiers including Mulroney cabinet minister Jean Charest who is to deliver a tribute, and ex Mulroney cabinet minister Lucien Bouchard.
A slew of business leaders are on the list, including Quebecor's Pierre Karl Péladeau, a former leader of the Parti Quebecois who will also deliver a eulogy, Onex's Gerry Schwartz, Galen Weston, Stephen Bronfman, Arthur Irving, Andrew Molson, Ed Rogers and Hydro-Quebec's Michael Sabia who is a former federal official who helped design the GST under Mulroney's government.
The Montreal Symphony Orchestra will perform, and The Tenors will reprise "Danny Boy," a sentimental Irish favourite.
And amid all the pomp and circumstance, there will be a classic Mulroney personal touch during the final recession, when a recording of Mulroney himself singing "We'll Meet Again" - which Canadian officials said was a personal wish of Mulroney's for his funeral.
CAPTION: State funeral in memory of the Right Honourable Brian MulroneyMila Mulroney visits the casket of her husband and former prime minister Brian Mulroney at St. Patrick's Basilica in Montreal on Friday.

March 23: Drop-in centres need support, having second thoughts about Liberals and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-23-drop-centres-need-support-having-second/docview/2973439211/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+23%3A+Drop-in+centres+need+support%2C+having+second+thoughts+about+Liberals+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Drop-in centres need support
Thank you to the staff and volunteers of the Cathedral Cafe for everything they are doing to take some pressure off of our ever-growing homeless situation. Can we move away from the concepts of warming and cooling centres and admit drop-in services are essential 24-7 and 12 months a year in feeding and supporting our most vulnerable citizens? Funding these drop-in centres year-round provides a return on investment that includes mitigating financial demands on health care and other social services, and offers caring community volunteers an opportunity to help out. Here is my modest proposal to the city as one taxpayer who sees the immense value of drop-in centres: compare their value against your bike lane budget, and allocate your dollars accordingly.
Mike Johnston, Hamilton
Bolstering comes too late
Re: Hamilton 'bolsters' anti-cyberattack team (March 21)
So, "Hamilton has added global accounting firm Deloitte to a team of outside experts hired to help it respond to a ransomware attack that has debilitated the municipality's IT network" as reported in the Spec. Quite apart from adding to this city's propensity to lavish money on "consultants," albeit probably unavoidable in this situation, I find the current hand-wringing and protestations of "bolstering" to be more indicative of panic control than anything else. Open stable door, meet bolted horse, that is to say.
Neville Newman, Hamilton
Police should investigate threats
Re: Theatre cancels Jewish Film Festival April screenings over 'safety concerns' (March 20)
The Playhouse Cinema has announced that, in response to emails, phone calls and social media messages, it will not show the Hamilton Jewish Film Festival because of "security and safety concerns." The festival films included a love story, a family drama, documentaries about a medical mission to Ukraine and peaceful coexistence in Haifa and more. Not one film was political, but they did share a common denominator - they were all Jewish movies. The owners of the cinema felt threatened because they wanted to host a Jewish event. I understand their fear; we have all seen Jewish institutions attacked and have heard the antisemitic chants. My question is, at what point do we label the pressure put on them for what it is: Jew hatred? When do we recognize that hiding behind political slogans should not protect those who commit hate crimes? The police should investigate anyone who sends hateful or threatening messages. The owners of the Playhouse should have felt protected from the bullies and the haters, not threatened by them.
Daniel Kollek, Dundas
Speak up about pending changes
Re: Hamilton Conservation Authority's important work threatened (March 20)
Thank you for the article by Brian McHattie. This is such an important issue for Hamilton, a city of waterfalls. Encroachment onto our conservation areas puts our homes at risk of flooding and everyone needs to speak up and let officials at all level of government know we are against this.
Our homes, our water, endangered species, so many elements of our lives at risk. This action will be particularly acute for the Indigenous population that lives along the Grand River. Without the work the conservation areas do to control flooding, we are all in trouble.
Thank you Hamilton Spectator for covering this pressing issue. I urged all to speak up about this destructive action to conservation lands and our homes.
Mary Ann Frerotte, Dundas
Second thoughts about Liberals
Re: Crombie promises no provincial carbon tax if her Liberals defeat Ford's Tories (March 18)
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, I am very disappointed to see that you do not support a carbon rebate program. I was happy to see you elected as leader, but this particular stance on global warming has given me second thoughts about supporting the Ontario Liberals.
My children and grandchildren will inherit a different world than I did and it will not be a better one. The carbon rebate program addresses the issue of leaving a better world for generations to come. Your non-support is a vote-getting ploy that will harm our planet.
I am unable to support a party that does not recognize the perils of climate change and will sacrifice future generations for votes. I will wait to see if you have a plan to replace the carbon rebate program; if I do not hear of a positive program to control/mitigate climate change, my time, money and vote will go to the NDP.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
Natural gas is the answer
Re: Poilievre threatens no confidence vote on carbon price (March 20)
The article contains the statement from Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault: "If there's someone, somewhere that can show me a measure that comes at no cost to Canadian taxpayers because it's revenue-neutral, that can give us a third of our emission reduction, I'd like to hear it." Canada emits roughly 600 million tonnes of carbon every year. A one-third reduction would be 200 million tonnes. China emits 3.9 billion tonnes through the use of coal to produce electricity. If Canada were to sell China sufficient liquefied natural gas (of which we have abundant supply) to replace only 10 per cent of the coal China uses for power generation, the net reduction in emissions would be 200 million tonnes. (Natural gas emits half the carbon of coal.) So Canada prospers, carbon emissions are reduced, the entire carbon tax scheme becomes redundant and Guilbeault should be satisfied. And the opportunity to reduce emissions would be vastly increased once the LNG infrastructure is in place. Someone should do the business case for this.
Peter Demik, Hannon
Developer's sincerity questioned
At the June 2023 public information session for Glen Echo School redevelopment, community members raised concerns regarding the size and nature of the housing development proposed by Losani Homes. At that meeting, the developer's representatives indicated that issues raised by the community would be considered in the redevelopment process.
In late February, a project update was circulated to residents in the surrounding Corman neighbourhood. Unfortunately, it didn't speak to the main concerns raised by the community in June 2023. The public notice posted on the Glen Echo School property does suggest a modest reduction in the size of the proposed development from 310 to 264 units.
In the closing statement of the update, the developer claims to be "committed to a transparent and collaborative planning process by engaging in meaningful dialogue with Corman neighbourhood residents and municipal officials." There has been no dialogue, meaningful or otherwise.
Also, Corman residents must question the developer's sincerity when the final sentence on Page 2 of the update reads: "Failure to resolve this matter through city council may lead to an appeal to the OLT (Ontario Land Tribunal), bypassing residents and city officials, in which case the original proposal may be the preferred option." With a statement like this over their heads, how can Corman residents trust the developer won't simply appeal to the OLT for the original 310 units even if the amended proposal is approved? A developer interested in the evolving needs of the community or engaging in meaningful dialogue? I think not!
Bryan Leece, Hamilton
CAPTION: A reader suggests the city should compare the value of programs like Cathedral Cafe against the bike lane budget, and allocate dollars accordingly.

Document 391 of 500

Province gives Hamilton $17.5 million for meeting housing targets - and a warning over 'four-storey towers'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-gives-hamilton-17-5-million-meeting/docview/2973439207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+gives+Hamilton+%2417.5+million+for+meeting+housing+targets+-+and+a+warning+over+%27four-storey+towers%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford criticized the idea of "four-storey towers" in single-family neighbourhoods Friday even as he awarded Hamilton $17.5 million for meeting new housing targets - a building boom that relied largely on multi-residential construction.
Hamilton was one of only 19 municipalities to beat provincial targets for housing starts in 2023 and thus qualify for extra cash under the Ontario Building Faster Fund.
The city reached around 4,100 housing starts last year, blowing by the target by several hundred homes.
At a city hall announcement, Ford congratulated Mayor Andrea Horwath and council for "punching above their weigh when it comes to housing" - but at the same time, repeated a campaign-style vow to never impose provincewide rules to allow "four-storey towers" in neighbourhoods of single-family homes.
"If I put a four-storey tower beside you or your neighbour, they'd lose their minds," said Ford, who accused the Liberal party of pushing to allow taller buildings "dead centre" in low-rise neighbourhoods.
Ontario's own Housing Affordability Task Force has recommended allowing four-storey or four-unit buildings on all residential-zoned land as a way to quickly increase housing availability and affordability.
The provincial Liberal party recently built the as-of-right fourplex recommendation into a private member's bill as part of a "gentle density" plan to address the housing crisis.
Federal Liberal Housing Minister Sean Fraser has also urged Ford to reconsider his opposition to the idea, evening poking fun online by posting photos of four-unit buildings with the heading "Warning: the following images of fourplexes may frighten some politicians."
The provincial political debate may have limited impact in Hamilton - mostly because council has already endorsed local zoning changes to allow fourplexes across much of the city. (The changes will be enacted next week.)
Hamilton's record number of housing starts also relied heavily on dense housing, with nearly 85 per cent of building permits handed out for multi-residential projects like stacked towns and condo towers.
Ford stressed it is up to municipal councils to adopt or reject building height rules for their communities, but also suggested denser projects belong near transit and major road corridors.
He added he is not opposed to homes with four units, but was adamant the province will not give default permission for four-, six- or eight-storey buildings in low-rise neighbourhoods.
Hamilton's success so far in directing growth upwards within the urban boundary comes despite years of criticism from - and conflict with - the Tory government over whether to expand new construction into farmland and the Greenbelt. The province earlier tried to force a mass urban boundary expansion on Hamilton and open some Greenbelt areas to housing, but has since reversed both decisions amid public outrage and an ongoing police probe.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com

Document 392 of 500

City earns $17.5M for meeting housing targets: Ford lauds efforts, but warns against 'four-storey towers' in residential areas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-earns-17-5m-meeting-housing-targets/docview/2973393903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+earns+%2417.5M+for+meeting+housing+targets%3A+Ford+lauds+efforts%2C+but+warns+against+%27four-storey+towers%27+in+residential+areas&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford criticized the idea of "four-storey towers" in single-family neighbourhoods Friday even as he awarded Hamilton $17.5 million for meeting new housing targets - a building boom that relied largely on multi-residential construction.
Hamilton was one of only 19 municipalities to beat provincial targets for housing starts in 2023 and thus qualify for extra cash under the Ontario Building Faster Fund.
The city reached around 4,100 housing starts last year, blowing by the target by several hundred homes.
At a city hall announcement, Ford congratulated Mayor Andrea Horwath and council for "punching above their weight when it comes to housing" - but at the same time, repeated a campaign-style vow to never impose provincewide rules to allow "four-storey towers" in neighbourhoods of single-family homes.
"If I put a four-storey tower beside you or your neighbour, they'd lose their minds," said Ford, who accused the Liberal party of pushing to allow taller buildings "dead centre" in low-rise neighbourhoods.
Ontario's own Housing Affordability Task Force has recommended allowing four-storey or four-unit buildings on all residential-zoned land as a way to quickly increase housing availability and affordability.
The provincial Liberal party recently built the as-of-right fourplex recommendation into a private member's bill as part of a "gentle density" plan to address the housing crisis.
Federal Liberal Housing Minister Sean Fraser has also urged Ford to reconsider his opposition to the idea, evening poking fun online by posting photos of four-unit buildings with the heading "Warning: the following images of fourplexes may frighten some politicians."
The provincial political debate may have limited impact in Hamilton - mostly because council has already endorsed local zoning changes to allow fourplexes across much of the city. (The changes will be enacted next week.)
Hamilton's record number of housing starts also relied heavily on dense housing, with nearly 85 per cent of building permits handed out for multi-residential projects like stacked towns and condo towers.
Ford stressed it is up to municipal councils to adopt or reject building height rules for their communities, but also suggested denser projects belong near transit and major road corridors. He added he is not opposed to homes with four units, but was adamant the province will not give default permission for four-, six- or eight-storey buildings in low-rise neighbourhoods.
Hamilton's success so far in directing growth upwards within the urban boundary comes despite years of criticism from - and conflict with - the Tory government over whether to expand new construction into farmland and the Greenbelt. The province earlier tried to force a mass urban boundary expansion on Hamilton and open some Greenbelt areas to housing, but has since reversed both decisions amid public outrage and an ongoing police probe.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: At a city hall announcement Friday, Premier Doug Ford congratulated Mayor Andrea Horwath and council for "punching above their weight when it comes to housing." Premier Doug Ford was in Hamilton on Friday for an announcement of provincial funding for the city, which exceeded new housing targets for 2023. Mayor Andrea Horwath, as well as MPPs Neil Lumsden and Donna Skelly, were also on hand.Cathie Coward photos The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter

Document 393 of 500

Brian Mulroney knew something important that today's conservative politicians are ignoring

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brian-mulroney-knew-something-important-that/docview/2973393812/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brian+Mulroney+knew+something+important+that+today%27s+conservative+politicians+are+ignoring&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1987, U.S. president Ronald Reagan and prime minister Brian Mulroney stood side by side to announce the Montreal Protocol, one of the world's most successful treaties, which would cut the use of ozone-destroying chlorofluorocarbons. Four years later, Mulroney would stand next to Reagan's successor, president George H.W. Bush, to announce the Canada-United States Air Quality Agreement, which would dramatically reduce the toxic air pollution that was causing acid rain.
In each instance, scientists had sounded the alarm, environmentalists had taken up the cause and public concern in Canada was growing. Satellite images showed a gaping wound in the ozone layer over the Antarctic. Rivers and lakes many hundreds of kilometres from any factory had died from acidification.
What happened next changed everything: conservative leaders listened and then they led.
It wasn't easy. Well-funded industry lobbyists denied the science, and Mulroney had to cajole his American counterparts more than once, reminding them of the cost of inaction. The result was two agreements, based on science, that were smart for both the environment and the economy. According to Reagan's secretary of state, George Shultz, the president saw the Montreal Protocol as an insurance policy. To him, it wasn't ideological. It was prudent.
And it was. According to the United Nations Environment Programme, the Montreal Protocol will likely be responsible for preventing as many as two million cases of skin cancer each year by 2030 - and untold billions of dollars in health-care costs and lost economic opportunity.
How did they do it? True to their conservatism and belief in free markets, Mulroney and Bush used the market to tackle acid rain: a cap-and-trade system in the U.S. coupled with targets and regulations in Canada. It proved far cheaper and worked much faster than almost anyone expected.
I knew only some of this when, as minister of environment and climate change, I was tasked with developing a strategy to meet Canada's international climate commitments. Fortunately, several of the first calls I made were to conservatives who had learned these lessons when in government.
In addition to speaking with Mulroney, I reached out to Gordon Campbell, Jean Charest and former California governor Arnold Schwarzenegger, as each had introduced a price on carbon pollution as an effective and efficient way to tackle climate change and foster clean innovation and economic growth.
But it was Shultz, whom I met several times, who persuasively argued for the most conservative approach of all. He believed that to be effective and publicly accepted, carbon pricing should be revenue neutral, with all the money transparently refunded to people. Ironically, it was conservatives who helped me as we designed the basic principles for what would be a signature policy of our Liberal government.
None of this draws a flattering comparison to today's conservatives. Pierre Poilievre, Doug Ford, Scott Moe and Danielle Smith profess to love markets but fight tooth and nail to sabotage carbon pricing - one of the most effective mechanisms that their predecessors devised to protect the planet. Rather than fight for the planet, they fight climate action.
They disregard not only science but Canada's future competitiveness by ignoring the trillion-dollar opportunity of the clean transition, penalizing renewable energy in favour of oil and gas, and refusing to build the industries of tomorrow. It would be comical if it wasn't so consequential.
In 2018, I was taking hits from all sides. I had just come back from the G7 environment ministers meeting in Halifax. Environmentalists were calling for me to quit because the government wasn't doing enough on climate change, and I was being relentlessly attacked by conservative politicians for doing too much. The phone rang and an assistant told me it was the prime minister. I picked up the receiver expecting to hear my boss. Instead, it was Mulroney.
More than almost anyone, Mulroney understood that taking bold positions doesn't always make you popular - that real leadership comes with costs.
He called to lift my spirits, but also to remind me that politics is about doing what's right, to keep at it, and that nothing was more important than building a cleaner, more prosperous future for our kids.
Mulroney understood something that's been lost on today's conservative politicians: Being a true conservative means thinking long-term and taking practical action to conserve the environment and grow the economy. Canada and the planet are better for it.
CAPTION: U.S. president Ronald Reagan and prime minister Brian Mulroney, shown in 1986.
CREDIT: Catherine McKenna, Special to the Star

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2973393500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Drop-in centres need support
Thank you to the staff and volunteers of the Cathedral Café for everything they are doing to take some pressure off of our ever-growing homeless situation. Can we move away from the concepts of warming and cooling centres and admit drop-in services are essential 24-7 and 12 months a year in feeding and supporting our most vulnerable citizens? Funding these drop-in centres year-round provides a return on investment that includes mitigating financial demands on health care and other social services, and offers caring community volunteers an opportunity to help out. Here is my modest proposal to the city as one taxpayer who sees the immense value of drop-in centres: compare their value against your bike lane budget, and allocate your dollars accordingly.
Mike Johnston, Hamilton
'Bolstering' comes too late
Re: Hamilton 'bolsters'
anti-cyberattack team (March 21)
So, "Hamilton has added global accounting firm Deloitte to a team of outside experts hired to help it respond to a ransomware attack that has debilitated the municipality's IT network" as reported in the Spec. Quite apart from adding to this city's propensity to lavish money on "consultants," albeit probably unavoidable in this situation, I find the current hand-wringing and protestations of "bolstering" to be more indicative of panic control than anything else. Open stable door, meet bolted horse, that is to say.
Neville Newman, Hamilton
Police should look into threats
Re: Theatre cancels Jewish Film Festival April screenings over 'safety concerns' (March 20)
The Playhouse Cinema has announced that, in response to emails, phone calls and social media messages, it will not show the Hamilton Jewish Film Festival because of "security and safety concerns." The festival films included a love story, a family drama, documentaries about a medical mission to Ukraine and peaceful coexistence in Haifa and more. Not one film was political, but they did share a common denominator - they were all Jewish movies.
The owners of the cinema felt threatened because they wanted to host a Jewish event. I understand their fear; we have all seen Jewish institutions attacked and have heard the antisemitic chants. My question is, at what point do we label the pressure put on them for what it is: Jew hatred? When do we recognize that hiding behind political slogans should not protect those who commit hate crimes? The police should investigate anyone who sends hateful or threatening messages.
The owners of the Playhouse should have felt protected from the bullies and the haters, not threatened by them.
Daniel Kollek, Dundas
Speak up on pending changes
Re: Hamilton Conservation Authority's important work threatened (March 20)
Thank you for the article by Brian McHattie. This is such an important issue for Hamilton, a city of waterfalls. Encroachment onto our conservation areas puts our homes at risk of flooding and everyone needs to speak up and let officials at all level of government know we are against this.
Our homes, our water, endangered species, so many elements of our lives at risk. This action will be particularly acute for the Indigenous population that lives along the Grand River. Without the work the conservation areas do to control flooding, we are all in trouble.
Thank you, Hamilton Spectator, for covering this pressing issue. I urged all to speak up about this destructive action to conservation lands and our homes.
Mary Ann Frerotte, Dundas
Second thoughts about Liberals
Re: Crombie promises no provincial carbon tax
if her Liberals defeat Ford's Tories (March 18)
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, I am very disappointed to see that you do not support a carbon rebate program. I was happy to see you elected as leader, but this particular stance on global warming has given me second thoughts about supporting the Ontario Liberals.
My children and grandchildren will inherit a different world than I did and it will not be a better one. The carbon rebate program addresses the issue of leaving a better world for generations to come. Your non-support is a vote-getting ploy that will harm our planet.
I am unable to support a party that does not recognize the perils of climate change and will sacrifice future generations for votes. I will wait to see if you have a plan to replace the carbon rebate program; if I do not hear of a positive program to control/mitigate climate change, my time, money and vote will go to the NDP.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton

March 22: Conservation authorities being eroded and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-22-conservation-authorities-being-eroded/docview/2973170445/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+22%3A+Conservation+authorities+being+eroded+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservation authorities being eroded
Re: Hamilton Conservation Authority directors blast province's 'outrageous' conservation changes (March 13)
This recent article concludes with HCA board chair Brad Clark stating, "They create us, they regulate us." "They" are the Ford government. Our conservation authorities were created in 1954 after the destructive effects of Hurricane Hazel galvanized the need for a system of managing Ontario's watersheds on a regional basis. It was premier Leslie Frost's government that created them.
The province does regulate our CAs, although Ford's new "regulations" are slowly eroding our conservation authorities. Just the province's asinine removal of pollution as a consideration for development permits while adding "health and safety" without a clear definition defies all reasonable thought.
This surreal "dumbing down" of definitions and regulations is deliberately weakening our CAs when now, more than ever in today's volatile climate state, CAs' powers should be reinforced. Ford is positioning our CAs as collateral damage just to benefit his developer friends. I truly believe the eventual demise of our CAs is this government's end game.
A pathetic situation abounds in this province when the quality of our clean air and water is shelved in favour of private interests.
Stan Nowak, Dundas
Conservation authorities hinder growth
Re: Hamilton Conservation Authority's important work threatened (March 20)
The hyperbole and ad hominem attacks on Premier Doug Ford detracts from Brian McHattie's op-ed about the changes to be made to conservation authorities.
I was a builder, but retired early at 65 because I don't do well dealing with government bureaucracy.
The areas where I built usually involved dealing with conservation authorities. This was not too difficult in the 1970s and the 1980s and I appreciated what they were mandated to do. Over the years, this has changed.
Setbacks were increased exponentially, permits and fees, along with associated studies, drove costs higher. The time taken to get to the point where a building permit was available went from taking a week or two, to months or even years. Dealing with different conservation authorities was confusing because there were different rules and costs, even within the same municipality.
When I quit building 10 years ago, there were two people working at the Hamilton Conservation Authority on the "Sunshine List" (earning $100,000 or more); now, there are 30.
I imagine things are not any easier today. Land prices have gone through the roof over the last 20 years due to the lack of readily available land to build on because of conservation restrictions. This, along with soft costs such as $90,000 per unit in development charges, is making housing unaffordable.
Sure, there are places to build, but nothing is happening. Look at the Ancaster high property, the Sir John A. Macdonald property, or the parking lot in Stoney Creek and many others we read about daily where nothing can get going.
If the current conservation rules with 420 buffer zones would have been in place 200 years ago, there would be no City of Hamilton, and most of Dundas would not exist.
Gary Aikema, Flamborough
'Progressive' Tories will be missed
As we mourn the passing of two of Canada's great Progressive Conservatives, it is interesting to compare their accomplishments on progressive issues to some of today's not-so-progressive Conservatives.
Brian Mulroney's "efforts to secure a treaty to control acid rain pollution" demonstrated that he took securing clean air and water for future generations seriously. Today's federal Conservatives still aren't even convinced climate change is real, let alone have plans to deal with it. Making rhymes ("Axe the tax!") is not a plan.
Likewise, Roy McMurtry fought for equal marriage for same-sex couples. Oddly, that still seems to be an issue at times for some Conservatives today. I'm looking at you, Leslyn Lewis.
May both gentlemen rest in peace.
George Olds, Hamilton
Put ad money into education, health
Industries and businesses are struggling to regain their stride due to COVID-19. The Conservative government and party are doing their share to help the advertising business get back on its feet. The "What if we told you" campaign is designed to let us know how well the government, that is the Conservative government, is doing.
On the way to our next election, the Progressive Conservative party is contributing to the welfare of the advertising industry by letting us know that the new leader of the Liberal party is hazardous to our health. Why not spend that money on health care or education?
Bill Jurgenson, Vineland
Fight health care privatization
In its latest report, Robbing the public to build the private (Feb. 21), the Ontario Health Coalition presents a damning case to prove, beyond any doubt, that the Ford government has deliberately worked to provoke a crisis in the public health system. ("Area hospitals using loans, lines of credit to pay for care" and "Hamilton's hospitals short $135 million and counting" Hamilton Spectator, Jan. 25, 26).
Premier Doug Ford has succeeded in creating this crisis in order to establish a competing private-for-profit duplicate system. He has imposed wage caps for hospital workers while shifting millions of dollars to private-for-profit staffing agencies without wage constraints. Private clinics take the less complex surgeries, leaving the more complicated, costlier ones to the public hospitals with fewer staff. Ford's claim that you never have to pay with your credit card is false as upselling is common, as is experienced in private-for-profit clinics for cataract surgeries.
The very term "for profit" should be a warning to the public. The question we should be asking is this: "Who benefits from these policy choices of the Ford government and what is their connection to this government?" Maybe we should be asking for an independent investigation - our public single-tier health system is something to value and protect.
Elizabeth Morelli, Nanticoke
Focus on the homeless first
Unbelievable. Mayor Andrea Horwath and city councillors need to get there priorities in order. While other cities such as St. Catharines and Kitchener, and provinces such as Nova Scotia and Quebec to name a few, built homes to house the homeless, Hamilton still has no solutions. Winter weather is almost over and we will be hearing the same problem next winter.
Today, landlords are being asked to kick out tenants to make way for the LRT. The big problem for these tenants is they are not going to be able to afford rent in other places. Rents would be doubled or tripled, meaning they can't afford that rent hike, so we are going to see more and more tents and homeless people on the street.
It's time Mayor Horwath and councillors solved one problem at a time before they start the LRT, which will be another headache. They all ran on platforms that they were going to solve homeless problem - do it. Enough is enough.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
CAPTION: Readers share differing opinions on the roles and responsibilities of Ontario's conservation authorities.

Subject: Conservatism; Homeless people

Document 396 of 500

Next government will be receiving a fiscal 'poisoned chalice' from Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/next-government-will-be-receiving-fiscal-poisoned/docview/2973170415/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Next+government+will+be+receiving+a+fiscal+%27poisoned+chalice%27+from+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since the Great Depression, western governments followed the economic theories of British economist John Maynard Keynes.
Keynes claimed the Great Depression had been made far worse as countries attempted to keep a balanced budget. Keynes argued during "bad times" such as a war, recession, etc., governments should run deficits to support their citizens.
When times improve, that debt should be paid off and governments must balance the budget.
Keynesian economic theory also called for high interest rates to slow down inflation (which we have seen over the last couple of years) and low interest rates to stimulate the economy during periods of higher unemployment. (High interest rates make borrowing money far more expensive. That slows down spending, usually creates a small recession and thus reduces inflation. Low interest rates do the opposite).
Pierre Trudeau came to power in April 1968. He inherited a balanced budget and a national debt of around $18 billion. When he left office in June 1984, the national debt had risen under his watch by 167 per cent. He only balanced the budget once (1969-70, fiscal year.) Trudeau left an appalling fiscal mess for subsequent governments.
Now some will argue in his defence that the 1970s were a period of "stagflation" which had never been seen before. Stagflation refers to high unemployment and high inflation. Keynesian economic models had no answer to stagflation. That being said, under Pierre Trudeau there was little to no attempt to balance the budget or control spending.
Brian Mulroney and the Progressive Conservatives came to power in 1984 and were able to balance the operating budget.
However, with interest rates closing in on 20 per cent, the deficit and debt continued to grow. Mulroney had a very capable finance minister in Michael Wilson. Wilson, if he had been allowed to make some very tough decisions on cutting spending, probably would have slain the deficit and started paying down the national debt.
But Mulroney wasn't prepared for the political fallout that would have caused. And to be fair, Canadians were probably not ready at that time for the necessary sacrifices that would be required to get the fiscal situation under control.
By the mid 1990s, there was growing international concern over Canada's debt situation. Prime Minister Jean Chretien and his Finance Minister Paul Martin went to work. Through a combination of the most severe spending cuts, and reductions in transfers to the provinces, Chrétien and Martin eliminated the deficit, balanced the budget, and began paying down the national debt.
As laudable an achievement as that was, remember that Chrétien was a cabinet minister for the entire time that Trudeau was in office.
One of his portfolios was that of finance minister. Chrétien was a key decision-maker in creating the fiscal disaster that was finally fixed in the late 1990s. We need to be thankful that the Chrétien government repaired our terrible fiscal situation, but can never forget he was very much at fault for assisting in creating that same fiscal mess.
Unfortunately for Canada, voters forgot those hard learned lessons on fiscal responsibility. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau came to office in 2015 promising to run deficits.
The Conservatives maintained he inherited a balanced budget and small surplus from former prime minister Stephen Harper. There was no earthly reason to go into deficit before the pandemic struck in 2020. Yet voters put him into office. He lived up to that promise of running deficits for sure. He has never balanced a budget and has no plans to do so.
The next government will be receiving a fiscal "poisoned chalice" from Trudeau and will find very little manoeuvring room when it comes to finances. Whoever is the next prime minister, their message will be austerity.
CAPTION: We need to be thankful that the Jean Chrétien government repaired our terrible fiscal situation, but can never forget that he assisted in creating that same fiscal mess, Craig Wallace writes.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Doug Ford has made 'almost no progress' on pledge to build new homes, Trudeau government charges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-made-almost-no-progress-on-pledge/docview/2973166438/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+made+%27almost+no+progress%27+on+pledge+to+build+new+homes%2C+Trudeau+government+charges&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's failure to meet federal affordable housing targets is jeopardizing $357 million in funding from Ottawa, Housing Minister Sean Fraser warns.
But Queen's Park counters it is being treated unfairly because its affordable housing stock is the oldest in Canada, meaning repairs to existing units have come at the expense of building new ones.
In an embarrassing revelation that emerged as Ford rewards municipalities for achieving provincial targets for new home construction, it turns out his own government is "lagging desperately" on its own objectives.
"Should Ontario fail to provide a revised plan demonstrating how it intends to meet its housing targets under the agreement, the province will not receive $357 million intended for affordable housing from the federal government," wrote Fraser in a letter to provincial Housing Minister Paul Calandra.
"Speaking frankly, the proposed Action Plan is a disappointment. It shows almost no progress toward reaching the affordable housing expansion target, and proposes to achieve only 1,184 units of the 19,660 required by the end of 2024-25," the minister wrote Thursday night.
"This leaves 94 per cent of the target to be achieved during the last three years of the agreement, which is not realistic," he said, stressing the "bilateral funding will lapse and the impact this will have on Ontarians in housing need will be devastating."
Fraser has given the province until the end of the month and "no further extensions are possible" because Ottawa already gave Ontario an additional year to revise its plan in 2023.
But Calandra said "it is unacceptable that you would choose to threaten our most vulnerable with ($357 million) in cuts."
"Ontario respectfully expects to be treated by the federal government as the true and equal partner," said Calandra on Friday.
"Withholding funding would simply be a punitive measure that will benefit no one, from jeopardizing the approximately $20 million commitment to the city of Toronto to address asylum seeker inflows, to impacting Ontario's fiscal plan as we have already covered the federal share of approximately ($357 million) in ... funding for 2023-24," he said.
The provincial minister pointed out "the economic landscape has shifted drastically with the rising costs of building materials, supply chain disruptions, gaps in the labour market, and most of all, higher interest rates" since the 2018 agreement on the targets.
Calandra also emphasized the province has Canada's oldest affordable housing stock and "by focusing on the repair backlog, Ontario has successfully staved off the risk to both affordability and availability of units for tens of thousands of families and overachieved on the ... repairs target."
"Given the age of Ontario's stock, focusing on creating new supply while limiting ourselves to meeting just the 20 per cent repairs target set out in the bilateral agreement would amount to gross negligence on our part," he said.
"The people of Ontario deserve the same treatment as other Canadians. I am asking that the federal government treat Ontario the same as it does British Columbia when it comes to counting units."
Their war of words comes as Ford has been criss-crossing Ontario handing out cheques to mayors for achieving provincially mandated municipal housing targets.
On Friday, the premier gave Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath $17.6 million for breaking ground on 4,142 new units last year.
"Hamilton is getting it done on housing and we are glad to reward them for their success," said Ford.
Last month, he gave Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow $114 million after her city started 31,656 new homes in 2023.
CAPTION: Ontario Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks during a press conference on housing development in the Greater Toronto Area at Toronto city hall on Feb. 22, 2024.

Document 399 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2973075911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservation
authorities are
being eroded
Re: Hamilton Conservation Authority directors blast province's 'outrageous' conservation changes (March 13)
This recent article concludes with HCA board chair Brad Clark stating, "They create us, they regulate us." "They" are the Ford government. Our conservation authorities were created in 1954 after the destructive effects of Hurricane Hazel galvanized the need for a system of managing Ontario's watersheds on a regional basis. It was premier Leslie Frost's government that created them.
The province does regulate our CAs, although Ford's new "regulations" are slowly eroding our conservation authorities. Just the province's asinine removal of pollution as a consideration for development permits while adding "health and safety" without a clear definition defies all reasonable thought.
This surreal "dumbing down" of definitions and regulations is deliberately weakening our CAs when now, more than ever in today's volatile climate state, CAs' powers should be reinforced. Ford is positioning our CAs as collateral damage just to benefit his developer friends. I truly believe the eventual demise of our CAs is this government's end game. A pathetic situation abounds in this province when the quality of our clean air and water is shelved in favour of private interests.
Stan Nowak, Dundas
Conservation
authorities are hindering growth
Re: Hamilton Conservation Authority's important work threatened (March 20)
The hyperbole and ad hominem attacks on Premier Doug Ford detracts from Brian McHattie's op-ed about the changes to be made to conservation authorities.
I was a builder, but retired early at 65 because I don't do well dealing with government bureaucracy.
The areas where I built usually involved dealing with conservation authorities. This was not too difficult in the 1970s and the 1980s and I appreciated what they were mandated to do. Over the years, this has changed.
Setbacks were increased exponentially, permits and fees, along with associated studies, drove costs higher. The time taken to get to the point where a building permit was available went from taking a week or two, to months or even years. Dealing with different conservation authorities was confusing because there were different rules and costs, even within the same municipality.
When I quit building 10 years ago, there were two people working at the Hamilton Conservation Authority on the "Sunshine List" (earning $100,000 or more); now, there are 30.
I imagine things are not any easier today. Land prices have gone through the roof over the last 20 years due to the lack of readily available land to build on because of conservation restrictions. This, along with soft costs such as $90,000 per unit in development charges, is making housing unaffordable.
Sure, there are places to build, but nothing is happening. Look at the Ancaster high property, the Sir John A. Macdonald property, or the parking lot in Stoney Creek and many others we read about daily where nothing can get going.
If the current conservation rules with 420 buffer zones would have been in place 200 years ago, there would be no city of Hamilton, and most of Dundas would not exist.
Gary Aikema, Flamborough
'Progressive'
Tories missed
As we mourn the passing of two of Canada's great Progressive Conservatives, it is interesting to compare their accomplishments on progressive issues to some of today's not-so-progressive Conservatives.
Brian Mulroney's "efforts to secure a treaty to control acid rain pollution" demonstrated that he took securing clean air and water for future generations seriously. Today's federal Conservatives still aren't even convinced climate change is real, let alone have plans to deal with it. Making rhymes ("Axe the tax!") is not a plan.
Likewise, Roy McMurtry fought for equal marriage for same-sex couples. Oddly, that still seems to be an issue at times for some Conservatives today. I'm looking at you, Leslyn Lewis.
May both gentlemen rest in peace.
George Olds, Hamilton
Focus on the homeless first
Unbelievable. Mayor Andrea Horwath and city councillors need to get there priorities in order. While other cities such as St. Catharines and Kitchener, and provinces such as Nova Scotia and Quebec to name a few, built homes to house the homeless, Hamilton still has no solutions. Winter weather is almost over and we will be hearing the same problem next winter.
Today, landlords are being asked to kick out tenants to make way for the LRT. The big problem for these tenants is they are not going to be able to afford rent in other places. Rents would be doubled or tripled, meaning they can't afford that rent hike, so we are going to see more and more tents and homeless people on the street.
It's time Mayor Horwath and councillors solved one problem at a time before they start the LRT, which will be another headache. They all ran on platforms that they were going to solve the homeless problem - do it. Enough is enough.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
Put ad money into education, health
Industries and businesses are struggling to regain their stride due to COVID-19. The Conservative government and party are doing their share to help the advertising business get back on its feet. The "What if we told you" campaign is designed to let us know how well the government, that is the Conservative government, is doing.
On the way to our next election, the Progressive Conservative party is contributing to the welfare of the advertising industry by letting us know that the new leader of the Liberal party is hazardous to our health. Why not spend that money on health care or education?
Bill Jurgenson, Vineland

Document 400 of 500

Unhappy about Ontario's carbon levy? Thank Doug Ford for making it happen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/unhappy-about-ontarios-carbon-levy-thank-doug/docview/2973075331/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unhappy+about+Ontario%27s+carbon+levy%3F+Thank+Doug+Ford+for+making+it+happen&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Carbon is in the air and Doug Ford smells victory.
"Axe the Tax" is pumping up his fellow Tories, from prairie premiers to federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. Alberta and Saskatchewan are flirting with refusing to remit carbon levies, turning their threatened boycott into a battering ram against Ottawa.
Now, Ford has seized on a convenient villain closer to home.
Ontario's new Liberal leader, Bonnie Crombie, is being cast by the Tories as a Trojan Horse who will saddle the province with a carbon levy all her own. Ever since she ran for the leadership last year, Ford has fulminated about her nefarious hidden agenda, and his Progressive Conservatives have bankrolled a massive advertising campaign about the alleged Crombie carbon peril.
So when she announced this week that a new climate panel of experts would advise her Liberals on future environmental policy, the Tories were ready to pounce.
Unfortunately for Ford, Crombie pointedly ruled out ever bringing in any new carbon levy to Ontario. Mindful of the political climate, she avoided their trap - and so the Tories lost their minds.
"We will ensure major polluters pay, but we will not have an Ontario carbon tax on consumers," Crombie proclaimed.
On cue, senior cabinet ministers were paraded out to the media to wag their fingers in formation: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy arched his eyebrows gravely, while Energy Minister Todd Smith summoned his baritone voice of doom.
Crombie is "queen of the carbon tax," Smith thundered, struggling to keep a straight face while dutifully reciting Ford's favourite barb.
The problem with that attack line? The political lineage of the carbon levy suggests otherwise.
In truth, Crombie cannot possibly be queen of the carbon tax, for the unrivalled king of the federal levy in Ontario is none other than Doug Ford. Proof of paternity lies in his provincial history.
Until Ford took power in 2018, there was no carbon levy in Ontario. The previous Liberal premier, Kathleen Wynne, looked long and hard at a provincial carbon price and decided against it, just as Crombie did this week.
Instead, Wynne's Liberals followed the path pioneered years before by right-leaning governments in Quebec and California by setting up a market-based mechanism known as cap and trade. Yes, it's a form of carbon pricing, but it's arguably more efficient in making industrial polluters pay.
With its cap and trade framework, Ontario joined Quebec in winning an exemption from the federal carbon levy.
All that changed when Ford dismantled the system, automatically triggering the federal backstop. Which means that the result of Ford's free-for-all was a carbon levy for Ontario.
Thank you, Doug Ford.
No, he didn't "Axe the Tax." He triggered the tax for all Ontarians.
All that said, there's no shame in carbon pricing, as Ford himself seems to realize. After all, his PC government quietly imposed its own made-in-Ontario carbon pricing regimes on industry shortly after taking power.
The 2022 emission performance standards program targets industrial polluters. The clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system was brought in last year.
Thank you, Doug Ford. Again.
In fact, Ford has undermined the federal carbon levy by leveraging it to win two elections. In the 2018 campaign, he promised to cut the provincial gas tax, and ultimately delivered on it in time for the 2022 election.
It is a shell game within a political game, playing on the contradictory impulses many Canadians harbour toward climate change. On the one hand, people worry about global warming; on the other hand, they don't want to dip into their own pockets.
Voters prefer to pay lip service to the environment rather than pay a price at the gas pump. As a populist politician, Ford understands that contradiction and exploits it at the polls.
Hence the relentless attacks on Crombie, with Ford's Tories demanding daily that she denounce the federal Liberals for putting a price on carbon. A better question is whether Ford, as Ontario's premier, would ever distance himself from any Western premier who transforms a routine policy disagreement over the carbon levy into constitutional illegality by refusing to remit tax revenues.
One might hope that Ontario's Tories learned their lesson after flailing and failing in their court case against the carbon levy after Ford allocated $30 million for a legal war chest - a tax on stupidity and futility.
Ontario lost at every stage, all the way up to the Supreme Court of Canada in 2021. A losing legal battle, perhaps, but an endless political crusade against the so-called carbon queen.
In this fight to the finish, Ford remains the undisputed king of carbon in Ontario.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford was a surprise guest speaker at the Grain Farmers of Ontario's March Classic trade show at RBC Place London in London, Ontario on March 19, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Emissions trading; Carbon; Climate change; Political advertising; Environmental tax

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Title: Unhappy about Ontario's carbon levy? Thank Doug Ford for making it happen

Document 401 of 500

In win for Doug Ford, Ottawa drops environmental assessment for Ontario's Highway 413

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/win-doug-ford-ottawa-drops-environmental/docview/2973248079/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+win+for+Doug+Ford%2C+Ottawa+drops+environmental+assessment+for+Ontario%27s+Highway+413&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a win for Premier Doug Ford, there's a tentative agreement to scrap a federal environmental assessment Ontario blames for delaying construction of the controversial Highway 413.
The province and the federal government have submitted a joint consent order to Federal Court to cancel a federal environmental impact study, Ontario Attorney General Doug Downey confirmed Thursday.
"We have come to an agreement," he said, noting a final decision is up to the court.
"We'll see what happens with that," Downey added, declining further comment.
The federal Impact Assessment Agency said in a statement that "Ontario and Canada have mutually agreed to resolve this litigation."
On Parliament Hill, federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault declined to comment because the agreement is before the court.
The legal development follows a push from Downey in December for a judicial review and a plea for Ottawa to bow out of an environmental assessment "so that our government can get shovels in the ground."
That call followed a Supreme Court of Canada decision in October that Ottawa "plainly overstepped" its constitutional authority in regard to a provincial project.
Highway 413 is a proposed 60-kilometre bypass route through Peel Region from Highway 401 at Milton northeast to Highway 400. It was designated for a federal assessment three years ago following concerns raised by environmental groups, area residents and municipalities.
Saying more housing and reducing gridlock are major priorities, the Ford government has brushed aside fears of urban sprawl and an internal report indicating 11 species are at risk, including the western chorus frog and a rare dragonfly known as the rapids clubtail.
"It has always been our priority, as everyone knows, to build Highway 413," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Thursday.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the proposed highway is a "waste" of $10 billion that would pave 2,000 acres of prime farmland and 400 acres of the Greenbelt along a route just north of the existing and "underutilized" toll Highway 407.
"Let's just pay the tolls for truckers on Highway 407 to relieve gridlock throughout the (Highway) 401 corridor," he suggested.
The lobby group Environmental Defence called the tentative deal a "gross abandonment of federal responsibility" and urged Ottawa to protect wildlife by designating Highway 413 for an assessment under a revised Impact Assessment Act.
"Highway 413 is the bulldozer front of farm-eating, nature-killing, climate-warming, developer-enriching sprawl that would cost Ontario billions," said executive director Tim Gray.
With files from Alex Ballingall
CAPTION: Attorney General Doug Downey speaks in the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 26, 2024.

Subject: Attorneys general; Judicial reviews

Title: In win for Doug Ford, Ottawa drops environmental assessment for Ontario's Highway 413

Document 402 of 500

March 21: We voted for change, LRT should go to Dundas and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-21-we-voted-change-lrt-should-go-dundas/docview/2972743213/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+21%3A+We+voted+for+change%2C+LRT+should+go+to+Dundas+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We voted for a change
Re: Horwath the mayor is not Horwath the NDP leader (March 19)
So our Mayor Andrea Horwath has stopped her fiery, unrestrained NDP leadership ways and settled into her cosy, comfy mayor's office chair to provide balance, confidence and the tools our city council needs to operate. I find this so sad, as we hoped she would carry on as aggressive and outspoken as she did as the NDP leader; after all, that's what we knew from her resume. Don't you normally hire someone based upon their resume? We didn't want the same as what we had; we wanted a change, a CEO with experience to provide fresh ideas and alternatives, keep us informed and be our spokesperson as the face of our city.
John Day, Hamilton
More transparency, please
One can understand the difference between the role of an opposition leader at Queen's Park and that of mayor of the City of Hamilton. However, Andrea Horwath ran with the promise of transparency and that has been lacking. One can imagine that dealing with the ransomware attack requires patience and a strategic plan. But it took too long for any comment to be made by the mayor. Indeed, we don't want a "mouthpiece," but we need to be informed in a reasonable timeline about the affairs of our city. That should be "transparent" in the role of any mayor.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Heritage in miniature
Re: 'Lego Dundas' exhibition celebrates historic architecture of the former town (March 19)
Thank you to the editors for front-page placement and feature article in Arts and Life of Mark McNeil's excellent piece on Lego miniatures of Dundas landmarks. In a turbulent world, it's a refreshing bit of positive news. Keep it coming. For those who don't know, Lego has a large adult selection of buildable sets. Rankin MacGillivray's custom design pieces are fantastic.
Al Kruzins, Dundas
Projects will improve city
Re: LRT and Main Street changes will transform Hamilton for the better (March 16)
I congratulate The Spectator for printing this brilliant column. It's high time you gave a forward-thinking educated expert a platform to refute Scott Radley. While I may not agree with everything city hall is doing, I'm totally onside with the Main Street conversion and the LRT. These two projects will completely transform the lower city for the better.
Siobhan Kelly, Hamilton
Appealing to lower instincts
The hike in the carbon tax scheduled for April 1 will give all Canadians a chance to fight an existential threat to livability here on this spaceship. There are political pressures being thrown out by premiers and the federal Conservative leader. They are, all but one, Conservatives.
Canadians will probably fall into line with these climate deniers and elect Conservatives in the federal election next year. It will be interesting to watch as the Conservatives remove incentives to move away from fossil fuels and ramp up more greenhouse gas. Or will they? Should they do those things, the history books will not be kind. Just as we in Ontario are not kind when we think of Mike Harris who sold off the 407 to private hands.
However, Ontarians elected Doug Ford after sitting daily in gridlock on the 400, 401, 403, DVP, etc. for the last 25 years or so. I for one don't put a lot of faith in the general public to make good choices when politicians use division and fear to win votes. Appealing to people's lower instincts will only tear at the fabric of the best place to live in the world.
Bill Brush, Hamilton
LRT should go to Dundas
I might as well add something to the never-ending LRT discussion, since I have been part of it from the very beginning. We had meetings at the convention centre under then-mayor Fred Eisenberger with round-table discussions, among others, on how far the line was supposed to go. I live in Dundas and saw it as ideal to start or end here at the University Plaza. Plenty of space for a turnaround and an abundance of parking spaces. It would cover all of Dundas and the surrounding areas, especially the backcountry of Flamborough.
It has puzzled me ever since, why this has not become part of the final plan. The decision has been made, McMaster it is, as if it is supposed to be for students only. That leaves everybody out from the before mentioned group of people from different areas. I am talking about all-day passengers, people going to work, seniors, women with children and all those that have to do their business in crowded downtown or just for the joy of it. That's where the money is, and not from students that use it in the morning and afternoon at a reduced rate. There is still time to do so. If not, scrap the entire project that we don't need in the first place, a burden for years to come. Use the money to fix our crumbling infrastructure.
Karl Schwarze, Dundas
No respect on offer here
I know I'm supposed to treat those who hold different political opinions from mine with respect and I know it's wrong to engage in the "culture wars," but come on!
How am I expected to treat with respect people who reject the overwhelming evidence pointing to human activity causing climate change and who oppose policies designed to mitigate it; who advocate for restricting the rights of marginalized groups based on religious doctrine and who allow a potentially fatal disease like measles to break out because of their belief in easily debunked conspiracy theories around vaccines? What's next, polio?
And don't even get me started on the people who think some narcissistic sociopath is the answer to their prayers because "God has used some very imperfect people to perform his works."
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Proposed changes ignore reality
Re: LRT and Main Street changes will transform Hamilton for the better (March 16)
Most Hamiltonians I know will not be among the "tens of thousands" Léa Ravensbergen believes will leave their cars at home in a brave new world of urban design. The ideology that underpins this conclusion fails to address the reality most of us face in our daily lives. Among the issues overlooked are the value of personal time, vehicle convenience, comfort, safety and security, travel with children and family groups, impacts of weather, transport of goods, groceries, work tools, suburban or out of town destinations, the aging population and so much more.
The "tens of thousands," according to the writer, will still have their cars at home, therefore requiring a safe and efficient road system. That is not to discount the large group which can function efficiently in certain urban settings that include proximity to employment, shopping, entertainment and recreation.
In my years working and living in downtown Toronto, I rarely used my car in the city and many colleagues in fact did not ever own one. Hamilton has a different set of circumstances and the approach considered by Ms. Ravensbergen will do more harm than good, especially as it relates to the unfortunate LRT plan, which will cost billions of dollars to simply replace a very efficient and flexible bus service with a street car. The "tens of thousands," according to the writer, will still have their cars at home, which means there will still be a need for safe and efficient road system for those many occasions when transit is not practical. After all, even the ivory towers of academia also have parking lots.
In my experience, the zealotry behind proposed changes to Hamilton street mobility usually involves dismissive personal attacks on those with other opinions. Ms. Ravensbergen begins her article by accusing Scott Radley of inciting "hysteria" in his commentary on proposed traffic changes. She could spend some profitable time testing her conclusions with residents who live and work beyond Main and Forsythe. She may find their views about getting around Hamilton somewhat different and perhaps better informed.
Bob Bratina, Hamilton
CAPTION: We hoped Andrea Horwath would carry on as aggressive and outspoken as she did as the NDP leader; after all, that's what we knew from her resume, a reader writes.
