The carbon levy will haunt Justin Trudeau whether he raises it or not

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carbon-levy-will-haunt-justin-trudeau-whether-he/docview/2957974223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+carbon+levy+will+haunt+Justin+Trudeau+whether+he+raises+it+or+not&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau acknowledged this week that he's no longer popular. Hearkening back to similar comments by former prime minister Brian Mulroney, whose funeral will be held Saturday, Trudeau said, "My job is not to be popular. ... My job is to do the right things for Canada now, and do the right things for Canadians a generation from now."
Friends and foes suggest a solution to Trudeau's popularity problem - one that may lead the Liberals towards an existential crisis in the next election - is a pause to the carbon price increase coming to Canadians' wallets on April 1.
But Trudeau has much more to lose if he amends his government's signature climate policy than if he stands his ground.
Next month, the levy will rise by 23 per cent, from $65 a tonne to $80. The renamed Canada Carbon Rebates - those quarterly payments Canadians receive to offset the carbon price - will rise to reflect the change. But everyday items will also be more expensive: gasoline for your vehicle, the cost to heat your home, food at the grocery store, airplane tickets for your summer vacation.
With Canadians feeling squeezed from inflation and higher interest rates, it's no surprise that provincial premiers - those who, unlike their peers in Quebec and British Columbia, chose not to enact their own carbon pricing regimes and rely on the federal backstop, making price hikes Ottawa's problems and not their own - now want Trudeau to pause the plan.
On Monday, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey, a fellow Liberal, penned a letter politely requesting Trudeau pause the increase, "at least until inflation stabilizes, interest rates lower and related economic pressures on the cost of living sufficiently cool."
On Tuesday, New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs called on Trudeau to "immediately cancel his punishing April 1st carbon tax hike. Even better, cancel the carbon tax altogether."
Later that evening, Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston wrote his own letter to the prime minister, noting the increase will add 3.3 cents per litre of gas and asking that Trudeau "cancel the carbon tax before any more financial damage is done."
On Wednesday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford told reporters he doesn't understand what the federal Liberals are thinking. "If they don't start ... putting money back into people's pockets instead of filling their pockets, guess what, they're going to get annihilated."
On Thursday, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith noted seven premiers are now united in demanding Ottawa reverse course.
On Friday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, in New Brunswick for a "spike the hike" rally, called on the prime minister to cancel his "heartless and cruel ... tax hike."
But Trudeau and federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault appear unmoved.
They argue their plan is an affordability measure; that it returns more money into most people's pockets; that without the carbon price and the carbon price rebates, more people would struggle to make ends meet.
But as Ford's comments reflect, many Canadians - including the premier - don't understand how the federal program works. It's designed to make things more expensive, so you're pushed to find alternatives and adopt new habits - things like installing a heat pump, buying a hybrid or electric vehicle, or biking to work.
The program was always designed to ensure most Canadians weren't financially disadvantaged, with a significant portion of the levy collected returned to residents. A family of four in urban Ontario, for example, will receive $1,120 this year; in Alberta it's $1,800. But, as Guilbeault acknowledges, too little attention was placed on the second part of the Grits' plan.
Carbon pricing was always going to be a tough sell because it is visible. That is both its strength and its vulnerability.
It may also be the Liberals' strength and vulnerability.
Last fall, after intense lobbying from his Atlantic MPs, Trudeau announced a nationwide pause of the carbon levy on home heating oil for three years, a federal-provincial plan to rebate the costs of installing heat pumps, and a doubling of the rural climate rebate top-up. There was little impact at the polls. It may have stopped Liberal hemorrhaging in the Atlantic region, but it did not reverse the slide. Abacus Data had the Liberals polling at 32 per cent support in September compared to 43 per cent for the Conservatives. This month, the Grits are at 33 per cent, and the Tories are at 48 per cent in Atlantic Canada (with a comparable margin of error of plus or minus 2.53 per cent, 19 times out of 20.)
Why would the Liberals think any new pause to the carbon pricing regime will lead to an uptick in support for them? It may instead lead to more bleeding as climate-conscious voters decide Trudeau is willing to sacrifice the predictability of his plan for short-term political gain. That would be the quickest path to Liberal irrelevance.
With a few percentage points shaved off of Liberal support - as Abacus CEO David Coletto notes happened in November, following the heating oil announcement - the Grits could be battling the NDP for second-place territory. And that, notes Coletto, would be a death blow to the Liberals.
It's one more reason Poilievre and his allies may be putting so much pressure on Trudeau to "spike the hike."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks to the media following a meeting with Quebec Premier Francois Legault in Montreal on March 15, 2024.

Document 435 of 500

An expected defeat was unexpectedly painful for Justin Trudeau's Liberals. That should set off alarm bells

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/expected-defeat-was-unexpectedly-painful-justin/docview/2957974165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+expected+defeat+was+unexpectedly+painful+for+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals.+That+should+set+off+alarm+bells&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
MG: I know better than to make predictions in politics. I'm not paid enough to risk that kind of embarrassment, and we live in such bizarrely turbulent times that I'm happy if any given day goes roughly as I expected. But I can't help but note that the Durham byelection result earlier this month is bad news for the Liberals.
I'm mostly firmly in the "byelections don't matter" camp, but this specific byelection isn't just sending back a new Tory to replace another Tory in a safely CPC seat. The vote totals in this election are a real-world validation of months of polling that is just brutal for PM Trudeau and his government. I'll agree to all the usual caveats about campaigns mattering and there being a lot of time left, but I don't see how anyone at Liberal HQ who was trying to hold onto much hope is going to be able to do that after Durham. The result doesn't matter, but getting confirmation that the polls are broadly accurate should.
SD: We promised to disagree last time we wrote, so let me get my two points of agreement out of the way quickly. Yes, that was a brutal byelection for the Liberals and I, too, am cautious about reading too much into the results of them. You are correct, too - this confirms what all those polls have been fairly screaming to the Liberals over this past year. Here is where we may disagree, though: I still think there's a path for the Liberals to recover before the next election, though it's a faint and narrow one.
MG: I mean, I'll let you make the case, but I don't reject the notion entirely. I think we're just getting into the kind of territory where the only man who can stop Pierre Poilievre is Pierre Poilievre, and he may well try. What's your sense of the path?
SD: First, the path comes from taking the Durham byelection seriously - as seriously as Stephen Harper did way back in 2003 after a byelection in the southwestern Ontario riding of Perth-Middlesex. It was the disastrous showing for the Canadian Alliance in Perth-Middlesex that convinced Harper it was time to get serious about uniting the right in Canada.
Now, I'm not saying Justin Trudeau needs to go out and merge with the New Democrats (some say that's already happened). But I am saying the progressive centre in Canadian politics is as fragmented as the conservative centre was in the early 2000s and Liberals seem to be losing their hold on the mainstream middle of the road.
MG: This won't be much of a reply. It's the inverse of that annoying thing people do: more of a question than a comment. But do you think there's a path back for them that's less dramatic than some kind of fundamental realignment of Canadian federal politics? I say this with no snark, but if this is their path back, they're in more trouble than I thought!
SD: They are in serious trouble. But I had been watching Durham for echoes of what we've seen in the U.S. Even though Republicans and Trump are supposed to be on a romp back to power in the White House, the Democrats keep doing better than predicted in midterms and special votes, such as the recent one to fill George Santos' seat in Congress. David Brooks wrote about this phenomenon in the New York Times late last year, saying: "Americans increasingly use polls to vent, not vote." The idea is that it's all well and good to vent right now, whether that's in a poll or byelection, but when it comes to the actual ballot-box choice, the moderate centrist voters, especially those frustrated with political turbulence as you put it, will lean progressive, not conservative.
The trick for the Liberals, as it is for (President Joe) Biden and the Democrats, is to make very sure that Canadians stare down that choice here.
MG: That's one of the reasons I won't make a firm prediction. It'll be easier for the two of us (and everyone else!) to turn their minds to this the day after we know the outcome of the next U.S. presidential election (which I do not assume will be the day after the election - we should be prepared for some chaos, if only to manage our expectations properly). So sure. I can sketch out a scenario along those broad lines, too: a VERY nasty U.S. election campaign, a re-elected Donald Trump, a few months in which Trump says and does things that horrify a big chunk of the population. I dare say that to some extent the Liberals are already campaigning along those lines. It could work. But I also am skeptical it'll work in the way they think. If Trump goes Full Trump on the world again, I'm not convinced attempts to link him to Poilievre succeed in alarming Canadians. It could easily just make Poilievre look vastly more palatable by comparison.
SD: Well that's a sobering view of the future. All too plausible, too, I'm sorry to say. (And apologies, here we are agreeing again.) I'll say this: I think Liberals believe they did a good job handling Trump once and voters will remember that when it counts. I'm not sure voters do. But I also think we're not paying enough attention to the polls showing some discomfort/unease with the idea of Prime Minister Poilievre.
MG: Yeah, I get that. But he's got some room to manoeuvre. The polling aggregators have him somewhere in line for 200-210 seats. Which means he can lose 30-40 seats to that discomfort and still have a majority for up to five years. I do agree, as noted above, that he could definitely alienate a lot of voters. He seems to have a problem controlling his acid tongue at times, and that could sour people. But as I said, he could alienate 30 seats worth of people and still win handily. So I do pay attention to those polls. But he definitely has some margin for error, no pun intended.
SD: Agreed, and Trudeau, unlike the 2019 and 2021 elections, does not. Moreover, if Liberals want the next election to be framed as a stark choice for the moderate middle in Canada, they need voters to come to that conclusion themselves, not jam it down their throats. One senior Liberal admitted to me a while back that they probably can't run a campaign of hope the next time, but they have to be very careful with a campaign of fear, too.
MG: They are in a tough spot. No doubt about it. They need to wait until the U.S. election shakes out, to some extent. But they won't have much time after that. I don't envy them their problems, that's for sure.
CAPTION: Conservative candidate Jamil Jivani won the Durham byelection on March 4, 2024.na-onthecontrary-mar16 printna-onthecontrary-mar16 web

Document 436 of 500

Prime Minister Pierre Poilievre? That's bad news for Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/prime-minister-pierre-poilievre-thats-bad-news/docview/2957743695/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prime+Minister+Pierre+Poilievre%3F+That%27s+bad+news+for+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By rights, these two right-wingers should be soul mates.
Yet they are anything but.
Premier Doug Ford and federal Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre barely know each other. Nor are they in a hurry to get better acquainted.
They have no private conversations to speak of. Nor any public interactions to take stock of.
Helming the Progressive Conservative government at Queen's Park, Ford is Canada's most powerful Tory. As leader of the federal Conservatives, Poilievre is putatively Canada's prime minister in waiting.
By tradition, these two top Tories should be as one. Yet it is hard to fathom two fellow travellers moving in such different directions - keeping their political distance when in close proximity.
The best evidence of that avoidance came last weekend, when Poilievre convened a mass political rally in Ford's home riding of Etobicoke North. Poilievre's call went out to all true believers to stand together against the federal carbon tax, but Ford sent word out to caucus that he'd sit it out.
Despite their shared antipathy to taxes and hostility to government meddling, they clearly feel no mutual felicity. In truth, they have no time for each other, never having attempted a meeting of minds in the 18 months since Poilievre became federal party leader and the six years since Ford took over the provincial Tories.
What's keeping these two conservatives so far apart? Why can't they be friends and allies, if not comrades-in-arms?
From talking to those in the know, it's apparent that the differences are as much personal as political, more stylistic than substantive.
Poilievre is a lifetime politician who never went to charm school. Ford had a life before politics, capable of turning on the charm to make a sale for the family business.
Where Poilievre is constantly chippy, Ford is alternately chipper to get his way. Where the federal leader is cantankerous, the premier can be gregarious when the mood moves him.
While Poilievre disdains Ottawa, he embodies the bubble of the federal capital - right down to the riding he represents. By contrast, Ford projects an everyman persona, cultivating the salty language of working folks while downplaying his privileged upbringing.
But there is a bigger divide between these two personalities, for Ford already wields power while Poilievre is merely on the cusp of it.
The federal opposition leader is ever the ideologue because he can afford to be a poseur rather than a policymaker. Ford is forever the populist, more mindful of the realities of governing and the early mistakes of harsh restraint.
Canada's Conservatives are anchored in Western regional alienation, oblivious to the instinct for accommodation in Ontario. As premier, Ford is more mindful of the province's propensity for centrism and compromise, no matter his initial impulse for conflict and upheaval.
Paradoxically, the premier sees his true counterpart as Liberal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. While the two leaders are far from soul mates, they are at least simpatico - breezily cutting cheques and cutting ribbons for new factories or shared programs.
If Ford is more seasoned than six years ago, Poilievre remains utterly untested after his 18 months in the spotlight - reflexively riding the anti-Trudeau bandwagon rather than steering his own course. Ford and Trudeau are not just partners in governing, but co-dependent in their politics.
While voting alliances can shift, the premier and prime minister have long shared an overlapping coalition of supporters across Ontario that crosses party lines. Voters in this province have historically sent opposing party leaders to Queen's Park and Parliament Hill.
That tradition of electoral contradiction is top of mind for Ford's Tories, who fear a Poilievre triumph in the next federal election (expected in late 2025) could set the stage for a similar alternance in power for the next provincial election (set for mid-2026). Put another way, Poilievre as PM would be bad for Ford's political prospects, whereas Trudeau remains a convenient foil and occasional friend.
Six years ago, the two counterparts had a counterproductive relationship, with Ford picking fights and Trudeau using the premier as an electoral punching bag. But COVID brought their two governments together, as Ford became close to Deputy PM Chrystia Freeland and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc; Ford's gushing praise for Freeland when she became finance minister exasperated the federal Conservatives, undercutting their hostile rhetoric.
The rapprochement between provincial PCs and federal Liberals has persisted even as Trudeau's popularity has slumped. While Ford has dialed down the public backslapping - dashing off finger-wagging open letters to the federal Liberals condemning high interest rates and the carbon tax - the private backchannels and political collegiality remain undiminished.
While Ford may not always be in Trudeau's corner publicly, he still whispers in his ear privately - both backstage and on the phone. That's more than can be said for the premier and Poilievre, where there is no discernible affection or connection - in public or private.
That could change as the political winds shift, forcing Canada's two top Tories to behave as bedfellows instead of strangers. But that would be an accident of electoral history, not solidarity.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's Tories fear a Pierre Poilievre triumph in the next federal election could set the stage for a change of power in Ontario, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Mindfulness; Provincial elections; Scandals; Power; Politics; Prime ministers; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Document 437 of 500

March 16: Trudeau hypocritical, Poilievre misleading Canadians and other Hamilton letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-16-trudeau-hypocritical-poilievre/docview/2957743573/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+16%3A+Trudeau+hypocritical%2C+Poilievre+misleading+Canadians+and+other+Hamilton+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau's comments hypocritical
Re: 'My job is not to be popular,' Trudeau says after pressed to ditch carbon price hike (March 13)
So, Justin Trudeau tells us his "job is to do the right things for Canada now and do the right things for Canadians a generation from now." How hypocritical. After years of his government's uncontrolled spending, massive deficits and accumulated debt, we now have his carbon tax. While countries like China, Russia, India and others continue to burn fossil fuels unabated, Trudeau punishes Canadians financially in an effort to reduce Canada's minuscule carbon pollution. I guess he believes "the right things" include pushing most Canadians further toward poverty.
When the next federal election comes around in 2025, I think Mr. Trudeau will find out average Canadians are also thinking about what's best for them now and in the future. He will find out those thoughts don't include continuing with a self-serving, ethics challenged, corrupt, fiscally incompetent, silver-spoon-sucking Justin Trudeau. Never mind a walk in the snow, the nearest exit will do just fine.
Cameron Stringer, Hamilton
Poilievre misleading Canadians
Why are people against the carbon levy? One word: Poilievre. He has misled Canadians from Day 1 on this policy. He would never tell Canadians that if he was to "axe the tax" they would have less money in their pockets in spite of the fact that four out of five Canadians find themselves benefitting financially from the Liberals' "cash for carbon" rebate. This policy is all about our changing climate, which Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative peers can't seem to bring themselves to acknowledge, in spite of the fact that an early forest fire season and prairie drought are already upon us. Polling is currently against the Liberals and so premiers are licking their chops looking for votes, climate change be damned. How pathetic and cynical. Where is Poilievre on climate change, corporate profits and high interest rates when it comes to affordability? Nowhere to be found. He chooses to play politics and take money out of Canadians' pockets; money put there by the Liberals "cash for carbon" rebate. Not a leader for difficult times.
Don Courtnage, Ancaster
Get tough on criminals
Re: Rise in vehicle thefts: How well-organized thieves exploit tech to steal cars (Feb. 20)
The situation is out of control. These bandits are more brazen than ever before and can score vast sums of money for their efforts without serious repercussions. They get caught, get out on bail and steal more vehicles.
First off, our legal system has to change. No more Mr. Nice Guy. When caught, these criminals should be incarcerated (no bail, please) until the issue is settled in court. Clean up the court backlog. There is technology out there to speed things up. There will be those who strongly oppose this based on Charter rights. What about the rights of all the victims? Take it one step further. Make them pay for their lodgings and court expenses. Confiscate all their assets as collateral. Premier Doug Ford wants to build more jails. I like it.
A lot of stolen vehicles exit Canada via shipping container to offshore destinations. Why aren't the contents scrutinized more? With all this "heat" applied, sooner or later this illegal undertaking will crumble. This proposal can also be used in drug and human trafficking cases as well. Time to roll up our sleeves and get tough.
Sinclair Tyios, Burlington
The downtown is a mess
Re: 19-year-old dead after brazen daytime shooting in downtown Hamilton (March 6)
So Mayor Andrea Horwath was "shocked" at the deadly lunch-hour shooting a block from city hall. Perhaps Ms. Horwath should get out of her faux-marble tower more often and walk the increasingly mean streets of downtown Hamilton.
It's a mess down there and the increasing incidents of violence, homelessness and drug use indicate things aren't getting any better any time soon.
The alcoves along the facade of the Fairclough building are havens for encampments and the reek of urine and garbage is sickening. To put it bluntly, madame mayor, your city is really beginning to stink.
Doug Foley, Hamilton
We need to ditch plastic
If glass and metal, including aluminum, are 100 per cent recyclable and plastic can only be recycled three times, how come everything is now packaged in plastic? Even vegetable-based plastic can only be broken down by a special process, otherwise it's just the same as all other plastic polluting the environment forever.
Irene Hough, Hamilton
Workers should be able to get by
Re: Cyberattack preventing overtime pay to City of Hamilton workers, say unions (March 12)
What a selfish comment and attitude by the union representing city workers regarding overtime pay. The city is not responsible for the cyberattack to its system and is trying to resolve the situation. City workers are not paid minimum wages and should be able to survive without overtime time pay until such time as the situation is corrected. Simple solution: bank the overtime hours for now and you will be able to collect these overtime wages at a future date. The city has indicated that all payments owed will be reconciled at some point.
Kaz Matecki, Stoney Creek
Spend money on housing, not LRT
Re: Metrolinx applies to expropriate 28 properties for Hamilton LRT (March 11)
It is hard to believe that during a time when people are finding it hard to find affordable housing we are eliminating it. Until the housing crisis in this city has improved, the last thing we should be doing is spending money on a light rail system that we don't need.
It seems like now that city council has made living in tents acceptable and normal they have forgotten about the homeless. This council needs to stop spending money senselessly and concentrate on curing the dire issues at hand, get people out of tents and not put more into them.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
An alternative to building more jails
Ontario's jails are overwhelmed and overcrowded. Our premier's answer is to build more, an idea as far removed from the ideal solution as was his Greenbelt fiasco.
The vast majority of spaces in Ontario's jails are occupied by individuals who are technically innocent, awaiting bail or trial. Another portion is made up of individuals with untreated mental health disorders whose behaviour has resulted in criminal convictions.
A wise premier would look, not at accommodating these ridiculous conditions, but instead at the root causes of the problem.
That premier would initiate significant improvements in treatment of mental health to help those who need it. Preventive actions such as this would empty a number of jail spaces and would have the added benefit of opening up court time and resources to process more bail and trial hearings.
Appointing more judges and court staff would move many more cases through the system and reduce the strain on jails, as well as releasing more innocent people currently incarcerated.
Replacing archaic drug policies with safe supply processes would also help significantly in removing many individuals from the justice stream.
These three strategies would eliminate the need for any new jail spaces, but would also free up police from social work involvement and deploy them instead to deal with real criminals.
It costs more than $1 billion to build a single jail. That money would be far better spent implementing socially progressive, evidence-based and proven policies that would benefit all Ontarians.
Paul Marrow, Winterbourne
CAPTION: Readers offer their opinions of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in regards to the carbon tax.

Document 439 of 500

Ontario needs to rein in its spending

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-needs-rein-spending/docview/2957692468/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+needs+to+rein+in+its+spending&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has often criticized his predecessor's fiscal approach, yet his government has consistently employed the same approach while in office. When the government tables its next budget March 26, it should finally back up the tough talk and change its approach to fiscal policy.
First, some quick history. From 2008-09 to 2017-18 (the last year before Ford was elected), Ontario governments had run 10 consecutive deficits totalling $103.3 billion, due in part to a substantial increase in spending - during this period, annual program spending (total spending minus debt interest) increased by 41.6 per cent. In other words, by 2017-18, the Ontario government was spending $1,207 more per person than in 2008-09 (after adjusting for inflation).
Meanwhile, the government refused to enact broad-based tax relief. Despite a minor tax cut for low-income residents in 2010, the government raised taxes on upper-income earners in 2012 and 2014 while middle-income earners saw no reduction to their personal income tax rates.
This approach has hurt the province's economy. Growing government debt can increase the cost of borrowing in the private sector, which reduces capital investment, while high and increasing tax rates reduce the incentive for people to work and invest by lowering the rewards for these activities. Both of these effects limit economic growth and produce lower living standards than there otherwise would be. Indeed, in recent years growth in per-person GDP, a common measure of living standards, has slowed in Ontario relative to the rest of Canada.
In 2018, on the campaign trail, then-candidate Ford criticized the Wynne government's "reckless spending" and continued deficits, and promised if elected to cut taxes and find "efficiencies" in the provincial budget. In its first budget in 2019, the Ford government promised to "fix Ontario's inherited fiscal mess ... before it hurts the economy" by balancing the budget, reducing the debt burden and cutting taxes.
Yet, while the Ford government has talked a big game, it's employed the same approach to government finances as its predecessors - increased spending, deficits and no broad-based tax relief.
By the end of this fiscal year, deficits under the Ford government will have totalled a projected $41.7 billion over six years. Although the Ford government did run a $2-billion surplus in 2021-22, this was due to an unexpected surge in revenues. The following year, the government promptly increased spending and returned to deficits.
It didn't need to be this way. Had the Ford government simply maintained the Wynne government's level of per-person spending, rather than increase spending, it would have accumulated $27.8 billion less debt. The government could have used these savings to reduce income taxes by an average of $888 per tax filer in 2023.
With Ontario's economy (and subsequent living standards) falling behind the rest of Canada, the Ford government should change course and finally keep its promise to restrain spending and deliver meaningful tax relief for Ontarians.
Grady Munro and Jake Fuss
are fiscal policy analysts
at the Fraser Institute.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's government should change its approach to fiscal policy, write Grady Munro and Jake Fuss.The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Grady Munro and Jake Fuss;

Subject: Provincial budget; Fiscal policy; Standard of living; Taxes; Tax rates; Economic development; Relief provisions; Budgets; Debt restructuring

Document 440 of 500

March 15: Build hospitals, not jails and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-15-build-hospitals-not-jails-other-letters/docview/2957265044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+15%3A+Build+hospitals%2C+not+jails+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Build hospitals, not jails
Re: Ford promises to build as many jails as needed to keep criminals behind bars (March 8)
I read recently that Premier Doug Ford intends to get tough on crime. He stated "I will build as many jails as we need." Wouldn't it be nice, with all of those available resources, he would say, "I will build as many hospitals as we need."
David McIsaac, Hamilton
Only city hall excited for LRT
Re: Who wants these projects? (March 14)
I think I can answer Philip Watkins' question about the LRT. Politicians who have been sold a bill of goods by contractors, planners and the like, who in turn try to sell this bill of goods to the public, are the people excited about the LRT. Merely adding a bit to the B Line is hardly as sexy as a new LRT with gleaming new trains. No evidence yet has been provided it will enhance traffic through the lower city. Along with a two-way conversion for Main and King, it seems apparent the city council really wishes to discourage people to be away from the lower city.
Steve Penner, Hamilton
Put LRT to a vote
Well, yet another example of politicians doing exactly what they want, when they want and how they want. The negativity of the LRT and changing the main thoroughfares of King and Main far outweighs any positive results from this projects. Not to mention the money that these changes will cost to the taxpayers. I would love to see a poll of either yea or nay and see what the residents of Hamilton really want. I know what we will get: more homeless begging on corners, more encampments and less housing. But the real issue here is where is Mayor Andrea Horwath on all of this? Whatever happened to the opinionated, take-charge lady?
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Change is up to Haitians
Re: Plan to install new leaders in Haiti appears to crumble (March 14)
Haiti should have been a jewel in the Caribbean to enjoy as many other nations in this area relish. Instead, through colonialism, greed, corruption and natural disasters, it is one of the poorest nations in the western hemisphere. There is no real functional government, gang warfare goes largely unchecked, people suffer from lack of food and basic necessities. Relief agencies try to help, but their members risk their lives by trying to be good Samaritans.
There is no easy fix for this nation that has seen these problems grow like a cancer for many decades. A genuine Haitian response is needed from the people of Haiti. Simply sending in foreign police or military personal might help for a time, but does little to help the overall situation. It is said, peace is not free, but is costly. Only the Haitian people can turn their nation around.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Roadways are too dangerous
If drivers must obey traffic speeds and signals, use brakes and the like, why do the pedestrians have the right to simply walk across roads looking at phones or talking and never looking? Every day, drivers are constantly narrowly avoiding major collisions and life-altering situations.
Today's traffic volumes are too large for our old street configurations. Young inexperienced speeders, street racing, motorcycles doing 120-130 kilometres per hour on the Linc. Where are the police when it's obvious these issues are commonplace?
My grandson was hit while taking his friend to the doctor by a large black pickup truck, driving in the fog, at night, with no lights. The driver blurted out to the traffic officer, his mother and my wife that "this is the second truck I've totalled in six months." The result? The officer sent the man home without any breathalyzer or drug impairment tests and charged my grandson with illegal left turn.
Please, can we get our act together and stop the stupidity in the vehicles and on the roadways by phoneholics?
Bob Tomlinson, Hamilton
Real action needed for Palestinians
Re: Gaza sealift all about saving face (March 12)
Millions of Palestinian men, women and children are starving to death right now, and U.S. President Joe Biden's ship is 15 days away. When it does arrive, they will be building a pier which will take 60 days to build. What Biden needs to do is cut off funding to the Israelis and stop supplying them with weapons. How many more Palestinian people are going to die before a ceasefire is called and food trucks are allowed in?
Jane Farrington, Hamilton
Carbon tax just a tax grab
The federal carbon tax is increasing to more than 17 cents per litre of gas and 15 cents per metre of natural gas on April 1. The carbon tax will cost the average household between $377 and $911 in 2024-25 even after rebates, according to the Parliamentary Budget Officer. This is nothing but a tax grab to help pay for the Liberals' wasteful, shameful fiscal policies that will have little or no effect on climate change and cause immense stress on the finances of the general public.
Keith Lankester, Ancaster
Ambitious oil, gas emissions cap needed
We need to hold big oil companies accountable for their climate pollution. As a technical writer/editor, I've lived all my life in Ontario - 30 years in Hamilton and now five years in Brantford. I can see that climate change isn't just a future problem. Extreme weather is taking a toll on communities in Canada and worldwide.
The oil and gas sector is the largest, fastest-growing source of greenhouse gas emissions in Canada, and we can't meet critical climate targets without reducing the emissions.
The federal government needs to announce an ambitious cap on oil and gas emissions before going to COP29, the United Nations Climate Conference, in November. The emissions cap needs to be written for the people of Canada - not the oil and gas companies.
Most Canadians want a fair cap to reduce oil and gas emissions by 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030, in line with the national goal.
For the planet's future, I look to the federal government to implement an ambitious and effective emissions cap policy for the worst polluting sector.
Anita Puronto, Brantford
CAPTION: "I'll build as many jails as we need to put these criminals behind bars for a long time," Premier Doug Ford said recently. A reader wishes he was as enthusiastic about building hospitals.

Subject: Prisons; Emissions; Politics; Carbon; Climate change; Roads & highways; Environmental tax

Build hospitals not more jails Re Ford promises to build ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/build-hospitals-not-more-jails-re-ford-promises/docview/2957098043/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Build+hospitals+not+more+jails+Re+Ford+promises+to+build+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Build hospitals,
not more jails
Re: Ford promises to build as many jails as needed to keep criminals behind bars (March 8)
I read recently that Premier Doug Ford intends to get tough on crime. He stated "I will build as many jails as we need." Wouldn't it be nice, with all of those available resources, he would say, "I will build as many hospitals as we need."
David McIsaac, Hamilton
Foreign intervention won't fix problems
Re: Plan to install new leaders
in Haiti appears to crumble (March 14)
Haiti should have been a jewel in the Caribbean to enjoy as many other nations in this area relish. Instead, through colonialism, greed, corruption and natural disasters, it is one of the poorest nations in the western hemisphere. There is no real functional government, gang warfare goes largely unchecked, people suffer from lack of food and basic necessities. Relief agencies try to help, but their members risk their lives by trying to be Good Samaritans.
There is no easy fix for this nation that has seen these problems grow like a cancer for many decades. A genuine Haitian response is needed from the people of Haiti. Simply sending in foreign police or military personnel might help for a time, but does little to help the overall situation. It is said, peace is not free, but is costly. Only the Haitian people can turn their nation around.

Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Only city hall
excited for LRT
Re: Who wants these projects? (March 14)
I think I can answer Philip Watkins' question about the LRT. Politicians who have been sold a bill of goods by contractors, planners and the like, who in turn try to sell this bill of goods to the public, are the people excited about the LRT. Merely adding a bit to the B Line is hardly as sexy as a new LRT with gleaming new trains. No evidence yet has been provided it will enhance traffic through the lower city. Along with a two-way conversion for Main and King, it seems apparent the city council really wishes to discourage people to be away from the lower city.
Steve Penner, Hamilton
Put LRT to a vote
Well, yet another example of politicians doing exactly what they want, when they want and how they want. The negativity of the LRT and changing the main thoroughfares of King and Main far outweighs any positive results from this projects. Not to mention the money that these changes will cost to the taxpayers. I would love to see a poll of either yea or nay and see what the residents of Hamilton really want.
I know what we will get: more homeless begging on corners, more encampments and less housing. But the real issue here is where is Mayor Andrea Horwath on all of this? Whatever happened to the opinionated, take-charge lady?
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown

Document 442 of 500

HCA directors blast province's 'outrageous' conservation changes: They are seeking legal advice on fighting regulatory changes they say threaten wetlands, watercourses and the health of watersheds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hca-directors-blast-provinces-outrageous/docview/2957097431/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HCA+directors+blast+province%27s+%27outrageous%27+conservation+changes%3A+They+are+seeking+legal+advice+on+fighting+regulatory+changes+they+say+threaten+wetlands%2C+watercourses+and+the+health+of+watersheds&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton Conservation Authority directors are seeking legal advice on what they can do to fight provincial regulatory changes they say threaten local wetlands, watercourses and the health of the city's watersheds.
After nearly two hours of impassioned debate at their March 7 board meeting, directors opted to not adopt interim measures recommended by staff to comply with the new regulations, which take effect on April 1.
They instead acknowledged staff must comply with the new rules while asking for a report on the real-world impacts, including from no longer being allowed to consider pollution and conservation of land when issuing development permits.
"The challenge now is we're faced with outrageous behaviour by the province of Ontario and (Premier Doug) Ford, and we're hearing that we don't really understand the implications," citizen member Brian McHattie said.
The changes also reduce the protected zone around provincially significant wetlands to 30 metres from 120, and redefine what constitutes a watercourse. Formerly "an identifiable impression in the ground," it is now a "defined channel, having a bed and banks or sides."
The minister of Natural Resources and Forestry also gains new powers to bypass the authority to issue permits, which will be valid for five years, up from two years.
McHattie said the changes continue the premier's push to fulfil a developers' wish list that has already stripped other wetland protections, tried to remove land from the Greenbelt and attempted to expand Hamilton's urban boundary over the city's objections.
He said it's important for the authority to assess the impact of weakened conservation regulations and "spotlight the implications of what Mr. Ford is having us do," including by informing city residents.
"We're dealing with someone who is a thug and being reasonable is not in the realm for that individual," McHattie said. "All of this during a biodiversity crisis; all of this during a climate-change crisis."
Other directors expressed concern about the conflict between following the new regulations and their commitment to protecting the environment and the city's watersheds.
"If our own staff has no choice but to comply, what can we do as a board?" asked Puslinch member Susan Fielding, noting the authority repeatedly opposed the changes in submissions to the province.
"I'm not sure how we can stop this careening car going down the road. We can see an accident happening, but how do we prevent it?"
Ward 13 Coun. Alex Wilson said directors need legal advice on whether the new regulations potentially put staff at risk "if something goes wrong" after a development permit is issued.
Wilson said no longer considering an identifiable impression in the ground to be a watercourse will affect major watercourses, a bit like removing capillaries from arteries, while reducing wetland protection zones could be equally devastating.
"Most of Ward 13 is a provincially significant wetland of some way, shape or form," Wilson said.
Citizen member Lisa DiCesare questioned the province's decision to remove pollution as a consideration for development permits while adding "health and safety" without defining the latter.
"I would just suggest that pollution is a health and safety issue," she said.
Authority chair Brad Clark said staff won't know the implications of the changes until they take effect and it's unclear if the city can take a more protective role on conservation issues that the province will no longer allow the authority to consider.
"The residents don't really understand what's been done and we need to somehow be able to explain that," he said, adding he shares the frustration with the province.
"They create us, they regulate us, they put their acts together and they ask us our opinion, and more often than not they don't really listen to our opinions. That's been my personal experience."
CAPTION: The changes also reduce the protected zone around provincially significant wetlands to30 metresfrom 120, and redefine what constitutes a watercourse.John Rennison Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Richard Leitner Reporter

Happy St. Paddy's Day: Event ideas this weekend in Hamilton and area

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/happy-st-paddys-day-event-ideas-this-weekend/docview/2956818205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Happy+St.+Paddy%27s+Day%3A+Event+ideas+this+weekend+in+Hamilton+and+area&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bars and restaurants will be buzzing this weekend, with city-wide celebrations leading up to St. Patrick's Day on Sunday, March 17.
Don't expect a repeat of last year's unseasonable warmth that extended festivities to restaurant patios, with Sunday's daytime high reaching just 5 C with a chance of showers.
But if green beer isn't your thing, you can also check out pedigree felines at the Hamilton Cat Show, learn about holistic healing, see Disney on Ice or take part in an author event.
There's something for everyone this weekend in the Hamilton area.
St. Patrick's Day events
Celebrate St. Patrick's Day weekend at the Stoney Creek Legion (12 King St. E.) on Friday, March 15, with live entertainment from 7 to 11 p.m. with the Pepper Shakers. All are welcome. Admission is $10 at the door.
Hamilton Mountain Legion Branch 163 (435 Limeridge Rd. E.) hosts St. Patrick's Day celebrations Saturday with Guinness and Kilkenny specials, a 2 p.m. meat draw and a 7 p.m. performance by Lionel Bernard. The club room is also open to the public Sunday from noon to 6 p.m.
The Casbah salutes St. Patrick's Day during a monthly FUR party this Saturday at 10 a.m. Wear your finest green and gold, sip green beer and strut your stuff on the dance floor with music by DJ Rose. There's a $10 cover before 11 p.m. See casbahlounge.ca for more details.
Celebrate St. Patrick's Day at Nickel Brook Brewing Co. (3426 Mainway, Burlington), with live music by Merritt Driven, Saturday at 8 p.m. Purchase tickets at eventbrite.ca.
Stonewalls (339 York Blvd., Hamilton) celebrates St. Paddy's Day with music by Freedom Train on Sunday, beginning at 4 p.m.
For an alternative to the pub scene, the Westdale Theatre (1014 King St. W., Hamilton) offers a lively night of Celtic music and standup comedy Sunday at 7 p.m. See thewestdale.ca for details.
Exhibits and trade shows
The Hamilton Cat Fanciers' annual cat show returns to the Ancaster Fairgrounds (630 Trinity Rd. S.) this weekend, March 16 and 17. See live judging in various categories from pedigree to household pets. Show hours are 9 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. each day inside Marritt Hall. See costumed cats on Sunday, dressed in a Minions theme. The event also includes feline-focused vendors and demonstrations.
The Ancaster Spring Holistic Healing Fair is Sunday from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. at the Ancaster Fairgrounds (630 Trinity Rd. S.) in the concession building. Learn about a variety of wellness topics with a range of vendors to explore.
Antique appraisal
The Burlington Seniors Centre (2285 New St.) hosts an antique appraisal day this Saturday from 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. Antique appraiser Alex Moshtagh will provide a free verbal appraisal on your items. The event takes place in community rooms 3 and 4.
Author event
Author Sarah Raughley chats with Louisa Onomé about her new book, "Pride and Joy" this Saturday at 2 p.m. at Hamilton Central Library, (55 York Blvd.) The 45-minute conversation is followed by a 15-minute question-and-answer period.
Live music
The Coach and Lantern Upper Coach (384 Wilson St. E., Ancaster) presents another Blues and Roots matinee session Saturday at 1 p.m., with special guest, Dave Rave of the Shakers and Teenage Head. Purchase tickets on Eventbrite.
Sports
The Brantford Bulldogs are back in action at Saturday at 4 p.m. at the Brantford Civic Centre (69 Market St. S.) when they take on the Ottawa 67's in the Ontario Hockey League. Purchase tickets at ticketmaster.ca.
Family Fun
The Art Gallery of Hamilton (123 King St. W.) presents Family Fun Day with the Hamilton Children's Choir during "Sing as You Are" Sunday, March 17 at 12 noon. Visit the Fischer Gallery for fun, hands-on artmaking inspired by the new exhibitions and permanent collection. Admission is free for art gallery members and $5 per family for nonmembers.
Maple syrup season
You can still enjoy maple syrup season at Mountsberg Conservation Area (2259 Milburough Town Line, Campbellville.) Maple Town returns Saturday and Sunday, 9:30 a.m. to 5 p.m. Learn about traditional maple syrup making and sample fresh pancakes and maple taffy on snow. Take part in activities, workshops and horse-drawn wagon rides. See conservationhalton.ca for tickets and full details.
The sap is also running at Westfield Heritage Village on Sundays throughout March at 1049 Kirkwall Rd., Rockton. On March 17 starting at 11 a.m., you can see how sap is turned into syrup, with demonstration in a modern sugar shack. See westfieldheritage.ca for full details.
Sweet water season continues at Crawford Lake Conservation Area, (3115 Conservation Rd., Milton.) Learn about the Indigenous origins and current practices of maple sugaring while touring an exhibit in a replica 15th-century longhouse. The event continues Saturday and Sunday, 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. See conservationhalton.ca for tickets.
Live shows
Disney on Ice presents "Magic in the Stars" with performances Friday and Saturday at 11 a.m., 3 p.m. and 7 p.m. The show concludes Sunday with performances at 11 a.m. and 3 p.m. at FirstOntario Centre, (101 York Blvd.) The show is inspired by Disney's newest animated musical feature film, "Wish." Tickets are available at Ticketmaster.ca or from the venue box office.
See Canadian actress Jayne Eastwood at Gilded Cages' fashion show, this Saturday at 7 p.m. at Crown & Press, 303 Ottawa St. N., Hamilton. Tickets are available at eventbrite.ca.
Top Tier Drag: Stars of Tomorrow visits the Casbah Lounge for one night only, Saturday, March 16 at 6 p.m. Catch a new show filled with hints of new choreography and music from your favourite divas. See casbahlounge.ca for tickets.
There's still time to see "Shirley Valentine" at Theatre Aquarius, (190 King William St.) with another performance Saturday at 1:30 p.m. It's an uplifting play about a Liverpool housewife who steps out of her normal routine with a life-changing trip to Greece. For more information and to purchase tickets, see theatreaquarius.org.
Comedy
Comedian and storyteller Justin Shaw is back with a new solo show Saturday at 8 p.m. at the Staircase Theatre, (27 Dundurn St. N.) Purchase tickets on Eventbrite.
Burlington Performing Arts Centre (440 Locust St.) presents "Girls Nite Out" with a cast of Canadian Comedy Award winners, Second City alums and CBC Comedy personalities. It's an evening of laughs with standup performances and improvisation by Canada's top female comics. See burlingtonpac.ca for tickets.
The Portuguese Kids, with special guest Mike Rita, are putting on a show at Carmen's Event Centre (1520 Stone Church Rd. E.) Saturday at 7 p.m. Purchase tickets at eventbrite.ca.
Although we endeavour to provide the most accurate description of the event listing and venue, we are not responsible or liable for errors and omissions in the event description, location or intended audience. If necessary, please contact the event organizer for additional information. Visit thespec.com/events for more listings.
CAPTION: Judge Leighann Blackmore interacts with a playful orange feline during last year's Hamilton Cat Fanciers annual cat show. The event returns to Marritt Hall at the Ancaster Fairgrounds March 16 and 17.Disney on Ice is back with an all-new show this week at FirstOntario Centre.In 2023, restaurant patios filled up on St. Patrick's Day, with double-digit temperatures on March 17. Shown here, clockwise from left: Shaun Douglas, Seamus Brooks and Patrick Conway spent the afternoon on the patio at Brewers Blackbird Brewery and Kitchen in Ancaster. While we don't expect a repeat of that nice weather this year, there's still lots to do indoors and outdoors this weekend in Hamilton and area.

Document 444 of 500

Keeping the doctors we have

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/keeping-doctors-we-have/docview/2956594666/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Keeping+the+doctors+we+have&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I didn't go into medical school for the money, but I certainly didn't sacrifice all the time and money and years of studying to enter a career just to be running a failing business." - Ottawa family physician Dr. Ramsey Hijazi
Recently, Dr. Hijazi announced that after a decade, he is leaving his family practice in Carp, Ont., to take on a hospital job in transitional care - leaving 1,500 patients.
Ironically, with fanfare on Feb. 9, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced an additional $3.1 billion in federal funding would be spent to improve health care in Ontario during the next three years - part of an $8.4-billion 10-year agreement.
This amount was much less than requested by the premiers. Targeted funding would more quickly assess the competency of foreign-trained physicians, enrolment in medical schools would be expanded, and 60 per cent of postgraduate positions would be devoted to family medicine.
The number of "orphan" patients without a family physician or nurse practitioner is currently 2.3 million in Ontario and 6.5 million across Canada. In Ontario, this number will double in the next two years. Patients will then be forced to rely on walk-in clinics or visits to emergency departments with wait times of many hours.
The main issue is the staffing crisis.
Consider the severe shortage of physicians. In a recent Medical Post article, Dr. Corli Barnes, a second-year resident in family medicine, describes the disincentives to going into traditional family practice. She is already $350,000 in debt, and one physician commented that the CRA allows her to deduct only 15 per cent of her tuition fees. Her income is too high to qualify for loan assistance.
Stress, burnout and paperwork have contributed to an exodus of existing family practitioners, as has a 20 per cent pay cut in the past few years. In a recent op-ed in the Toronto Star, OMA leaders Drs. Nadia Alam, Sohail Gandhi, and Silvy Mathew outlined why new graduates should not yet set up a family practice in Ontario.
According to a poll, 47 per cent of physicians are considering leaving their practice in the next five years.
Clearly, short-term measures are urgently needed to reverse this trend. I have two suggestions pertaining to older MDs: reduce their annual licence fee and provide government-funded short-term disability coverage.
Some 16 per cent of Ontario physicians are at least age 65 and eight per cent age 70. Earlier in their careers, most medical and surgical specialists and many family physicians derived much of their income from hospital work, where there was little or no overhead. In later years, most work part time in private clinics, and are charged overhead of 30 to 40 per cent plus HST.
Most colleges of physicians and surgeons charge about $2,000 per year for a licence, regardless of age or hours worked per week. Ontario's fee is $1,725.
For older MDs on a tight budget, receipt of an invoice for this amount may be the "last straw" that induces them to retire. Older MDs in P.E.I. and New Brunswick have recently been given a discounted licence fee, and annual dues for senior MDs are also much lower for all Canadian medical associations.
In the U.S., the licence fee is much lower, i.e. $735 in New York, $389 in Florida, $500 in Illinois and Arizona, and $423 in Oregon.
In September 2022, I urged Dr. Nancy Whitmore, CEO of the College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario, to discount the licence fee for older doctors. I have received no written reply.
Older physicians lack adequate short-term income protection when ill. They may require time off work because of a heart attack, knee or hip replacement, cancer or cardiac surgery, etc. Private insurance is unavailable.
Therefore, Ontario and all provincial governments should provide them with income protection with no waiting time, so that they eventually return to the workforce rather than retire permanently.
Consider some precedents: All provinces except for P.E.I. have parental leave programs that pay up to $2,000 per week - one for up to 26 weeks.
Recall that in 2003, Ontario created the "SARS Income Stabilization Program." This compensated doctors for 80 per cent of their previous income if lost due to illness with SARS, quarantine, or an income drop because of closure or an office or loss of operating room time.
Hopefully, my suggestions will help to lessen the rising critical physician manpower shortage, as creation of health teams and other reforms are implemented, rates of remuneration are significantly increased, new graduates emerge from medical schools and foreign graduates are "fast-tracked."
Ottawa physician Dr. Charles Shaver is past-chair of the
Section on General Internal Medicine of the Ontario
Medical Association.
The views here are his own.
CAPTION: There are 2.3 million "orphan" patients without a family physician or nurse practitioner in Ontario, Dr. Charles Shaver warns, forcing many to head to clinics or emergency rooms for care.Richard Buchan The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Dr. Charles Shaver Ottawa physician Dr. Charles Shaver is past-chair of the Section on General Internal Medicine of the Ontario Medical Association. The views here are his own.;

Document 445 of 500

Ontario's finance minister is stuck in the middle of Doug Ford's muddle

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-finance-minister-is-stuck-middle-doug/docview/2956594534/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+finance+minister+is+stuck+in+the+middle+of+Doug+Ford%27s+muddle&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Peter Bethlenfalvy insists he's the man with a plan.
Mostly he's the man in the middle.
As Ontario's finance minister prepares to deliver the provincial budget on March 26, he remains caught between his own bottom line and economic hard times. For his job is to juggle Premier Doug Ford's populist politics with his own buttoned-down Bay Street background.
Meeting the Star's editorial board this week, Bethlenfalvy cast himself as a pragmatic steward of the provincial treasury. Yet as Ford's longest-serving - and surviving - finance minister, he presides over a kaleidoscope of tax-cutting, free-spending, crowd-pleasing impulses from the premier that never seem to add up.
While pretending that he couldn't scoop himself, the finance minister cheerfully telegraphed that his budget will maintain the gas tax holiday(s) first brought in as temporary measures by Ford's Progressive Conservative government years ago. They are now a part of the permanent political landscape as voters focus on affordability - and Ford's Tories aren't alone in seeing the signals.
The week before, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew sat in the same chair and told the editorial board why his NDP government had also cut the provincial tax on gas and diesel. Tax-fighting is clearly catching among parties of both left and right, I conceded.
Bethlenfalvy didn't miss a beat, noting that Kinew's new finance minister, Adrien Sala had reached out shortly after taking power last year to compare notes: "He called me and asked for advice; and notwithstanding he's from a different stripe, I gladly gave it to him."
Despite their shared tax cuts, they are hardly on the same wavelength.
Manitoba's premier told the Star he strongly supports the federal pharmacare plan - hatched by the governing Liberals with NDP support. Bethlenfalvy, by contrast, refused to be pinned down - neither supportive nor opposed.
Just straight down the middle.
"I'm not going to argue against the merits of it, but I definitely want to see the details," the finance minister said, choosing his words carefully.
"In principle, we're always open to something that helps Ontarians and helps Canadians," he added. "We're not instinctively saying no, and we do want to look at what the program looks like and the sustainability."
But at what point does prudence become hesitance? When does due diligence become undue delay?
In our meeting, Bethlenfalvy spoke eloquently about his political role models. But in politics, if you want to make a difference, diffidence won't do it.
At some point you need to pick a lane. Whether on protecting the Canada Pension Plan, embracing a national child care plan or endorsing a belated pharmacare plan, he sometimes seems reluctant to play a leadership role - or bring his premier along.
To his credit, Bethlenfalvy talked up Ontario's long-term needs in health care, education and immigration, along with federal-provincial collaboration - all delivered with avuncular reassurance to the assembled journalists and editors. But for this finance minister, actions - and numbers - speak louder than words.
He talked up Ontario's critical infrastructure plans - the subways and highways, hospitals and schools that make up the province's capital stock, all of which require steady infusions of investment capital and human capital to maintain our talent pool.
But unless he starts spending some of his own political capital - by persuading and pushing the premier to make the long-term investments in education and health care that bankroll our human capital needs - Bethlenfalvy will remain a prisoner of the short-term election cycle he long criticized before entering politics.
In his old job at a Bay Street credit rating agency, Bethlenfalvy fearlessly called out politicians' contradictions. Now at Queen's Park, the finance minister embodies that incongruity, faithfully carrying out Ford's impecunious agenda of unaffordable hydro subsidies and tax cuts amounting to billions of dollars a year while shortchanging health and education.
He is passionate about the importance of immigration for Ontario, not merely as the son of immigrants but as a finance minister mindful of the province's looming labour shortages and economic needs, stressing that it "keeps me up at night more than anything." Bethlenfalvy worries about the "demographics of the aging population - it's relentless, it's getting worse and worse."
Bethlenfalvy then launched into a critique of the recent federal crackdown on runaway student visas and work permits (overcrowding was putting pressure on local housing stock). When I pointed to his own government's refusal to properly fund colleges and universities - which is what prompted so many campuses to rely excessively on higher tuition from so many foreign students in the first place - the minister ducked.
But the question kept coming back, notably when Torstar chair David Peterson, a former Liberal premier - and ex-chancellor at the University of Toronto - reminded Bethlenfalvy that "on post-secondary, everybody in the system believes they're in a crisis." After years of tuition cuts and freezes, without the government coming close to making up the shortfall, how can Ontario meet its future labour force needs if it refuses to invest in them now?
The minister pivoted again - criticizing the new federal cap on student visas, even though it's a response to a problem of the province's own making. He defended the tuition shortfall as an affordability question, even though the real question was why he couldn't come close to finding the funds to make up the difference.
What else could the finance minister say, except that he's the man in the middle - of a muddle.
CAPTION: Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy speaks to the Toronto Star editorial board on March 12, 2024.

Document 446 of 500

‘My job is not to be popular,’ PM says of carbon pricing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/my-job-is-not-be-popular-pm-says-carbon-pricing/docview/2956593542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%26lsquo%3BMy+job+is+not+to+be+popular%2C%26rsquo%3B+PM+says+of+carbon+pricing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau pushed back Wednesday on growing demands from premiers to dump the planned April 1 hike to the consumer carbon levy, saying leaders must tackle both affordability and climate change.
"My job is not to be popular," Trudeau said, briefly pausing and adding with a wry smile, "Although it helps."
"My job is to do the right things for Canada now and do the right things for Canadians a generation from now."
Trudeau made the comments to reporters in Calgary after meeting earlier in the day with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith for the first time since last summer.
She and six other premiers have called on Trudeau to abandon the 23 per cent hike to help Canadians already dealing with squeezed household budgets.
Conservative Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre has also railed against the levy and in a statement Wednesday said his party plans to force multiple votes in Parliament next week to nix the hike.
Trudeau told the news conference it's easy for governments to put off taking action on climate change so that it's not their problem while they're in office and for "short-term thinker politicians" to fight putting a price on carbon.
"Why are so many people still against (the carbon levy)?" he asked rhetorically at an unrelated announcement about the government's dental care program.
"Well, you know, that's a question we all have to ask."
He said using market mechanisms, like a carbon price, to lower greenhouse gas emissions is the best way to address the issue, rather than with the "heavy hand of government" through measures like regulations and subsidies.
"I prefer a cleaner solution, a market-based solution, of saying, 'You know what? If you're behaving in ways that are gonna cause pollution, that is going to impact the whole community, you should pay for that pollution, so the community then doesn't suffer the negative sides of it or have to clean it up on their own dime.'"
Earlier Wednesday, as the premier and prime minister smiled and shook hands for the cameras, Smith thanked Trudeau's government for pushing through the Trans Mountain pipeline expansion to the B.C. coast.
But she said they still have areas of contention.
"An area where we don't quite see eye-to-eye (is) the carbon tax," said Smith. "Seven premiers have suggested that we need a pause on April 1, so I'm very hopeful that we can maybe come to some solution on that (and) address issues of affordability."
Trudeau responded by saying as the levy rises, so do rebates.
"As of the beginning of April, the average family of four in Alberta will get $1,800 a year with the Canada Carbon Rebate," he said.
"That's money in people's pockets even as we fight climate change."
Poilievre, in a statement, said it's time to force votes in the house to help families.
"If Trudeau insists on hiking the tax on food, heating and gas, then it's time for Liberal and NDP MPs to stand up to Justin Trudeau to bring home lower prices," he said.
Canada's carbon price is set to rise by $15 to $85 on April 1.
Some premiers have said this is concerning, given Canadians are already dealing with ever-rising costs for day-to-day living.
In addition to Alberta, the governments of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador, Prince Edward Island, Ontario and Saskatchewan have spoken out against Ottawa's approach to carbon pricing.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau meets with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith in Calgary on Wednesday.Todd Korol The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Bill Graveland and Lauren Krugel The Canadian Press

Document 447 of 500

Fixing the prison transfer process

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fixing-prison-transfer-process/docview/2956593054/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fixing+the+prison+transfer+process&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa's controversial transfers of high-profile inmates raise plenty of legitimate concerns but the federal Conservatives' chief criticism isn't one of them.
The issue first arose last year, when notorious murderer and rapist Paul Bernardo was quietly transferred from the maximum security Millhaven penitentiary to the medium-security La Macaza Institution.
Ottawa's secretive approach to the transfer was particularly galling: Although the law permits the Correctional Service of Canada to inform victims of the reasons for the transfer, the service refused to provide them to Tim Danson, who represents the families of the girls murdered by Bernardo.
Danson was instead only informed of the transfer after it happened, which suggests that the service values offenders' privacy rights over the rights of victims and their families.
Last week, the issue was back in the news, with the Toronto Sun reporting that Bernardo wasn't the first high-profile inmate subject to a clandestine transfer. Luka Magnotta, who recorded and posted online his brutal murder of international student Jun Lin, was transferred to La Macaza nine months before Bernardo, yet we're only learning about it nearly two years after the fact.
Fortunately, the Bernardo affair led to a review of the transfer decision, which concluded that the service could engage in "more proactive and meaningful discussions with victims," and it recommended establishing a committee to facilitate that engagement. Marco Mendicino, who was public safety minister at the time, added that engagement should occur before transfers are made, and to that end he directed the service to consider an updated victim impact statement as part of the transfer decision-making process.
These are positive developments, but with the Magnotta case reigniting the controversy, it's time for a broader discussion of the transfer process, including how we can better balance offenders' and victims' rights and restore the public's confidence in the justice system.
The federal Conservatives could and should be part of this conversation, but they have instead chosen to foment outrage by claiming prisons have become carefree country clubs under Liberal policies, especially Bill C-83. The bill, which was passed in 2019 and designed to end solitary confinement, mandated inmates be housed in the "least restrictive environment" consistent with protecting the public and prison staff and inmates.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre charged that Bill C-83 was responsible for the Bernardo transfer. That charge was directly contradicted by a spokesperson for Public Safety Canada who told The Canadian Press the transfer would have occurred under the pre-Bill C-83 version of the law, which permitted the Correctional Service to impose "necessary restrictions" on inmates.
If that weren't enough, the "least restrictive" standard wasn't dreamt up by the Liberals. It was, in fact, included when the law - the Corrections and Conditional Release Act - was first passed in 1992 by Brian Mulroney's Progressive Conservative government. In 2012, Stephen Harper's Conservative government amended the law with the "necessary restrictions" wording, which means Bill C-83 merely restored the language introduced by a Conservative government.
And if that still weren't enough, consider this: Inmates don't suddenly lose their rights when they pass through the prison entrance. Certainly, imprisonment results in limitations on certain rights, but the Constitution places the onus on the government to prove those limitations impair - or restrict - the rights as little as possible.
The "least restrictive" principle is therefore derived from the Constitution, and is firmly embedded in Canadian law, including in roughly 70 statutes and regulations. That alone is good reason to maintain the standard, especially when there's no evidence it was responsible for the Bernardo or Magnotta transfers.
Nonetheless, much as he did with the Bernardo transfer, Poilievre was quick to blame the Magnotta transfer on Bill C-83. That might improve his political fortunes, but it won't help victims or anyone else. The only thing that will is an honest, sincere discussion about improving the transfer process. And all parties, including the Conservatives, owe it to Canadians to participate.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Conservatism; Public safety; Prisoners; Murders & murder attempts; Constitutional amendments
