Document 448 of 500

As the cyber-woes continue, where is all that transparency Horwath and council talked about?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-cyber-woes-continue-where-is-all-that/docview/2956592924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+the+cyber-woes+continue%2C+where+is+all+that+transparency+Horwath+and+council+talked+about%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's now been more than a week since Hamilton residents got their last official briefing from Mayor Andrea Horwath on the city's response to the ransomware attack. Ten days, in fact.
Since then? Not much.
At this point, it's probably worth remembering what she said the day she and this council were sworn in.
"We will rethink how the city engages Hamiltonians and stakeholders on these and other matters, through a new approach to communications and public engagement that reflects our commitment to openness, transparency and evidence-based policy," she said.
Funny how things change when good intentions crash into reality. Because 15 months after offering those words, the cyberattack seems to have taken a bite out of that pledge.
This is clearly the pressing issue in the city today. It's obviously a crisis. In big moments, leaders usually make themselves conspicuous. Just think back to the early days of the pandemic when we saw Doug Ford and Justin Trudeau giving updates and taking questions every day.
Yet we've had no official statement or press conference with Horwath since last Monday. In that time, she's posted no comments about the situation on her X account, and there have been no additional video updates or quotes from her on the city's Cybersecurity Incident Response web page. There's been little proactive visibility on this, in other words.
Her office says some updated information will be released on Thursday, she's free to do an interview Friday and she'll probably be holding another press conference sometime next week.
It's not just her. Councillors aren't speaking about what's going on, committee and council meetings have been cancelled and won't restart until at least the end of this month, and what information we do have about what's going on is murky.
Let's be clear. It's entirely reasonable for the city to not divulge a number of details right now that could create further issues or exacerbate the current ones. Exactly how the hackers got in, for example. Doing so might invite others to try. Same with explaining what strategies it's using to fight back. That would be self-defeating.
But there are some issues about which people have concerns that deserve clearer answers than they've received. At the top of that list is the status of their personal data.
At a briefing last week, city manager Marnie Cluckie said she doesn't believe residents' information has been accessed by the hackers. But in a piece she wrote for The Spectator the next day, she said: "The city has reported any potential breach of personal information to the information and privacy commissioner."
So is there reason to think some has been exposed or isn't there?
There are other important questions that people surely want to know, some of which hardly seem like security risks. The city was asked a number of these on Monday. Answers were promised but still hadn't been provided by Wednesday afternoon. Other journalists have reported similar experiences.
Then there's the issue of council and committees not meeting until at least March 27 and not planning to get back to a regular schedule until April at the earliest.
"Council decided to cancel meetings to allow staff to focus on what they need to do to keep services running while responding to the cyber-incident," a city spokesperson said in an email this week.
If staff is needed for other tasks to keep the city running, what happens if this turns out to be like the ransomware attack in Baltimore that messed things up for months? Same in Atlanta. Or even the Toronto Public Library, which is just now returning to 100 per cent after an October attack.
If it takes Hamilton as long to recover as those places, will council have to continue holding off on meetings so staff can do its work? If not, why can't meetings go ahead so council business that's going to start backing up can be done now?
Council has been briefed on what's going on. Those 16 people, who are the official faces of the city have a better idea of what's happening than almost anyone in town. Their example sets the tone. The longer they stay on the sidelines, the more people are going believe the real story is worse than they're being told.
And with due respect to Cluckie, who only started in this position not long before attack and has found herself in a really tough spot, she was not elected. Horwath was. She is the one we should be hearing from regularly.
Which brings us back to the transparency issue.
This council vowed to be different. City hall was going to be more open than in the past. It wasn't just the mayor's inaugural address. Several made this a key plank of their campaign platforms.
What we're seeing now is that transparency is an effective catchphrase to throw around when it's easy to do so. But when it comes to actually providing it at a tough moment, the concept seemingly becomes a whole lot less appealing.
CAPTION: Mayor Andrea Horwath hasn't made an official statement or held a media briefing about the cyber-situation in over a week.

Subject: Meetings; Press conferences; Councils; Ransomware

Business indexing term: Subject: Ransomware

People: Horwath, Andrea

Title: As the cyber-woes continue, where is all that transparency Horwath and council talked about?

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 14, 2024

Section: opinion/columnists

Scott Radley is a columnist with The Hamilton Spectator focusing on sports and politics. Reach him at
sradley@thespec.com.

Document 450 of 500

Here's how Canadians can pay their final respects to Brian Mulroney

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-canadians-can-pay-their-final-respects/docview/2956889308/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+Canadians+can+pay+their+final+respects+to+Brian+Mulroney&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Canadians wishing to pay respects to former prime minister Brian Mulroney may do so when a four-day period of public mourning begins next week, starting in the nation's capital before moving to Quebec.
Amid ongoing security concerns about how protests around the war in Gaza are affecting public events, the federal government has scheduled two days for Mulroney's lying in state in Ottawa, beginning March 19.
Mulroney would have turned 85 on March 20.
The former prime minister's casket is to then be transported to Montreal, where another two days has been set aside, starting March 21, for Mulroney's lying in repose - the name given when the solemn formal event takes place outside of Ottawa.
Mulroney's state funeral will take place March 23 at the majestic Notre-Dame Basilica in old Montreal.
Parliament is currently adjourned and scheduled to resume on Monday, when MPs and party leaders are expected to deliver formal tributes.
The former Progressive Conservative party leader, who was treated for prostate cancer last year, died on Feb. 29 after a fall in Palm Beach, Florida, where he and his wife Mila had a vacation home.
His death occurred just before the start of a two-week constituency break, and so only brief tributes were offered on the morning after the news broke.
Further details about the state funeral, expected guests and speakers will be disclosed by the federal Heritage Department on Friday. Those details have been worked out with the family.
In Pickering, Premier Doug Ford said he and his Progressive Conservative caucus are working to ensure MPPs are able to attend the lying in state next week even though the legislature is sitting at Queen's Park.
The late prime minister's eldest child, Caroline Mulroney, is Ford's Treasury Board president and a key cabinet minister.
"We're going to work that out through our chair of caucus," the premier said Wednesday, adding he will be at Mulroney's funeral in Montreal.
"I'm going to be attending, our finance minister (Peter Bethlenfalvy) is going to be attending," he said.
"Our heart goes out to Caroline and the Mulroney family. He was a tremendous leader."
CAPTION: A framed portrait of former prime minister Brian Mulroney leans against the Centennial Flame on Parliament Hill on March 1, 2024.

Subject: Prime ministers; Funerals

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Mulroney, Brian

Document 451 of 500

They've waited years for the help they say will improve their lives. Where is the money the Trudeau government promised?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theyve-waited-years-help-they-say-will-improve/docview/2956889210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=They%27ve+waited+years+for+the+help+they+say+will+improve+their+lives.+Where+is+the+money+the+Trudeau+government+promised%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-For years, Sarah Colero has been waiting eagerly, watching for when a long-awaited national disability benefit would arrive.
Due to her disability, she said she's no longer able to find work and support herself. She spends most of her time at home, relying on $1,308 per month from Ontario's disability benefit and her parents to get to the occasional doctor's appointment.
For her, a national disability benefit would mean money to pay her own rent and access therapy, giving her the boost she needs to eventually find work. But despite a federal promise more than three-and-a-half years ago, Colero is still waiting.
Nearly a year since the Canada Disability Benefit became law, the legislation has not been fleshed out with the regulations that would lay out what it will look like and when financial help will come. Some advocates say Ottawa has abandoned them and they're growing anxious another federal budget will go by without the benefit being funded.
"Even though it's a blank bill, they were delaying, delaying, delaying, and it really broke a lot of people," Colero told the Star. "They've been dangling it in front of us ever since."
What's known so far is that the benefit would be available to working-age people with disabilities, and that it will take into account the poverty line and additional costs people with disabilities face. It won't replace provincial benefits, prompting calls for clarity from provinces and fears from advocates existing benefits will be clawed back.
All other details, like the eligibility criteria and the benefit amount, are still being developed. And the legislation gives Ottawa up to two years for the regulations to be finalized - just a few months away from the next scheduled election in 2025.
First promised by the federal government in 2020, the Canada Disability Benefit Act was tabled in the Commons in 2021 but died when an election was called that year. The Liberals campaigned on reintroducing it and did so in June 2022, passing it a year later with bipartisan support.
"People just can't wait any longer, and we're dragging our feet on delivering a benefit," said Krista Carr, the executive vice-president of Inclusion Canada. "Decisions are made every day on where money is going to get allocated, and people with disabilities never seem to be at the top of that list."
The Canadian Human Rights Commission has weighed in, saying it's aware of reports that people with disabilities are choosing medical assistance in dying because they have not been able to access basic supports.
That's in part why advocates had pushed for an interim emergency benefit while the regulations were developed. But the government argued that might require additional legislation and could cause further delays.
At the urging of stakeholders, Yves Giroux, the Parliamentary Budget Officer, estimated how much the benefit would cost. But the lack of detail in the legislation meant the estimate ranged from between $523 million and $5.1 billion in the first year, to between $2.1 billion and $20.5 billion annually after that.
"There was a void created by the government, in the absence of any indications on the cost of the program and the benefits level," Giroux said.
He said given those estimates, it's less likely the government is able to fund the benefit this year as it faces pressure to fund housing, a national pharmacare program and the military, while meeting its fiscal commitments.
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's office would not say what may or may not be included in this year's budget.
A spokesperson for Kamal Khera, the minister for Persons with Disabilities, said in a statement the government is working on a first draft of the regulations after consultations with thousands of Canadians, but would not provide more details.
"The priority is to get the benefit to Canadians with disabilities as quickly as we can while also making sure that we get it right by working with provinces and territories to ensure that everyone who receives the (Canada Disability Benefit) is better off," Laurent de Casanove said in an email.
But those comments are no longer providing hope to some advocates.
Colero says she feels there is a lack of political will to prioritize this issue, and it's making her feel disenfranchised.
She believes the Liberals and NDP have "kicked us to the curb" and the Conservatives pretend to care but "never advocate for anything to improve our quality of life."
"Disabled people have been screaming about this. People are dying," she said. "We really get gaslit by politicians all the time on it."
CAPTION: Diversity, Inclusion and Persons with Disabilities Minister Kamal Khera talks to media in the foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Jan. 30, 2024.

Subject: Legislation; Inclusion; People with disabilities; Disability

Location: Canada

Document 452 of 500

Privately and publicly, Doug Ford warns Justin Trudeau's Liberals they'll be 'annihilated' if they raise the carbon price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/privately-publicly-doug-ford-warns-justin/docview/2956889175/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privately+and+publicly%2C+Doug+Ford+warns+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals+they%27ll+be+%27annihilated%27+if+they+raise+the+carbon+price&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is appealing - not just publicly but also privately - to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to not increase the carbon levy April 1, warning the Liberals risk being "annihilated."
"I do not understand for the life of me what the federal government is thinking," the Progressive Conservative premier told reporters Wednesday in Pickering.
"If they don't start ... putting money back into people's pockets instead of filling their pockets, guess what, they're going to get annihilated," he said of an election expected next year.
"They're done - done like dinner."
Behind the scenes, sources say Ford - who speaks frequently with Trudeau, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc - has been giving the Liberals similarly pointed advice.
Insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the premier reminds his federal counterparts of public opinion polling that shows opposition to the carbon levy as cost-of-living concerns mount.
He repeated that counsel publicly in Pickering.
"The number one issue in every single poll - doesn't matter what party, who does it - is affordability, affordable gas, affordable homes and affordable groceries," said the premier, an avid student of political polling who pores over research findings.
Ford's opposition to carbon-pricing for consumers is a rare point of policy agreement with federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
On Sunday, Poilievre held an "Axe the Tax" rally in the premier's Etobicoke North riding, a mere five kilometres from his home, attracting some 2,500 people.
But Ford, who works closely with Trudeau's Liberals on a slew of shared priorities and is therefore leery of Poilievre's Tories, did not attend.
Nor did any of his cabinet or caucus, who were discouraged from participating after the newest federal Tory MP, Jamil Jivani, a former provincial staffer, attacked the "liberal elites in the Ontario Ministry of Education" in his March 4 byelection victory speech.
That unprovoked attack infuriated Ford, still smarting from Poilievre poaching former provincial cabinet minister Parm Gill eight weeks ago to run federally in Milton.
"I gave that young man an opportunity. He showed up - no one even knew who he was - he came in my office, I gave him an incredible opportunity. To be very frank - and go to root cause - he wouldn't even be where he is if I didn't give him that opportunity," the premier thundered March 8.
"So I just want to wish Jamil all the best. He should focus on maybe the carbon tax. Maybe he should come in, I'll give him a couple of lessons on how to speak to ... his constituents and that," he said.
On Wednesday, Ford was asked about Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's hint at the Star's editorial board the day before that there will be another extension of the temporary cut to gas and fuel taxes announced in the March 26 budget.
"Stay tuned to that," the premier said with a smile.
First announced before the 2022 election - and twice extended - the 5.7 cents per litre gasoline tax cut is currently set to expire on June 30. It costs the treasury some $1.2 billion annually, saving the average family about $260 a year.
Bethlenfalvy told the Star on Tuesday that "it would be tough" to hike the provincial fuel tax just three months after federal carbon-pricing levies rise on April 1.
Ford indicated his government would have no choice but to keep the tax break in place longer with the federal price of carbon set to jump from $65 per tonne to $80 in two weeks.
That change will add 3.3 cents to a litre of gasoline and about 2.9 cents to a cubic metre of natural gas.
"The carbon tax is the worst tax you could ever put on the backs of the people," said Ford.
His comments came one day after Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey, the last remaining Liberal provincial premier, issued a public plea to Trudeau not to raise the levy.
"In Newfoundland and Labrador, for the most vulnerable, it has been a particularly difficult time," Furey wrote in a letter to Trudeau made public on Tuesday.
"After years of increased living costs, there is a need to fully grasp and address the magnitude of inflation's effect on citizens, particularly those most in need," he wrote.
The push comes against the backdrop of a planned "Spike the Hike" ad blitz on radio and television from the Conservatives.
Poilievre's party is also planning rallies across Canada and events outside the constituency offices of Liberal and NDP MPs.
Ford's private exhortations to top federal Grits come after his unsuccessful Supreme Court of Canada challenge to the carbon levy and as his party is using its own ads to link provincial Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie to Trudeau's measure.
It also follows the two carbon pricing schemes the provincial Tories have introduced - the 2022 emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and the clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system brought in last year.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, and Premier Doug Ford attend an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont. on, Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Carbon; Prime ministers; Environmental tax

Doug Ford claims union chief is 'lying' about him selling off the LCBO

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-claims-union-chief-is-lying-about-him/docview/2956231101/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+claims+union+chief+is+%27lying%27+about+him+selling+off+the+LCBO&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is accusing a major union leader of "lying" to members at the LCBO - who are in contract negotiations - that the government is planning to sell the liquor store to private interests.
But JP Hornick, president of the powerful Ontario Public Service Employees Union, says the premier is trying to paper over a gradual privatization of the Crown-owned booze retailer that has already seen warehousing contracted out and jobs lost.
"Doug Ford can't be trusted," Hornick said Wednesday. "Remember, these are the same guys that were trying to sell off the Greenbelt."
Ford's accusation came after unionized LCBO workers held rallies outside his Etobicoke North constituency office and in 10 other cities on Tuesday to draw attention to their concerns.
"We're going to stand together and do whatever it takes to stop Doug Ford from selling off the LCBO and to protect good union jobs in every community across Ontario," said Hornick.
That is not true, Ford insisted in Pickering as LCBO contract talks began.
"Their leader is actually misleading and, let me be more blunt, lying to their members about us closing the LCBO," the premier told reporters, noting that Hornick is "up for re-election in April."
"We will never, ever sell the LCBO. Matter of fact, they're going to be the exclusive wholesaler for when we do the Beer Store," the premier said in reference to the expiry of the province's master agreement with the Beer Store next year.
That will clear the way for beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails to be sold in convenience stores in addition to grocery and big box stores no later than Jan. 1, 2026, with all the products to be distributed by the LCBO.
"They're always going to be selling the spirits," he added, referring to liquor such as whisky, gin, rum and vodka on LCBO shelves that cannot be sold in supermarkets, big box or convenience stores even as Ontario eases restrictions on the sale of beverage alcohol.
"You have a stable job as long as we're in office," Ford said to LCBO workers.
Hornick said the government's long-term strategy for the liquor retailer is "death by 1,000 cuts," which is why the union is trying to protect members' jobs and the $2.5 billion in profits the LCBO puts into provincial coffers annually to help pay the cost of public services like health care and education.
"LCBO workers are organized like never before, and they're ready to fight for the future of the LCBO."
Liquor store workers won an arbitration award last weekend for what their union calls "three years of lost wages" after Ford's ill-fated public sector wage restraint legislation, Bill 124, was twice struck down by Ontario courts as unconstitutional for restricting collective bargaining.
The award adds 6.5 per cent to the wage grid.
Ford's government announced last month it would abandon the legislation and not appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference in Milton, Ont., on March 8, 2024.

'I'm not sure how we can stop this careening car going down the road': Hamilton Conservation Authority directors blast province's 'outrageous' conservation changes: Ontario premier 'a thug' doing developers' bidding, citizen members says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/im-not-sure-how-we-can-stop-this-careening-car/docview/2956230897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+not+sure+how+we+can+stop+this+careening+car+going+down+the+road%27%3A+Hamilton+Conservation+Authority+directors+blast+province%27s+%27outrageous%27+conservation+changes%3A+Ontario+premier+%27a+thug%27+doing+developers%27+bidding%2C+citizen+members+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton Conservation Authority directors are seeking legal advice on what they can do to fight provincial regulatory changes they say threaten local wetlands, watercourses and the health of the city's watersheds.
After nearly two hours of impassioned debate at their March 7 board meeting, directors opted to not adopt interim measures recommended by staff to comply with the new regulations, which take effect on April 1.
They instead acknowledged staff must comply with the new rules while asking for a report on the real-world impacts, including from no longer being allowed to consider pollution and conservation of land when issuing development permits.
"The challenge now is we're faced with outrageous behaviour by the province of Ontario and (Premier Doug) Ford, and we're hearing that we don't really understand the implications," citizen member Brian McHattie said.
The changes also reduce the protected zone around provincially significant wetlands to 30 metres from 120 and redefine what constitutes a watercourse. Formerly "an identifiable impression in the ground," it is now a "defined channel, having a bed and banks or sides."
The minister of Natural Resources and Forestry also gains new powers to bypass the authority to issue permits, which will be valid for five years, up from two years.
McHattie said the changes continue the premier's push to fulfil a developers' wish list that has already stripped other wetland protections, tried to remove land from the Greenbelt and attempted to expand Hamilton's urban boundary over the city's objections.
He said it's important for the authority to assess the impact of weakened conservation regulations and "spotlight the implications of what Mr. Ford is having us do," including by informing city residents.
"We're dealing with someone who is a thug and being reasonable is not in the realm for that individual," McHattie said. "All of this during a biodiversity crisis; all of this during a climate-change crisis."
Other directors expressed concern about the conflict between following the new regulations and their commitment to protecting the environment and the city's watersheds.
"If our own staff has no choice but to comply, what can we do as a board?" asked Puslinch member Susan Fielding, noting the authority repeatedly opposed the changes in written submissions to the province.
"I'm not sure how we can stop this careening car going down the road. We can see an accident happening, but how do we prevent it?"
Ward 13 Coun. Alex Wilson said directors need legal advice on whether the new regulations potentially put staff at risk "if something goes wrong" after a development permit is issued.
Wilson said no longer considering an identifiable impression in the ground to be a watercourse will affect major watercourses, a bit like removing capillaries from arteries, while reducing wetland protection zones could be equally devastating.
"Most of Ward 13 is a provincially significant wetland of some way, shape or form," Wilson said.
Citizen member Lisa DiCesare questioned the province's decision to remove pollution as a consideration for development permits while adding "health and safety" without defining the latter.
"I would just suggest that pollution is a health and safety issue," she said.
Authority chair Brad Clark said staff won't know the implications of the changes until they take effect and it's unclear if the city can take a more protective role on conservation issues that the province will no longer allow the authority to consider.
"The residents don't really understand what's been done and we need to somehow be able to explain that," he said, adding he shares the frustration with the province.
"They create us, they regulate us, they put their acts together and they ask us our opinion, and more often than not they don't really listen to our opinions. That's been my personal experience."
CAPTION: Hamilton Conservation Authority directors are seeking legal advice on what they can do to fight provincial regulatory changes they say threaten local wetlands, watercourses and the health of the city's watersheds.

Subject: Permits; Directors; Watersheds; Wetlands

Title: 'I'm not sure how we can stop this careening car going down the road': Hamilton Conservation Authority directors blast province's 'outrageous' conservation changes:   Ontario premier 'a thug' doing developers' bidding, citizen members says

Document 455 of 500

Cracking down on shady landlords

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cracking-down-on-shady-landlords/docview/2956057260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cracking+down+on+shady+landlords&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The saying goes that the punishment must fit the crime. And yet, when it comes to shady landlords in Ontario, it would seem the punishment inadvertently rewards the crime.
This is the takeaway from a recent analysis by the Toronto Star's Victoria Gibson which determined that even when landlords break the rules by improperly evicting tenants, they often face "far milder punishments than are available." Even more damning, landlords caught breaking the rules and fined as a result are often able to quickly recoup their losses by raising the rents on their properties.
Indeed, between November 2022 and November 2023, the Star identified 11 rulings at the Landlord and Tenant Board where a landlord was caught giving an eviction notice in bad faith. In six of those cases where the rent was raised after tenants were inappropriately evicted, the landlord "could have recouped the cost of their punishment by the hearing date."
What makes this trend particularly troubling, beyond the fact that Ontario faces a growing housing crisis, is that the province has already vowed tougher consequences for scofflaw landlords.
In 2020, the provincial government introduced new legislation that increased the maximum fines for housing law offences from $25,000 to $50,000. Yet, according to the Star's reporting, none of the aforementioned landlords caught giving bad faith eviction notices were slapped with an LTB fine of more than $10,000. And even when tenants took their concerns to the supposedly more discerning Rental Housing Enforcement Unit - the steepest fine that unit levied in the 2022-23 cycle was $20,000.
It is unacceptable and unconscionable that amid a severe housing shortage, many landlords who defy the rules for their own gain do not feel anything resembling the full extent of the law. The provincial government is keen to remind the people of Ontario how committed it is to building housing; Premier Doug Ford recently handed out millions to cities that met their provincially defined housing targets.
But he has an equal responsibility to do right by renters in the province, a growing demographic typically underserved and ignored by the political class.
It should go without saying that the glaring lack of affordable rental housing in our society is not a problem that can be solved nor even slightly mitigated by way of steeper fines. However, bad actors must know that legitimate consequences exist for their actions; there is little point introducing punitive measures if authorities are hesitant to use them.
It is our view that if it is at all serious about improving the housing situation for every Ontarian, the provincial government must take a firmer stance with landlords who evict tenants for no good reason. And that stance must include giving landlords a very good reason to abstain from bad behaviour, such as a financial penalty they can't immediately recoup.
Additionally, we urge municipalities to pitch in to curb the problem wherever they can: more specifically, they can follow Hamilton's lead, which will become the first municipality in Ontario to implement a bylaw whose stated goal is to prevent so-called "renovictions."
The bylaw will require that all landlords in the city apply for and obtain a "renovation licence" prior to beginning any renovation work that would require the delivery of an N-13 eviction notice. Such a licence would include a building permit and an engineer's report, the latter to determine that the landlord is telling the truth - that the unit will actually be unlivable during construction.
In the end, a Hamilton-style bylaw is not a silver bullet. Nor are hefty fines for exploitative landlords. But taken together both measures are an added layer of protection against the flagrant mistreatment of Ontario tenants and a small yet consequential step toward a fair housing future for all.
CAPTION:

Document 456 of 500

Justin Trudeau ally joins Pierre Poilievre to demand a halt to the April 1 carbon levy increase

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-ally-joins-pierre-poilievre-demand/docview/2956403850/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+ally+joins+Pierre+Poilievre+to+demand+a+halt+to+the+April+1+carbon+levy+increase&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are launching a full-court political press against the Trudeau Liberals' April 1 carbon price increase - and there's already a Liberal premier signing on.
Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey - an ally of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - added his voice Tuesday to Poilievre's demand the Liberals pause the planned increase, a request also made in the last week by Ontario's Doug Ford, P.E.I.'s Dennis King and New Brunswick's Blaine Higgs, all conservatives.
They all argue going ahead with the scheduled increase to the levy asks too much of Canadians already feeling the pinch from higher living costs.
"In Newfoundland and Labrador, for the most vulnerable, it has been a particularly difficult time," Furey wrote in a letter to Trudeau released Tuesday.
"After years of increased living costs, there is a need to fully grasp and address the magnitude of inflation's effect on citizens, particularly those most in need."
Poilievre's current push began at a massive rally in Etobicoke last Sunday under the banner of "Spike the Hike." Coming next, a Conservative source told the Star: a new ad campaign on radio and television, events outside Liberal and NDP MP offices, numerous rallies across the country, debates in the House of Commons, as well as parliamentary committees, to press their case between now and April 1.
Poilievre urged supporters to join the cause, calling on them to sign his party's petition, organize protests at constituency offices and blast their own email and social media networks to get the word out.
"Many of you will say, oh, that'll never make a difference," he said at the rally.
"Well, if you think that you're too small to be effective, you've never been in a tent with a mosquito."
As of April 1, the federal levy placed on gasoline and natural gas will rise by $15 per tonne, adding 3.3 cents to a litre of gasoline and about 2.9 cents to a cubic metre of natural gas.
The Liberals say placing a consumer price on carbon encourages Canadians to limit their use of fossil fuels to save money, and in turn help reduce the emissions that cause climate change.
Residents of B.C., Quebec and the Northwest Territories are exempt from the federal levy, as those regions have their own programs.
The federal government cuts Canadians a cheque to cover the additional costs of the levy, but the debate continues to rage over whether those payments - also going up in April - are sufficient to cover the costs of the additional charge.
The latest political skirmish comes in the wake of the Liberals making recent changes to the program, driven in part by Poilievre's pressure. Those changes include removing the carbon price on home heating oil and increasing the rebate rural Canadians receive.
The changes also flowed from Liberals, many from Atlantic Canada who were getting earfuls from constituents over their high bills.
Newfoundland and Labrador's Furey, who endorsed Trudeau in the last election, had also asked the prime minister to remove the carbon price on heating oil.
Those twin changes to the program were then followed by a third: a rebrand of the rebate, an acknowledgment that many Canadians don't realize what it is.
In response to Furey Tuesday, a spokesperson for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland did not directly answer the Star's question about whether they would entertain a pause, instead pointing out the carbon tax rebate helps Canadians with affordability issues. Her office also did not offer a response to Poilievre's call for protests and other actions to fight the increase.
Conservatives and Liberals have sparred over carbon pricing for more than a decade.
While the Liberals have since made slashing greenhouse gas emissions a signature piece of their policy agenda, Poilievre in turn has made a promise to end consumer carbon pricing a signature piece of his, making it a centrepiece of his leadership bid for the party.
He has said he believes in climate change, and that emissions cause it, pledging he'd reduce them by focusing on technology. He's not said exactly by how much he'd reduce emissions.
Now, as he prepares to hit 18 months as leader, the new anti-carbon tax campaign marks a fresh phase of his leadership: moving from introducing himself to voters to finding ways to mobilize them to stick by his side on the path to the next election.
With some $35 million raised last year alone, the Tories are pouring money into voter identification and motivation efforts, buying ads, billboards and hosting events to help drive the Poilievre brand, and a connection to it - in addition to tossing quips into the audience in Etobicoke, Poilievre also tossed an "axe the tax" T-shirt into the crowd.
By encouraging his supporters in Etobicoke, and likely again in rallies over the next several days, to sign petitions and engage on social media, for example, it opens up the opportunity to harvest the names and social media profiles of an untold number of potential new voters, to then target in the future.
"Politics is not a spectator sport," he told them. "It is a participation sport. If you're not at the table, you're on the menu. So you need to be at the table."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre holds an "Axe the Tax" event at the Toronto Congress Centre in Toronto on March 10, 2024.

Ford's finance minister hints at extension of gas tax break

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-finance-minister-hints-at-extension-gas-tax/docview/2956403683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+finance+minister+hints+at+extension+of+gas+tax+break&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Read his lips: no new taxes.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is signalling an extension of the Ford government's temporary cut to gas and fuel taxes is looming in the March 26 budget.
In a wide-ranging discussion with the Star editorial board on Tuesday, Bethlenfalvy said the impact of inflation is top of mind as he prepares the provincial spending plan.
"I'm not going to betray what may or may not be in the budget. I know we've been very focused on helping people with the cost of living, which includes the gas tax cut," the treasurer said.
"Clearly, that's not just driving kids to school or to work, but it's part of the whole supply chain," he said, emphasizing how the savings can impact a variety of goods and services.
First announced by Premier Doug Ford before the 2022 election - and twice extended - the 5.7 cents per litre gasoline tax cut is currently supposed to expire on June 30.
But Bethlenfalvy acknowledged "it would be tough" to hike the provincial fuel tax just three months after the federal carbon levy rises on April 1.
"I'm not looking to increase any taxes or fees," he said.
The tax break costs the treasury about $1.2 billion annually and saves the average family around $260 a year.
While bullish on Ontario's prospects, Bethlenfalvy stressed, "there's no question that our economy has slowed down."
"I hope that we don't go into recession technically," the finance minister said, noting that is why the province maintains significant contingency funds that opposition parties have criticized.
"It has an impact on revenue so there's no question - notwithstanding that the technical definition of a recession is two back-to-back quarters (of a shrinking economy), which we haven't seen," he said, pointing to Ontario's close ties to the booming U.S.
"People really feel like it's a recession everywhere I go. The U.K. is in recession. Japan is in recession ... (but) south of the border, of course, they keep beating expectations on the upside."
In Pollara Strategic Insights' annual economic outlook published in the Star in January, four in five respondents said they think Canada had already been plunged into a recession.
Bethlenfalvy said he understood why that sentiment is prevalent even though the province's economy is performing better than most jurisdictions, thanks to encouraging job growth and more investment from businesses.
"You talk to a lot of people and they're hurting. The price of food - I think we'd all would agree, wow, when you go to the grocery store, you're getting hit hard," he said.
The finance minister said he is also worried about the Bank of Canada's interest rates because of the toll that takes on people's mortgages.
With more than two years before the June 2026 election, Bethlenfalvy was mum on when the Progressive Conservatives would finally balance the budget.
In his fall economic statement last November, he had said the 2023-24 deficit would be $5.6 billion with the shortfall projected to be $5.3 billion in 2024-25.
At that time, the minister said Ontario would be back in the black with a $500-million surplus in 2025-26, in time for the next provincial vote.
That was a more gloomy picture than last March's budget, which had forecast a $200-million surplus this year and for the treasury to have a $4.4-billion surplus in 2025-26.
The change was largely due to tax revenues being $2.6 billion lower than expected - a sign the economy was slowing after it rebounded from the COVID-19 pandemic.
CAPTION: Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy meets with the Toronto Star editorial board on March 12, 2024.

Document 458 of 500

Ontario's system of naming judges is well-respected

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-system-naming-judges-is-well-respected/docview/2955212580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+system+of+naming+judges+is+well-respected&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I was startled, not long after I returned to Toronto in 1988 from several years of living in New York, to receive a call from attorney general Ian Scott inviting me to discuss a new system of appointing judges. As a known lefty feminist, married to a former leader of the Ontario NDP, I was unaccustomed to a Liberal siren song. But Scott was so highly regarded as an exceptional attorney general that I was eager to hear him out.
"We're going to change the entire system," he told me exuberantly when we met. In my columns in the Toronto Star, I had been harshly critical of those judges I saw as sexist, homophobic and racist, men (always men) who openly expressed their biases, even as their decisions reinforced every kind of inequality and injustice prevalent in our complacent province.
Scott proposed that we, the first Ontario Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), would freely create a new system of recruiting and selecting judges. Furthermore, he swore that he would faithfully choose one name from each short list of three. "I want you to open up the bench to a wide representation of Ontarians. Even people ... like me," he said, with a mischievous grin. It was well known in political and legal circles that Scott was gay, though closeted.
At first, the JAAC was seen as so radical that we were nearly deafened by howls of outrage from lawyers and wannabe judges. "But it's my turn to be appointed!" whined one lawyer from a small city. "I was promised this position by my MPP!" fumed another.
And these were minor complaints compared to the storm unleashed when we appointed a francophone judge, for the first time, to a heavily French-speaking area.
We were accused of everything from ignorance to outright communism. As we discovered the depth of cronyism and patronage in the Ontario court system, we felt strongly affirmed in our work - unexpectedly onerous work, with oppressive hours and not a penny of recompense - but work we were sure was way past due. We advertised judicial vacancies in the Star, and, despite the outrage from lawyers about this process ("so demeaning"), we were soon inundated with hundreds of applications. Within a month we were lugging giant briefcases on wheels to every meeting.
We were looking for judges rooted in their communities, with broad life experience and impeccable law practices. Personal interviews turned out to be a test of lurking arrogance: Some applicants were so furious at having to be interviewed by a bunch of "amateurs" that they sounded off to the committee's secretary in the anteroom, and then entered the room with sullen suppressed fury.
Our committee was lively and diverse, and we loved working together, exhilarated by hammering out our differences while keeping our focus on the public good. Peter Russell, professor and renowned constitutional expert, was our chair. He led with a light hand, but he established a guiding framework of firm nonpartisanship, transparency and legal scrupulousness.
Our JAAC became a model for jurisdictions around the world, admired by judges and legal experts. Among our appointments, nearly half were women, many were from diverse communities, and we chose the first openly gay judge.
The JAAC faced its first big threat in 1999, when then-premier Mike Harris (Doug Ford's mentor) tried to seize control. He refused to appoint a judge for a vacancy in his home riding of North Bay because the short list did not include a close Conservative friend of his. The delay dragged on for months, until, confronting a growing uproar, Harris backed down. Now his follower, Ford, is attempting to impose conservative ("like-minded") judges on the bench, an effort clothed in populist jargon about keeping "bad guys" in jail.
Unless the public speaks out forcibly again to safeguard this little-known linchpin of our democracy, we stand to lose something that we will bitterly regret in hindsight.
Michele Landsberg is a
former Toronto Star
columnist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Michele Landsberg Michele Landsberg is a former Toronto Star columnist.;

national briefs: Driver charged in deadly crash

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs%3A+Driver+charged+in+deadly+crash&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Two women die in Guelph fire
Two women are dead after a fire at a home in Guelph. Police say emergency crews were called to the home around 12:40 a.m. Monday. They say two women living in the home died as a result of the fire. Two other people inside were able to escape and were treated in hospital for smoke inhalation and minor injuries. The Ontario Fire Marshal's office has been contacted and will be investigating. Police say their own investigation is also ongoing.
Driver charged in deadly crash
A 19-year-old has been arrested for alleged impaired driving after a collision that left a pedestrian dead in north Toronto. Police say officers were called to the area of Lawrence Avenue West and Varna Drive at around 5 a.m. Monday for reports of a collision. They say a man was struck and was taken to hospital with life-threatening injuries. Police say the man died in hospital. They say the 19-year-old driver remained on the scene. Police say he has been arrested for allegedly driving impaired.

Maritimes
Rough weather, high tide alert
More unpleasant weather marched across much of Atlantic Canada on Monday. Environment Canada issued a series of weather warnings and special weather statements as residents of New Brunswick and Newfoundland were still digging out from heavy snowfalls. In eastern Newfoundland, heavy rain was in the forecast, leading to warnings that flooding could result because the frozen ground would be unable to absorb the precipitation. On Saturday, residents of the northern Avalon Peninsula, which includes St. John's, began digging out from under 50 centimetres or more of snow that fell over a two-day period.
Alberta
Nenshi joins
NDP race
Former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi has ended his self-imposed political retirement to run for Alberta's NDP leadership. Nenshi, who is now 52, was elected mayor of Calgary in 2010 and won three terms before deciding to bow out before the 2021 municipal election. He says it was time to choose a team and says looking at what the NDP stands for made it an easy choice. Nenshi says Alberta deserves better than the current United Conservative Party government of Premier Danielle Smith. He says he considers himself an underdog but has been encouraged by NDP supporters from across the province. Nenshi is the sixth candidate to toss his hat into the ring to replace current leader Rachel Notley, with a new leader set to be chosen in June.

Document 460 of 500

'It makes no sense': Trudeau government promised rural residents a carbon rebate top up - so why aren't these people getting it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/makes-no-sense-trudeau-government-promised-rural/docview/2955210959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It+makes+no+sense%27%3A+Trudeau+government+promised+rural+residents+a+carbon+rebate+top+up+-+so+why+aren%27t+these+people+getting+it%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MONO, Ont.-It might be the two provincial parks, the ski resort and the 278 square kilometres of rivers, hills and agricultural land. Or the fact it has more cell-service dead zones than public transit options or hospitals.
To Mayor John Creelman, however, the constant debate over whether to pave the roads of Mono, Ont. or keep them gravel is the biggest indicator of just how rural the town of 9,000 is.
But when it comes to the federal government's carbon levy and rebates, it's not rural enough. Mono, about an hour's drive from Toronto on a good day and even longer on the bus from nearby Orangeville - gets grouped into the Toronto area, making its residents ineligible for the cash Ottawa has set aside to help rural Canadians with the costs of the carbon levy.
Faced with heavy criticism over their carbon pricing program, the federal Liberals have been putting more emphasis on the quarterly rebates the program gives to Canadians - and the extra cash doled out to those living in rural areas. It's all part of an effort to repaint the price on pollution as an affordability measure that leaves Canadians better off. But not all rural Canadians impacted by the carbon levy, which aims to disincentivize people from emitting harmful greenhouse gases, are eligible for the rural top-up.
That's because since the introduction of the rural top-up, the federal government has used the boundaries of census metropolitan areas (CMA) - a tool created by Statistics Canada that groups densely populated centres, like the Greater Toronto Area, with closely-linked nearby municipalities - to define which Canadians are "rural." Even StatCan acknowledges you can still be living in a rural area if you are within CMA boundaries.
Mono just makes it into the Toronto CMA. But its neighbours and other Dufferin County municipalities, except for Orangeville and its 34,000 residents, fall outside of the CMA and are eligible to receive the extra cash.
"I can see Mono out my window, and we can get it and they can't," said Diana Morris, the executive director of the Dufferin Board of Trade. "There's no difference between the needs of my family living in Mulmur or the needs of a family living in Mono."
Six million Canadians live outside of CMAs and qualify for the rural top-up, with about 2.5 million in Ontario. But just over 500,000 Ontarians live in rural areas within CMAs, like Mono, and are denied the extra cash. For a family of four in the province, it adds up to an extra $224 this year.
"That's $224 less that you have to spend on anything your family needs," Creelman said.
Government officials defended the use of CMAs, arguing it's a "clearly defined and well-understood criterion" and that the rebates put more money back into the pockets of most Canadians.
"It also guarantees that Canadians are treated in a consistent manner across the country," said Katherine Cuplinskas, press secretary for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland.
But looking at his propane bill, Creelman is not so sure. The last time he filled up his tank, he got charged an extra $131.49 for a "carbon tax," nearly the same amount as the HST charge. Since the carbon fee applies to all polluting fuels, the cost to producers gets passed down to consumers when they fill up their cars with gas or fill a propane tank. Until recently, Creelman said he didn't even know rural Canadians should be getting extra cash, and it has weighed on his mind ever since he found out, he said.
He said Mono's geography and infrastructure makes its residents car-dependent, forcing many people to drive to the Toronto area for work. It's that relationship that sees it lumped into the Toronto CMA.
"It disturbs me to think that somebody in an office in Ottawa just decided that this was the best way to distribute the rural top-up, with no consultation and no discussion with us," he told the Star. "It's completely arbitrary. It makes no sense."
Because of these inconsistencies, Scot Davidson, the Conservative MP for nearby York-Simcoe, says the Liberals' approach to carbon pricing has been politically charged. While Liberal strongholds in the Atlantic Provinces, like the cities of Fredericton and Charlottetown, classify as rural and get the top-ups, his constituents get the same rebates those in downtown Toronto do. (The rebate amount given to residents of Ontario still exceeds any of the Atlantic Provinces.)
His riding contains the Chippewas of Georgina Island First Nation, an Indigenous reserve accessible to land by ferry only. But when it comes to the carbon rebates, it too counts as part of Toronto.
After a heavy push from Liberal Atlantic MPs last fall, the federal government announced a temporary exemption of home-heating oil from the carbon price, while doubling the rural top-up to 20 per cent from 10. It drew fury from across the country with premiers and First Nations demanding fairness, and gave ammo to a Conservative campaign to "axe the tax." Now, the program as a whole is under threat by Saskatchewan, which is threatening to stop gathering the federal levy on all fossil fuels.
The doubling of the rural top-up also meant the Liberals had to decrease the amount of money set aside for other groups, like small businesses. The Canadian Federation of Independent Business says Ottawa owes businesses $2.5 billion. To find out now that the money is not actually going to all rural communities is a "double whammy," and a "kick in the teeth," said Greg Wildeboer, a landscaper working in Dufferin County.
For the Tories, it's just another reason why the federal price on carbon needs to go.
"As Conservatives, our plan is to axe the carbon tax," Davidson told the Star. "But in the meantime, I want my constituents to get everything owed to them."
Speaking about the inconsistencies in the rural rebates, the conversation among Creelman and other professionals in Mono and the surrounding areas quickly turns to questioning the premise of carbon pricing at a time of high inflation. Bill McCutcheon, a local farmer and chairperson of the Dufferin Federation of Agriculture, spoke candidly about his attempts to invest in more climate friendly business practices. The money to invest, he said, is simply not there.
"My hands are tied," he said. "We have no alternatives."
Nearby, Creelman sat quietly, thinking about the outrage the people of Mono will feel when they find out why they've been missing out on the extra cash. Maybe, he wonders out loud, if they were acknowledged as the rural town they know they are, some of the economic pressures on the town could ease.
CAPTION: John Creelman, mayor of the town of Mono, stands along Hockley Road. His town is not considered rural under the rules used to decide who gets extra carbon rebate money.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Rural areas; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Environmental tax

Title: 'It makes no sense': Trudeau government promised rural residents a carbon rebate top up - so why aren't these people getting it?

Document 461 of 500

When Doug Ford tried to get out of jury duty - and earned himself a lecture on the rule of law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-doug-ford-tried-get-out-jury-duty-earned/docview/2955208068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Doug+Ford+tried+to+get+out+of+jury+duty+-+and+earned+himself+a+lecture+on+the+rule+of+law&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ten years ago, then-Toronto city councillor Doug Ford arrived at the Superior Court for jury duty.He didn't want to be there.He said as much in an interview with the Toronto Sun, saying he wanted to be excused - after all, he wasn't anonymous like his fellow prospective jurors."I believe I should be a jury member, but I don't think the person I convict should know my address, where I work, how to get hold of my kids," he told the newspaper in a Feb. 4, 2014 article. "The person I convict" - the next day in court, those four words were enough to earn Ford and the whole jury panel a lecture from the judge on a basic but crucial legal principle: that every person accused of a crime is presumed innocent until proven guilty.The episode is revealed in a court recording recently uncovered by the Star at a time when the now-premier of Ontario has sparked a constitutional crisis over his desire to appoint "like-minded" judges.Just like in 2014, the presumption of innocence is at the heart of the issue, with Ford "quintupling" down on wanting judges who will lock up even more people - including by denying bail to individuals who, as the judge reminded him a decade ago, are legally innocent.Back in 2014, Ford's statements to the Sun had raised concerns for the defence in a firearms possession case that was picking its jury. The defence was worried that his public comments suggested he would find someone guilty without hearing any evidence, and this may taint the other potential jurors and could throw the case off the rails."I do wish to remind all of you, at this particular juncture, of a very fundamental - a fundamental - rule of the law of this land," Superior Court Justice Michael Quigley told Ford and the others. "That fundamental rule, ladies and gentlemen, is the presumption of innocence."That's the foundation of the law of this land, and it protects each and every one of us." A decade later, legal organizations have decried Ford's call for tough "like-minded" judges as a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts; former chief justices have called out his comments as a "political litmus test" for judges. His stance was first revealed last month in response to a Star exclusive that his government had placed two of his ex-staffers on the arm's-length committee that recommends judges for the Ontario Court of Justice."I have a message to all these criminals: We're coming for you," Ford said at an unrelated announcement Friday."We're going to catch you and you're going to jail, no matter what it takes ... including making sure we have judges that do not let these people out on bail." There's also a wider context to Ford's comments. In recent years, his government has taken more control over what was a non-partisan appointment process, once described as one of the best in the world. The Tories have also placed a heavy focus on policing and prosecution while underresourcing legal aid and failing to properly staff courthouses, resulting in delays that have led to the collapse of serious criminal cases.Most of the people in Ontario's jails are legally innocent. About 80 per cent of inmates are on remand - meaning they've been denied bail and are awaiting trial or sentencing - a number that has only increased over the years. As The Canadian Press recently reported, the majority of jails were over capacity last year; Ford said Friday his government would build as many jails as they need. For the premier's growing chorus of critics, his words betray a fundamental misunderstanding of the rule of law - the same kind of thinking that led to Quigley's remedial-law rebuke a decade ago. Ford's actions, meanwhile, risk damaging the public's confidence in a vital democratic institution, while likely failing to do much of anything to actually keep Ontarians safe.Ontario is "detaining more people in custody than we ever have before, and crime is still happening," noted Ottawa criminal defence lawyer Michael Spratt, a frequent critic of the government on the judicial appointments process."The unfortunate thing that is apparent about Ford is that slogans and simplicity rule the mind," but "simple solutions" won't do what Ford says they will, Spratt said.The premier's office did not return the Star's request for comment about his jury duty experience and the judge's lecture. 'He doesn't get ... to just be able to walk out'Back in February 2014, the lawyers and the judge had a pressing question: What to do about Doug Ford?His statements to the Sun were one issue, but the defence was also concerned by comments Ford had made in court that again suggested he already thought the accused were guilty. Ford had told the judge during jury selection: "Your Honour, I don't know the accused, but they know who I am, and I don't feel comfortable with them knowing who I am."Quigley told Ford he was aware of his concerns reported in the Sun, but wasn't prepared to excuse him from jury duty just yet. Then, over the lunch break, the defence saw a tweet from a Sun reporter saying Ford had taken members of the jury pool to visit the nearby office of his brother, Mayor Rob Ford; they were seen leaving with "Ford Nation" t-shirts.After lunch, with just the lawyers and the judge in the courtroom, the defence expressed concern about Ford's sentiments and what might have been communicated to others in the jury pool. The accused persons hadn't been convicted of anything, but Ford had spoken like they were already guilty. What else might he have said to the other prospective jurors?A number of options were floated: Do they bring Ford into the courtroom alone to ask him questions? Do they discharge the whole jury panel? Declare a mistrial?"Insofar as Coun. Ford seems to have created a little bit of difficulty with comments that he has made so far, we may not be able to eliminate his ability to make those comments; it's a public nation, it's a free country, he can say what he wants," Quigley said. "Unfortunately, the press actually wants to quote what these people say."Ford could, however, be removed from the jury panel. Quigley told the lawyers that if Ford's name did get picked to potentially sit on the jury, he was unlikely to permit it, "for the very simple reason that the jurors won't focus on what their job is, they'll focus on him. And I don't mind recording that that's exactly what I just said."But Ford would have to stay awhile longer, the judge said, "because he doesn't get the entitlement, unlike other people, to just be able to walk out. He has a civic duty to serve just like everybody else does, and he can wait here until that jury is picked, and that's exactly what's going on here."And so Quigley decided he would "admonish" the whole jury panel on the presumption of innocence - without mentioning Ford and his comments - before resuming jury selection.'An instrument of partisanship and ideology'Provincial and federal governments appointing judges who broadly share their world view is hardly new or surprising, but one of the concerns with Ford's stance is that he's made clear he wants a specific ruling from judges who are meant to be, and invariably take pride in being, independent. "The way in which the premier is expressing himself - and sort of bombastically, as one might expect - it gives the impression that yes, he wants this outcome: regardless of the law, regardless of the facts, he always wants them to come down hard on crime," said Troy Riddell, chair of the political science department at the University of Guelph, who researches judicial politics. "I don't mind the needle being shifted a little bit, but that has to be done with care and sensitivity and nuance, and that's not what you're seeing in this case."It's also not clear that denying bail ultimately lowers crime and keeps people safe; research more often points to the need for mental health and substance-use supports, affordable housing and a livable income - largely provincial responsibilities. If the government wants to increase public safety, and ensure the justice system is sustainable and jails aren't overcrowded, "the evidence is very clear that many of the policies that the premier seems to favour are just not good policies," Osgoode Hall law professor Palma Paciocco said. "They may seem intuitive or appealing to many, but they're just not evidence-based." Ford's "like-minded" comments come on the heels of changes his government has made to the 13-person Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), which is made up of judges, lawyers, and members of the public and was created in the 1980s to ensure an independent, non-partisan appointment process. It was "widely considered to be one of the best judicial appointments processes in North America" at the time of its creation, three former chief justices of the Ontario Court of Justice said in a statement this month. And until recently, they said, the basic structure and function of the committee "remained largely unchanged and has been honoured by successive Liberal, NDP and Conservative governments."The committee was also seen as superior to the system that vets candidates for federally-appointed judicial positions, which has faced its own accusations of politicization. In 2021, Ford's attorney general, Doug Downey, implemented major changes that legal groups warned could lead to patronage appointments to the bench.Those included taking away the power of the three legal organizations on the committee - the Law Society of Ontario, the Federation of Ontario Law Associations and the Ontario Bar Association - to name their own representatives; instead, they must now submit lists of candidates for his selection, allowing Downey to appoint almost all of the committee's members. As the Star previously reported, Downey has still not selected a Law Society representative, despite the regulator sending him a list of candidates.Downey also increased the number of judicial candidates on the shortlist submitted to him by the committee; previously, a ranked shortlist of at least two candidates was submitted. It's now six, with critics warning that giving the government access to the wider application pool could lead to the appointment of lower-quality candidates.And now, Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy chairs the JAAC, while Ford's ex-director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has been a public member of the committee since December.A process that was once a model for the world "has degenerated into an instrument of partisanship and ideology," said University of Toronto law professor Kent Roach."The public will and should have less confidence in the administration of justice in this province because of this change."'The foundation of the law of this land'In the end, a mistrial was declared in the firearms case for reasons unrelated to Ford, and he never did end up on a jury. But he nevertheless walked away with an important lesson that day about the criminal justice system."Accused persons start out trials with a clean slate," Quigley told the prospective jurors, future-premier included. "It is only when the Crown has satisfied those 12 people (on the jury) that the accused person is guilty of one or more offences that the presumption of innocence leaves."Everyone engages in "lunch hour talk, breakfast table talk, street talk, about people and about guilt and innocence," Quigley said. But, he emphasized, jurors are asked to try a case based solely on the evidence, and not on any outside influence, bias, or partiality."That's what it's all about; that's the foundation of the law of this land and it protects each and every one of us, OK? So I admonish you and remind you of that presumption of innocence," the judge said."And we'll just leave the matter there."
CAPTION: Doug Ford speaks at City Hallin a Dec. 13, 2013, file photo.

Subject: Bail; Rule of law; Juries; Court hearings & proceedings; Judicial appointments; Defense; Attorneys; Partisanship; Judges & magistrates

Ottawa commits funding to help survivors of sexually-based attacks in Israel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-commits-funding-help-survivors-sexually/docview/2955702656/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+commits+funding+to+help+survivors+of+sexually-based+attacks+in+Israel&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly says Ottawa will commit $1 million to support survivors of sexually-based attacks in Israel on Oct. 7.
"We believe Israeli women. And we stand with them in their fight for justice," Joly announced on social media, adding support from the RCMP has also been offered to assist in investigations of the reported crimes.
Joly's decision to provide funding follows repeated pressure from a coalition of high-profile Canadian women who urged the Liberal government to act in response to reports that Hamas assailants sexually assaulted people during their unprecedented attack on Israel on Oct. 7.
Former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne, former deputy Conservative party leader Lisa Raitt, and others wrote letters and opinion pieces urging Ottawa to take action and push back against those who were downplaying or denying that Hamas used sexual violence.
Wynne, who helped spearhead the push, said Monday she was "very glad this decision has been made."
"I'm sorry it took so long," Wynne said.
"There's nothing partisan about helping people who have suffered - through no fault of their own - in a war," she said.
Others involved include former B.C. Liberal premier Christy Clark, former interim federal Tory leader Rona Ambrose, former Ontario education minister Liz Sandals, and Laureen Harper, a prominent federal Tory and the wife of former prime minister Stephen Harper.
Last week, the UN envoy focusing on sexual violence in conflict said there are "reasonable grounds" to believe Hamas committed rape, "sexualized torture" and other cruel and inhumane treatment of women during its attack.
Pramila Pratten, who visited Israel and the West Bank from Jan. 29 to Feb. 14 with a nine-member technical team, presented her report to the United Nations' Security Council on Monday.
She said their findings included determining some victims were subjected to rape and then killed, and there was also a pattern of women found partially or totally naked, tied up and shot, which indicates sexualized violence, among other things.
Pratten urged Israel to allow a fuller investigation by other UN agencies into what happened on Oct. 7, saying truth and recognition of all violations are critical to helping victims overcome their trauma and regain a sense of agency.
"It is also essential to prevent any denial of the atrocities committed on the seventh of October," she told the UN.
"Truth is the only path toward peace."
Joly made Canada's pledge during her latest tour of the Middle East, largely focused on increased humanitarian support for Gaza, where more than 30,000 Palestinians are believed to have been killed in Israel's response to the Hamas attacks.
Around 250 people were taken hostage and 1,200 were killed when Hamas militants stormed into Israel on Oct. 7.
Ariella Kimmel, a former Conservative staffer who was involved in the effort to press Ottawa for funding, said she was grateful the women of Israel are getting the recognition they deserve.
"For months, Israelis have dealt with skepticism and denialism," she said.
"If you advocate for women, then that advocacy cannot be dependent on whether you agree with the government of the country from which the victims come."
Next week, a delegation of families of Hamas' victims, including those with Canadian roots, will travel to Parliament Hill in a bid to raise further awareness about violence against women.
CAPTION: Minister of Foreign Affairs Melanie Joly listens to questions from reporters in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on on Feb. 29, 2024.

Subject: Violence; Sex crimes; Funding; Rape

Location: Israel
