Document 494 of 500

Trudeau ministers admit Donald Trump poses risk to key industries in Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ministers-admit-donald-trump-poses-risk/docview/2937417550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+ministers+admit+Donald+Trump+poses+risk+to+key+industries+in+Canada&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A re-elected Donald Trump would likely act on his campaign warning to slap a 10-per-cent tariff on all imports, and threaten several key Canadian economic sectors, senior Trudeau government ministers admit.
And while they said it doesn't matter to Trudeau's newly-relaunched "Team Canada" effort who sits in the White House as president, the top diplomat and two politicians leading Canada's offence-defence strategy outlined to the Star's editorial board exactly how Ottawa is moving to counter that threat.
Kirsten Hillman, Canadian ambassador in Washington, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne and International Trade Minister Mary Ng said the strategy draws in part on the 2016-2019 playbook Canada used to fight Trump's aggressive renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement.
But this time it's different, said Hillman, because there is no specific renegotiation on the horizon - despite a 2026 mandatory review of the rewritten NAFTA deal - bringing with it specific trade questions on the table.
Instead, she said, Canada is lining up "strategic allies" to push back against any protectionist or isolationist measures, no matter which party is in the White House.
Still, Hillman and Ng said Canadian diplomats in the U.S. are taking Trump's campaign rhetoric seriously.
"What I think we've learned from the last Trump administration is that he will say things that seem outrageous, but you should listen to him when he says them because he will do them," said Hillman. "And I think that that is something we know very deeply, and we should and we need to understand that. And then we need to understand how we will react to that situation."
Canadian officials have reached out to unions, business leaders, and state lawmakers "across the aisle" in the U.S. and are travelling beyond Washington, New York, L.A. and San Francisco to go to Arizona, Nevada, Arkansas and Louisiana - "all places where Canadian companies are ... putting huge amounts of money into projects in those states, which are largely Republican states."
She said it is "an exercise in illustrating to Americans across the country, why they need us and for what, because that's - to be crass about it - that's leverage" at a future time when things aren't working out.
That much is similar to Trudeau government's NAFTA renegotiation strategy where Canada sought allies in the U.S.
"What's different this time than last time," Hillman said, is that Republican and Trump-friendly think tanks have issued a series of policy papers and policy advice for the Trump team "or whoever the nominee is," and that is providing a road map for the Trudeau government to track.
"I think there's two tomes that are about 900 pages each. We've gone through all of them," she said.
Canadian diplomats have met the authors of those policy pieces to discuss where the positions are relevant to the Canada-U.S. relationship, and are clearly highlighting the consequences that could result "if a policy is implemented in a certain way without thinking about the facts on the ground about the Canada-U.S. relationship."
Hillman added "we're also reaching out to Republicans across the country who we know are close to the leadership and close to the former president Trump who may well have a very good chance of being in his cabinet, and we're refreshing those relationships and again emphasizing the stuff that's going well."
"It's not a PR exercise, because it is a truly substantive exercise that can make a big difference," she said.
Hillman said that kind of legwork was vital during the first Trump term after he imposed tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum products. She said Canada had the Coors brothers, "who are not Democrats, on side with us, going to the president, talking to the White House ... in very detailed ways around the implications of that (tariff) policy on their business. That isn't just profits for their board, but it's beer cans for Americans ... and coke cans and ... it's very personal."
Ng said Canadian steel, aluminum and softwood lumber business leaders are particularly concerned about Trump's threats and "we should take it seriously."
Ottawa is fielding thousands of calls and offers from Canadian business and union leaders who want to help make Canada's case south of the border, she said.
However, the ministers confirmed the Trudeau government has not specifically reached out to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to engage him on its "Team Canada" effort, though Hillman engaged with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith when she recently travelled to Washington, and Champagne said he has spoken to Premier Doug Ford about the strategy.
"Every voice matters" said Champagne, adding they would "be happy to hear from" Poilievre.
Meanwhile, the Trudeau government is aggressively pursuing more "diversification" of trade with other countries and allies in Europe, Asia, the Indo-Pacific and what Ng called "frontier economies."
"We sort of want our cake and eat it too. And that's the work we've been pursuing," she said.
Champagne underscored that "Canada is the only G7 country which has a free-trade agreement with all the other G7 nations. That give us preferential market access to 1.5 billion consumers. So I'd say Canada has a lot of options."
But he said the "asymmetrical relationship" between Canada and the U.S. is still Canada's most integrated and strategically important one.
"We understand the integrated nature of our relationship but down south of the border, I'm not sure they always think that way."
The two ministers and the ambassador named by Trudeau to lead the latest Team Canada effort say the goal is to ensure the "integrated" economic ties are understood in the U.S., but also the bilateral security ties, and the role of Canada in securing North American supply chains, recently disrupted by the pandemic, the war in Ukraine and now the Middle East.
CAPTION: Kirsten Hillman, Canadian ambassador in Washington, left,Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne, centre, and International Trade Minister Mary Ng at the Star's editorial board on March 4, 2024.

Ford government defeats attempt to exempt trucks from Hwy. 407 tolls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-defeats-attempt-exempt-trucks-hwy/docview/2937417003/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+defeats+attempt+to+exempt+trucks+from+Hwy.+407+tolls&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have put the brakes on a New Democrat push to scrap tolls for transport trucks on Hwy. 407.
The Tories used their majority in the legislature Monday to defeat a motion from NDP Leader Marit Stiles seeking to exempt commercial truckers from tolls.
Her move was designed to lure thousands of trucks daily to Hwy. 407 from heavily congested Hwy. 401 and easing traffic for all drivers - and to mock a government omnibus bill introduced two weeks ago that bans tolls on all roads except the 407.
"This government has a chance right now, right here in front of them to make life cheaper and easier for people in Ontario," Stiles said.
"Anybody you know who has been near the 407 knows that it's very deeply underutilized," she told reporters, calling her idea "a common sense" solution.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria brushed off suggestions the government is not helping motorists, noting it has removed tolls for all drivers on Hwy. 412 and Hwy. 418 in Durham region before the 2022 election and cut the gas tax by 10 cents a litre.
"Every step of the way, we have been there for truck drivers," he told MPPs before the vote.
The government's Bill 162, the Get It Done Act, would "prohibit tolls from being charged for travel on a highway where the road authority is the Crown, unless the toll is authorized by an Act."
That specifically excludes Hwy. 407, leased to a private consortium by Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris before the 1999 election.
Given that most of the 407 is run by a private company under lease from the province, it would likely take a new agreement to exempt transport trucks from tolls and compensatory payments from the government.
Sarkaria, who last month defended the legislation that protects the tollway, acknowledged it would be difficult to scrap the tolls, saying, "Look, the 407 is a private entity."
He was referring to how the toll costs have shot up on the 108-km private section of the highway from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to Brock Road in Pickering.
Queen's Park owns and operates the remaining 43 km of Hwy. 407 from Brock Road east to Orono.
It can cost as much as $70.66 to drive 151 km from the QEW to Hwys. 35/115 near Orono.
Stiles said it is "malarkey" that the government would propose the omnibus bill and not take tolls of an existing highway like the 407.
Stiles said that original 407 lease agreement made by the Harris government was a "bad deal."
The lobby group Environmental Defence said a study by global transport analysts at Eunomia concluded lifting truck tolls on Hwy. 407 would move between 12,000 and 21,000 trucks a day off Hwy. 401 and slash truck travel times across the GTA by 80 minutes.
CAPTION: Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria during question period in the Ontario Legislature on Feb.20, 2024.

Subject: Tolls; Roads & highways; Trucks

March 4: Mulroney was just the worst and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-4-mulroney-was-just-worst-other-letters/docview/2934403432/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+4%3A+Mulroney+was+just+the+worst+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mulroney was just the worst
Brian Mulroney has passed away and the tributes and accolades are flowing in like the high tide in the Bay of Fundy. But before we all reach for a box of Kleenex, let's just review some of the high points from whom I regard as one of Canada's worst prime ministers.
Tuna-gate scandal, 1985 where under Mulroney's leadership a million cans of tainted tuna were allowed onto Canadian grocery shelves, despite being declared unfit by federal inspectors.
The Meech Lake fiasco, 1987: This not so brilliant piece of work not only divided Canada, but resulted in a surge of Quebec nationalism. In the end, it was sunk by a feather.
The free trade nightmare, 1988. Mulroney basically killed Canadian industry and manufacturing with this move to appease the Americans and allow things to be built cheaper and for more profit for corporations in Mexico and the U.S. It did nothing for the average Canadian Joe except make things cost more.
The GST, 1991: Whatever business he didn't kill off with free trade he performed the coup de gras with this insidious tax.
The Airbus Affair, 1995. Mulroney reportedly scored $225,000 in kickbacks from Karlheinz Schrieber in this scam, then he went on afterward to sue the Government of Canada: read us, the taxpayer, over these allegations for $37 million.
And the best part of this guy's legacy: Rather than face the voters' wrath, he cowardly resigned in 1993 with the lowest approval rating in Canadian history, and left the mess to Kim Campbell, who suffered the greatest defeat the Conservatives have ever had in Canadian history.
No, no tears being shed over here.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
History may be repeating itself
I'm old enough to remember Mr. Mulroney's accomplishments (the free trade agreement, international recognition for Canada) and his mistakes (the GST; failure of Meech Lake and the Charlottetown Accord).
When prime minister Pierre Trudeau's government was in trouble in 1984 and he refused to resign, Mr. Mulroney won the next election by a huge majority. Unheard of for the Progressive Conservative Party. Mr. Mulroney won two consecutive majority governments. He was prime minister for almost a decade.
Is it happening again with Pierre's son, Justin? He is very unpopular for his overspending and other gaffs. If you believe the polls, and if the election were held today, the Conservatives would win. Then we would have Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as prime minister. Some think he is the "Canadian Trump." Strange coincidence or what? The French have a saying that fits here: The more things change, the more they stay the same. History has lessons to teach that, if ignored, lead to disaster.
Dan Smith, Stoney Creek
History will look kindly on Mulroney
I can imagine our departed for the heavens charismatic Brian Mulroney, who crushed monotone John Turner with a landslide 211 seat, eclipsing "Dief the Chief's" 208-seat victory, being welcomed on the other side by the Gipper and Iron Lady.
As a Baby Boomer, I recall that it was Mulroney's spirited speeches and memorable quip to Turner "you had an option, sir" that compelled me to make it to the ballet box in 1984. Watching Mulroney's clashing with the Liberal Rat Pack, especially lively debating with MP Sheila Copps, on CPAC always had me anxiously tuning in for the start of question period.
While many dreaded his GST, the fact is it was at a much lower rate than the MST (manufacturer's sales tax) it replaced that in concert with his free trade agreement smashed through the walls U.S. protectionism bolstering Canadian exports to new highs.
Mulroney, a cold war hawk, in his "defence white paper" proposed eight to 12 nuclear-propelled, Arctic-capable submarines. However, Jean Chrétien torpedoed that with his acquisition of four bargain priced, lease-to-own used British submarines that have been plagued with breakdowns and enormous repair costs that I would cite as "deterrent-redundant."
His singing "When Irish Eyes are Smiling" with ally president Ronald Reagan at the Shamrock Summit was most entertaining; however, his large, protracted deficits gave rise to Preston Manning's truly conservative "Reform Party" that along with rebel Lucien Bouchard's Bloc Québécois paved the way for Kim Campbell's inevitable defeat.
I remember Statesmen Mulroney's eulogy to fighter pilot and president George H.W. Bush, which was most heartwarming indeed.
My condolences go out to Mulroney's wife of over a half century Mila, MPP Caroline Mulroney, and his family.
David C. Searle, Toronto
Follow the science, get your shot
As an aging Baby Boomer in the last phase of life that as a grumpy old man, I can recall my childhood school days in the '50s and early '60s when classrooms would be full of crying children in the first month of attendance. Without exception, every child would have their sleeve rolled up and would be administered vaccines for measles, chickenpox, polio, smallpox, scarlet fever and other life-threatening acute infectious diseases.
Diligence has paid of over the decades and many have been declared eradicated by the World Health Organization. While Baby Boomers still embrace life well into their 80s and 90s and just won't die off, younger generations are seeing outbreaks in measles and innocent babies born with venereal diseases has risen by 13 times over the last four years in Canada. There are of course exceptions to every rule, but perhaps young people would be wise to follow the science rather than take the advice of Grade-D celebrities and snake oil salesmen who push bleach as a wonder drug cure all.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
Speeding through the core
Hamilton is one of very few cities that has four-lane thoroughfares running directly through its core. Our forefathers tore down our old city and built a mall with "for profit parking" then allowed developers to build suburban super malls with free parking, thus killing the core. Since there's no reason to stop, people have been spoiled by the ability to quickly drive through our city without even slowing down. Now we're tearing down the core and building condos with limited free parking and the panic is not being able to speed from one end to the other without stopping. The real panic might be the developers tearing down the suburban malls and building condos with free parking. Welcome to Toronto west.
Gary Ellis, Hamilton
CAPTION: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney waits to speak at a 2007 corporate function in downtown Toronto. Readers are divided on Mulroney's legacy.

Subject: Measles; Conservatism; Canadian history; Free trade; Trade agreements; Baby boomers; Prime ministers

People: Mulroney, Brian

Doug Ford names Milton byelection candidate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-names-milton-byelection-candidate/docview/2934403375/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+names+Milton+byelection+candidate&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has tapped local councillor Zee Hamid to be the Progressive Conservative candidate in Milton.
Ford, leery of new Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie getting a seat in the legislature, has moved quickly to find a successor to former cabinet minister Parm Gill, who jumped ship last month for Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives.
No byelection has yet been set for Milton and the premier has until July to call one.
The premier hasn't even scheduled a byelection for Lambton-Kent-Middlesex after former minister Monte McNaughton resigned in September, although one must be called there by the end of this month.
But up in the polls - and with the Greenbelt land swap scandal fading from the headlines for now - Ford is eager to talk about a key Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area swing riding.
He is mindful that Gill only won the riding by 1,680 votes over Liberal runner-up Sameera Ali in a province-wide Tory landslide.
"Zee Hamid is a champion for Milton who will help us get it done by building Highway 413 and extending two-way, all-day GO train service along the Milton line," the premier said Sunday.
"While Bonnie Crombie and the Liberals support a carbon tax and oppose Highway 413, Zee is ready to hit the ground running to deliver on Milton's priorities," he said of the three-term Milton councillor.
Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor who won the Liberal leadership in December, has not yet ruled out seeking a seat in Milton.
Her office said Monday no decision has yet been made when asked if she might contest the riding.
In a statement, Hamid, a veteran of the tech sector, made the point that he is a local candidate.
"Milton is my home. For over a decade, I served Milton on local and regional councils by putting families first and listening to our community," he said.
"Milton deserves strong local representation at Queen's Park from someone who knows and understands their concerns, which is why I'm running to build Highway 413 and fight the Liberal carbon tax on groceries and home heating."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Monday that her Official Opposition party does "not have someone nominated yet" in Milton.
The Greens have also not selected a candidate.
In the sprawling London-area riding of Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, Chatham-Kent councillor Steve Pinsonneault will try to hold the seat for Ford's Tories.
Lucan Biddulph Mayor Cathy Burghardt-Jesson is the Liberal candidate while community activist Kathryn Shailer is the New Democrats' standard-bearer and the Greens are fielding registered respiratory therapist Andraena Tilgner.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a news conference in Toronto on Feb. 22, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Environmental tax

Document 498 of 500

Ontario courts trouble with its judiciary

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-courts-trouble-with-judiciary/docview/2934178437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+courts+trouble+with+its+judiciary&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judging by his recent comments, Premier Doug Ford doesn't even understand part of democracy.
Witness his defence of his appointing two of his former staff members to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee, which vets and recommends judicial candidates: At a news conference last week, Ford opined that "it's part of democracy" for governments to appoint "like-minded" judges.
You won't find that part of democracy in any civics text. But in the face of withering criticism, the premier insisted that he was tripling and quadrupling down, even after it was revealed Wednesday that the man he appointed chair of the committee is a lobbyist for a gun manufacturer.
By way of explanation, Ford suggested "like-minded" means being tough on crime, as he condemned judges who grant bail to those accused of serious offences. He neglected to mention, however, that judges and justices of the peace are already tough on people accused of crimes, as nearly 80 per cent of inmates in Ontario's prisons are awaiting trial.
Also missing from his pop-up civics lecture was any mention of the Constitution, including its guarantees of the presumption of innocence and the right not to be denied bail without just cause. Those rights are, it seems, mysteriously absent from Ford's conception of democracy.
That's not the worst of it. The premier's recent musing isn't a one-off, off-the-cuff remark; it is, rather, part of his government's continuing campaign to erode judicial independence. The campaign was first revealed in 2019, when attorney general Doug Downey told TVO he wanted to select judges who "reflect some of the values that I have."
Downey offered those comments in support of proposed changes to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee, changes that would grant the attorney general greater control over committee appointments and much greater latitude in selecting judicial candidates.
Downey's proposals were met with widespread condemnation, including from the architect of the committee's judicial selection process, who said the changes risked needlessly politicizing judicial appointments. But the attorney general nonetheless pushed through the changes in 2021.
That still wasn't enough, though. Upon the impending retirement of the chief justice of Ontario, Downey decided in 2022 he needed to be personally involved in vetting her replacement, rather than, as was customary, allowing the outgoing chief to vet candidates and make recommendations.
The custom existed for the same reason as the JAAC: To maintain judicial independence and ensure sufficient separation between two co-equal branches of government. Yet by insinuating himself into the process, Downey required members from one branch of government to make entreaties directly to another branch - a gross violation of both judicial independence and the separation of powers.
Indeed, Downey's and Ford's efforts over the past five years amount to a sustained attack on judicial independence, which holds that judges must make their decisions free from outside pressures, especially pressure from the government.
Since the government is one of the main litigants in court, including in bail hearings, the public must have confidence judges are guided by the evidence and the law, rather than by the whims of the government of the day. Yet Ford's comments, and measures like those imposed by Ford and Downey, only serve to dilute that confidence. Even if every judge they appoint is beyond reproach, there will remain the suspicion that judges were selected not on merit, but on their allegiance to the government's interests.
And once trust in judicial independence is lost, so too is faith in the rule of law since the spectre of bias is ever present when "like-minded" judges adjudicate disputes between the government and its citizens.
This is precisely why we have independent, arm's-length committees like the JAAC. Or to put it in words the premier might understand: It isn't part of democracy for governments to appoint like-minded judges; it's part of democracy to maintain safeguards that prevent governments from doing so.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Committees; Attorneys general; Judicial appointments; Democracy

Title: Ontario courts trouble with its judiciary

Document 499 of 500

Will Canada meet its EV sales target?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-canada-meet-ev-sales-target/docview/2934178294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Canada+meet+its+EV+sales+target%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In December 2022, Canada moved to mandate electric vehicle sales starting in 2025. Transport Canada set targets to have 10 per cent of all light-duty cars be electric by 2025. So with only 10 months to go until 2025, how are we doing with that?
Data published in December 2023 by Statistics Canada show in the first nine months of 2023, 132,783 new battery electric or plug-in hybrid vehicles were registered across the country, making up 10.3 per cent of the new registrations. So we may just meet our 2025 target of 10 per cent.
Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault also proposed that one-fifth of all passenger cars, SUVs and trucks sold in Canada in 2026 will need to run on electricity under new regulations. So how are we going to double the sale of electric vehicles (EV) by 2026?
According to the most recent data released in November by S&P Global Mobility, registrations of battery electric (BEV) and plug-in hybrid (PHEV) vehicles surged to 13.3 per cent in Canada in the third quarter of 2023. Not only is this another record, but it's a 2.7 per cent jump over the second quarter of the zero emission vehicles (ZEV) market share, which was the previous all-time high of 10.5 per cent.
The reason? Because two provinces in Canada are excelling in EV sales. Why? Because along with Yukon and Prince Edward Island, they offer the best provincial EV rebates.
British Columbia continues to lead the country in EV adoption, and for the first time, the ZEV market share in B.C. topped one in four vehicles. According to S&P, 26.4 per cent of all vehicles registered were ZEVs. In terms of volume, this represented 24.1 per cent of all ZEVs registered in Canada.
B.C. offers a $4,000 rebate for the purchase or lease of a new battery electric vehicle or extended range EV. Combined with the $5,000 federal rebate, this adds up to $9,000 toward a new EV.
Quebec also reached a new benchmark, with ZEV registrations topping 20 per cent for the first time at 22.8 per cent. It also leads the country in volume, with 40.4 per cent of all ZEV registrations in Canada coming from that province. Quebec also offers Canada's largest provincial EV rebate - $7,000 for the purchase of a new EV. Combined with the $5,000 federal EV rebate, this adds up to $12,000 toward a new EV.
Yukon and Prince Edward Island both topped Ontario in terms of ZEV market share, hitting marks of 10.4 and 9.3 per cent respectively. Yukon and P.E.I. both offer a $5,000 rebate on the purchase of a new EV - adding up to $10,000 with the $5,000 federal rebate.
Unfortunately, Ontario's ZEV market share continues to lag behind the national average at just 8.7 per cent. So why isn't Ontario keeping up with the program? One reason is because Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government cancelled all EV rebates and incentives in 2018.
The federal Liberal government offers a $5,000 rebate on purchases and leases (of at least 48 months) on new battery electric vehicles, hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (FCEVs), and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVS) that can travel more than 50 kilometres on electricity alone. PHEVs with a lower electric range receive a $2,500 rebate.
The federal rebates will help keep EV purchases somewhat affordable, but we also need provincial rebates to help make them more affordable.
According to Automotive News Canada, the Conservative Party of Canada won't commit to keeping an EV rebate program if it wins the next election. This indicates to me it will probably cancel all federal EV rebates entirely.
Conservative leaders will probably blame the Trudeau government if EV sales targets aren't met. However, it's not up to Trudeau to meet Canada's 2025-26 EV sales target. It's up to each individual province to get with the program and offer enticing rebates.
I dread to see everything the current federal government has accomplished be wiped out if a Conservative government is elected in 2025.
One of the main reasons Canadians cite for not buying an EV is because they can't afford one. If you think the Ford government should offer a provincial rebate for the purchase of a new EV in Ontario, please write the premier and ask him to match the federal government's $5,000 rebate.
Tricia Clarkson is co-chair of the Peterborough Alliance for Climate Action.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Tricia Clarkson Tricia Clarkson is co-chair of the Peterborough Alliance for Climate Action.;

Full text: Mulroney was just the worst
Brian Mulroney has passed away and the tributes and accolades are flowing in like the high tide in the Bay of Fundy. But before we all reach for a box of Kleenex, let's just review some of the high points from whom I regard as one of Canada's worst prime ministers.
Tuna-gate scandal, 1985 where under Mulroney's leadership a million cans of tainted tuna were allowed onto Canadian grocery shelves, despite being declared unfit by federal inspectors.
The Meech Lake fiasco, 1987: This not so brilliant piece of work not only divided Canada, but resulted in a surge of Quebec nationalism. In the end, it was sunk by a feather.
The free trade nightmare, 1988. Mulroney basically killed Canadian industry and manufacturing with this move to appease the Americans and allow things to be built cheaper and for more profit by corporations in Mexico and the U.S. It did nothing for the average Canadian Joe except make things cost more.
The GST, 1991: Whatever business he didn't kill off with free trade he performed the coup de gras with this insidious tax.
The Airbus Affair, 1995. Mulroney reportedly scored $225,000 in kickbacks from Karlheinz Schrieber in this scam, then he went on afterward to sue the Government of Canada - read us, the taxpayer - over these allegations for $37 million.
And the best part of this guy's legacy: Rather than face the voters' wrath, he cowardly resigned in 1993 with the lowest approval rating in Canadian history, and left the mess to Kim Campbell, who suffered the greatest defeat the Conservatives have ever had in Canadian history.
No, no tears being shed over here.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
History may be repeating itself
I'm old enough to remember Mr. Mulroney's accomplishments (the free trade agreement, international recognition for Canada) and his mistakes (the GST; failure of Meech Lake and the Charlottetown accord).
When prime minister Pierre Trudeau's government was in trouble in 1984 and he refused to resign, Mr. Mulroney won the next election by a huge majority. Unheard of for the Progressive Conservative Party.
Mr. Mulroney won two consecutive majority governments. He was prime minister for almost a decade.
Is it happening again with Pierre's son, Justin? He is very unpopular for his overspending and other gaffs. If you believe the polls, and if the election were held today, the Conservatives would win. Then we would have Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as prime minister. Some think he is the "Canadian Trump." Strange coincidence or what?
The French have a saying that fits here: The more things change, the more they stay the same. History has lessons to teach that, if ignored, lead to disaster.
Dan Smith, Stoney Creek
Farewell to a statesman
I can imagine our departed for the heavens charismatic Brian Mulroney, who crushed monotone John Turner with a landslide 211 seat, eclipsing "Dief the Chief's" 208-seat victory, being welcomed on the other side by the Gipper and Iron Lady.
As a baby boomer, I recall that it was Mulroney's spirited speeches and memorable quip to Turner "you had an option, sir" that compelled me to make it to the ballet box in 1984.
Watching Mulroney's clashing with the Liberal Rat Pack, especially lively debating with MP Sheila Copps, on CPAC always had me anxiously tuning in for the start of question period.
While many dreaded his GST, the fact is it was at a much lower rate than the MST (manufacturer's sales tax) it replaced that in concert with his free trade agreement smashed through the walls U.S. protectionism bolstering Canadian exports to new highs.
Mulroney, a Cold War hawk, in his "defence white paper" proposed eight to 12 nuclear-propelled, Arctic-capable submarines. However, Jean Chrétien torpedoed that with his acquisition of four bargain priced, lease-to-own used British submarines that have been plagued with breakdowns and enormous repair costs that I would cite as "deterrent-redundant."
His singing "When Irish Eyes are Smiling" with ally president Ronald Reagan at the Shamrock Summit was most entertaining; however, his large, protracted deficits gave rise to Preston Manning's truly conservative Reform Party that along with rebel Lucien Bouchard's Bloc Québécois paved the way for Kim Campbell's inevitable defeat.
I remember statesmen Mulroney's eulogy to fighter pilot and president George H.W. Bush, which was most heartwarming indeed.
My condolences go out to Mulroney's wife of over a half century Mila, MPP Caroline Mulroney, and his family.
David C. Searle, Toronto
